# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 📘 PART A (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define characteristic strength of concrete. How is it statistically defined per IS 456:2000?**

**Model Answer:**

The **characteristic strength of concrete ($f_{ck}$)** is defined as the **compressive strength of concrete below which not more than 5% of test results are expected to fall**.

**Statistical Formulation:**
$$f_{ck} = f_m - 1.65 \cdot \sigma$$

Where:
- $f_m$ = Mean strength of test cubes (MPa)
- $\sigma$ = Standard deviation of test results (MPa)
- $1.65$ = Standard normal variate for **5% fractile** (95% confidence)

**Example:** For M25 concrete, $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa means the concrete mix is designed such that the probability of any cube test result falling below 25 MPa is less than 5%.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any THREE differences between Working Stress Method (WSM) and Limit State Method (LSM).**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | WSM | LSM |
|---|-----------|-----|-----|
| 1 | **Basis** | Elastic theory | Probability-based |
| 2 | **Factor of Safety** | Global (single) factor | Partial factors (for loads & materials separately) |
| 3 | **Serviceability** | Not explicitly checked | Explicitly checked (deflection, cracking) |
| 4 | **Steel Grade** | Fe 250 (Mild Steel) commonly | Fe 415 / Fe 500 (HYSD) |
| 5 | **Use Today** | Largely obsolete | Mandatory per IS 456:2000 |

---

## 📗 PART B (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Adapted]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the various grades of concrete and steel as per IS 456:2000. What is the significance of characteristic strength in design?**

**Model Solution:**

**Grades of Concrete (IS 456 Cl. 6.1):**

The code specifies concrete grades from **M10 to M80**, where "M" denotes the mix and the numerical value is the **characteristic compressive strength ($f_{ck}$) in MPa at 28 days** under standard cube testing (150 mm cube, IS 516).

| Category | Grades |
|----------|--------|
| **Ordinary Concrete** | M10, M15, M20, M25, M30, M35, M40, M45, M50 |
| **High-Strength Concrete** | M55, M60, M65, M70, M75, M80 |

**Grades of Steel (IS 1786):**

| Grade | Yield Strength ($f_y$) | Application |
|-------|----------------------|-------------|
| Fe 250 (Mild Steel) | 250 MPa | Small structures, stirrups |
| **Fe 415 (HYSD)** | **415 MPa** | **Most common in KTU syllabus** |
| Fe 500 (HYSD) | 500 MPa | Modern high-rise construction |
| Fe 550 (HYSD) | 550 MPa | Special heavy-load cases |

**Significance of Characteristic Strength:**

The characteristic strength provides a **statistical basis for safety**. Since concrete is a heterogeneous material with inherent variability, the design strength is based on the **5% fractile value**, ensuring that **95% of the produced concrete meets or exceeds the design assumption**. This probabilistic approach leads to:
1. **Consistent safety margins** across structures.
2. **Economical design** without over-conservatism.
3. **Reliable quality control** through systematic testing.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A reinforced concrete beam of width 250 mm and effective depth 500 mm is subjected to a factored bending moment of 200 kNm. Using M20 concrete and Fe 415 steel, determine whether the section can be designed as singly reinforced. If not, suggest a remedy.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $b = 250$ mm
- $d = 500$ mm
- $M_u = 200$ kNm = $200 \times 10^6$ N·mm
- $f_{ck} = 20$ MPa
- $f_y = 415$ MPa

**Step 1: Calculate the Limiting Moment of Resistance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{u,lim} &= 0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2 \\
\\
&= 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times (500)^2 \\
\\
&= 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times 250000 \\
\\
&= 172,500,000 \text{ N·mm} \\
\\
M_{u,lim} &= 172.5 \text{ kNm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compare $M_u$ with $M_{u,lim}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_u = 200 \text{ kNm} &> M_{u,lim} = 172.5 \text{ kNm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Conclusion**

Since $M_u > M_{u,lim}$, the section **CANNOT be designed as singly reinforced**. The applied moment exceeds the maximum moment that a singly reinforced section of these dimensions can resist.

**Step 4: Remedies (any ONE of the following):**

1. **Option 1 — Increase effective depth ($d$):**
   - Required $d$ for $M_u = 200$ kNm:
   - $d_{req} = \sqrt{\dfrac{M_u}{0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{200 \times 10^6}{0.138 \times 20 \times 250}} = 538$ mm
   - Adopt $d = 550$ mm.

2. **Option 2 — Increase width ($b$):**
   - $b_{req} = \dfrac{M_u}{0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot d^2} = \dfrac{200 \times 10^6}{0.138 \times 20 \times 250000} = 289.85$ mm
   - Adopt $b = 300$ mm.

3. **Option 3 — Use a Doubly Reinforced Section:**
   - Retain $b = 250$ mm and $d = 500$ mm.
   - Design compression steel ($A_{sc}$) and additional tension steel ($A_{st2}$).
   - This is the most economical when section dimensions are restricted.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> **Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. ❌ Forgetting to convert kNm to N·mm (multiply by $10^6$).
> 2. ❌ Using the wrong formula — $0.138$ is ONLY for **Fe 415** with **$f_{ck} \le$ M50**. For other steel grades, the coefficient changes.
> 3. ❌ Recommending increasing $b$ or $d$ without computing the **required** values.
> 4. ❌ Forgetting to mention the **doubly reinforced section** as an alternative.
> 5. ❌ Not showing the numerical comparison $M_u > M_{u,lim}$ clearly.

---

### **Question B (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Adapted]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**State and explain the various limit states considered in the design of RCC structures per IS 456:2000.**

**Model Solution:**

The **Limit State Method (LSM)** defines a **limit state** as a condition beyond which the structure **ceases to perform its intended function**. IS 456:2000 classifies limit states into two main categories:

### **1. Limit State of Collapse (Strength) — Cl. 35**

Ensures that the structure does not collapse under the **worst combination of loads**. Sub-limit states include:

| Sub-Limit State | Failure Mode |
|----------------|--------------|
| Flexure (tension failure) | Yielding of steel, crushing of concrete |
| Compression | Crushing of concrete |
| Shear | Diagonal tension cracking |
| Torsion | Combined shear + torsion failure |
| Axial Load + Bending | Eccentric loading failure |

**Design Equation:**
$$R_d \geq S_d$$

Where:
- $R_d$ = Design resistance (factored strength)
- $S_d$ = Design action (factored load effect)

### **2. Limit State of Serviceability — Cl. 35.3**

Ensures the structure remains **comfortable and functional** under service loads. Sub-limit states include:

| Sub-Limit State | Acceptance Criterion |
|----------------|---------------------|
| Deflection | $\delta \leq \dfrac{L}{250}$ (per IS 456) |
| Cracking | Crack width $\leq 0.3$ mm (general) |
| Vibration | Natural frequency $\geq 5$ Hz for floors |

### **Design Philosophy Summary**

The **two-tier check** in LSM:
1. **First:** Ensure **strength** (limit state of collapse).
2. **Then:** Ensure **comfort & durability** (limit state of serviceability).

> The **partial safety factor** for materials in collapse state ($\gamma_m = 1.5$ for concrete, $1.15$ for steel) is **higher** than in serviceability ($\gamma_m = 1.0$) — reflecting increased safety requirement at ultimate conditions.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A concrete mix has a mean strength of 35 MPa and standard deviation of 5 MPa. Determine (i) the characteristic strength, and (ii) the design strength using LSM. Also identify the grade of concrete.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $f_m = 35$ MPa
- $\sigma = 5$ MPa

**Part (i): Characteristic Strength**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{ck} &= f_m - 1.65 \cdot \sigma \\
\\
&= 35 - 1.65 \times 5 \\
\\
&= 35 - 8.25 \\
\\
f_{ck} &= 26.75 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii): Design Strength (LSM)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_d &= \frac{f_{ck}}{\gamma_c} = \frac{26.75}{1.5} \\
\\
f_d &= 17.83 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Identification of Concrete Grade:**

Since $f_{ck} = 26.75$ MPa, and standard grades are M25 ($f_{ck} = 25$) and M30 ($f_{ck} = 30$):
- The mix is **between M25 and M30** in performance.
- **Conservatively designated as M25 grade** (the next higher standard grade is M30, but 26.75 < 30, so we conservatively adopt M25).

> [!WARNING] **KTU Pitfall Callout**
> **Valuation Mistake Alert:**
> 1. ❌ Many students wrongly take $1.64$ instead of $1.65$ — both are accepted, but **$1.65$ is the IS 456 standard**.
> 2. ❌ Forgetting to identify the grade based on $f_{ck}$ value — examiners **explicitly ask** for grade identification.
> 3. ❌ Confusing characteristic strength with design strength in the final answer.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- ✅ **IS 456:2000** is the **4th Revision** of the Indian Standard for Plain and Reinforced Concrete.
- ✅ **Primary method:** Limit State Method (LSM). **Alternative:** Working Stress Method (WSM).
- ✅ **Characteristic strength formula:** $f_{ck} = f_m - 1.65 \sigma$ (always remember the constant **1.65**).
- ✅ **Partial safety factors (LSM):** $\gamma_c = 1.5$, $\gamma_s = 1.15$.
- ✅ **Concrete grades** start from M10; KTU exam uses M20, M25, M30 most often.
- ✅ **Steel grades:** Fe 415 is the most common; Fe 500 is the modern choice.
- ✅ **Limiting moment formula** for Fe 415 + M20–M50: $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$.
- ✅ **5 exposure conditions:** Mild, Moderate, Severe, Very Severe, Extreme.
- ✅ **Min. % tension steel** (Fe 415): $\dfrac{0.85}{f_y} \cdot bd$.
- ✅ **Max. % tension steel:** $4\%$ of gross cross-section.
- ✅ **Nominal cover** ranges from **20 mm (mild)** to **75 mm (extreme)** exposure.
- ✅ **If $M_u > M_{u,lim}$:** either redesign section dimensions or use a **doubly reinforced section**.

> [!NOTE] **Last-Minute Memory Aid**
> If you remember just **5 numbers**, remember these:
> 1. $1.65$ → characteristic strength fractile
> 2. $1.5$ → $\gamma_c$ for concrete
> 3. $1.15$ → $\gamma_s$ for steel
> 4. $0.138$ → $M_{u,lim}$ coefficient (Fe 415)
> 5. $0.48$ → $x_{u,max}/d$ for Fe 415

**Best of luck for your KTU exams! 🎓**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limit State of Collapse in Flexure: Singly Reinforced Rectangular Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET404 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Limit State of Collapse in Flexure: Singly Reinforced Rectangular Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 / IS 456:2000 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limit State of Collapse in Flexure (as per IS 456:2000, Cl. 38.1)**
> A state at which the structural element reaches its **ultimate load-carrying capacity** in bending such that it fails to perform its intended function, leading to collapse. The design ensures that the section possesses adequate **flexural strength** $M_u$ to resist the factored design moment $M$ acting on it, while satisfying the condition: $M_u \geq M$ (factored).

In a **Singly Reinforced Rectangular Beam**, steel reinforcement is provided **only in the tension zone** to resist the bending tensile stresses, while the concrete in the compression zone resists the bending compressive stresses. The beam cross-section is rectangular, and the main longitudinal tension steel is placed near the bottom face.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "See-Saw with Weights" Intuition

Imagine a **see-saw (a lever)** where a heavy adult sits at one end (compression from a downward load $w$) and a basket is tied to the other end (tension side). As the adult pushes down, the plank tries to rotate about a pivot point — the **neutral axis**. The top of the plank gets crushed (compression — concrete handles this), while the bottom stretches apart (tension — steel reinforcement holds it together). The **neutral axis** is the imaginary line inside the beam where fibres experience **zero strain** (neither stretch nor compress). The **limit state of collapse** is reached when either the top concrete crushes or the bottom steel snaps — whichever happens first. We design so that steel yields *first* (giving warning before failure) — this is the **under-reinforced section** philosophy.

## 1.3 Key Design Philosophy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Flexural Design (IS 456:2000)**
> A flexural member **must always be designed as under-reinforced**, so that the tension steel reaches its yield strain **before** the concrete reaches its ultimate crushing strain. This ensures a **ductile failure** with visible warning (excessive cracking and deflection) — preventing sudden, catastrophic collapse.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Material Grades

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concrete compressive strength grade | $f_{ck}$ | **M20, M25, M30, M35, M40, M45, M50** | N/mm² |
| Steel yield strength grade | $f_y$ | **Fe415, Fe500, Fe550** | N/mm² |
| Partial safety factor for concrete | $\gamma_m$ (concrete) | **1.50** | dimensionless |
| Partial safety factor for steel | $\gamma_m$ (steel) | **1.15** | dimensionless |
| Maximum usable strain in concrete | $\varepsilon_{cu}$ | **0.0035** | dimensionless |
| Design compressive stress in concrete (rectangular block) | $0.67 f_{ck}$ | For $f_{ck} \leq 35$ N/mm² | N/mm² |
| Design yield stress in steel | $0.87 f_y$ | As per IS 456:2000 | N/mm² |
| Modulus of elasticity of steel | $E_s$ | **2.0 × 10⁵** | N/mm² |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions for Limit State Design in Flexure
*(IS 456:2000, Clause 38.1)*

> [!NOTE]
> These assumptions form the bedrock of every numerical problem you will solve in Module 1. Memorize them in order.

- **Assumption 1 — Plane Sections Remain Plane:** Sections normal to the axis of bending that were plane before bending remain plane after bending. This implies a **linear strain distribution** across the depth of the section.
- **Assumption 2 — Maximum Strain in Concrete:** The maximum usable compressive strain at the extreme compression fibre is taken as **$\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$** for all grades of concrete.
- **Assumption 3 — Tensile Strength of Concrete Ignored:** The tensile strength of concrete is neglected in flexural design, since concrete is weak in tension (≈ 1/10 of its compressive strength).
- **Assumption 4 — Perfect Bond:** There is perfect bond (monolithic action) between steel and the surrounding concrete — they strain together at the same level.
- **Assumption 5 — Stress-Strain Curve for Concrete:** For **$f_{ck} \leq 35$ N/mm²**, the design stress-strain curve is a **rectangular-parabolic** distribution. The actual parabolic stress block is replaced by an **equivalent rectangular stress block** (Whitney's Equivalent Block) for design simplicity.
- **Assumption 6 — Stress-Strain Curve for Steel:** The stress-strain relationship for steel is an **idealized elasto-plastic** (bi-linear) curve. The design yield stress is **$0.87 f_y$**, accounting for the partial safety factor $\gamma_{ms} = 1.15$.

## 2.2 Equivalent Rectangular Stress Block (Whitney's Block)

For design purposes, the actual complex parabolic stress distribution in the compression zone is **idealized** into a **uniform rectangular block** with the following properties (per IS 456:2000, Cl. 39.1 and Fig. 4 of IS 456):

| Property | For $f_{ck} \leq 35$ N/mm² | For $f_{ck} > 35$ N/mm² |
|---|---|---|
| Shape of stress block | Rectangular | Parabolic-rectangular |
| Uniform compressive stress | $0.67 f_{ck}$ | $0.67 f_{ck}$ (approx) |
| Depth of stress block | $a = 0.42 x_u$ | $a = 0.42 x_u$ (approx) |

Here, $x_u$ is the **depth of the actual neutral axis**, and $a$ is the **depth of the equivalent rectangular stress block**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for all numerical problems on this topic. Revise it before every test.

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Depth of neutral axis | $x_u = \dfrac{0.87 f_y A_{st}}{0.36 f_{ck}\, b}$ | From force equilibrium $C = T$ |
| 2 | Limiting depth ratio (Fe415) | $x_{u,\max}/d = 0.48$ | When $f_y = 415$ N/mm² |
| 3 | Limiting depth ratio (Fe500) | $x_{u,\max}/d = 0.46$ | When $f_y = 500$ N/mm² |
| 4 | Limiting depth ratio (Fe550) | $x_{u,\max}/d = 0.44$ | When $f_y = 550$ N/mm² |
| 5 | Limiting moment of resistance | $M_{u,\lim} = 0.36\, \dfrac{x_{u,\max}}{d} \left(1 - 0.42\, \dfrac{x_{u,\max}}{d}\right) b\, d^2 f_{ck}$ | General form |
| 6 | $M_{u,\lim}$ for Fe415 | $M_{u,\lim} = 0.138\, b\, d^2 f_{ck}$ | Direct numerical form |
| 7 | $M_{u,\lim}$ for Fe500 | $M_{u,\lim} = 0.1336\, b\, d^2 f_{ck}$ | Direct numerical form |
| 8 | $M_{u,\lim}$ for Fe550 | $M_{u,\lim} = 0.129\, b\, d^2 f_{ck}$ | Direct numerical form |
| 9 | Actual moment of resistance | $M_u = 0.87 f_y A_{st}\, d \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{st} f_y}{b\, d\, f_{ck}}\right)$ | Simplified form for under-reinforced |
| 10 | Lever arm | $z = d\left(1 - \dfrac{0.42 x_u}{d}\right) = d - 0.42 x_u$ | For $f_{ck} \leq 35$ N/mm² |
| 11 | Compressive force | $C = 0.36 f_{ck}\, b\, x_u$ | From rectangular block |
| 12 | Tensile force | $T = 0.87 f_y A_{st}$ | From steel area |

## 2.4 Classification of Flexural Sections

The behaviour of a singly reinforced rectangular beam under flexure is classified based on the **actual depth of the neutral axis** ($x_u$) compared to the **limiting depth** ($x_{u,\max}$):

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Categories of Beams**

- **Balanced Section:** $x_u = x_{u,\max}$ — Both concrete crushing and steel yielding occur **simultaneously** at the ultimate load. This is a *theoretical* condition — practically difficult to achieve.
- **Under-Reinforced Section:** $x_u < x_{u,\max}$ — Tension steel reaches its yield strain ($0.87 f_y$) **before** concrete reaches its ultimate crushing strain ($0.0035$). The beam fails by **excessive yielding of steel**, accompanied by wide cracks and large deflections. This is the **preferred and recommended** design.
- **Over-Reinforced Section:** $x_u > x_{u,\max}$ — Concrete reaches its crushing strain **before** steel yields. Failure is **sudden and brittle (shear-like)** with no warning. **IS 456:2000 prohibits** this design, as it offers no ductility.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

This theoretical framework is the **foundation of every building, bridge, and structural slab** ever designed. The limit state method ensures:
- **Economy** — exact quantification of safety margins using partial safety factors.
- **Safety** — through ductile failure mechanism, occupants get visual warning.
- **Reliability** — probabilistic calibration of material strengths ($f_{ck}, f_y$).
- **Versatility** — applicable to RC frames, water tanks, chimneys, retaining walls, etc.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Depth of Neutral Axis ($x_u$) from Force Equilibrium

**Statement:** In a singly reinforced rectangular beam at the limit state of collapse in flexure, the total compressive force in concrete equals the total tensile force in steel.

**Step 1: Define the cross-section parameters.**
- Width of beam: $b$
- Effective depth: $d$ (distance from extreme compression fibre to centroid of tension steel)
- Overall depth: $D$
- Area of tension steel: $A_{st}$
- Depth of neutral axis: $x_u$
- Depth of equivalent rectangular stress block: $a = 0.42 x_u$

**Step 2: Calculate the compressive force in concrete ($C$).**

The equivalent rectangular stress block has:
- Uniform stress intensity: $0.67 f_{ck}$
- Area of the block: $b \times a = b \times 0.42 x_u$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \text{Stress} \times \text{Area} \\[4pt]
C &= (0.67 f_{ck}) \times (b \times 0.42 x_u) \\[4pt]
C &= 0.67 \times 0.42 \times f_{ck} \times b \times x_u \\[4pt]
C &= 0.2814\, f_{ck}\, b\, x_u
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logical Note:** IS 456:2000 rounds $0.67 \times 0.42 = 0.2814 \approx 0.36$ (with $f_{ck}$ replaced by $1.0\, f_{ck}$ in simplified design charts). We will adopt the standard textbook simplification $C = 0.36 f_{ck}\, b\, x_u$ for consistency with KTU textbooks.

**Step 3: Calculate the tensile force in steel ($T$).**

For an idealized elasto-plastic steel, the design stress is $0.87 f_y$ (already includes $\gamma_{ms} = 1.15$):

$$
T = 0.87 f_y \times A_{st}
$$

**Step 4: Apply force equilibrium $C = T$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.36 f_{ck}\, b\, x_u &= 0.87 f_y A_{st} \\[6pt]
x_u &= \frac{0.87 f_y A_{st}}{0.36 f_{ck}\, b}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Result:** This is the **fundamental equation** used to compute the depth of the neutral axis for any singly reinforced rectangular beam in KTU exams.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Limiting Depth of Neutral Axis ($x_{u,\max}$)

**Statement:** For an under-reinforced design, we need the **maximum permissible** $x_u$ at which steel reaches its yield strain **exactly when** concrete reaches its crushing strain.

**Step 1: Write the linear strain compatibility.**

From similar triangles in the strain diagram (assuming plane sections remain plane):

$$
\frac{x_{u,\max}}{d} = \frac{0.0035}{0.0035 + \varepsilon_{y}^{*}}
$$

where $\varepsilon_{y}^{*}$ is the design yield strain of steel including partial safety factor consideration.

**Step 2: Compute design yield strain $\varepsilon_{y}^{*}$.**

The design yield stress of steel is $0.87 f_y$. Therefore, the corresponding strain (using Hooke's law) is:

$$
\varepsilon_{y}^{*} = \frac{0.87 f_y}{E_s} = \frac{0.87 f_y}{2.0 \times 10^{5}}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute for Fe415 ($f_y = 415$ N/mm²).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{y}^{*} &= \frac{0.87 \times 415}{2.0 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{361.05}{200000} \\[4pt]
\varepsilon_{y}^{*} &= 0.001805
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the limiting depth ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{x_{u,\max}}{d} &= \frac{0.0035}{0.0035 + 0.001805} \\[6pt]
\frac{x_{u,\max}}{d} &= \frac{0.0035}{0.005305} \\[6pt]
\frac{x_{u,\max}}{d} &= 0.48
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Result for Fe415:** $x_{u,\max} = 0.48\, d$ (Adopted in IS 456:2000, Cl. 38.1)

> **Final Result for Fe500 ($f_y = 500$ N/mm²):** $x_{u,\max} = 0.46\, d$
> **Final Result for Fe550 ($f_y = 550$ N/mm²):** $x_{u,\max} = 0.44\, d$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Limiting Moment of Resistance ($M_{u,\lim}$)

**Statement:** The maximum moment of resistance that a *singly reinforced balanced section* can carry, used as a design benchmark to ensure under-reinforced behaviour.

**Step 1: Take moments of the compressive force about the tension steel centroid.**

The lever arm from the line of action of $C$ to the centroid of tension steel is:

$$
z = d - \frac{a}{2} = d - \frac{0.42 x_u}{2} = d \left(1 - 0.42\, \frac{x_u}{d}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Write the moment equation.**

$$
M_u = C \times z = (0.36 f_{ck}\, b\, x_u) \times d \left(1 - 0.42\, \frac{x_u}{d}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the limiting depth ratio for Fe415 ($x_{u,\max}/d = 0.48$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{u,\lim} &= 0.36 f_{ck}\, b\, d^2 \times 0.48 \times \left(1 - 0.42 \times 0.48\right) \\[6pt]
M_{u,\lim} &= 0.36 f_{ck}\, b\, d^2 \times 0.48 \times (1 - 0.2016) \\[6pt]
M_{u,\lim} &= 0.36 f_{ck}\, b\, d^2 \times 0.48 \times 0.7984 \\[6pt]
M_{u,\lim} &= 0.138\, f_{ck}\, b\, d^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Result for Fe415:** $M_{u,\lim} = 0.138\, b\, d^2 f_{ck}$

**Step 4: Verify for Fe500 ($x_{u,\max}/d = 0.46$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{u,\lim} &= 0.36 f_{ck}\, b\, d^2 \times 0.46 \times (1 - 0.42 \times 0.46) \\[6pt]
M_{u,\lim} &= 0.36 f_{ck}\, b\, d^2 \times 0.46 \times 0.8068 \\[6pt]
M_{u,\lim} &= 0.1336\, f_{ck}\, b\, d^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Result for Fe500:** $M_{u,\lim} = 0.1336\, b\, d^2 f_{ck}$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Moment of Resistance of an Under-Reinforced Section

**Statement:** When $A_{st} < A_{st,\lim}$ (under-reinforced), the steel yields first and the section fails by yielding of steel. The moment of resistance is governed by the **tensile force** alone.

**Step 1: At failure, the stress in steel is $0.87 f_y$ (yielded).**

$$
T = 0.87 f_y A_{st}
$$

**Step 2: Lever arm expression.**

For an under-reinforced section, we cannot directly use the $x_u$ derived for the balanced case. We must solve for $x_u$ first from $C = T$, then compute $z$.

**Step 3: Derive a closed-form $M_u$ expression.**

Using $x_u = \dfrac{0.87 f_y A_{st}}{0.36 f_{ck}\, b}$, and substituting into the moment equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_u &= 0.87 f_y A_{st} \times d \left(1 - \frac{0.42 \times 0.87 f_y A_{st}}{0.36 f_{ck}\, b\, d}\right) \\[6pt]
M_u &= 0.87 f_y A_{st}\, d \left(1 - \frac{0.87 f_y A_{st}}{f_{ck}\, b\, d}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Final Result:** This is the **fundamental design equation** used to compute the moment of resistance of any under-reinforced singly reinforced rectangular beam in KTU problems.

---

## 3.5 Design Strategy — Step-by-Step Workflow

| Step | Action | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compute factored design moment $M = 1.5 M_{DL} + 1.5 M_{LL}$ | $M$ in N·mm |
| 2 | Assume overall depth $D$ and width $b$ (or read from problem) | $b$, $D$ |
| 3 | Compute effective depth $d = D - \text{effective cover}$ | $d$ in mm |
| 4 | Compute $M_{u,\lim} = 0.138\, b\, d^2 f_{ck}$ (for Fe415) | $M_{u,\lim}$ in N·mm |
| 5 | **Compare:** If $M \leq M_{u,\lim}$ → **Singly reinforced design is sufficient** | Proceed |
| 6 | Compute $A_{st}$ from $M_u = 0.87 f_y A_{st}\, d\, (1 - A_{st} f_y / b d f_{ck})$ | $A_{st}$ in mm² |
| 7 | **Verify:** $x_u < x_{u,\max}$ to confirm under-reinforced behaviour | Check |
| 8 | Select reinforcement bars (12 mm ϕ, 16 mm ϕ, etc.) | Spacing/diameter |
