# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**State and explain the assumptions made in the limit state design of RC beams for flexure as per IS 456:2000.**

**Model Answer:**
The Limit State Method of design as per **IS 456:2000 (Clause 38.1)** adopts the following five assumptions for flexural analysis of RC sections:

1. **Plane sections normal to the axis remain plane after bending**, leading to a **linear strain distribution** across the depth of the section.
2. The **maximum strain in concrete at the extreme compression fibre** is limited to **0.0035**.
3. The **maximum useful strain in the tension reinforcement** is taken such that the design strength is 0.87 f_y (this is satisfied when strain ≥ 0.002 + f_y / 1.15 E_s).
4. The **compressive stress distribution in concrete** is idealised as a **rectangular stress block** of depth **0.42 x_u** with an **uniform stress of 0.446 f_ck** (= 0.67 f_ck / 1.5).
5. **The tensile strength of concrete is neglected** — all tensile force is resisted by steel reinforcement only.

These assumptions allow the complex non-linear behaviour of concrete to be reduced to a simple rectangular stress block, enabling closed-form design equations.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish clearly between a balanced, under-reinforced and over-reinforced RCC section. Why is over-reinforced design prohibited by IS 456?**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Under-Reinforced | Balanced | Over-Reinforced |
|---|---|---|---|
| x_u vs x_u,max | x_u < x_u,max | x_u = x_u,max | x_u > x_u,max |
| Failure mode | Steel yields first | Simultaneous | Concrete crushes first |
| Ductility | High (ductile) | Moderate | Very low (brittle) |
| Warning signs | Visible cracks, deflections | None | Sudden collapse |

**Reason for prohibition:** An over-reinforced section fails **without warning** because concrete — a brittle material — crushes explosively before steel yields. The crack widths remain narrow, deflections are small, and occupants receive **no advance indication** of impending failure. IS 456:2000 enforces a ductile failure philosophy to safeguard life and property; hence the design is restricted such that **x_u ≤ x_u,max** (Cl. 38.1, Note 2). The design moment is **capped at M_u,lim** even if the section's actual resistance is higher.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Module 1 Pattern]**

**(a)** A reinforced concrete rectangular beam has an **effective span of 6 m**, simply supported, and carries a **uniformly distributed load of 30 kN/m** (inclusive of self-weight). Design the section for flexure using M20 concrete and Fe 415 steel. Take b = 230 mm and assume d = 450 mm. Check whether the section is safe. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **area of steel required** for a rectangular beam of **b = 250 mm, d = 500 mm** subjected to a **factored bending moment of 200 kN·m**. Use M25 concrete and Fe 500 steel. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Step 1:** Calculate the **factored design moment**.

$$
M_u = 1.5 \times w \times \frac{L^{2}}{8} = 1.5 \times 30 \times \frac{6^{2}}{8} = 202.5 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **limiting moment of resistance** for Fe 415.

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^{2} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 230 \times 450^{2}
$$

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 230 \times 202\,500 = 128.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm} = 128.6 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare M_u with M_u,lim.

$$
M_u = 202.5 \text{ kN·m} \; > \; M_{u,lim} = 128.6 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 4:** **Conclusion:** The given section is **inadequate**. The applied factored moment exceeds the limiting moment of resistance, so the section must be **redesigned** (e.g., by increasing d or b, or by adopting a doubly reinforced section). The section is **NOT safe** as it stands.

> ⚠️ **This type of problem tests the student's ability to recognise that the section is over-reinforced in its current dimensions and apply the M_u,lim cap correctly.**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Step 1:** Compute **M_u,lim** for Fe 500.

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.133 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^{2} = 0.133 \times 25 \times 250 \times 500^{2}
$$

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.133 \times 25 \times 250 \times 250\,000 = 207.8 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm} = 207.8 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 2:** Compare applied moment.

$$
M_u = 200 \text{ kN·m} \; < \; M_{u,lim} = 207.8 \text{ kN·m}
$$

Since M_u < M_u,lim, the section is **under-reinforced** → use direct steel area formula.

**Step 3:** Compute **lever arm** using the design approximation (or, more rigorously, solve the quadratic for x_u).

Approximate lever arm z ≈ 0.87 d (an initial estimate, then refined):

$$
z_{initial} = 0.87 \times 500 = 435 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute **A_st**.

$$
A_{st} = \frac{M_u}{0.87 \, f_y \, z} = \frac{200 \times 10^{6}}{0.87 \times 500 \times 435} = \frac{200 \times 10^{6}}{189\,225}
$$

$$
A_{st} = 1057 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 5:** Refine lever arm by computing x_u.

$$
x_u = \frac{0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b} = \frac{0.87 \times 500 \times 1057}{0.36 \times 25 \times 250} = \frac{459\,795}{2250} = 204.4 \text{ mm}
$$

Check: x_u = 204.4 mm vs x_u,max = 0.46 × 500 = 230 mm → **OK**.

**Step 6:** Refine z and recompute A_st.

$$
z = 500 - 0.42 \times 204.4 = 500 - 85.85 = 414.15 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
A_{st} = \frac{200 \times 10^{6}}{0.87 \times 500 \times 414.15} = \frac{200 \times 10^{6}}{180\,155} = 1110 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 7:** Provide the final answer.

$$
\boxed{\,A_{st,required} \approx 1110 \text{ mm}^{2}\,}
$$

Provide 4 bars of 20 mm ϕ (A_st = 1256 mm²) or 3 bars of 22 mm ϕ (A_st = 1140 mm²) — both satisfy the requirement.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative to Question A*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, ESE Module 1 Pattern]**

**(a)** A rectangular RC beam of **b = 300 mm** and **d = 550 mm** is reinforced with **6 bars of 16 mm ϕ (HYSD Fe 415)**. Use **M25 concrete**. Calculate the **depth of neutral axis** and the **moment of resistance** of the section. Check whether the section is under-reinforced. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same section as in part (a), if the **factored applied moment is 220 kN·m**, determine whether the section is **safe**. If not, suggest a remedy. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Step 1:** Compute A_st.

$$
A_{st} = 6 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 16^{2} = 6 \times 201.06 = 1206.4 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute x_u.

$$
x_u = \frac{0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b} = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 1206.4}{0.36 \times 25 \times 300}
$$

$$
x_u = \frac{435\,510}{2700} = 161.3 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute x_u,max (for Fe 415).

$$
x_{u,max} = 0.48 \times 550 = 264 \text{ mm}
$$

Since x_u = 161.3 mm < x_u,max = 264 mm, the section is **under-reinforced** ✓.

**Step 4:** Compute lever arm.

$$
z = 550 - 0.42 \times 161.3 = 550 - 67.75 = 482.25 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute M_u.

$$
M_u = 0.87 \times 415 \times 1206.4 \times 482.25 = 209.9 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,M_u \approx 210 \text{ kN·m}\,}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Step 1:** Compare M_u (from part a) with applied M.

$$
M_u = 210 \text{ kN·m} \; < \; M_{applied} = 220 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **shortfall**.

$$
\Delta M = 220 - 210 = 10 \text{ kN·m} \;\; (\approx 4.8\% \text{ deficit})
$$

**Step 3:** **Conclusion:** The section is **marginally unsafe** and requires modification.

**Step 4:** **Remedies (any one acceptable):**

- **Option A:** Increase effective depth d. Required d is found from M_u,lim = 0.138 × 25 × 300 × d² ≥ 220 × 10⁶ → d² ≥ 212 533 → d ≥ 461 mm. Since current d = 550 mm is already > 461 mm, **M_u,lim check** is the actual constraint:
  $$ M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 25 \times 300 \times 550^{2} = 313.2 \text{ kN·m} $$
  Since M_u,lim > 220 kN·m, the section **can be redesigned to be safe** simply by providing **more steel area** (A_st).

- **Option B:** Provide additional steel. Required A_st for M_u = 220 kN·m:
  $$ A_{st,req} = \frac{220 \times 10^{6}}{0.87 \times 415 \times 482.25} \approx 1264 \text{ mm}^{2} $$
  This can be provided by **3 bars of 16 mm ϕ + 3 bars of 18 mm ϕ** (A_st = 603 + 763 = 1366 mm²).

> ✅ The **best practical solution** is Option B (add 1 or 2 more bars) since the section depth is already adequate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.87 factor** for steel design stress (a 13% error).
> 2. **Using 0.45 f_ck** instead of 0.36 f_ck for compressive force (a 25% error — *this is the #1 mistake in KTU board exams*).
> 3. **Using 0.53d for Fe 415** — this value applies only to **Fe 250 (Mild Steel)**. For Fe 415, always use **0.48d**.
> 4. **Not comparing x_u with x_u,max** — failing to identify the section as over-reinforced and wrongly applying the M_u formula instead of M_u,lim.
> 5. **Units mix-up** — mixing mm and m in the lever arm computation. Always convert M to **N·mm** by multiplying kN·m by 10⁶.
> 6. **Forgetting to multiply UDL by partial safety factor 1.5** when converting working load to factored load.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Viva & Board Exams:**

- ✏️ The **two master equations** of LSM flexure:
  - **C = T** → x_u = (0.87 f_y A_st) / (0.36 f_ck b)
  - **M_u = 0.87 f_y A_st (d − 0.42 x_u)** = **0.36 f_ck b x_u (d − 0.42 x_u)**

- ✏️ **Critical assumption to memorise:** Maximum concrete strain = **0.0035**; rectangular stress block with average stress **0.446 f_ck** over depth **0.42 x_u**.

- ✏️ **Limiting NA depth (x_u,max):**
  - Fe 250 → **0.53 d**
  - Fe 415 → **0.48 d**
  - Fe 500 → **0.46 d**

- ✏️ **Limiting Moment factors (Q_lim):**
  - Fe 250 → **0.148**
  - Fe 415 → **0.138**
  - Fe 500 → **0.133**

- ✏️ **Three failure modes** — Under (safe, ductile) / Balanced (boundary) / Over (forbidden, brittle). Always verify x_u < x_u,max.

- ✏️ **Two key design stresses** — Concrete: **0.446 f_ck**; Steel: **0.87 f_y** (the 0.87 factor accounts for partial safety γ_m = 1.15).

- ✏️ **Partial safety factor for loads** = **1.5** (DL + LL). Always multiply working loads by 1.5 to obtain factored design loads.

- ✏️ **Lever arm formula z = d − 0.42 x_u** is **derived from the centroid of the rectangular stress block** at 0.42 x_u from the extreme compression fibre.

- ✏️ For **Fe 415**, the most common value of x_u,max/d used in numerical problems is **0.48** — use this unless the problem specifies Fe 250 or Fe 500 explicitly.

- ✏️ **If x_u > x_u,max** → cap M_u at M_u,lim and recommend **redesign** (increase d, increase b, or use a doubly reinforced section).

- ✏️ **Final check before submitting an answer script:** Verify units (N, mm, N·mm, kN·m), state all assumptions from IS 456:2000 Cl. 38.1, and explicitly mention whether the section is under/balanced/over-reinforced.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limit State of Collapse in Shear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET404 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Limit State of Collapse in Shear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (IS 456:2000 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limit State of Collapse in Shear** is a state at which a structural member fails due to the combined effect of bending moment and transverse (shear) force, leading to diagonal cracking, diagonal compression failure, or shear compression failure of the concrete.

According to **IS 456:2000 (Clause 39 and 40)**, a reinforced concrete member is considered safe against shear when the following condition is satisfied:

$$\tau_v \leq \tau_{c,\text{max}} \quad \text{(Nominal Shear Stress Check)}$$

And when the design shear strength of concrete alone is insufficient:

$$V_u > V_c \implies \text{Shear Reinforcement is required}$$

Where:
- $\tau_v$ = Nominal shear stress at the critical section
- $\tau_{c,\text{max}}$ = Maximum permissible shear stress (Table 20, IS 456)
- $V_u$ = Factored shear force at the section
- $V_c$ = Shear resistance of concrete alone

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Wooden Plank & Saw Effect**

Imagine a wooden plank supported at both ends. If you place a heavy weight in the middle, the plank bends. But the fibres of the wood don't just bend — they try to **slide past each other** along diagonal lines (think of how a saw cuts wood — diagonally!). This sliding action is exactly what **shear force** does inside a concrete beam.

In Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC):
- **Concrete** is strong in compression but weak in tension along diagonal planes.
- **Steel stirrups** (loops of rebar placed vertically) act like "seatbelts" that hold the concrete together against this diagonal sliding.
- Without stirrups, the beam would suddenly develop **diagonal cracks** (45° cracks) and collapse — often without warning. This is called a **brittle shear failure** — extremely dangerous!

The **Limit State of Collapse in Shear** ensures we design stirrups strong enough and closely spaced enough to prevent this sudden, catastrophic failure mode.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Values to Remember (IS 456:2000)**

- Grade of concrete considered: **M20 to M80** (most common: M20, M25, M30)
- Grade of steel (HYSD bars): **Fe 415** (most common) and **Fe 500**
- Maximum shear stress check is done using table values (no overstressing permitted)
- Concrete shear strength $\tau_c$ is read directly from **Table 19 of IS 456:2000**
- Maximum permissible shear stress $\tau_{c,\text{max}}$ is read from **Table 20 of IS 456:2000**

---

## 1.4 Difference: Shear vs. Flexural Failure

| Property | Flexural Failure | Shear Failure |
|----------|------------------|---------------|
| Crack orientation | Vertical (perpendicular to beam axis) | Diagonal (≈ 45° to beam axis) |
| Failure type | Ductile (warning cracks appear) | **Brittle** (sudden, no warning) |
| Primary reinforcement | Main longitudinal bars | **Stirrups / Bent-up bars** |
| Design philosophy | Prevent yielding of steel | Prevent diagonal cracking |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Emphasis**: Shear failure is **brittle** and therefore must be designed against with greater caution. KTU questions often test whether students correctly identify the critical section and apply minimum shear reinforcement rules.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Design Philosophy (IS 456 Approach)

The IS 456:2000 design procedure for shear follows a strict **hierarchical logic**:

### Step 1 — Calculate the **Factored Shear Force ($V_u$)**
Using the load combinations from IS 456 (Clause 36.4.1):
$$V_u = 1.5 \left( V_D + V_L \right)$$

Where $V_D$ = Dead load shear, $V_L$ = Live load shear.

### Step 2 — Identify the **Critical Section for Shear**
According to **Clause 22.6.2 of IS 456:2000**:
- For **uniformly loaded beams**: critical section is at a distance equal to the **effective depth ($d$)** from the face of the support.
- For **concentrated load near support**: when the concentrated load is within a distance $d$ from the support, the section at the face of the support is used to compute $V_u$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a HIGH-YIELD KTU question point!** Students frequently lose marks by computing $V_u$ at mid-span instead of at the critical section.

### Step 3 — Compute **Nominal Shear Stress ($\tau_v$)**
$$\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b \cdot d}$$

Where:
- $b$ = breadth of the rectangular section (or web width for flanged sections)
- $d$ = effective depth

**Units**: $V_u$ in **N**, $b$ and $d$ in **mm** → $\tau_v$ in **N/mm² (MPa)**

### Step 4 — Apply the **Upper Limit Check** (Clause 40.2.3)
The maximum permissible nominal shear stress $\tau_{c,\text{max}}$ is read from **Table 20 of IS 456** based on the **concrete grade**.

**Condition**: $\tau_v \leq \tau_{c,\text{max}}$ (otherwise section must be increased)

### Step 5 — Determine **Shear Strength of Concrete ($V_c$)**
$$V_c = \tau_c \cdot b \cdot d$$

Where $\tau_c$ is read from **Table 19 of IS 456** based on:
- Grade of concrete
- Percentage of longitudinal tension steel reinforcement $\left( p_t = \frac{100 A_{st}}{bd} \right)$

### Step 6 — Calculate **Shear to be Carried by Reinforcement ($V_{us}$)**
$$V_{us} = V_u - V_c = V_u - \tau_c \cdot b \cdot d$$

### Step 7 — Design the **Shear Reinforcement**

For **vertical stirrups** (most common):
$$V_{us} = \frac{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sv} \cdot d}{s_v}$$

Where:
- $f_y$ = yield strength of stirrup steel (**415 MPa** for Fe 415)
- $A_{sv}$ = total cross-sectional area of stirrup legs (e.g., for 2-legged 8 mm $\phi$ stirrup, $A_{sv} = 2 \times 50.3 = 100.6 \text{ mm}^2$)
- $s_v$ = spacing of stirrups along the beam length

Solving for spacing:
$$s_v = \frac{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sv} \cdot d}{V_{us}}$$

For **bent-up bars** (combined with stirrups):
$$V_{us} = V_{us,\text{stirrups}} + V_{us,\text{bent bars}}$$

Where:
$$V_{us,\text{bent bar}} = 0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sb} \cdot \sin\alpha$$

Here, $\alpha$ = angle of bent-up bar with the longitudinal axis (typically **45°**, sometimes 60°).

### Step 8 — Apply **Minimum Shear Reinforcement** (Clause 26.5.1.6)
Even if $\tau_v < \tau_c$ (theoretically no shear reinforcement needed), minimum stirrups must be provided:
$$\frac{A_{sv}}{b \cdot s_v} \geq \frac{0.4}{0.87 \cdot f_y}$$

Rearranging for maximum spacing:
$$s_v \leq \frac{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sv}}{0.4 \cdot b}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum shear reinforcement IS MANDATORY** in all beams where the overall depth exceeds **450 mm** OR where the design shear stress exceeds the shear strength of plain concrete by a significant margin (Clause 26.5.1.6).

### Step 9 — Apply **Spacing Limits** (Clause 26.5.1.5)
The spacing of vertical stirrups shall **not exceed**:
- **0.75 d** (effective depth)
- **300 mm**

For inclined stirrups and bent-up bars, the spacing measured along the member axis shall not exceed **d**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize These for KTU ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units | IS Clause |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | $V_u = 1.5 (V_D + V_L)$ | Factored shear force | N | Cl. 36.4.1 |
| 2 | $\tau_v = \dfrac{V_u}{bd}$ | Nominal shear stress | MPa | Cl. 40.1 |
| 3 | $\tau_v \leq \tau_{c,\text{max}}$ | Upper limit check | MPa | Cl. 40.2.3, Table 20 |
| 4 | $V_c = \tau_c \cdot bd$ | Shear strength of concrete | N | Cl. 40.2.1, Table 19 |
| 5 | $p_t = \dfrac{100 A_{st}}{bd}$ | % tension steel | % | Cl. 40.2.1 |
| 6 | $V_{us} = V_u - V_c$ | Shear for stirrups | N | Cl. 40.4 |
| 7 | $V_{us} = \dfrac{0.87 f_y A_{sv} d}{s_v}$ | Stirrup strength (vertical) | N | Cl. 40.4 |
| 8 | $V_{us,b} = 0.87 f_y A_{sb} \sin\alpha$ | Bent-up bar contribution | N | Cl. 40.4 |
| 9 | $\dfrac{A_{sv}}{b s_v} \geq \dfrac{0.4}{0.87 f_y}$ | Minimum stirrup rule | — | Cl. 26.5.1.6 |
| 10 | $s_v \leq 0.75d$ AND $s_v \leq 300$ mm | Max stirrup spacing | mm | Cl. 26.5.1.5 |
| 11 | $\tau_v \leq 1.0$ MPa (extra check) | Plain concrete beams | MPa | Cl. 40.2.2 |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Practice**

- **Bridge Girders**: Heavy wheel loads create massive concentrated shears near supports → stirrup design is critical.
- **Transfer Girders** (in high-rises): Carry heavy columns above → extreme shear demand.
- **Deep Beams ($L/d < 2$)**: Governed by **Clause 31 of IS 456** (Strut-and-Tie Model), not standard shear design.
- **Seismic Design**: Cyclic loading causes brittle shear failure before flexural yielding → stirrups must be closely spaced (special confining hoops) in beam-column joints as per **IS 13920**.

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Nominal Shear Stress Formula

Starting from basic statics, the average shear stress across a rectangular section is:

$$\tau_{\text{avg}} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{V}{b \cdot h}$$

But for design purposes, IS 456 uses the **effective depth $d$** instead of total depth $h$ (since the lever arm is based on $d$):

$$\boxed{\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b \cdot d}}$$

**Reasoning**: The compression zone (above the neutral axis) and the tension reinforcement (below) form a couple. The shear is transferred through the **effective compression zone of depth $d$**, hence $d$ is used in design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Shear Strength of Concrete $V_c$

From **Clause 40.2.1 of IS 456**: the shear strength of concrete in a section cracked in flexure is given by:

$$V_c = \tau_c \cdot b \cdot d$$

Where $\tau_c$ depends on:
- Concrete grade (e.g., M20, M25, M30)
- Percentage of longitudinal tension steel $p_t$

**Empirical Basis**: $\tau_c$ values in Table 19 were derived from extensive laboratory tests on beams failing in shear without shear reinforcement.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Vertical Stirrup Spacing

Equating the shear to be carried by stirrups to the shear capacity of the stirrup system:

$$V_{us} = \text{(Yield strength of one stirrup set)} \times \text{(Number of stirrups per unit length)}$$

Consider two-legged 8 mm $\phi$ stirrups at spacing $s_v$:
- Area of one stirrup = $2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 8^2 = 2 \times 50.27 = 100.55 \text{ mm}^2$
- Yield force of one stirrup = $0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sv} = 0.87 \times 415 \times 100.55$
- Number of stirrups per unit length = $\dfrac{1}{s_v}$ per mm (or $\dfrac{1000}{s_v}$ per metre)

Therefore, the shear force resisted per unit length = $\dfrac{0.87 f_y A_{sv}}{s_v}$

Total shear resisted over the effective depth $d$:

$$V_{us} = \frac{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sv} \cdot d}{s_v}$$

Solving for spacing:

$$\boxed{s_v = \frac{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sv} \cdot d}{V_{us}}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Minimum Shear Reinforcement (Clause 26.5.1.6)

The code requires that the shear reinforcement be capable of resisting at least **0.4 N/mm²** of nominal shear stress:

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{b \cdot s_v} \cdot 0.87 \cdot f_y \geq 0.4$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{b \cdot s_v} \geq \frac{0.4}{0.87 \cdot f_y}$$

For Fe 415 steel: $\dfrac{0.4}{0.87 \times 415} = \dfrac{0.4}{361.05} = 1.108 \times 10^{-3}$

Therefore minimum $\dfrac{A_{sv}}{b} = 1.108 \times 10^{-3} \cdot s_v$, OR equivalently:

$$s_{v,\text{max}} = \frac{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sv}}{0.4 \cdot b}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Type)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — Comprehensive Shear Design**

**Given Data**:
- Beam dimensions: $b = 230 \text{ mm}, d = 450 \text{ mm}, D = 500 \text{ mm}$
- Concrete: **M25**, Steel: **Fe 415**
- Factored shear force at critical section: $V_u = 160 \text{ kN}$
- Tension reinforcement provided: $A_{st} = 4 \text{ bars of } 16 \text{ mm } \phi = 4 \times 201 = 804 \text{ mm}^2$

**Step 1: Calculate percentage of tension steel**
$$p_t = \frac{100 A_{st}}{bd} = \frac{100 \times 804}{230 \times 450} = \frac{80400}{103500} = 0.7767\%$$

**Step 2: Calculate nominal shear stress**
$$\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{bd} = \frac{160 \times 10^3}{230 \times 450} = \frac{160000}{103500} = 1.546 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Check upper limit (Table 20, IS 456)**
For M25 concrete: $\tau_{c,\text{max}} = 3.1 \text{ MPa}$

$$\tau_v = 1.546 \text{ MPa} < 3.1 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

**Step 4: Read $\tau_c$ from Table 19**
For M25 concrete, $p_t = 0.7767\% \approx 0.75\%$, interpolating:

$$\tau_c = 0.61 + \frac{(0.7767 - 0.75)}{(1.00 - 0.75)} \times (0.67 - 0.61) = 0.61 + 0.107 \times 0.06 = 0.6164 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Calculate shear strength of concrete**
$$V_c = \tau_c \cdot b \cdot d = 0.6164 \times 230 \times 450 = 63796 \text{ N} = 63.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Calculate shear to be carried by reinforcement**
$$V_{us} = V_u - V_c = 160 - 63.8 = 96.2 \text{ kN} = 96200 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7: Design vertical stirrups (using 2-legged 8 mm $\phi$)**
$$A_{sv} = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 8^2 = 100.55 \text{ mm}^2$$

Spacing required:
$$s_v = \frac{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sv} \cdot d}{V_{us}} = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 100.55 \times 450}{96200}$$

Calculating numerator step by step:
$$0.87 \times 415 = 361.05$$
$$361.05 \times 100.55 = 36300.58$$
$$36300.58 \times 450 = 16335261$$

$$s_v = \frac{16335261}{96200} = 169.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8: Apply maximum spacing limit**
$$0.75d = 0.75 \times 450 = 337.5 \text{ mm}; \quad 300 \text{ mm}$$

Since $169.8 < 300 \text{ mm}$ and $< 337.5 \text{ mm}$, we use **$s_v = 165 \text{ mm}$** (rounded down for safety).

**Step 9: Check minimum shear reinforcement**
$$\frac{A_{sv}}{b s_v} = \frac{100.55}{230 \times 165} = \frac{100.55}{37950} = 2.65 \times 10^{-3}$$

Required minimum: $\dfrac{0.4}{0.87 \times 415} = 1.108 \times 10^{-3}$

Since $2.65 \times 10^{-3} > 1.108 \times 10^{-3}$ ✓ **Minimum requirement satisfied**

**Final Answer**:
> Provide **2-legged 8 mm $\phi$ vertical stirrups at 165 mm c/c spacing** throughout the beam.

---

## 3.6 Combined Design with Bent-Up Bars

If part of the shear is to be carried by bent-up bars, the design changes as follows:

$$V_{us,\text{total}} = V_{us,\text{stirrups}} + V_{us,\text{bent bars}}$$

For a 45° bent-up bar (16 mm $\phi$, one bar):
$$A_{sb} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 16^2 = 201 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$V_{us,\text{bent}} = 0.87 \times 415 \times 201 \times \sin 45° = 0.87 \times 415 \times 201 \times 0.707$$
$$= 72526 \times 0.707 = 51275 \text{ N} = 51.3 \text{ kN}$$

The remainder is then designed with stirrups.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Shear Failure Modes — Conceptual Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Beam Subjected to Transverse Load] --> B{Diagonal Crack Forms?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Crack Type?}
    C -- 45 deg shear crack --> D[Web Shear Failure]
    C -- Inclined from flexure --> E[Shear Compression Failure]
    C -- At tension face --> F[Flexure Shear Failure]
    D --> G[Stirrups MUST be designed]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    B -- No --> H[Minimum stirrups only]
    G --> I[Check spacing limits 0.75d and 300mm]
    I --> J[Check minimum shear reinforcement rule]
    J --> K[Finalize Stirrup Diameter and Spacing]
    H --> K
```

---

## 4.2 Shear Design Methodology — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1: Calculate Factored Shear Force V_u] --> step2[Step 2: Identify Critical Section at d from Support Face]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Calculate Nominal Shear Stress tau_v = V_u by bd]
    step3 --> step4{Step 4: tau_v less than tau_c_max?}
    step4 -- No --> step5[INCREASE SECTION DEPTH]
    step4 -- Yes --> step6[Step 5: Read tau_c from Table 19 using p_t]
    step6 --> step7[Step 6: Compute V_c = tau_c times bd]
    step7 --> step8[Step 7: V_us = V_u minus V_c]
    step8 --> step9{Step 8: Shear Reinforcement Needed?}
    step9 -- V_us greater than 0 --> step10[Step 9: Design Stirrups s_v]
    step9 -- V_us equals 0 --> step11[Step 10: Provide Minimum Stirrups]
    step10 --> step12[Step 11: Check Spacing Limits and Minimum Rules]
    step11 --> step12
    step12 --> step13[Step 12: Finalise Detailing]
```

---

## 4.3 Internal Force Distribution in a Shear-Critical Beam

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Load1[Concentrated Load] --> Beam1[Simply Supported Beam]
    Beam1 --> Support1[Support A]
    Beam1 --> Support2[Support B]
    Beam1 --> ShearDia[Diagonal Shear Cracks at 45 deg]
    ShearDia --> Stirrup[Vertical Stirrups Crossing Crack]
    Stirrup --> SteelYield[Stirrup Steel Yields in Tension]
    SteelYield --> CrackControl[Crack Width Controlled]
    CrackControl --> BeamSafe[Beam Achieves Shear Capacity]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic Description: Typical Stirrup Detailing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BeamShape[Rectangular RCC Beam] --> TopBar[Top Longitudinal Bars 2 Nos]
    BeamShape --> BottomBar[Bottom Longitudinal Bars 3 to 4 Nos]
    BeamShape --> StirrupLoop[Stirrups 2 Legged 8mm dia]
    StirrupLoop --> Spacing1[Spacing 165mm c/c near support]
    StirrupLoop --> Spacing2[Spacing increased to 250mm at midspan]
    Spacing1 --> HookDetail[Hooks of 135 deg at top]
    Spacing2 --> HookDetail
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "Nominal Shear Stress" as per IS 456:2000. How is it computed in the design of RC beams?**

**Model Answer**:
> **Nominal shear stress ($\tau_v$)** is defined as the average shear stress acting on a cross-section of a beam, computed by dividing the factored shear force $V_u$ by the product of breadth $b$ and effective depth $d$ of the section.
> $$\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b \cdot d}$$
> It is used as a reference parameter to compare with the permissible shear strength values given in Tables 19 and 20 of IS 456:2000. The use of $d$ (instead of $D$) accounts for the effective shear transfer zone in the cracked section. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the minimum shear reinforcement requirement as per IS 456:2000. Why is it mandatory even when theoretically not required?**

**Model Answer**:
> As per **Clause 26.5.1.6 of IS 456:2000**, the minimum shear reinforcement in beams is given by:
> $$\frac{A_{sv}}{b \cdot s_v} \geq \frac{0.4}{0.87 \cdot f_y}$$
> This is mandatory because: (i) shear failure is **brittle** and sudden; (ii) concrete may have unforeseen tensile cracks due to shrinkage, temperature, or stress concentrations; (iii) minimum stirrups hold the longitudinal bars in position and resist any unforeseen diagonal tension. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**(a)** An RC beam of rectangular section, 250 mm wide and 500 mm overall depth, is reinforced with 4 bars of 20 mm $\phi$ at the bottom (Fe 415 steel). Effective cover is 50 mm. The factored shear force at the critical section is **180 kN**. Concrete grade is **M25**. Design the shear reinforcement using vertical stirrups. (7 marks)

**(b)** A beam of section 300 mm × 600 mm overall depth (effective depth 550 mm) is subjected to a factored shear force of **280 kN** at the critical section. Grade of concrete M30 and steel Fe 415. Design a combination of **vertical stirrups and bent-up bars**, given that 2 bars of 20 mm $\phi$ can be bent up at 45° near the support. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a)

**Step 1: Effective depth and steel details**
$$d = D - \text{effective cover} = 500 - 50 = 450 \text{ mm}$$
$$A_{st} = 4 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 4 \times 314.16 = 1256.6 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Percentage of steel**
$$p_t = \frac{100 \times 1256.6}{250 \times 450} = \frac{125660}{112500} = 1.117\%$$

**Step 3: Nominal shear stress**
$$\tau_v = \frac{180 \times 10^3}{250 \times 450} = \frac{180000}{112500} = 1.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Check $\tau_{c,\text{max}}$**
For M25: $\tau_{c,\text{max}} = 3.1 \text{ MPa}$
$$1.6 \text{ MPa} < 3.1 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Read $\tau_c$ from Table 19**
For M25, $p_t = 1.117\%$, interpolate between 1.00% and 1.25%:
$$\tau_c = 0.67 + \frac{(1.117 - 1.00)}{(1.25 - 1.00)} \times (0.71 - 0.67) = 0.67 + 0.468 \times 0.04 = 0.6887 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6: Shear carried by concrete**
$$V_c = 0.6887 \times 250 \times 450 = 77479 \text{ N} = 77.48 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7: Shear to be carried by stirrups**
$$V_{us} = 180 - 77.48 = 102.52 \text{ kN} = 102520 \text{ N}$$

**Step 8: Design 2-legged 8 mm $\phi$ stirrups**
$$A_{sv} = 2 \times 50.27 = 100.55 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$s_v = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 100.55 \times 450}{102520} = \frac{16335261}{102520} = 159.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 9: Spacing limit check**
$$0.75d = 0.75 \times 450 = 337.5 \text{ mm}; \quad s_v \leq 300 \text{ mm}$$
Since $159.3 < 300 \text{ mm}$: **provide $s_v = 155 \text{ mm c/c}$** (rounded down)

**Step 10: Minimum stirrup check**
$$\frac{A_{sv}}{b s_v} = \frac{100.55}{250 \times 155} = 2.595 \times 10^{-3} > 1.108 \times 10^{-3} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Design**: **Provide 2-legged 8 mm $\phi$ vertical stirrups at 155 mm c/c spacing**. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b)

**Step 1: Nominal shear stress**
$$\tau_v = \frac{280 \times 10^3}{300 \times 550} = \frac{280000}{165000} = 1.697 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Upper limit check (M30)**
$\tau_{c,\text{max}} = 3.5 \text{ MPa}$, $1.697 < 3.5$ ✓

**Step 3: Assume $p_t = 1.0\%$ (typical), read $\tau_c$ from Table 19 for M30**
For M30, $p_t = 1.0\%$: $\tau_c = 0.74 \text{ MPa}$ (interpolated)
$$V_c = 0.74 \times 300 \times 550 = 122100 \text{ N} = 122.1 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Total shear to be carried by reinforcement**
$$V_{us} = 280 - 122.1 = 157.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Bent-up bar contribution**
Two 20 mm $\phi$ bars at 45°:
$$A_{sb} = 2 \times 314.16 = 628.32 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$V_{us,\text{bent}} = 0.87 \times 415 \times 628.32 \times \sin 45° = 226656 \times 0.7071 = 160273 \text{ N} = 160.3 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Check whether bent-up bars alone are sufficient**
$$V_{us,\text{bent}} = 160.3 \text{ kN} > V_{us} = 157.9 \text{ kN}$$
Bent-up bars alone are sufficient! However, **IS 456 (Clause 40.4)** requires that **at least 50% of the shear reinforcement be provided in the form of stirrups** (when bent-up bars are used). So we must design stirrups anyway.

**Step 7: Provide stirrups for 50% of $V_{us}$**
$$V_{us,\text{stirrups}} = 0.5 \times 157.9 = 78.95 \text{ kN}$$

Using 2-legged 8 mm $\phi$ stirrups:
$$s_v = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 100.55 \times 550}{78950} = \frac{19987541}{78950} = 253.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8: Final detailing**
- Provide **2-legged 8 mm $\phi$ stirrups at 250 mm c/c**
- Provide **2 bars of 20 mm $\phi$ bent up at 45°** in the shear region
- Maximum spacing along bent-up bar plane: ≤ $d = 550$ mm ✓ (7 marks)

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the various **modes of shear failure** in reinforced concrete beams with neat sketches. (7 marks)

**(b)** A simply supported RC beam of clear span 5 m, cross-section 230 mm × 450 mm overall (effective depth 400 mm), is loaded with a **uniformly distributed factored load of 50 kN/m (including self-weight)**. Grade of concrete M20 and steel Fe 415. Design the shear reinforcement at the critical section. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a)

**Modes of Shear Failure in RC Beams**:

1. **Web Shear Failure**: Occurs in the web of deep beams where the principal tensile stress exceeds the tensile strength of concrete before flexural cracking. Diagonal cracks form in the web independent of flexure.

2. **Flexure-Shear Failure**: Most common type. Begins with flexural cracks at the tension face. As load increases, one of these flexural cracks propagates diagonally (inclined at ~30° to 45°) towards the compression zone. This happens in slender beams.

3. **Shear Compression Failure**: The diagonal crack extends into the compression zone, crushing the concrete above the crack. This is more common in beams with low span-to-depth ratios.

4. **Shear Tension Failure**: A pure diagonal crack forms without any prior flexural cracking. Common in beams with high shear and low flexural demand.

5. **Diagonal Compression Failure**: The concrete in the diagonal strut between the load point and the support crushes due to high compressive stress. Critical in deep beams.

> All these failures are **brittle** and must be prevented by adequate shear reinforcement. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b)

**Step 1: Calculate maximum shear force at support**
$$V_u = \frac{w \cdot L}{2} = \frac{50 \times 5}{2} = 125 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Critical section at distance $d$ from support**
At distance $d = 400 \text{ mm} = 0.4 \text{ m}$ from support:
$$V_u = \frac{w \cdot (L/2 - d)}{1} = 50 \times (2.5 - 0.4) = 50 \times 2.1 = 105 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Nominal shear stress**
$$\tau_v = \frac{105 \times 10^3}{230 \times 400} = \frac{105000}{92000} = 1.141 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Upper limit check (M20)**
$\tau_{c,\text{max}} = 2.8 \text{ MPa}$, $1.141 < 2.8$ ✓

**Step 5: Assume $A_{st} = 0.6\%$, read $\tau_c$ from Table 19**
For M20, $p_t = 0.6\%$: $\tau_c \approx 0.48 \text{ MPa}$ (interpolated)
$$V_c = 0.48 \times 230 \times 400 = 44160 \text{ N} = 44.16 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Shear for reinforcement**
$$V_{us} = 105 - 44.16 = 60.84 \text{ kN} = 60840 \text{ N}$$

**Step 7: Design 2-legged 8 mm $\phi$ stirrups**
$$s_v = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 100.55 \times 400}{60840} = \frac{14520240}{60840} = 238.7 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8: Apply spacing limits**
$0.75d = 0.75 \times 400 = 300 \text{ mm}$, $s_v \leq 300 \text{ mm}$
Since $238.7 < 300$: **provide $s_v = 235 \text{ mm c/c}$**

**Step 9: Minimum shear reinforcement check**
$$\frac{A_{sv}}{b s_v} = \frac{100.55}{230 \times 235} = 1.86 \times 10^{-3} > 1.108 \times 10^{-3} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Design**: **Provide 2-legged 8 mm $\phi$ vertical stirrups at 235 mm c/c spacing near supports.** (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU ESE**

1. **Wrong critical section**: Computing $V_u$ at mid-span instead of at distance $d$ from support face. **Loss: 2 marks**

2. **Forgetting to convert $V_u$ to factored load**: Using working load shear force instead of $1.5(D+L)$. **Loss: 1 mark**

3. **Not checking $\tau_{c,\text{max}}$ upper limit**: IS 456 strictly prohibits overstressing. If $\tau_v > \tau_{c,\text{max}}$, the section MUST be increased. **Loss: 1 mark**

4. **Wrong units in stirrup spacing formula**: Using $V_u$ in kN instead of N, or $d$ in m instead of mm. **Loss: 2 marks**

5. **Ignoring minimum shear reinforcement clause**: Even if $V_{us}$ is theoretically zero, minimum stirrups are mandatory. **Loss: 1 mark**

6. **Not applying spacing limit (0.75d and 300 mm)**: Designing with spacing larger than the maximum allowed. **Loss: 1 mark**

7. **Using plain concrete $\tau_c$ when bent-up bars are used incorrectly**: Forgetting the 50% rule for combined stirrup + bent-up design. **Loss: 1 mark**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Limit State of Collapse in Shear**

### Core Concepts
- Shear failure is **brittle** → always design with extra caution
- Critical section is at **distance $d$ from the face of the support** (not at mid-span)
- Nominal shear stress: $\tau_v = \dfrac{V_u}{bd}$
- Two-table lookup: **Table 19** for $\tau_c$ and **Table 20** for $\tau_{c,\text{max}}$
- Maximum spacing: $s_v \leq \min(0.75d, 300 \text{ mm})$
- Minimum stirrup rule: $\dfrac{A_{sv}}{b s_v} \geq \dfrac{0.4}{0.87 f_y}$
- When bent-up bars are used, **at least 50%** of shear must be carried by stirrups

### Key Design Steps (Mnemonic: **C-C-D-S-M**)
1. **C**alculate $V_u$ at critical section
2. **C**heck $\tau_v \leq \tau_{c,\text{max}}$ (Table 20)
3. **D**etermine $\tau_c$ and $V_c$ (Table 19, using $p_t$)
4. **S**hear to be resisted: $V_{us} = V_u - V_c$
5. **M**inimum shear reinforcement check + spacing limits

### High-Yield Numbers
- For **M25 concrete**: $\tau_{c,\text{max}} = 3.1$ MPa
- For **M20 concrete**: $\tau_{c,\text{max}} = 2.8$ MPa
- For **Fe 415** stirrups: $\dfrac{0.4}{0.87 \times 415} = 1.108 \times 10^{-3}$
- For **2-legged 8 mm $\phi$** stirrups: $A_{sv} = 100.55 \text{ mm}^2$
- For **2-legged 10 mm $\phi$** stirrups: $A_{sv} = 157.08 \text{ mm}^2$

### IS 456 Clauses to Memorize
- **Clause 22.6.2** — Critical section for shear
- **Clause 26.5.1.5** — Maximum spacing of stirrups
- **Clause 26.5.1.6** — Minimum shear reinforcement
- **Clause 36.4.1** — Partial safety factors for loads
- **Clause 40.1 to 40.4** — Design for shear
- **Table 19** — Shear strength of concrete
- **Table 20** — Maximum shear stress

### Common Bent-Up Bar Angles
- **45°** (most common): $\sin 45° = 0.7071$
- **60°** (sometimes used in shallow beams): $\sin 60° = 0.8660$

### When NOT to Use Standard Shear Design
- **Deep beams** ($L/d \leq 2$ for simply supported, $L/d \leq 2.5$ for continuous) → use **Strut-and-Tie Model (Clause 31)**
- **Flat slabs** → use **Clause 31.6**
- **Waffle slabs** → use specialised design
- **Beams in earthquake-prone zones** → use **IS 13920** (special confining reinforcement)

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examination Tip**
> In Part B questions (14 marks), always present your answer in the following order:
> 1. Given data & assumptions
> 2. Calculation of $V_u$ at critical section
> 3. Nominal shear stress $\tau_v$
> 4. Upper limit check (Table 20)
> 5. $V_c$ from Table 19
> 6. $V_{us}$ calculation
> 7. Stirrup design with spacing
> 8. Spacing and minimum reinforcement checks
> 9. Final detailing (with sketch if possible)
>
> This structured approach scores **full marks** and is what KTU board examiners expect.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bond
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET404 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bond

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BOND (IS 456:2000, Clause 5.4.1):**
> *Bond* is the **adhesion and grip** established between the surface of reinforcing steel and the surrounding hardened concrete such that they deform together as a **single composite section** without any relative slip, thereby ensuring force transfer through shear at the steel-concrete interface.

The **bond stress** ($\tau_{bd}$) is defined as the **shear stress per unit nominal surface area of the bar** developed at the interface between steel reinforcement and the surrounding concrete, acting tangentially along the surface of the bar to transfer axial force from concrete to steel (or vice versa).

**Units of bond stress**: N/mm² (MPa)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Train-Track Analogy:**
> Imagine a railway track and a train wheel. The wheel transfers its driving force to the track through **friction + mechanical locking** of the wheel flange. If there were no friction/grip, the wheel would simply **spin in place** (no force transfer).
>
> - **Concrete** = the railway track
> - **Steel rebar** = the wheel flange
> - **Bond stress** = the gripping friction + mechanical lock
>
> If the grip fails, the bar **slips** (analogous to "pull-out failure"), and the RC member collapses without the steel ever reaching its yield strength. **Bond is what makes Reinforced Concrete work.**

## 1.3 Why Bond is Indispensable in RC Design

For a simply supported RC beam to resist bending:

1. Concrete in tension **cracks** (its tensile strength is neglected in design).
2. The tensile force is carried **entirely by the steel rebar**.
3. For this force to be transferred INTO the bar at any section, the surrounding concrete must grip the bar through **bond**.
4. Without sufficient bond length (→ **development length, Ld**), the bar will **pull out** before yielding — a **brittle, catastrophic failure**.

## 1.4 Key Standard Reference Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 456:2000 — Design Bond Stress ($\tau_{bd}$)** for **Plain Bars (Table 21, Clause 5.4.2):**

| Concrete Grade | M15 | M20 | M25 | M30 | M35 | M40 & above |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_{bd}$ (MPa) | **0.6** | **0.8** | **1.0** | **1.1** | **1.2** | **1.3** |

> **IS 456:2000 — Design Bond Stress ($\tau_{bd}$)** for **Deformed Bars (Clause 5.4.2, multiplied by 1.6):**

| Concrete Grade | M15 | M20 | M25 | M30 | M35 | M40 & above |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_{bd}$ (MPa) | **0.96** | **1.28** | **1.60** | **1.76** | **1.92** | **2.08** |

**Characteristic Tensile Strength of Steel, $f_y$ (IS 1786):** **415 MPa** (Fe415), **500 MPa** (Fe500), **550 MPa** (Fe550).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Mechanistic Components of Bond

> [!NOTE]
> Bond is **not a single phenomenon** — it is the **combined effect of three distinct mechanisms** acting simultaneously at the steel-concrete interface. Understanding the hierarchy is critical for KTU derivations.

### Mechanism 1 — Chemical Adhesion (Initial Stage)
- Originates from the **chemical glue** formed by cement hydration products (C-S-H gel) bonding microscopically to the steel surface.
- Activated at **very low slip values** (~0.01 mm).
- Resists only **small stresses** (~0.2–0.5 MPa).
- **Brittle** in nature — collapses suddenly once exceeded.
- Dominant in **plain (mild steel) bars** with smooth surfaces.

### Mechanism 2 — Frictional Resistance (Intermediate Stage)
- Once chemical adhesion fails, **friction** between the bar surface and surrounding concrete takes over.
- Caused by **radial compressive stresses** from concrete shrinkage around the bar, plus Poisson's ratio effects under tension.
- Force = $\mu \times N_{radial}$, where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction (≈ 0.3–0.5 for steel-concrete).
- Higher in **deformed bars** (ribs press radially, increasing $N_{radial}$).

### Mechanism 3 — Mechanical Interlock (Dominant in Deformed Bars)
- The **ribs / lugs / deformations** on HYSD bars physically bear against concrete keys formed between ribs.
- This is the **primary load-transfer mechanism** in Fe415, Fe500 deformed bars.
- Generates a combination of **bearing + shearing** of concrete keys.
- Provides **3–5× higher bond capacity** than plain bars (which is why IS 456 allows 1.6× multiplier).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> The 1.6× multiplier in IS 456 Clause 5.4.2 for deformed bars empirically accounts for the **combined frictional + mechanical interlock** contribution beyond plain bar adhesion.

## 2.2 Types of Bond in Design

### (a) Anchorage Bond (Local Bond)
- Bond required at the **simple end / cut-off point** of a bar to **develop its full design stress** ($0.87 f_y$) from zero.
- This determines the **development length $L_d$**.
- Governed by the **maximum force** the bar must transmit.

### (b) Flexural Bond
- Bond required along the bar between **consecutive flexural cracks** due to **variation in tensile force** ($dT = (dM/z)$) along the length.
- Computed from the **rate of change of bar force**: $\tau_b = \frac{V}{\Sigma L_o \cdot \phi}$ where $V$ = shear at section, $\Sigma L_o$ = sum of perimeters of bars resisting tension, $\phi$ = bar diameter.
- In modern limit-state design (IS 456), flexural bond is **not explicitly checked** because the **cracked section analysis** + **shear design** already ensure adequate bond indirectly.

## 2.3 Development Length — The Master Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DEVELOPMENT LENGTH ($L_d$):**
> The minimum length of the bar that must be **embedded in concrete** beyond any critical section so that the bar can develop its **full design stress** ($f_s = 0.87 f_y$) through bond without slipping.

### KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Design bond stress (plain bars) | $\tau_{bd}$ from IS 456 Table 21 | Grade-dependent | MPa |
| Design bond stress (deformed bars) | $1.6 \times \tau_{bd,plain}$ | Clause 5.4.2 | MPa |
| Equilibrium of bond force | $\pi \phi L_d \tau_{bd} = \frac{\pi \phi^2}{4} \sigma_s$ | Force balance | N |
| **Development Length (master eq.)** | $L_d = \dfrac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \tau_{bd}}$ | IS 456 Cl. 5.4.4 | mm |
| With $\sigma_s = 0.87 f_y$ | $L_d = \dfrac{0.87 f_y \cdot \phi}{4 \tau_{bd}}$ | Full stress development | mm |
| Simplified for Fe415, deformed, M20 | $L_d = 47\phi$ (≈ $47\phi$ to $53\phi$ by hand calc) | Rule-of-thumb | mm |
| Simplified for Fe500, deformed, M20 | $L_d = 57\phi$ | Rule-of-thumb | mm |
| Flexural bond stress | $\tau_b = \dfrac{V}{\Sigma L_o \cdot \phi}$ | For analysis (not design check) | MPa |
| Equivalent perimeters | $\Sigma L_o = n \cdot \pi \phi$ | $n$ = number of bars | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **NEVER use the symbol $\vert$ inside the table — use $\mid$ or $\vert$ (LaTeX) as the user requested.**

### 2.4 Boundary Conditions & Code Restrictions (IS 456)

- $L_d$ shall be provided on **both sides** of every critical section (positive AND negative moment regions).
- For **bundled bars**, $L_d$ must be increased by **10% for 2-bar bundle, 20% for 3-bar bundle, 33% for 4-bar bundle** (Clause 5.4.4).
- Hooks may be provided at the end of bars — a **standard hook** (180° bend + 4$\phi$ straight extension, or 90° bend + 12$\phi$ extension) is equivalent to **16$\phi$ of straight embedment** for plain bars (Clause 5.4.5).
- Minimum $L_d$ shall **not be less than** the value given in IS 456 (typically $L_d \geq 25\phi$ for HYSD bars in M20).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Beam-column joints**: Bond determines the anchorage of beam bottom bars into columns — critical in seismic design.
2. **Cantilever/slab reinforcement**: Bond governs the curtailment length and lap length.
3. **Precast concrete**: Bond governs the connection between precast units and in-situ concrete topping.
4. **Prestressed concrete**: Bond in pretensioned members transfers prestress from steel to concrete.
5. **Failure forensics**: Pull-out failures in collapsed structures (e.g., balcony slabs with inadequate anchorage) — common in field investigations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Development Length Master Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most important derivation** for KTU 2024 — appearing in nearly every ESE.

### Setup
Consider a single bar of diameter $\phi$ embedded in concrete over a length $L_d$. The bar develops a tensile force equal to its full design capacity. Equating the **bond force on the cylindrical surface** to the **tensile force in the bar cross-section**:

### Step 1 — Force carried by the bar (Tensile Capacity)
The bar has a circular cross-section:

$$A_{st} = \frac{\pi \phi^2}{4}$$

The tensile force that the bar can develop is:

$$T = A_{st} \times \sigma_s = \frac{\pi \phi^2}{4} \times \sigma_s$$

where $\sigma_s$ is the design stress in the bar (in limit state, $\sigma_s = 0.87 f_y$).

### Step 2 — Bond force on the cylindrical surface of the bar
The bar is cylindrical with diameter $\phi$ and embedded length $L_d$. The lateral surface area in contact with concrete is:

$$A_{bond} = \pi \phi L_d$$

This surface transmits a shear (bond) stress $\tau_{bd}$. The total bond force is:

$$F_{bond} = A_{bond} \times \tau_{bd} = \pi \phi L_d \times \tau_{bd}$$

### Step 3 — Force equilibrium (no slip condition)
For the bar to develop its full capacity without pulling out:

$$F_{bond} = T$$

$$\pi \phi L_d \cdot \tau_{bd} = \frac{\pi \phi^2}{4} \cdot \sigma_s$$

### Step 4 — Cancel the common factor $\pi \phi$ from both sides

$$L_d \cdot \tau_{bd} = \frac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4}$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $L_d$ (final form)

$$\boxed{L_d = \frac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}}$$

### Step 6 — Substitute $\sigma_s = 0.87 f_y$ (limit state, IS 456)

$$L_d = \frac{\phi \cdot 0.87 f_y}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}} = \frac{0.87 f_y \cdot \phi}{4 \tau_{bd}}$$

### Step 7 — Quick numerical evaluation for Fe415 + Deformed bar + M20 concrete

$$L_d = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times \phi}{4 \times 1.28}$$

Let us compute the numerator and denominator:

- Numerator: $0.87 \times 415 = 361.05$ MPa
- Denominator: $4 \times 1.28 = 5.12$ MPa

Therefore:

$$L_d = \frac{361.05}{5.12} \times \phi \approx 70.5\phi$$

For Fe500 + Deformed + M20:

$$L_d = \frac{0.87 \times 500 \times \phi}{4 \times 1.28} = \frac{435 \times \phi}{5.12} \approx 85.0\phi$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Practice:** Designers commonly use **rounded values** $L_d = 50\phi$ to $60\phi$ for Fe415 and $L_d = 57\phi$ to $70\phi$ for Fe500, subject to IS 456 minimums.

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Flexural Bond Stress

### Step 1 — Consider an elemental length $dx$ of the bar
The bar force changes from $T$ to $T + dT$ over length $dx$. The change in force $dT$ is transferred to concrete through bond.

### Step 2 — Express change in bar force in terms of bending moment
The lever arm is $z$. The change in moment is:

$$dM = z \cdot dT \quad \Rightarrow \quad dT = \frac{dM}{z}$$

### Step 3 — Shear force relation
By statics: $V = dM / dx$. So:

$$dT = \frac{V \cdot dx}{z}$$

### Step 4 — Bond force over length $dx$
The lateral surface area over $dx$ is $\pi \phi \, dx$. The bond stress is:

$$\tau_b = \frac{dT}{\pi \phi \, dx} = \frac{V \cdot dx / z}{\pi \phi \, dx} = \frac{V}{\pi \phi \, z}$$

### Step 5 — For $n$ bars of diameter $\phi$ in tension zone
Total perimeter $\Sigma L_o = n \cdot \pi \phi$. So:

$$\boxed{\tau_b = \frac{V}{\Sigma L_o \cdot z} \approx \frac{V}{\Sigma L_o \cdot d}}$$

(using $z \approx d$ in limit state design). The expression is also written as:

$$\tau_b = \frac{V}{(\text{sum of perimeters of bars in tension}) \times (\text{effective depth})}$$

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Equivalent Anchorage Value of a Standard Hook

A standard 180° hook is taken to provide anchorage equivalent to straight embedment. Energy/work balance approach:

- A straight bar of length $L_{eq}$ develops pull-out resistance = $\pi \phi L_{eq} \tau_{bd}$.
- A hook develops resistance through bearing of the curved portion against concrete (splitting + pull-out cone).

IS 456 (Clause 5.4.5) empirically states:

$$L_{eq} \text{ (hook)} = 16 \phi \text{ (for plain bars in M20)}$$

Therefore a standard hook **replaces** 16$\phi$ of straight embedment in plain bars. In deformed bars, the equivalent anchorage value is **taken with reduction** (≈ 12$\phi$ in M20).

---

## 3.4 WORKED NUMERICAL EXAMPLE (KTU Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**
> *A simply supported RC beam of effective depth 450 mm, width 230 mm, is reinforced with 4 bars of 16 mm diameter (Fe415, deformed). The design shear at the support is 90 kN. Concrete grade is M20. Check whether the flexural bond stress is within the permissible limit. Also compute the development length required at the support.*

### Solution:

**Given:**
- $b = 230$ mm, $d = 450$ mm
- Bars: 4 nos. of 16 mm $\phi$ (Fe415, HYSD)
- $V = 90$ kN = $90{,}000$ N
- $f_y = 415$ MPa, M20 concrete
- $\tau_{bd} = 1.28$ MPa (deformed, M20)

### Step 1: Total perimeter of bars in tension
$$\Sigma L_o = 4 \times \pi \times 16 = 201.06 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 2: Flexural bond stress
$$\tau_b = \frac{V}{\Sigma L_o \cdot d} = \frac{90{,}000}{201.06 \times 450}$$

$$\tau_b = \frac{90{,}000}{90{,}477} = 0.995 \text{ MPa} \approx 1.0 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 3: Compare with permissible $\tau_{bd} = 1.28$ MPa
Since $0.995 < 1.28$, **flexural bond is SAFE**. ✓

### Step 4: Development length
$$L_d = \frac{0.87 f_y \cdot \phi}{4 \tau_{bd}} = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 16}{4 \times 1.28}$$

Numerator: $0.87 \times 415 \times 16 = 5776.8$

Denominator: $4 \times 1.28 = 5.12$

$$L_d = \frac{5776.8}{5.12} = 1128.28 \text{ mm} \approx 1128 \text{ mm} \approx 1130 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 5: Available length
If the bar is extended only up to the support face plus effective cover (say 50 mm), the available anchorage is ≈ 50 mm. Since 50 mm < 1128 mm, **the bar MUST be hooked** at the support, or extended further, to provide equivalent anchorage.

Using a 90° standard hook (equiv. 12$\phi$ for HYSD):

$$L_{eq,hook} = 12 \times 16 = 192 \text{ mm}$$

Even with the hook, available anchorage (50 + 192 = 242 mm) is **insufficient**. The bar must be continued past the support into the column (or a U-bend loop provided) to develop the required $L_d$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Assuming a hook alone is sufficient. Hooks are a *supplement* to development length, not a *replacement*. Always check the straight embedment first.

---

## 3.5 Quick Reference Summary of All Key Equations

| # | Equation | Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_d = \dfrac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \tau_{bd}}$ | General development length |
| 2 | $L_d = \dfrac{0.87 f_y \phi}{4 \tau_{bd}}$ | Limit state (IS 456) |
| 3 | $\tau_b = \dfrac{V}{\Sigma L_o \cdot d}$ | Flexural bond check |
| 4 | $\tau_{bd,\text{def}} = 1.6 \times \tau_{bd,\text{plain}}$ | Deformed bar multiplier |
| 5 | $L_{d,\text{bundle}} = L_d \times (1 + k)$ | $k=0.1,0.2,0.33$ for 2,3,4-bar bundle |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Bond Mechanism Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Bond Mechanism in RC"]:::main --> B["Chemical Adhesion"]:::lvl1
    A --> C["Friction Resistance"]:::lvl1
    A --> D["Mechanical Interlock"]:::lvl1
    B --> B1["Cement hydration C-S-H gel"]
    B --> B2["Activates at slip 0.01 mm"]
    B --> B3["Brittle contribution"]
    C --> C1["Shrinkage radial stress"]
    C --> C2["Poisson effect under tension"]
    C --> C3["Friction coefficient 0.3 to 0.5"]
    D --> D1["Ribs of HYSD bar"]
    D --> D2["Concrete key shearing"]
    D --> D3["Dominant in Fe415 Fe500"]
    A --> E["Combined Effect"]:::lvl2
    E --> E1["Plain bars adhesion plus friction"]
    E --> E2["Deformed bars all three plus multiplier 1.6"]
    classDef main fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef lvl1 fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef lvl2 fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Force Transfer via Bond

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Applied Load P"]:::in --> Q["Concrete compression zone"]:::zone
    Q --> R["Bond shear stress tau_bd"]:::bond
    R --> S["Reinforcing bar tension"]:::bar
    S --> T["Resistance equals Pi phi squared by 4 times sigma_s"]:::out
    R -.-> U["Surface area equals Pi phi times L_d"]:::area
    classDef in fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef zone fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000,stroke:#000
    classDef bond fill:#c00000,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef bar fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef out fill:#548235,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef area fill:#7030a0,color:#fff,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Development Length Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Bar end at support face"]:::end1 --> B["Critical section at face of support"]:::crit
    B --> C["Development length L_d region"]:::reg
    C --> D["Bar develops full sigma_s = 0.87 f_y"]:::full
    D --> E["Free body diagram of bar"]:::fbd
    E --> F["Bond force Pi phi L_d tau_bd"]:::bf
    E --> G["Tensile force Pi phi squared by 4 sigma_s"]:::tf
    F --> H["Equilibrium gives L_d formula"]:::eq
    G --> H
    H --> I["L_d equals 0.87 f_y phi divided by 4 tau_bd"]:::final
    classDef end1 fill:#c00000,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef crit fill:#ed7d31,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef reg fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff,stroke:#000

    classDef fbd fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000,stroke:#000
    classDef bf fill:#7030a0,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef tf fill:#7030a0,color:#fff,stroke:#000

```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Bond Failure Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Bond Failure in RC"]:::main --> B["Pull-out Failure"]:::po
    A --> C["Splitting Failure"]:::sp
    A --> D["Combined Failure"]:::cm
    B --> B1["Smooth bar, low cover, weak concrete"]
    B --> B2["Bar slides out without splitting"]
    C --> C1["Deformed bar, inadequate cover or transverse reinf"]
    C --> C2["Concrete cover splits off"]
    D --> D1["Most common in practice"]
    D --> D2["Splitting with partial pull-out"]
    A --> E["Prevention Strategies"]:::prev
    E --> E1["Provide adequate cover"]
    E --> E2["Use confinement stirrups"]
    E --> E3["Use hooks at bar ends"]
    E --> E4["Extend bars to develop L_d"]
    classDef main fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef po fill:#c00000,color:#fff,stroke:#000

```

## 4.5 Bar Profile Schematics (ASCII Representation)

```
PLAIN (MILD STEEL) BAR              DEFORMED (HYSD) BAR
  _____________________             _____________________
  |                   |             |  ___    ___    ___ |
  |  smooth surface   |             | |   |  |   |  |   ||
  |  (only adhesion   |             | |   |  |   |  |   ||
  |   and friction)   |             | |___|  |___|  |___||
  |___________________|             |____________________|
                                      ^   ^    ^    ^
                                      ribs/lugs for mechanical
                                      interlock with concrete
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar]**
> **Q1. Define bond and list the three mechanisms by which bond is established between steel and concrete.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Bond (1 mark):** Bond is the adhesion and grip between the surface of reinforcing steel and surrounding concrete that enables them to act as a composite section, with shear stress ($\tau_{bd}$) being developed at the steel-concrete interface to transfer force.
>
> **Three mechanisms (2 marks — 1 each + brief description):**
> 1. **Chemical Adhesion** — Microscopic bonding between cement hydration products (C-S-H gel) and the steel surface. Brittle, activates at very low slip (~0.01 mm).
> 2. **Friction** — Arises from radial compressive stresses (shrinkage + Poisson effect) gripping the bar. Coefficient of friction ≈ 0.3–0.5.
> 3. **Mechanical Interlock** — Physical bearing of ribs/lugs on deformed HYSD bars against concrete keys. Dominant mechanism in Fe415 / Fe500 bars, providing 1.6× higher bond capacity per IS 456.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between anchorage bond and flexural bond.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Anchorage Bond (1.5 marks) | Flexural Bond (1.5 marks) |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Definition** | Bond required to develop full design stress in the bar at its end/cut-off | Bond required along the bar due to variation in tensile force between cracked sections |
> | **Cause** | Pull-out of the bar from concrete | Change in bending moment along the length |
> | **Critical region** | Bar ends, supports, cut-off points | Along the span between flexural cracks |
> | **Design relevance** | Determines **development length $L_d$** — explicitly checked per IS 456 Cl. 5.4 | Not explicitly checked in modern LSM; covered indirectly by shear design |
> | **Governing equation** | $L_d = \dfrac{\phi \sigma_s}{4 \tau_{bd}}$ | $\tau_b = \dfrac{V}{\Sigma L_o \cdot d}$ |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - Module Choice, Module 1 - Pattern]**
> **(a)** Derive the expression for **development length of a reinforcing bar** as per IS 456. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A simply supported RC beam of effective span 6 m, width 250 mm, and effective depth 500 mm is reinforced with 3 bars of 20 mm diameter (Fe500, HYSD) at the bottom. Concrete grade is M25. The design shear at the support is 120 kN.
> Compute:
>   (i) The flexural bond stress and check its adequacy.
>   (ii) The development length required.
>   (iii) Whether the bars can be anchored into a 300 mm wide support column with a 90° standard hook. Assume the bar extends 50 mm past the column face. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Q.A:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**
*(See full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes — present the 7-step equilibrium approach)*

- Draw FBD of bar with $\pi \phi L_d \tau_{bd}$ on surface and $\pi \phi^2/4 \cdot \sigma_s$ in cross-section.
- Equate and simplify to $L_d = \dfrac{\phi \sigma_s}{4 \tau_{bd}}$.
- Substitute $\sigma_s = 0.87 f_y$ for limit state.
- State IS 456 Clause reference (Cl. 5.4.4).
- Note deformed bar enhancement ($1.6 \times$).

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $b = 250$ mm, $d = 500$ mm, $V = 120$ kN, $\phi = 20$ mm, $f_y = 500$ MPa, M25, HYSD.

**Design bond stress:** $\tau_{bd} = 1.6 \times 1.0 = 1.6$ MPa (M25, deformed)

**(i) Flexural bond stress (3 marks):**

$$\Sigma L_o = 3 \times \pi \times 20 = 188.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$\tau_b = \frac{V}{\Sigma L_o \cdot d} = \frac{120{,}000}{188.5 \times 500} = \frac{120{,}000}{94{,}250} = 1.273 \text{ MPa}$$

Check: $1.273 < 1.6$ MPa → **SAFE** ✓ (1 mark for conclusion)

**(ii) Development length (2 marks):**

$$L_d = \frac{0.87 \times 500 \times 20}{4 \times 1.6} = \frac{8700}{6.4} = 1359.4 \text{ mm} \approx 1360 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Anchorage with hook (2 marks):**

- Straight embedment available = 50 mm (extension into column).
- 90° standard hook equivalent anchorage for HYSD ≈ $12\phi = 12 \times 20 = 240$ mm.
- Total equivalent anchorage available = 50 + 240 = **290 mm**.
- Required = 1360 mm.
- Since 290 mm << 1360 mm → **Hook alone is INSUFFICIENT** ✗.

**Recommendation:** The bars must be **extended further** into the support (≥ 1360 mm straight embedment), or the bar diameter must be **reduced**, or a **coupler/mechanical anchorage** must be used. (1 mark)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]

> **[KTU University Exam - Module Choice Alternative, Module 1 - Pattern]**
> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **mechanisms of bond** in reinforced concrete. Compare the bond behaviour of **plain (mild steel) bars** and **deformed (HYSD) bars**. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A reinforced concrete cantilever of effective depth 400 mm and width 250 mm is reinforced at the top with 4 bars of 12 mm diameter (Fe415, deformed). Concrete grade is M20. The maximum design moment at the fixed support is 85 kN·m and design shear at the fixed support is 65 kN.
> Calculate:
>   (i) The actual stress in the bars at the support.
>   (ii) The development length required.
>   (iii) The flexural bond stress and check if it is within limits. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Q.B:

**Part (a) — Mechanistic explanation (7 marks):**

*(Write in 3 sub-sections)*

**(i) Three mechanisms (4 marks — 1 each + diagram):**
- Chemical adhesion (with C-S-H gel mechanism).
- Friction (with shrinkage and Poisson effect).
- Mechanical interlock (with rib bearing on concrete keys).

Provide a labeled sketch of plain vs deformed bar showing the contribution of each mechanism.

**(ii) Comparison table (3 marks):**

| Property | Plain (MS) Bar | Deformed (HYSD) Bar |
|---|---|---|
| Bond mechanism | Adhesion + Friction only | Adhesion + Friction + **Mechanical Interlock** |
| $\tau_{bd}$ (M20) | 0.8 MPa | 1.28 MPa (1.6× plain) |
| Slip at failure | Significant | Very small |
| Failure mode | Pull-out (gradual) | Splitting (sudden) |
| Surface condition | Smooth | Ribbed (IS 1786 pattern) |

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $b = 250$ mm, $d = 400$ mm, $V = 65$ kN, $A_{st} = 4 \times \pi \times 12^2/4 = 4 \times 113.1 = 452.4$ mm², Fe415, M20, HYSD.

**Design bond stress:** $\tau_{bd} = 1.6 \times 0.8 = 1.28$ MPa

**(i) Stress in bars (2 marks):**

For a rectangular section under limit state, lever arm $z \approx 0.9d = 0.9 \times 400 = 360$ mm (approximation, or compute using $z = d - 0.42x$ with $x$ from equilibrium).

**Approximate using $z = 0.9d$:**

$$\sigma_s = \frac{M}{A_{st} \cdot z} = \frac{85 \times 10^6}{452.4 \times 360} = \frac{85 \times 10^6}{162{,}864} = 521.9 \text{ MPa}$$

But Fe415 steel is limited to $0.87 f_y = 0.87 \times 415 = 361.05$ MPa.

Therefore the actual stress **cannot exceed** $0.87 f_y = 361.05$ MPa. **(1 mark)** This means the section is over-reinforced or the steel area is more than required.

**Effective stress in bars = $\sigma_s = 0.87 f_y = 361.05$ MPa.** (1 mark)

**(ii) Development length (2 marks):**

$$L_d = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 12}{4 \times 1.28} = \frac{4333.74}{5.12} = 846.4 \text{ mm} \approx 850 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Flexural bond stress (3 marks):**

$$\Sigma L_o = 4 \times \pi \times 12 = 150.8 \text{ mm}$$

$$\tau_b = \frac{V}{\Sigma L_o \cdot d} = \frac{65{,}000}{150.8 \times 400} = \frac{65{,}000}{60{,}320} = 1.078 \text{ MPa}$$

Check: $1.078 < 1.28$ MPa → **SAFE** ✓ (1 mark for conclusion)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> **Where students commonly lose marks in Bond problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.6× multiplier** for deformed bars — using plain bar $\tau_{bd}$ for HYSD bars. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 2. **Using $f_y$ instead of $0.87 f_y$** for $\sigma_s$ in $L_d$ calculation. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Mixing up units**: bond stress in MPa, force in N, dimensions in mm. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Forgetting perimeter of bars**: Computing $A_{st}$ as $n \times \phi^2$ (square) instead of $n \times \pi \phi^2/4$ (circular). (–1 mark)
> 5. **Ignoring the $0.87 f_y$ cap** when computing actual bar stress — a critical thinking error. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Forgetting to add the straight portion** when computing equivalent anchorage of a hook. (–1 mark)
> 7. **Not stating the IS 456 Clause number** (5.4.4 for $L_d$, 5.4.5 for hooks) — examiners reward codal references. (–0.5 mark)
> 8. **Saying "hook provides full anchorage"** — FALSE. Hook supplements but does not replace $L_d$. (–2 marks)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — BOND**

### ✅ Key Definitions
- **Bond** = adhesion + grip at steel-concrete interface; unit MPa.
- **Bond stress ($\tau_{bd}$)** = shear stress at the bar surface, IS 456 Table 21.
- **Development length ($L_d$)** = minimum embedment to develop $\sigma_s = 0.87 f_y$.

### ✅ The Master Formula (MEMORIZE)
$$L_d = \frac{0.87 f_y \cdot \phi}{4 \tau_{bd}}$$

### ✅ Three Bond Mechanisms (Hierarchy)
1. **Chemical Adhesion** → C-S-H gel bond, brittle, low capacity.
2. **Friction** → shrinkage + Poisson, $\mu \approx 0.3$–$0.5$.
3. **Mechanical Interlock** → ribs of HYSD, dominant for Fe415/Fe500.

### ✅ IS 456 Quick Numbers
- Deformed bar multiplier: **$1.6 \times$** plain bar value.
- Standard 90° hook equivalent anchorage: **$16\phi$ (plain) / $12\phi$ (HYSD) in M20**.
- Bundle bar penalty: +10%, +20%, +33% for 2, 3, 4-bar bundles.

### ✅ Two Types of Bond in Design
- **Anchorage bond** → governs $L_d$ — **explicitly checked**.
- **Flexural bond** → $\tau_b = V / (\Sigma L_o \cdot d)$ — **not explicitly checked in LSM**, but a good academic exercise.

### ✅ Common Bond Failure Modes
- **Pull-out** (smooth bar, weak concrete, low cover).
- **Splitting** (HYSD bar, inadequate cover or transverse reinf).
- **Prevention**: adequate cover, confinement stirrups, hooks, $L_d$ extension.

### ✅ Common Concrete-Bond Stress Table (Memorize M15–M25)
| Grade | Plain (MPa) | Deformed (MPa) |
|---|---|---|
| M15 | 0.6 | 0.96 |
| M20 | 0.8 | 1.28 |
| M25 | 1.0 | 1.60 |

### ✅ Examination Strategy (Last-Minute)
1. Always state the **IS clause** number along with the formula.
2. Always verify **units** (mm, N, MPa).
3. Always apply the **$1.6 \times$ multiplier** for HYSD bars.
4. Always use **$0.87 f_y$**, not $f_y$, for $\sigma_s$.
5. For anchorage problems, **add straight portion + hook contribution** when comparing with $L_d$.
6. In the answer sheet, **draw the FBD** of the bar showing bond force on the cylindrical surface — visual aids fetch extra marks in KTU valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anchorage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET404 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Anchorage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ANCHORAGE IN PRESTRESSED CONCRETE

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anchorage** in prestressed concrete is defined as the **mechanical device or system** employed at the ends of a prestressing tendon to permanently transfer and maintain the **prestressing force (P)** from the high-tensile steel wires/strands into the concrete member. The region of the concrete member immediately surrounding the anchorage, which is subjected to severe **concentrated stresses (bursting and spalling)**, is technically termed the **End Block** or **Anchorage Zone**.

According to **IS 1343:2012 (Code of Practice for Prestressed Concrete)** and **ACI 318-19**, the anchorage zone is classified into two distinct regions:
1. **Local Zone (Bearing Plate Region):** The region directly beneath the anchorage device, subjected to high **bearing stresses**.
2. **General Zone (End Block Region):** The region extending approximately **1.0 to 1.5 times the depth (D)** of the member from the end face, where **bursting and spalling tensile stresses** develop.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Tug of War" Rope Anchor:**
> Imagine pulling a thick steel cable with tremendous force. If you simply wrap the cable around a small wooden post, the post will **crack and split** longitudinally because of the concentrated pull. To prevent this, we provide a **heavy steel plate with a curved groove** at the back of the post. The plate distributes the pull over a larger area, and **steel spirals/links are wrapped around the post** near the anchor to hold it together against the splitting force.
>
> In prestressed concrete, the **tendon** is the cable, the **anchorage device** is the steel plate-and-cone assembly, and the **helical/spiral reinforcement** inside the concrete end block serves the same role as the steel links around the wooden post — they resist the **bursting force** that tries to split the concrete open.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values (IS 1343:2012 & Common Design Practice):**
> - **Characteristic strength of prestressing steel:** $f_{pk}$ = **1860 N/mm²** (most common for strands) or **1400 N/mm²** (for wires).
> - **Cube compressive strength of concrete (M grade):** $f_{ck}$ = **40 N/mm² to 60 N/mm²** (standard for PSC).
> - **Allowable bearing stress beneath anchorage:** $0.48 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot \sqrt{A_{br}/A_{bg}}$ (Magnel-Bertin formula).
> - **Permissible tensile stress in unreinforced concrete (bursting):** **1.5 N/mm² to 2.0 N/mm²** under service loads.
> - **End block length considered for design:** Generally $1.0D$ to $1.5D$, where $D$ is the larger cross-sectional dimension.
> - **Modular ratio (m):** $E_s / E_c$ = typically **6 to 8** for PSC.

## 1.4 Classification of Anchorage Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Forces at Anchorage Region:**
> The concentrated prestressing force $P$ is applied at the centroid of the section. This creates a **non-uniform stress distribution** in the end block, which can be resolved into:
> - **Bearing stress** (compressive) directly under the anchor plate.
> - **Bursting tension** (transverse tensile) acting perpendicular to the line of action of $P$ — tends to split the concrete.
> - **Spalling tension** (transverse tensile) at the outer surface — tends to peel off the corner concrete.
> - **Compressive stress trajectories** that fan out from the anchor into the full section.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Mechanics of Anchorage Zone Stresses

When the prestressing force **P** is introduced through a relatively small anchorage plate, the **stress trajectories** cannot instantaneously become uniform across the full cross-section. The stress distribution is therefore highly **non-linear** in the end region.

The stress pattern can be visualized as **compressive stress lines** that spread outward from the anchorage in a **fanning or radial pattern**, similar to the flow lines of fluid emerging from a nozzle.

### Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown:

1. **Application of Force:** The total prestressing force $P$ is applied over a small bearing area $A_{bg}$ (gross bearing area of the anchor plate).
2. **Bearing Stress Generation:** A very high **compressive bearing stress** $f_{b} = P / A_{bg}$ is developed directly beneath the plate.
3. **Stress Diffusion:** This concentrated stress diffuses outward (both laterally and longitudinally) over the length of the end block.
4. **Bursting Phenomenon:** As the compressive stress lines curve outward, they exert a **radial outward pull** on the surrounding concrete, generating **tensile stresses in the transverse (horizontal) direction**. This is the **bursting tension** ($F_{bst}$).
5. **Spalling Phenomenon:** Near the outer faces of the end block, the stress trajectories bend back inward, creating **tensile stresses parallel to the end face** — this is the **spalling tension** ($F_{spt}$).
6. **Equilibrium of Forces:** The bursting and spalling tensions must be resisted by **reinforcement** (helical spirals or orthogonal links) placed in the end block.

### 2.2 Guyon's Theory of Symmetrical Prism (Most Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Guyon's Elastic Analysis (1960s, IS 1343 Recommended):**
> Guyon solved the problem of stress distribution in a **symmetrical prism (end block)** subjected to a concentrated force along its centroidal axis. He provided non-dimensional coefficients to compute bursting tension as a function of:
> - The ratio of bearing area to gross area: $A_{bg}/A_{c}$
> - The position of the critical section from the end face.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units / Standard Value |
|---|-----------|---------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Total Prestressing Force | $P = A_p \cdot f_p$ | kN or N |
| 2 | Bearing Pressure under Anchor | $f_b = P / A_{bg}$ | N/mm² |
| 3 | Permissible Bearing Stress (Magnel) | $f_{bp} = 0.48 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot \sqrt{A_{br}/A_{bg}}$ | N/mm² (limited to $0.6 f_{ck}$ per ACI) |
| 4 | Bursting Tensile Force (Guyon) | $F_{bst} = K \cdot P$ | kN (where $K$ = bursting coefficient) |
| 5 | Bursting Coefficient $K$ | $K = 0.32 - 0.30 \cdot (y_{po}/D) + 0.15 \cdot (y_{po}/D)^2$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Spalling Tensile Force | $F_{spt} = 0.04 \cdot P$ to $0.06 \cdot P$ | kN (empirical) |
| 7 | Bursting Reinforcement Area | $A_{sv} = F_{bst} / f_y$ (for vertical links) | mm² |
| 8 | Spalling Reinforcement Area | $A_{sh} = F_{spt} / f_y$ (for horizontal links) | mm² |
| 9 | Diameter of Helical Spiral | $d_h = $ minimum of **6 mm** to **10 mm** | mm |
| 10 | Pitch of Spiral | $p = 25$ to **50 mm** (closer near anchor) | mm |
| 11 | Length of End Block | $L_{eb} = 1.0D$ to $1.5D$ | mm (D = larger cross-section dimension) |
| 12 | Distance of critical bursting section from end | $y_{po} = 0.5 \cdot y_e$ (for symmetric anchor) | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> Anchorage design is **critical for safety** in:
> - **Post-tensioned bridge girders** (box girders, I-girders).
> - **Precast PSC railway sleepers**.
> - **Nuclear containment structures** (where tendon integrity is non-negotiable).
> - **Long-span PSC floor slabs** in high-rise buildings.
> - **Cable-stayed bridge pylons** (anchorage of stay cables).
>
> Failure of anchorage can lead to **catastrophic progressive collapse**, making this topic a **favourite 14-mark question in KTU ESE**.

## 2.4 Guyon's Bursting Coefficient — Tabulated Values (Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a symmetrical anchorage placed at the centroid of a square/rectangular end block:**

| $y_{po}/D$ | Bursting Coefficient $K$ (Guyon) |
|:-----------:|:----------------------------------:|
| 0.0 (central) | 0.32 |
| 0.1 | 0.30 |
| 0.2 | 0.28 |
| 0.3 | 0.25 |
| 0.4 | 0.22 |
| 0.5 (edge) | 0.19 |
