| $\geq$ 1.0 | 0.00 |

The table clearly shows that **bursting force is maximum when the anchorage is centrally placed** and reduces as the anchor moves towards the edge.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Bursting Tensile Force (Guyon's Approach)

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
Consider a **prismatic concrete end block** of width $D$ (larger cross-section dimension) and thickness $b$, subjected to a concentric prestressing force $P$ applied through an anchorage plate of width $a$ (along $D$).

The critical bursting section is located at a distance $y_{po}$ from the end face, where:

$$y_{po} = \sqrt{a \cdot D} \cdot \left[1 - \sqrt{\frac{a}{D}}\right] \quad \text{(for rectangular block)}$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Compute the ratio of bearing area to gross area:

$$\beta = \frac{A_{bg}}{A_c} = \frac{a \cdot b}{D \cdot b} = \frac{a}{D}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the location of the critical bursting section (non-dimensional form):

$$\frac{y_{po}}{D} = \sqrt{\beta} \cdot (1 - \sqrt{\beta})$$

**Step 3:** Compute the bursting coefficient $K$ from Guyon's empirical equation:

$$K = 0.32 - 0.30 \cdot \left(\frac{y_{po}}{D}\right) + 0.15 \cdot \left(\frac{y_{po}}{D}\right)^2$$

**Step 4:** Compute the total bursting tensile force:

$$F_{bst} = K \cdot P$$

**Step 5:** Distribute this force over the critical section (which lies at depth $y_{po}$ from the end face) to find bursting stress:

$$f_{bt} = \frac{F_{bst}}{b \cdot y_{po}}$$

**Step 6:** Design bursting reinforcement. Since the bursting tension acts perpendicular to the direction of the prestressing force, it is resisted by **closed links/stirrups** placed in planes perpendicular to the tendon:

$$A_{sv} = \frac{F_{bst}}{\sigma_{sv}}$$

where $\sigma_{sv}$ is the allowable tensile stress in the bursting reinforcement (typically **0.87 $f_y$** as per IS 456/IS 1343, or limited to **200 N/mm²** to control crack widths).

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Typical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Given Data for a Typical KTU Numerical:**
> - Post-tensioned beam: $D$ = 600 mm (depth), $b$ = 300 mm (width).
> - Anchorage plate dimensions: $a$ = 200 mm × $b$ = 300 mm.
> - Prestressing force: $P$ = 1800 kN.
> - Yield strength of bursting links: $f_y$ = 500 N/mm² (Fe 500).
> - Design tensile stress in links: $\sigma_{sv}$ = 0.87 × 500 = **435 N/mm²**.

**Step 1:** Compute area ratios:

$$A_{bg} = 200 \times 300 = 60{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_c = 600 \times 300 = 180{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\beta = \frac{60{,}000}{180{,}000} = 0.333$$

**Step 2:** Compute $y_{po}/D$:

$$\frac{y_{po}}{D} = \sqrt{0.333} \cdot (1 - \sqrt{0.333}) = 0.577 \cdot (1 - 0.577) = 0.577 \cdot 0.423 = 0.244$$

**Step 3:** Compute bursting coefficient $K$:

$$\begin{aligned} K &= 0.32 - 0.30 \cdot (0.244) + 0.15 \cdot (0.244)^2 \\ &= 0.32 - 0.0732 + 0.0089 \\ &= 0.2557 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Compute bursting tensile force:

$$F_{bst} = K \cdot P = 0.2557 \times 1800 = 460.26 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5:** Locate the critical bursting section:

$$y_{po} = 0.244 \times 600 = 146.4 \text{ mm} \approx 150 \text{ mm from end face}$$

**Step 6:** Check bearing stress:

$$f_b = \frac{P}{A_{bg}} = \frac{1{,}800{,}000}{60{,}000} = 30 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 7:** Check against allowable bearing stress (Magnel-Bertin):

$$f_{bp} = 0.48 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{A_{br}}{A_{bg}}}$$

Assuming $f_{ck} = 40$ N/mm² and $A_{br}/A_{bg} = 4$ (typical confining reinforcement):

$$f_{bp} = 0.48 \times 40 \times \sqrt{4} = 38.4 \text{ N/mm}^2 \geq 30 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

**Step 8:** Design bursting reinforcement:

$$A_{sv} = \frac{F_{bst}}{\sigma_{sv}} = \frac{460{,}260}{435} = 1058 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 9:** Provide closed links. Using 12 mm dia 2-legged links:

$$A_{\phi 12} = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 12^2 = 226.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

Number of links required:

$$n = \frac{1058}{226.2} = 4.68 \approx 5 \text{ closed links}$$

**Step 10:** Distribute over a length of $1.0 D$ = 600 mm. Provide **5 closed links of 12 mm dia** @ **100 mm c/c** (i.e., at 50, 150, 250, 350, 450 mm from end face) over a length of 450 mm.

**Step 11:** Check spalling reinforcement. Empirical formula:

$$F_{spt} = 0.05 \cdot P = 0.05 \times 1800 = 90 \text{ kN}$$

$$A_{sh} = \frac{90{,}000}{435} = 207 \text{ mm}^2$$

Provide **3 closed horizontal links of 10 mm dia** @ **100 mm c/c** near the outer face of the end block.

### 3.2 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Step | Quantity | Calculated Value | Significance |
|------|----------|------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Bearing stress $f_b$ | 30 N/mm² | Must be $\leq 0.48 f_{ck} \sqrt{A_{br}/A_{bg}}$ |
| 2 | $y_{po}/D$ ratio | 0.244 | Locates critical bursting section |
| 3 | Bursting coefficient $K$ | 0.2557 | From Guyon's formula |
| 4 | Bursting force $F_{bst}$ | 460.26 kN | Resisted by closed vertical links |
| 5 | Bursting steel area $A_{sv}$ | 1058 mm² | Distributed over $1.0D$ length |
| 6 | Spalling force $F_{spt}$ | 90 kN | Resisted by horizontal links |
| 7 | Spalling steel area $A_{sh}$ | 207 mm² | Placed near outer face |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Anchorage Stress Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional flow of forces** and **stress generation mechanism** in a typical post-tensioned anchorage zone. It maps the interactions between the tendon, anchorage hardware, end block concrete, and the bursting/spalling reinforcement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prestressing Steel Tendon] --> B[High Tensile Force P Applied]
    B --> C[Concentrated Load at Anchorage Plate]
    C --> D[Local Zone Bearing Stress fb]
    D --> E[Stress Diffusion in End Block]
    E --> F1[Bursting Tension Fbst - Transverse Vertical]
    E --> F2[Spalling Tension Fspt - Transverse Horizontal]
    E --> F3[Compressive Stress Trajectories - Radial Fan Pattern]
    F1 --> G1[Closed Vertical Links / Stirrups Resist Bursting]
    F2 --> G2[Horizontal Links Near Face Resist Spalling]
    F3 --> H[Uniform Stress Distribution Achieved at Distance 1.0D]
    G1 --> I[End Block Equilibrium Satisfied]
    G2 --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[PSC Member Safe Under Prestress]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#ea580c,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#ca8a04,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#0d9488,color:#ffffff
    style F1 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style F2 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style F3 fill:#166534,color:#ffffff
    style G1 fill:#6b21a8,color:#ffffff
    style G2 fill:#6b21a8,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#0891b2,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Schematic of Anchorage Hardware Layout (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ANCHOR_HARDWARE [ANCHORAGE ASSEMBLY]
        T1[Anchoring Head / Chuck] --> T2[Bearing Plate]
        T2 --> T3[Trumpet / Duct Former]
        T3 --> T4[Helical Spiral Reinforcement]
        T4 --> T5[End Block Concrete Region]
        T5 --> T6[General Section Far from End]
    end

    style T1 fill:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    style T2 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style T3 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style T4 fill:#6b21a8,color:#ffffff
    style T5 fill:#ca8a04,color:#ffffff
    style T6 fill:#166534,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: Design Procedure for End Block (KTU Exam Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Given P, D, b, a, fck, fy] --> S2[Compute Area Ratios Abg and Ac]
    S2 --> S3[Compute ypo by D ratio]
    S3 --> S4[Compute Bursting Coefficient K]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Fbst = K times P]
    S5 --> S6[Compute Bearing Stress fb and Check against Magnel Limit]
    S6 --> S7{Is Bearing Stress Safe?}
    S7 -- No --> S8[Increase Anchor Plate Size]
    S8 --> S2
    S7 -- Yes --> S9[Compute Bursting Steel Asv]
    S9 --> S10[Compute Spalling Force Fspt]
    S10 --> S11[Compute Spalling Steel Ash]
    S11 --> S12[Provide Closed Links and Helical Spiral]
    S12 --> S13[Check Detailing: Cover, Spacing, Anchorage Length]
    S13 --> S14[End Block Design Complete]

    style S1 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style S14 fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style S7 fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style S8 fill:#ea580c,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Common Anchorage Systems (Comparative)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Common Anchorage Systems for PSC] --> M2[Freyssinet System]
    M1 --> M3[Magnel System]
    M1 --> M4[Gifford Udall System]
    M1 --> M5[Lee McCall System]
    M1 --> M6[BBRV System]

    M2 --> N1[Uses Conical Wedges and Female Cone - For Wires]
    M3 --> N2[Uses Sandwich Plates - For Multiple Wires]
    M4 --> N3[Uses Split Wedges and Anchor Plate - For Wires]
    M5 --> N4[Uses Solid Bar and Nut - For Single Bars]
    M6 --> N5[Uses Button Head Wires - For Multi Wire Tendons]

    style M1 fill:#0f172a,color:#ffffff
    style M2 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style M3 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style M4 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style M5 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style M6 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style N1 fill:#0d9488,color:#ffffff
    style N2 fill:#0d9488,color:#ffffff
    style N3 fill:#0d9488,color:#ffffff
    style N4 fill:#0d9488,color:#ffffff
    style N5 fill:#0d9488,color:#ffffff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define anchorage zone in prestressed concrete. Mention its two classifications as per IS 1343:2012.**

**Model Answer:**
The **anchorage zone** is the region of a prestressed concrete member where the prestressing force is transferred from the tendon to the surrounding concrete through the anchorage device.

As per **IS 1343:2012**, the anchorage zone is classified into:
1. **Local Zone:** The region immediately surrounding the anchorage device, subjected to high **bearing stresses**.
2. **General Zone:** The region in which the **bursting and spalling tensile stresses** develop, extending up to about **1.0D to 1.5D** from the end face.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish clearly between bursting tension and spalling tension in an end block.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bursting Tension ($F_{bst}$) | Spalling Tension ($F_{spt}$) |
|--------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Direction | Perpendicular to tendon axis (transverse vertical) | Parallel to end face, at outer surface (transverse horizontal) |
| Cause | Curvature of compressive stress lines in interior | Stress trajectories bending back at outer corners |
| Magnitude | **20% to 30%** of $P$ (large) | **4% to 6%** of $P$ (small) |
| Location | Within the end block interior | Near the free outer surface |
| Resisted by | Closed vertical links / helical spirals | Horizontal links near the face |

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**A post-tensioned PSC beam has the following data:**
- Cross-section: 400 mm (width) × 800 mm (depth).
- Prestressing force: $P$ = 2000 kN applied through an anchorage plate of 250 mm × 400 mm.
- Concrete grade: M50 ($f_{ck}$ = 50 N/mm²).
- Steel for bursting links: Fe 500 ($f_y$ = 500 N/mm²).
- Allowable tensile stress in steel: $\sigma_{sv}$ = 0.87 $f_y$ = 435 N/mm².

**Design the end block reinforcement. Show all steps clearly.**

#### (a) Design of Anchorage Zone (Bearing Check) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Compute areas.

$$A_{bg} = 250 \times 400 = 100{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_c = 800 \times 400 = 320{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{bg}/A_c = 0.3125$$

**Step 2:** Compute actual bearing stress.

$$f_b = \frac{P}{A_{bg}} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000}{100{,}000} = 20 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Compute allowable bearing stress (Magnel-Bertin). Assume the bearing plate is set on a confining helical spiral such that $A_{br}/A_{bg} = 4$:

$$f_{bp} = 0.48 \times 50 \times \sqrt{4} = 48 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Since $f_b = 20 \text{ N/mm}^2 < f_{bp} = 48 \text{ N/mm}^2$, **the bearing is SAFE**. ✓

**Step 4:** Compute $y_{po}/D$.

$$\frac{y_{po}}{D} = \sqrt{0.3125} \cdot (1 - \sqrt{0.3125}) = 0.559 \cdot (1 - 0.559) = 0.559 \cdot 0.441 = 0.246$$

**Step 5:** Compute bursting coefficient $K$ using Guyon's formula:

$$\begin{aligned} K &= 0.32 - 0.30 \cdot (0.246) + 0.15 \cdot (0.246)^2 \\ &= 0.32 - 0.0738 + 0.0091 \\ &= 0.2553 \end{aligned}$$

**Step 6:** Compute bursting tensile force:

$$F_{bst} = K \cdot P = 0.2553 \times 2000 = 510.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7:** Locate critical bursting section.

$$y_{po} = 0.246 \times 800 = 196.8 \text{ mm} \approx 200 \text{ mm from end face}$$

#### (b) Design of Bursting and Spalling Reinforcement — 7 Marks

**Step 8:** Required bursting steel area:

$$A_{sv} = \frac{F_{bst}}{\sigma_{sv}} = \frac{510{,}600}{435} = 1174 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 9:** Choose 12 mm dia 2-legged closed links.

$$A_{\phi 12} = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 12^2 = 226.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 10:** Number of closed links required:

$$n = \frac{1174}{226.2} = 5.19 \approx 6 \text{ links}$$

**Step 11:** Distribute over $1.0 D$ = 800 mm. Provide **6 closed links of 12 mm dia @ 125 mm c/c** over a length of **750 mm** from the end face.

**Step 12:** Compute spalling force using empirical formula:

$$F_{spt} = 0.05 \cdot P = 0.05 \times 2000 = 100 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 13:** Required spalling steel area:

$$A_{sh} = \frac{100{,}000}{435} = 230 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 14:** Provide **3 closed horizontal links of 10 mm dia @ 100 mm c/c** near the outer face (i.e., 30, 130, 230 mm from outer face) within the first 250 mm of the end block.

**Step 15:** Provide a **helical spiral of 10 mm dia** with **pitch 40 mm** wound over a length of **800 mm** around the anchorage zone for additional confinement.

**Step 16:** Detailing check:
- Minimum cover to links: **50 mm** (for PSC).
- Spacing of links $\leq$ **150 mm** as per IS 1343.
- All links fully closed and properly anchored at corners. ✓

---

### QUESTION B [14 Marks] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**A pretensioned PSC member uses 12 numbers of 7 mm dia high-tensile wires stressed to 1200 N/mm². The end block is 300 mm wide and 450 mm deep. The anchorage is concentric.**

**(a) Compute the bursting tensile force and the bursting stress. — 7 Marks**

**(b) Design suitable bursting reinforcement. — 7 Marks**

#### (a) Computation of Bursting Force and Stress — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Compute total area of prestressing steel.

$$A_p = 12 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 7^2 = 12 \times 38.48 = 461.8 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Compute total prestressing force.

$$P = A_p \cdot f_p = 461.8 \times 1200 = 554{,}160 \text{ N} = 554.16 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3:** For pretensioned members, bursting is computed using a slightly modified approach. The end block is reinforced with helical binding.

For concentric anchorage, the bursting coefficient $K \approx 0.32$ (from Guyon's formula at $y_{po}/D \approx 0$).

**Step 4:** Bursting tensile force:

$$F_{bst} = 0.32 \times 554.16 = 177.33 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5:** Critical section location. The end block length is taken as $1.0D$ = 450 mm. The bursting force acts over a depth approximately equal to $0.25D$ from the loaded face:

$$y_{po} \approx 0.25 \times 450 = 112.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6:** Bursting tensile stress:

$$f_{bt} = \frac{F_{bst}}{b \cdot y_{po}} = \frac{177{,}330}{300 \times 112.5} = 5.25 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Bursting vs Spalling Confusion:**
> A common student error is to **confuse bursting with spalling**. Bursting occurs **inside the end block** due to radial outward thrust, while spalling occurs **at the outer face** due to peeling action. They have **different magnitudes, locations, and reinforcement requirements**. Marking deduction of **2 to 3 marks** is common if not distinguished.

#### (b) Design of Bursting Reinforcement — 7 Marks

**Step 7:** Use Fe 500 links with $\sigma_{sv} = 0.87 \times 500 = 435$ N/mm².

**Step 8:** Required bursting steel area:

$$A_{sv} = \frac{177{,}330}{435} = 408 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 9:** Choose 8 mm dia 2-legged closed links.

$$A_{\phi 8} = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 8^2 = 100.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 10:** Number of links:

$$n = \frac{408}{100.5} = 4.06 \approx 5 \text{ links}$$

**Step 11:** Distribute over $1.0 D$ = 450 mm. Provide **5 closed links of 8 mm dia @ 100 mm c/c** over a length of 400 mm from the end face.

**Step 12:** Also provide **helical spiral of 8 mm dia** with **pitch 30 mm** and **diameter 200 mm**, wound over the end block length.

**Step 13:** Detailing:
- Cover: **30 mm** minimum.
- Pitch: $\leq$ 50 mm (closer near the anchor).
- Total helical weight: $W = \pi \cdot d_{helix} \cdot n_{turns} \cdot w_{8mm}$ where $w_{8mm} = 0.395$ kg/m.

$$\text{Number of turns} = \frac{450}{30} = 15 \text{ turns}$$

$$W = \pi \times 0.2 \times 15 \times 0.395 = 3.72 \text{ kg of 8 mm spiral}$$

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING & PITFALLS

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in KTU ESE (14-Mark Questions):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to check bearing stress:** Students often directly compute bursting force without verifying $f_b \leq 0.48 f_{ck} \sqrt{A_{br}/A_{bg}}$. **Deduct 2 marks** if missed.
> 2. **Wrong sign convention in Guyon's formula:** $K$ is **positive** (a coefficient, not a stress). Forgetting the **0.15 × ($y_{po}/D$)² term** leads to a 5% error. **Deduct 1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing units:** $P$ in kN vs N — a single decimal shift can cascade into wrong reinforcement. **Deduct 2 marks**.
> 4. **Not providing helical spiral:** For end blocks, **closed links alone are insufficient**. A helical spiral of 8–10 mm dia @ 30–50 mm pitch is **mandatory** as per IS 1343. **Deduct 2 marks** if missed.
> 5. **Spalling reinforcement omitted:** Often neglected by students. **Deduct 1–2 marks** if absent.
> 6. **Detailing errors:** Spacing, cover, anchorage length of links — all checked. **Deduct 1 mark** for any non-conformance.
> 7. **Sketch not drawn:** A neat end-block sketch with reinforcement labelled carries **1–2 marks** weightage in KTU 14-mark answers.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Anchorage" (Module 1)**
>
> - **Anchorage zone** has two parts: **Local zone (bearing)** and **General zone (bursting + spalling)**.
> - **Bearing stress** $f_b = P / A_{bg}$ must be $\leq$ **0.48 $f_{ck} \sqrt{A_{br}/A_{bg}}$** (Magnel-Bertin, IS 1343).
> - **Bursting tension $F_{bst} = K \cdot P$**, where $K$ is from **Guyon's empirical formula** (0.32 for central, lower for edge anchors).
> - **Critical bursting section** is at $y_{po} \approx 0.25D$ from the end face for a centrally loaded symmetric prism.
> - **Spalling tension** $F_{spt} \approx$ **0.04 P to 0.06 P** (empirical).
> - **Bursting resisted by:** Closed vertical links + helical spiral.
> - **Spalling resisted by:** Horizontal links near the outer face.
> - **End block length for design:** **1.0 D to 1.5 D** (D = larger cross-section dimension).
> - **Minimum helical spiral pitch:** 25–50 mm (closer near the anchor, looser away).
> - **Modular ratio m** for PSC: typically **6 to 8** (use 6.67 for M40–M60 grades).
> - **Allowable tensile stress in PSC (no auxiliary reinforcement):** 1.5 to 2.0 N/mm².
> - **Common anchorage systems:** Freyssinet, Magnel, Gifford Udall, Lee-McCall, BBRV.
> - **Bursting steel stress** limited to **$\sigma_{sv} = 0.87 f_y$** (≈ 435 N/mm² for Fe 500).
> - **IS 1343:2012** is the governing code in KTU examinations for all PSC design.
> - **Sketch is compulsory** in KTU 14-mark answers — carry at least **1–2 marks** weightage.
> - **Always end with a final detailing check:** cover, spacing, anchorage length, and link closure.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Development Length and Splicing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET404 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Development Length and Splicing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (IS 456:2000 Terminology)

**Development Length (Ld)** is the length of embedded reinforcement bar required on either side of a critical section, sufficient to develop the full design strength of the bar through bond stress transfer between steel and surrounding concrete. It is governed by **Clause 26.2 of IS 456:2000**.

$$\text{Ld} = \frac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}$$

where:
- $\phi$ = Nominal diameter of the bar
- $\sigma_s$ = Stress in the bar at the section considered (typically **0.87 × fy**)
- $\tau_{bd}$ = Design bond stress (MPa) as per Table 26.1 of IS 456

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Splicing** is the technique of joining two reinforcement bars end-to-end to transfer the load continuously. The transfer of force is achieved through **lap splicing, welded splicing, or mechanical couplers** (Clause 26.5 of IS 456:2000).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are holding a steel rod and trying to pull it out of a block of clay. The harder you pull, the deeper it must be embedded in the clay so that the friction along its sides can resist the pull. **Development length is precisely this "embedment depth"** required so that the friction (called *bond*) between steel and concrete is strong enough to develop the bar's full tensile capacity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy**: Think of a plant creeper on a trellis. The more force the wind applies, the longer the section of the creeper that must wrap around the support to avoid slipping. Similarly, a rebar needs a certain *anchored length* inside concrete to "hang on" without slipping.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Grade of concrete**: M20, M25, M30, M35, M40 (most common in KTU design problems)
- **Grade of steel**: Fe 415, Fe 500, Fe 550 (HYSD bars per IS 1786)
- **Design bond stress ($\tau_{bd}$)**: ranges from **0.6 MPa to 1.1 MPa** for plain bars, and **1.6 × these values** for deformed bars
- **Design stress in bar ($\sigma_s$)**: **0.87 × fy** (where fy = characteristic yield strength of steel)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Splicing** is a critical detailing requirement because reinforcement bars come in standard lengths (typically **12 m**). On-site continuity is achieved by lapping bars side-by-side, which makes the **lap length** a direct extension of the development length concept.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Concept Breakdown — Why and How

The development length concept rests on **equilibrium of forces** along the embedded bar:

1. **Tensile force in bar** at a critical section tries to pull the bar out of concrete.
2. **Bond stress** (shear) acts along the surface of the bar, resisting this pull-out.
3. **Equilibrium** is reached when the cumulative bond force equals the bar's tensile capacity.
4. The **minimum length** over which this bond force must be developed is the *development length* (Ld).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this concept critical in design?** If a bar terminates too close to a critical section (e.g., near a support of a simply supported beam), it may slip out under load, causing catastrophic **bond failure** even if the bar's flexural capacity is theoretically adequate.

## 2.2 Design Bond Stress (τbd) — Table 26.1 of IS 456:2000

| Grade of Concrete | $\tau_{bd}$ for Plain Bars (MPa) | $\tau_{bd}$ for Deformed Bars (MPa) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| M20 | 0.6 | 0.96 |
| M25 | 0.8 | 1.28 |
| M30 | 0.9 | 1.44 |
| M35 | 1.0 | 1.60 |
| M40 | 1.1 | 1.76 |
| M45 | 1.2 | 1.92 |
| M50 | 1.3 | 2.08 |

> For design purposes, **$\tau_{bd}$ values given above are already multiplied by 1.6 for deformed bars** as per the code convention, so the same formula $\text{Ld} = \frac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}$ applies directly to both.

## 2.3 Modification Factors (Clause 26.2.1 of IS 456)

| Condition | Modification Factor |
| :--- | :---: |
| Bars in **compression** | **0.75** (Ld reduced by 25%) |
| Bars in **tension with > 300 mm of fresh concrete below** (top bars) | **1.33** for deformed bars |
| Bars in **flexural tension near supports** | Ld for full tensile stress |
| Bars in **bundled bars** | Ld increased by factors per IS 456 Cl. 26.2.1.3 |
| Epoxy-coated bars | 1.5 × Ld |
| Lightweight aggregate concrete | 1.33 × Ld (depends on density) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Reference |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Basic Development Length | $\text{Ld} = \frac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}$ | IS 456 Cl. 26.2.1 |
| Design stress in bar (tension) | $\sigma_s = 0.87 \cdot f_y$ | IS 456 Cl. 26.2.1.1 |
| Design stress in bar (compression) | $\sigma_s = 0.80 \cdot f_y$ (or actual stress) | IS 456 Cl. 26.2.1.2 |
| Top bar factor (deformed) | Multiply Ld by **1.33** | IS 456 Cl. 26.2.1.1 |
| Compression factor | Multiply Ld by **0.75** | IS 456 Cl. 26.2.1.2 |
| **Lap splice (tension, in zone of max moment)** | **1.3 × Ld** (not < 30$\phi$ or 300 mm) | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.2.1 |
| **Lap splice (tension, elsewhere)** | **1.0 × Ld** (not < 30$\phi$ or 300 mm) | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.2.1 |
| **Lap splice (compression)** | **0.85 × Ld** (or Ld in compression, not < 24$\phi$) | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.2.1b |
| Maximum bar diameter for lapping | 36 mm (for larger bars, use welding) | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1 |
| Maximum % of bars spliced at one section (flexural tension) | 50% at any section | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.2.1 |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Continuous Beams and Slabs**: Top bars in negative moment regions must extend past the point of inflection by the development length to prevent bond failure.
- **Cantilever Beams**: Main reinforcement at the fixed end must be fully developed into the column/slab to resist the maximum bending moment.
- **Column Lapping**: Vertical bars in columns are lapped at floor levels with a pre-determined lap length.
- **Retrofitting**: Splicing is essential when existing reinforcement needs to be extended for strengthening works.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Basic Development Length Formula

### Setup of the Problem

Consider a straight reinforcing bar of diameter $\phi$ embedded in concrete. Suppose the bar is subjected to a tensile force $T$ at the critical section. To prevent the bar from being pulled out, bond stress $\tau_{bd}$ must act along the surface area of the bar in contact with concrete.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1: Express the tensile force in the bar.**

The cross-sectional area of the bar is given by:

$$
A_s = \frac{\pi \cdot \phi^2}{4}
$$

The tensile force in the bar at the section of maximum stress is:

$$
T = \sigma_s \cdot A_s = \sigma_s \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot \phi^2}{4}
$$

**Step 2: Express the bond (resisting) force along the embedment length.**

The surface area of the bar in contact with concrete over a length $L_d$ is:

$$
A_{bond} = \pi \cdot \phi \cdot L_d
$$

The bond (shear) force resisting pull-out is:

$$
F_{bond} = \tau_{bd} \cdot A_{bond} = \tau_{bd} \cdot \pi \cdot \phi \cdot L_d
$$

**Step 3: Apply equilibrium condition.**

For the bar to be in equilibrium (i.e., not slip out), the bond force must equal the tensile force:

$$
F_{bond} = T
$$

Substituting the expressions from Step 1 and Step 2:

$$
\tau_{bd} \cdot \pi \cdot \phi \cdot L_d = \sigma_s \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot \phi^2}{4}
$$

**Step 4: Cancel common terms and solve for $L_d$.**

Dividing both sides by $\pi \cdot \phi$:

$$
\tau_{bd} \cdot L_d = \sigma_s \cdot \frac{\phi}{4}
$$

Solving for $L_d$:

$$
L_d = \frac{\sigma_s \cdot \phi}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}
$$

This is the **fundamental expression for development length** as per IS 456:2000 (Clause 26.2.1).

### Final Result (Highlight Box)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Development Length Formula:**
> $$L_d = \frac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}$$
> This is the most heavily tested formula in KTU Module 1 derivations and numerical problems.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Ld Calculation

**Problem:** A simply supported RC beam of effective span 5 m is reinforced with **4 bars of 20 mm diameter** at the bottom. Concrete grade is **M20** and steel is **Fe 415 (HYSD deformed bars)**. Determine the development length of the tension reinforcement at mid-span, considering the bars as **top bars** (cast monolithically with more than 300 mm of concrete below).

**Step 1: Identify parameters.**

- $\phi = 20 \text{ mm}$
- $f_y = 415 \text{ MPa}$ (HYSD bars)
- Grade of concrete = M20
- $\sigma_s = 0.87 \cdot f_y = 0.87 \cdot 415 = 360.45 \text{ MPa}$

**Step 2: Identify bond stress.**

For M20, deformed bars (HYSD conforming to IS 1786):

$$
\tau_{bd} = 0.96 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate basic development length.**

$$
L_d = \frac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}} = \frac{20 \cdot 360.45}{4 \cdot 0.96}
$$

Performing the calculation:

$$
L_d = \frac{7209.0}{3.84} = 1877.34 \text{ mm} \approx 1877 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Apply top bar modification factor.**

Since the bars are "top bars" (more than 300 mm of fresh concrete below), multiply by **1.33**:

$$
L_{d,\text{effective}} = 1.33 \cdot 1877.34 = 2496.86 \text{ mm} \approx 2497 \text{ mm}
$$

Or equivalently, expressed as a multiple of bar diameter:

$$
L_{d,\text{effective}} = \frac{2496.86}{20} \approx 124.8 \cdot \phi
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result**: The development length is approximately **2.5 m** for 20 mm Fe 415 bars in M20 concrete when cast as top bars. This shows why the standard bar length of 12 m is critical in design — anchorage consumes a significant portion of available bar length.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Splicing Rules Summary

| Splicing Scenario | Lap Length Formula | Minimum Length | Code Reference |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Bars in **tension, zone of max moment** | **1.3 × Ld** | 30$\phi$ or 300 mm (whichever is greater) | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.2.1a |
| Bars in **tension, zone of low moment** | **1.0 × Ld** | 30$\phi$ or 300 mm (whichever is greater) | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.2.1a |
| Bars in **compression** | **0.85 × Ld (compression)** | 24$\phi$ for bars; 30$\phi$ for spirals | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.2.1b |
| **> 50% of bars spliced at one section** (flexural tension) | **1.5 × Ld** | 30$\phi$ or 300 mm | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.2.1a |
| Bars of **different diameters spliced** | Based on **smaller $\phi$** | As above | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.2 |
| **Welded splice** for bars > 36 mm | Full design strength of bar | Weld length per IS 2751 | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.4 |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation — Lap Splice Length from Ld

**Conceptual Basis:**

A lap splice works by transferring force from one bar to the other through the surrounding concrete. Therefore, the bond developed over the lap length in the donor bar must equal the full tensile capacity of the bar.

**Step 1: Identify the bond force developed over lap length $L_{lap}$.**

$$
F_{bond} = \tau_{bd} \cdot \pi \cdot \phi \cdot L_{lap}
$$

**Step 2: Set this equal to the full design tensile force in the bar.**

$$
\tau_{bd} \cdot \pi \cdot \phi \cdot L_{lap} = \sigma_s \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot \phi^2}{4}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $L_{lap}$.**

$$
L_{lap} = \frac{\sigma_s \cdot \phi}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}} = L_d
$$

So the basic lap length equals the development length in tension. To account for the simultaneous stress in both bars at the splice (which is more critical), the **code multiplies Ld by a factor**.

**Step 4: Apply the code factor of 1.3 (in zone of maximum moment).**

$$
L_{lap} = 1.3 \cdot L_d
$$

**Step 5: Apply the minimum length rule.**

$$
L_{lap} = \max \left( 1.3 \cdot L_d,\ 30 \cdot \phi,\ 300 \text{ mm} \right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 1.3 × Ld and not 1.0 × Ld?** At a lap splice, BOTH bars are at full stress simultaneously, so the bond demand is doubled. The factor 1.3 (and 1.5 in critical zones) accounts for this additional safety requirement.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Development Length Concept — Force Transfer Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the physical mechanism of force transfer in a development length scenario:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Steel Bar with Tensile Force T] -->|T at critical section| B[Embedded Portion Ld]
    B -->|Bond stress τbd acting| C[Concrete Surrounding the Bar]
    C -->|Resists pull-out| D[Equilibrium: Bond force = T]
    A -->|Bar diameter = phi| E[Cross section: Area = pi * phi^2 / 4]
    E -->|Stress in bar| F[sigma_s = 0.87 fy for HYSD]
    F -->|Formula| G[Ld = phi sigma_s / 4 tau_bd]
    G -->|Modifications| H{Type of bar}
    H -->|Top bar| I[Ld_eff = 1.33 Ld]
    H -->|Compression bar| J[Ld_eff = 0.75 Ld]
    H -->|Standard tension bar| K[Ld_eff = 1.0 Ld]
```

## 4.2 Splicing Mechanism — Lap Joint Architecture

The following flowchart details the decision-making process for splicing design:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Need for Splicing Identified] --> B{Bar diameter exceeds 36 mm?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Use Welded Splice or Mechanical Coupler]
    B -->|No| D{Location of Splice}
    D -->|Flexural Tension Zone| E{Percentage of bars spliced at one section}
    D -->|Compression Zone| F[Lap length = 0.85 Ld compression]
    D -->|General Tension Zone| G[Lap length = 1.0 Ld tension]
    E -->|Up to 50 percent of bars| H{Stress at splice location}
    E -->|More than 50 percent of bars| I[Lap length = 1.5 Ld tension]
    H -->|Maximum stress zone| J[Lap length = 1.3 Ld tension]
    H -->|Low stress zone| K[Lap length = 1.0 Ld tension]
    F --> L[Apply minimum length rule]
    G --> L
    I --> L
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L[Check: max of lap length, 30 phi, 300 mm]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Design Workflow

The following diagram represents the complete KTU design workflow for development length and splicing in a typical RC beam:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Bar diameter phi, Steel grade fy, Concrete grade] --> B[Step 1: Determine design stress sigma_s]
    B --> C[Step 2: Select tau_bd from IS 456 Table 26.1]
    C --> D[Step 3: Compute basic Ld = phi sigma_s / 4 tau_bd]
    D --> E{Apply modification factors}
    E -->|Top bar 1.33| F[Compute effective Ld]
    E -->|Compression 0.75| F
    E -->|Standard 1.0| F
    F --> G[Step 4: Verify with minimum length rules]
    G --> H[Step 5: Check anchorage conditions at supports]
    H --> I[Step 6: Decide on splicing strategy]
    I --> J{Lap splice permitted?}
    J -->|Yes bar diameter le 36 mm| K[Compute lap length = factor times Ld]
    J -->|No bar diameter gt 36 mm| L[Specify welded splice or mechanical coupler]
    K --> M[Step 7: Apply minimum lap length rule]
    M --> N[Final Design: Effective Ld and Lap Length]
    L --> N
```

## 4.4 Typical Detailing — Beam Tension Anchorage (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Beam[Simply Supported Beam - Effective Span 5 m]
        direction LR
        P1[Support A] --- M[Mid-span Maximum Moment]
        M --- P2[Support B]
    end
    subgraph Bars[Bottom Tension Reinforcement]
        direction LR
        B1[Straight bar of length 12 m] -->|Extended past support| B2[Anchorage Length Ld required at both ends]
    end
    subgraph TopBars[Top Reinforcement at Supports]
        direction LR
        T1[Top bars at supports] -->|Top bar factor 1.33| T2[Ld effective = 1.33 Ld]
    end
    Beam --- Bars
    Beam --- TopBars
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**Define the term "development length" as per IS 456:2000. State the factors affecting development length.**

**Model Answer:**

Development length is defined as the length of embedment of a reinforcing bar required to develop the full design strength of the bar through bond between steel and concrete (IS 456 Cl. 26.2.1).

The factors affecting development length are:

1. **Grade of concrete** — Higher grade gives higher $\tau_{bd}$, reducing Ld.
2. **Type of steel** — Deformed (HYSD) bars have 1.6 times higher bond stress than plain bars.
3. **Bar diameter ($\phi$)** — Ld is directly proportional to $\phi$.
4. **Design stress in bar ($\sigma_s$)** — Ld is directly proportional to $\sigma_s$.
5. **Position of bar** — Top bars (cast with more than 300 mm of fresh concrete below) require 1.33 times the basic Ld.
6. **Type of structural action** — Compression bars require only 0.75 times the Ld of tension bars.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
**Differentiate between lap splice and welded splice. When is welded splice preferred over lap splice?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Lap Splice | Welded Splice |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Mechanism | Force transfer through bond | Force transfer through weld metal |
| Maximum bar diameter allowed | 36 mm | No upper limit (suitable for large bars) |
| Length consumed | Long (1.3 to 1.5 times Ld) | Short (weld length per IS 2751) |
| Congestion at site | Causes congestion in heavily reinforced sections | Less congestion |
| Skill required | Simple | Requires skilled welder |

**Welded splice is preferred** when:
- Bar diameter exceeds **36 mm** (where lapping is not permitted per IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1).
- Congestion of reinforcement is critical (e.g., beam-column joints).
- The section cannot accommodate long lap lengths.
- Rapid construction is required.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Derive the expression for development length of a tension bar as per IS 456:2000, clearly stating the assumptions.

**(b)** A simply supported RC beam of effective span **6 m** and width **250 mm** is reinforced with **5 bars of 20 mm diameter** at the bottom. Concrete grade is **M25** and steel is **Fe 500 (HYSD)**. The bars are cast as bottom bars. Calculate the development length required at the supports. Verify if straight anchorage is sufficient or hooks are required.

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Assumptions:**

1. The bar is straight and embedded in concrete of uniform quality.
2. Bond stress ($\tau_{bd}$) is uniformly distributed along the embedment length.
3. The bar reaches its design stress $\sigma_s = 0.87 f_y$ at the critical section.
4. Plane sections remain plane, ensuring compatibility of strain.
5. Concrete in the surrounding region is uncracked and provides full bond.

**Derivation:**

Let $\phi$ = diameter of the bar.

Tensile force in the bar at the critical section:

$$
T = \sigma_s \cdot A_s = \sigma_s \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot \phi^2}{4}
$$

Bond force resisting pull-out over length $L_d$:

$$
F_{bond} = \tau_{bd} \cdot \pi \cdot \phi \cdot L_d
$$

For equilibrium:

$$
\tau_{bd} \cdot \pi \cdot \phi \cdot L_d = \sigma_s \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot \phi^2}{4}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
L_d = \frac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}
$$

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Given Data**

- Span $L = 6 \text{ m} = 6000 \text{ mm}$
- Width $b = 250 \text{ mm}$
- Reinforcement: 5 bars of 20 mm $\phi$
- Concrete grade: M25
- Steel grade: Fe 500
- Bars are bottom bars (no top bar factor)

**Step 2: Identify Parameters**

$$
f_y = 500 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_s = 0.87 \cdot 500 = 435 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\phi = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

For M25, deformed bars (HYSD):

$$
\tau_{bd} = 1.28 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Basic Development Length**

$$
L_d = \frac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}} = \frac{20 \cdot 435}{4 \cdot 1.28}
$$

$$
L_d = \frac{8700}{5.12} = 1699.22 \text{ mm} \approx 1699 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Modification Factors**

Since the bars are **bottom bars** (less than 300 mm of concrete below):

$$
\text{Top bar factor} = 1.0
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{effective}} = 1.0 \cdot 1699.22 = 1699.22 \text{ mm} \approx 1700 \text{ mm}
$$

Expressed in terms of bar diameter:

$$
L_{d,\text{effective}} = \frac{1700}{20} = 85 \cdot \phi
$$

**Step 5: Check for Anchorage at Supports**

Available anchorage length at each support (assuming 50% of span is in positive moment):

Wait — a more practical check uses the IS code recommendation that bars must extend $L_d / 3$ past the face of the support (for simply supported beams) or the actual available straight length beyond the support centerline.

For this beam, assuming bars extend 100 mm into the support:

$$
\text{Available straight length} \approx 100 \text{ mm (in the support)} + \text{half effective depth}
$$

Effective depth $d$ ≈ 500 mm (assumed). Therefore, available straight length for anchorage beyond mid-span is roughly equal to half the span:

$$
\text{Available length from mid-span to support face} = \frac{6000}{2} = 3000 \text{ mm}
$$

Since $L_d = 1700 \text{ mm} < 3000 \text{ mm}$, **straight anchorage is sufficient**, and hooks are **not required**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b)**: Students often forget to apply the **top bar factor** correctly. If the bar has more than 300 mm of concrete cast below it (e.g., bars at the bottom of a deep beam), it is still a "top bar" only if cast in that position. For typical bottom bars in shallow beams, no top bar factor applies.

---

### Question B — Option 2 **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the IS 456:2000 provisions for splicing of reinforcement in flexural members. Discuss lap length requirements for tension and compression bars.

**(b)** A continuous RC beam has a column support at its center. The bottom reinforcement consists of **4 bars of 16 mm diameter** (Fe 415) in M25 concrete. The maximum positive moment occurs at the center support. If the bars must be lapped within the zone of maximum moment, determine the required lap length as per IS 456:2000. Assume the bars are bottom bars (no top bar factor).

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

**IS 456:2000 Provisions for Splicing (Clause 26.5):**

**General Rules:**
- Splicing of bars shall be done by **lap welding, mechanical coupling, or welding**.
- **Bars exceeding 36 mm diameter** shall not be spliced by lapping; welded splice or mechanical couplers shall be used.
- Splices shall be **staggered** as far as possible.
- Splices in **flexural members** shall be located at sections where the bending moment is less than 50% of the maximum moment.

**Lap Length in Tension (Cl. 26.5.2.1a):**

| Condition | Lap Length |
| :--- | :---: |
| In zone of **maximum stress** | **1.3 × Ld** (or 1.5 × Ld if more than 50% of bars are spliced) |
| In zone of **low stress** (less than 50% of max moment) | **1.0 × Ld** |

Minimum lap length: **30$\phi$** or **300 mm**, whichever is greater.

**Lap Length in Compression (Cl. 26.5.2.1b):**

$$
L_{lap,\text{compression}} = 0.85 \cdot L_{d,\text{compression}}
$$

But not less than **24$\phi$** for bars and **30$\phi$** for spirals.

**Additional Provisions:**
- All bars in compression may be spliced at one section.
- Splices in tension shall be designed for **full tensile stress**.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Given Data**

- Reinforcement: 4 bars of 16 mm diameter at bottom
- Steel grade: Fe 415
- Concrete grade: M25
- Splice is in **zone of maximum moment** (critical condition)
- Bars are bottom bars (top bar factor = 1.0)

**Step 2: Identify Parameters**

$$
f_y = 415 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_s = 0.87 \cdot 415 = 360.45 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\phi = 16 \text{ mm}
$$

For M25, deformed (HYSD) bars:

$$
\tau_{bd} = 1.28 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Basic Development Length**

$$
L_d = \frac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}} = \frac{16 \cdot 360.45}{4 \cdot 1.28}
$$

$$
L_d = \frac{5767.2}{5.12} = 1126.41 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Apply Modification Factors**

Since bars are bottom bars:

$$
L_{d,\text{effective}} = 1.0 \cdot 1126.41 = 1126.41 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate Lap Length in Zone of Maximum Moment**

For tension splice in zone of max moment:

$$
L_{lap} = 1.3 \cdot L_d = 1.3 \cdot 1126.41 = 1464.33 \text{ mm} \approx 1465 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6: Apply Minimum Length Rule**

Check the minimum lap length:

$$
30 \cdot \phi = 30 \cdot 16 = 480 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
300 \text{ mm} = 300 \text{ mm}
$$

Maximum of 1.3 × Ld, 30$\phi$, and 300 mm:

$$
L_{lap} = \max \left( 1465,\ 480,\ 300 \right) = 1465 \text{ mm}
$$

**Final Result:** The required lap length is **1465 mm** (or approximately **91.6$\phi$**).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Splicing Problems)**: Students frequently make the following mistakes:
> 1. **Forgetting the 1.3 factor** in zone of maximum moment — always check the splice location.
> 2. **Not applying the minimum length rule** — even if 1.3 × Ld is small, the lap cannot be less than 30$\phi$ or 300 mm.
> 3. **Confusing tension and compression lap factors** — compression lap uses **0.85 × Ld (compression)**, not 1.3 × Ld (tension).
> 4. **Missing the staggered splicing rule** — more than 50% of bars should not be spliced at one section in flexural tension.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Development Length and Splicing**

- [ ] **Development Length Formula**: $L_d = \frac{\phi \cdot \sigma_s}{4 \cdot \tau_{bd}}$ — the single most important equation in Module 1.
- [ ] **Design stress in tension bar**: $\sigma_s = 0.87 \cdot f_y$ (HYSD bars per IS 1786).
- [ ] **Design stress in compression bar**: $\sigma_s = 0.80 \cdot f_y$ (or actual computed stress).
- [ ] **Top bar factor = 1.33** for deformed bars in tension when more than 300 mm of fresh concrete is cast below.
- [ ] **Compression factor = 0.75** for bars in compression (25% reduction in Ld).
- [ ] **Bond stress $\tau_{bd}$** for M20 plain bars is **0.6 MPa**; for deformed bars multiply by **1.6** (i.e., 0.96 MPa for M20 HYSD).
- [ ] **Lap splice in tension (max moment zone)** = **1.3 × Ld** (or 1.5 × Ld if > 50% of bars are spliced).
- [ ] **Lap splice in tension (low moment zone)** = **1.0 × Ld**.
- [ ] **Lap splice in compression** = **0.85 × Ld (compression)**.
- [ ] **Minimum lap length** = max(calculated lap, **30$\phi$**, **300 mm**).
- [ ] **Bars > 36 mm diameter** cannot be lapped — use welded splice or mechanical couplers.
- [ ] **Stagger splicing** in flexural tension — do not splice more than 50% of bars at one section.
- [ ] **Welded splice** preferred for large bars, congested sections, and rapid construction.
- [ ] **Hook provision** required when available straight anchorage length is less than $L_d$ (typical hook adds ~16$\phi$ to anchorage capacity).
- [ ] **Bundled bars** require increased Ld per IS 456 Cl. 26.2.1.3.
- [ ] **Epoxy-coated bars** require 1.5 × Ld per IS 456 Cl. 26.2.1.4.
- [ ] **Always verify Ld** against available straight length at supports/critical sections before deciding on hooks or bends.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsion in Beams: Limit State of Collapse
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET404 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Torsion in Beams: Limit State of Collapse

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (IS 456:2000 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torsion in Reinforced Concrete Beams** refers to the twisting moment (T) acting about the longitudinal axis of a beam, producing diagonal tensile stresses (principal stresses inclined at ~45°) which combine with flexural and shear stresses. The **Limit State of Collapse** for torsion ensures the beam can resist the **factored torsional moment (T_u)** simultaneously with factored bending moment (M_u) and shear force (V_u) without reaching the ultimate flexural, shear, or torsional capacity.

In the limit state philosophy, the design is based on the condition:

$$\text{Factored Load Effects} \leq \text{Design Strength of Section}$$

For a beam subjected to **combined bending, shear, and torsion**, the strength is governed by IS 456:2000 **Clauses 40, 41, and Annex B**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Twisted Towel" 🌀

Imagine wringing water out of a wet towel. Your two hands twist the towel in opposite directions. The fibers on the surface try to spiral — this is **torsion**.

In a concrete beam:
- The **top and bottom fibers** experience diagonal tension (like the spiral fibers of the towel).
- The **longitudinal bars** (running along the length) act like the long threads that resist the twist.
- The **transverse stirrups** (wrapping around) act like rubber bands holding the threads together.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Torsion never acts alone in RC structures. It always appears **with bending and shear**, especially in:
> - Edge beams of a two-way slab
> - Beams supporting a cantilever slab
> - Spiral staircases
> - Beams curved in plan

---

## 1.3 Types of Torsion (Critical for KTU)

| Type | Nature | Design Treatment | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Equilibrium (Primary) Torsion** | Required for static equilibrium of the structure | **MUST be designed for full value of T_u** | Edge beam supporting a cantilever slab |
| **Compatibility (Secondary) Torsion** | Arises from continuity/compatibility of deformations | **Can be reduced** by moment redistribution | Interior secondary beam framing into a stiff main beam |

### Reduction of Compatibility Torsion (IS 456 Cl. 41.1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **maximum design torsional moment** for a beam is:
> $$T_u = T_{u1} - \frac{T_{u2}}{2}$$
> where $T_{u1}$ = max. torsional moment from elastic analysis, $T_{u2}$ = torsional moment at the section where the **bending moment of resistance equals the flexural capacity**. If $T_u \leq T_{u2}/2$, **torsion may be neglected entirely**.

---

## 1.4 When Must Torsion Be Considered?

As per IS 456:2000 Cl. 41.1:

1. **If torsional stiffness is neglected**, the structure must remain in equilibrium (i.e., the torsion must be **equilibrium torsion**). Otherwise, torsion must be considered.
2. **For continuous beams**, even if neglected, ensure adequate reinforcement to control cracking.
3. **Practically**, torsion is significant when $T_u \geq 0.1 \times M_u$ (thumb rule from textbooks).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** All design forces are **factored** (i.e., multiplied by the partial safety factor $\gamma_f = 1.5$ for DL+LL). Constants used throughout: $\gamma_m = 1.5$ for concrete, $\gamma_m = 1.15$ for steel.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Mechanics of Torsional Failure

When torsion acts on an RC beam, **principal tensile stresses** develop on the surface at approximately **45° to the longitudinal axis**. The collapse mechanism is governed by:

- A **space truss analogy** (Lampert & Thürlimann, adopted by IS 456):
  - **Longitudinal bars** (top + bottom chords) resist the **axial component** of the diagonal compression.
  - **Closed stirrups** (side verticals) resist the **shear component**.

### Why Both Longitudinal AND Transverse Steel?
- **Stirrups alone** cannot resist torsion because they lack anchorage in the diagonal strut.
- **Longitudinal bars alone** would simply twist freely.
- **Combined**, they form a tubular truss that resists twisting — analogous to a hollow shaft.

---

## 2.2 IS 456:2000 Design Procedure — Stepwise Logic

### **STEP 1: Compute Equivalent Shear Force (V_e)**

The actual shear is augmented by the torsional shear contribution:

$$V_e = V_u + \frac{1.6 \, T_u}{b}$$

where:
- $V_u$ = Factored shear force (kN)
- $T_u$ = Factored torsional moment (kN·m)
- $b$ = Breadth of the web (m or mm — keep units consistent)

> **Why 1.6?** It is the empirical factor derived from the space-truss model, accounting for the equivalent shear contribution of torsion on the side face of the web.

---

### **STEP 2: Compute Equivalent Bending Moment (M_uv)**

The bending moment is increased by the flexural contribution of torsion:

$$M_{uv} = M_u + M_t \quad ; \quad M_t = \frac{T_u \left(1 + D/b\right)}{1.7}$$

where:
- $M_u$ = Factored bending moment (kN·m)
- $D$ = Overall depth, $b$ = width
- $M_t$ = Additional flexural moment due to torsion

> **Why divide by 1.7?** Empirical factor representing the partial effectiveness of the top and bottom flanges in resisting the torsional moment in a rectangular section.

---

### **STEP 3: Section Adequacy Check**

**(a) Flexure check (Fe415):**

$$M_{uv} \leq 0.148 \, f_{ck} \, b \, D^2$$

If $M_{uv}$ exceeds the limit, **increase the section depth $D$**.

**(b) Shear check:**

$$\tau_{ve} = \frac{V_e}{b \, d} \leq \tau_{c,\max}$$

From **IS 456 Table 20**, $\tau_{c,\max}$ depends on concrete grade:
- M20 → **2.8 N/mm²**
- M25 → **3.1 N/mm²**
- M30 → **3.5 N/mm²**

---

### **STEP 4: Longitudinal Tension Steel (A_st)**

Using the **limit-state flexural formula** for a singly reinforced rectangular section (Fe415):

$$A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \, f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \, M_{uv}}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2}}\right] \, b \, d$$

This is the same formula as for pure bending, applied to $M_{uv}$.

---

### **STEP 5: Side Face Reinforcement (for beams > 500 mm deep)**

Per IS 456, for beams subjected to combined bending and torsion:

$$A_{st, \text{side}} = 0.001 \, b \, d = 0.1\% \text{ of web area}$$

This is split equally between **both side faces** (i.e., $0.05\%$ on each side).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for KTU:** Many students forget the side face steel — examiner's classic deduction point!

---

### **STEP 6: Transverse Reinforcement (Stirrups)**

For combined shear and torsion, the **equivalent shear method** is used:

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{s_v} = \frac{V_e - \tau_c \, b \, d}{0.87 \, f_y \, d}$$

where $\tau_c$ is from **IS 456 Table 19** (depends on $p_t$ and $f_{ck}$).

---

### **STEP 7: Stirrup Spacing Restrictions (IS 456 Cl. 41.4.3)**

$$s_v \leq \frac{x_1 + y_1}{4} \quad \text{and} \quad s_v \leq 300 \text{ mm}$$

where:
- $x_1$ = centre-to-centre distance between **vertical legs** of stirrups
- $y_1$ = centre-to-centre distance between **horizontal legs** of stirrups

Minimum: $s_v \geq 100$ mm.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_e = V_u + \dfrac{1.6 \, T_u}{b}$ | Equivalent shear force (kN) |
| 2 | $M_t = \dfrac{T_u(1 + D/b)}{1.7}$ | Torsional flexural contribution (kN·m) |
| 3 | $M_{uv} = M_u + M_t$ | Equivalent bending moment (kN·m) |
| 4 | $M_{uv} \leq 0.148 \, f_{ck} \, b \, D^2$ (Fe415) | Flexure adequacy check |
| 5 | $\tau_{ve} = \dfrac{V_e}{b \, d} \leq \tau_{c,\max}$ | Shear adequacy check |
| 6 | $A_{st} = \dfrac{0.5 \, f_{ck}}{f_y}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4.6 M_{uv}}{f_{ck} b d^2}}\right] b d$ | Longitudinal tension steel (mm²) |
| 7 | $A_{st,\text{side}} = 0.001 \, b \, d$ | Side face reinforcement (mm²) |
| 8 | $\dfrac{A_{sv}}{s_v} = \dfrac{V_e - \tau_c \, b \, d}{0.87 \, f_y \, d}$ | Transverse steel (stirrups) |
| 9 | $s_v \leq \dfrac{x_1 + y_1}{4} \leq 300$ mm | Maximum stirrup spacing |
| 10 | $p_t = \dfrac{100 \, A_{st}}{b \, d}$ | Percentage steel (for Table 19) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Checklist (Most Common Mistake!):**
> - $b, D, d$ → in **mm** for stress, in **m** for $T_u/b$ in $V_e$ formula. **Stay consistent.**
> - $M_u, M_{uv}$ → in **N·mm** when using formula 6 (multiply kN·m by $10^6$)
> - $f_{ck}, f_y$ → in **N/mm²** (MPa)
> - $V_e$ → in **N** when computing $\tau_{ve}$

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge Girders**: Curve in plan → torsion dominates.
- **Helical/Spiral Staircases**: Pure torsional loading.
- **Parking Structures**: Ramp beams with heavy eccentric live loads.
- **Outrigger Beams in High-Rises**: Transfer girders carrying eccentric column loads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Equivalent Shear Formula?

Consider a beam of width $b$ subjected to pure torsion $T$. The **shear stress on the side face** from St. Venant's classical theory (approximated for rectangular sections):

$$\tau_t = \frac{T}{\alpha \, b^2 \, D}$$

where $\alpha$ is a shape coefficient. IS 456 simplifies this to an **equivalent uniform shear stress** acting on the web:

$$\tau_t \approx \frac{1.6 \, T}{b^2 \, D} \quad \text{(approximately)}$$

This stress, integrated over the **web area** $b \cdot d$, gives the equivalent shear force:

$$V_{\text{torsion}} = \tau_t \times b \, d = \frac{1.6 \, T}{b^2 \, D} \times b \, d = \frac{1.6 \, T \, d}{b \, D}$$

For the simplified IS 456 formulation (assuming $d \approx D$ in proportion):

$$V_e = V_u + \frac{1.6 \, T_u}{b}$$

---

## 3.2 Complete Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Design a rectangular RC beam of section $b = 300$ mm, $D = 600$ mm, subjected to the **factored** actions:
> $$M_u = 200 \text{ kN·m}, \quad V_u = 150 \text{ kN}, \quad T_u = 40 \text{ kN·m}$$
> Use **M20 concrete** and **Fe415 steel**. Take clear cover = 40 mm, and assume 25 mm $\phi$ bars.

**Given Data:**

$$b = 300 \text{ mm}, \quad D = 600 \text{ mm}, \quad d = 600 - 40 - 8 - \frac{25}{2} = 539.5 \approx 540 \text{ mm}$$

$$f_{ck} = 20 \text{ N/mm}^2, \quad f_y = 415 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

---

### **STEP 1: Equivalent Shear Force (V_e)**

$$V_e = V_u + \frac{1.6 \, T_u}{b}$$

$$V_e = 150 + \frac{1.6 \times 40 \times 10^6}{300} \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{(converting kN to N)}$$

> Converting units: $T_u = 40$ kN·m $= 40 \times 10^6$ N·mm. Then $1.6 T_u / b = 1.6 \times 40 \times 10^6 / 300 = 213{,}333$ N $= 213.33$ kN.

$$V_e = 150 + 213.33 = 363.33 \text{ kN}$$

---

### **STEP 2: Equivalent Bending Moment (M_uv)**

$$M_t = \frac{T_u(1 + D/b)}{1.7} = \frac{40 \times (1 + 600/300)}{1.7} = \frac{40 \times 3}{1.7} = 70.59 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{uv} = M_u + M_t = 200 + 70.59 = 270.59 \text{ kN·m}$$

---

### **STEP 3: Section Adequacy**

**(a) Flexure check:**

$$M_{uv, \text{lim}} = 0.148 \times 20 \times 300 \times (600)^2 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$= 0.148 \times 20 \times 300 \times 360000 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$= 319.68 \text{ kN·m}$$

> Since $M_{uv} = 270.59 < 319.68$ → **Section is adequate** in flexure. ✓

**(b) Shear check:**

$$\tau_{ve} = \frac{V_e}{b \, d} = \frac{363.33 \times 10^3}{300 \times 540} = 2.243 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

> From IS 456 Table 20 (M20): $\tau_{c,\max} = 2.8$ N/mm². Since $\tau_{ve} = 2.243 < 2.8$ → **Section is adequate** in shear. ✓

---

### **STEP 4: Longitudinal Tension Steel (A_st)**

$$A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 270.59 \times 10^6}{20 \times 300 \times (540)^2}}\right] \times 300 \times 540$$

Let us compute the term under the square root step-by-step:

$$\frac{4.6 \times M_{uv}}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2} = \frac{4.6 \times 270.59 \times 10^6}{20 \times 300 \times 291600}$$

$$= \frac{1.2447 \times 10^9}{1.7496 \times 10^9} = 0.7115$$

$$1 - 0.7115 = 0.2885$$

$$\sqrt{0.2885} = 0.5371$$

$$1 - 0.5371 = 0.4629$$

$$A_{st} = 0.02410 \times 0.4629 \times 162000 = 1807.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

> **Provide 4 bars of 25 mm $\phi$** → $A_{st,\text{prov}} = 4 \times 490.9 = 1963.6$ mm² ✓

---

### **STEP 5: Side Face Reinforcement**

$$A_{st, \text{side}} = 0.001 \times b \times d = 0.001 \times 300 \times 540 = 162 \text{ mm}^2$$

> **Provide 2 bars of 12 mm $\phi$ on each side face** → $A_{\text{side, prov}} = 2 \times 113.1 = 226.2$ mm² per face ✓

---

### **STEP 6: Transverse Reinforcement (Stirrups)**

**Step 6a — Estimate percentage steel $p_t$:**

$$p_t = \frac{100 \times A_{st}}{b \, d} = \frac{100 \times 1807.5}{300 \times 540} = 1.116\%$$

> From IS 456 Table 19, M20, $p_t = 1.116\%$: $\tau_c = 0.77$ N/mm² (interpolated)

**Step 6b — Compute $A_{sv}/s_v$:**

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{s_v} = \frac{V_e - \tau_c \, b \, d}{0.87 \, f_y \, d}$$

$$= \frac{363.33 \times 10^3 - 0.77 \times 300 \times 540}{0.87 \times 415 \times 540}$$

> Numerator: $363330 - 124740 = 238590$ N
> Denominator: $0.87 \times 415 \times 540 = 195{,}147$ N

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{s_v} = \frac{238590}{195147} = 1.2226 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6c — Choose stirrup diameter and spacing:**

Using 10 mm $\phi$ 2-legged stirrups: $A_{sv} = 2 \times 78.54 = 157.08$ mm²

$$s_v = \frac{A_{sv}}{1.2226} = \frac{157.08}{1.2226} = 128.5 \text{ mm}$$

> **Provide 10 mm $\phi$ 2-legged stirrups @ 125 mm c/c**

**Step 6d — Check spacing limits:**

$$x_1 = 300 - 2(40) - 2(10) = 200 \text{ mm}$$
$$y_1 = 540 - 2(40) - 2(10) = 440 \text{ mm}$$
$$\frac{x_1 + y_1}{4} = \frac{200 + 440}{4} = 160 \text{ mm}$$

> Since $s_v = 125 < 160 < 300$ → **Spacing is OK** ✓

---

### 📊 ANALYTICAL MATRIX — Summary of Design

| Parameter | Required | Provided | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_e$ | 363.33 kN | — | — |
| $M_{uv}$ | 270.59 kN·m | 319.68 kN·m (limit) | ✓ |
| $\tau_{ve}$ | 2.243 N/mm² | 2.8 (max) | ✓ |
| $A_{st}$ (bottom) | 1807.5 mm² | 1963.6 mm² (4-25$\phi$) | ✓ |
| $A_{st, \text{side}}$ | 162 mm²/face | 226.2 mm²/face (2-12$\phi$) | ✓ |
| Stirrups | $A_{sv}/s_v = 1.223$ | 10$\phi$ @ 125 c/c | ✓ |
| Stirrup spacing | $\leq 160$ mm | 125 mm | ✓ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Design Procedure for Combined Loading

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 0: Given M_u, V_u, T_u, section b x D, d] --> B[Step 1: Compute V_e = V_u + 1.6 T_u / b]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute M_t and M_uv = M_u + M_t]
    C --> D{Step 3a: M_uv <= 0.148 f_ck b D squared?}
    D -- NO --> D1[Increase D, restart]
    D -- YES --> E{Step 3b: tau_ve <= tau_c_max from Table 20?}
    E -- NO --> E1[Increase b or D, restart]
    E -- YES --> F[Step 4: Compute A_st for M_uv]
    F --> G[Step 5: Add side face steel, 0.1 percent of web area]
    G --> H[Step 6: Compute A_sv by s_v for equivalent shear V_e]
    H --> I[Step 7: Check s_v less than x1 + y1 by 4, less than 300 mm]
    I --> J{Spacing OK?}
    J -- NO --> J1[Reduce stirrup spacing]
    J -- YES --> K[Step 8: Final detailing, longitudinal bars, side bars, stirrups]
    K --> L[DESIGN COMPLETE]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Torsion-Resisting Mechanism (Space Truss Analogy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TW[Twisting Moment T Applied]
    direction TB
    end
    TW --> A[Diagonal Compression Struts, inclined at 45 deg]
    A --> B[Longitudinal Bars, top and bottom chords]
    A --> C[Stirrups, vertical tension members]
    B --> D[Resists axial component of diagonal compression]
    C --> E[Resists vertical shear component]
    D --> F[Combined Action: Space Truss Resists Torsion]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Beam does not collapse under torsion]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — Equilibrium vs Compatibility Torsion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Beam has Torsional Moment T_u] --> B{Is torsion required for EQUILIBRIUM?}
    B -- YES, equilibrium --> C[Design for FULL T_u per IS 456 Cl 41.4]
    B -- NO, only compatibility --> D{Is T_u greater than T_u2 by 2?}
    D -- YES --> E[Reduced T_u = T_u1 minus T_u2 by 2, then design]
    D -- NO --> F[NEGLECT torsion entirely, design for M_u and V_u only]
    C --> G[Compute V_e and M_uv]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Proceed with full design procedure]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Reinforcement Layout in Torsion Beam (Cross-Section)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Beam Cross Section, b x D] --> B[Bottom Layer: A_st main bars, 4 of 25 phi]
    A --> C[Top Layer: Hanger bars, 2 of 16 phi]
    A --> D[Left Side Face: 2 of 12 phi]
    A --> E[Right Side Face: 2 of 12 phi]
    A --> F[Closed Stirrups: 10 phi 2 legged at 125 c/c]
    B --> G[Resists flexure plus torsion]
    C --> G
    D --> H[Resists torsional cracking on side face]
    E --> H
    F --> I[Resists shear plus vertical component of torsion]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Differentiate between equilibrium torsion and compatibility torsion. How is the design torsional moment reduced in the case of compatibility torsion?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Equilibrium Torsion | Compatibility Torsion |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | Required for static equilibrium of the structure | Arises from continuity/compatibility of deformations |
| **Negligible?** | **No** — must be fully designed for | **Yes** — can be reduced or even neglected |
| **Example** | Edge beam supporting a cantilever slab | Secondary beam framing into a main beam |

**Reduction of Compatibility Torsion (IS 456 Cl. 41.1):**

The design torsional moment is reduced as:

$$T_u = T_{u1} - \frac{T_{u2}}{2}$$

where $T_{u1}$ is the **maximum torsional moment** from elastic analysis and $T_{u2}$ is the **torsional moment at the section where the moment of resistance equals the flexural capacity**. If $T_u \leq T_{u2}/2$, torsion may be **neglected completely**.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**What is meant by equivalent shear and equivalent bending moment in a beam subjected to combined bending, shear, and torsion? Write the IS 456 formulas.**

**Model Answer:**

When a beam is subjected to combined bending ($M_u$), shear ($V_u$), and torsion ($T_u$), the **actual bending and shear are amplified** by the torsional contribution to obtain "equivalent" design values. This is necessary because:
- Torsion produces **diagonal tension** equivalent to additional shear.
- Torsion produces **flexural tension** on side faces equivalent to additional bending.

**IS 456 Formulas:**

**Equivalent Shear Force:**

$$V_e = V_u + \frac{1.6 \, T_u}{b}$$

**Equivalent Bending Moment:**

$$M_{uv} = M_u + M_t, \quad \text{where} \quad M_t = \frac{T_u(1 + D/b)}{1.7}$$

> Here, $T_u$ must be in consistent units (use kN·m with $b$ and $D$ in metres when computing $T_u/b$).

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE RULE:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split as 7 + 7.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** A rectangular RC beam of section $300 \text{ mm} \times 600 \text{ mm}$ (effective) is subjected to the following **factored** actions: $M_u = 180$ kN·m, $V_u = 140$ kN, $T_u = 35$ kN·m. Using M20 concrete and Fe415 steel, design the **longitudinal reinforcement**. Assume effective cover = 50 mm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the beam in part (a), design the **transverse reinforcement (stirrups)** as per IS 456:2000. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**Given:**
$b = 300$ mm, $D = 600$ mm, $d = 600 - 50 = 550$ mm
$M_u = 180$ kN·m, $V_u = 140$ kN, $T_u = 35$ kN·m
$f_{ck} = 20$ N/mm², $f_y = 415$ N/mm²

---

### Part (a) — Longitudinal Reinforcement

**Step 1: Equivalent Shear Force**

$$V_e = V_u + \frac{1.6 \, T_u}{b} = 140 + \frac{1.6 \times 35 \times 10^6}{300 \times 10^3} \text{ kN}$$

$$V_e = 140 + 186.67 = 326.67 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Equivalent Bending Moment**

$$M_t = \frac{T_u (1 + D/b)}{1.7} = \frac{35 \times (1 + 600/300)}{1.7} = \frac{35 \times 3}{1.7} = 61.76 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{uv} = M_u + M_t = 180 + 61.76 = 241.76 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3: Section Adequacy Check**

$$M_{uv, \text{lim}} = 0.148 \times 20 \times 300 \times 600^2 \times 10^{-6} = 319.68 \text{ kN·m}$$

> Since $241.76 < 319.68$ → **Section is adequate** in flexure ✓

$$\tau_{ve} = \frac{V_e}{b \, d} = \frac{326.67 \times 10^3}{300 \times 550} = 1.98 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

> $\tau_{c,\max} = 2.8$ N/mm² (M20) → $\tau_{ve} < \tau_{c,\max}$ ✓

**Step 4: Longitudinal Steel**

$$A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 241.76 \times 10^6}{20 \times 300 \times 550^2}}\right] \times 300 \times 550$$

Compute:
$$\frac{4.6 \times 241.76 \times 10^6}{20 \times 300 \times 302500} = \frac{1.1121 \times 10^9}{1.815 \times 10^9} = 0.6127$$

$$1 - 0.6127 = 0.3873 \quad ; \quad \sqrt{0.3873} = 0.6224 \quad ; \quad 1 - 0.6224 = 0.3776$$

$$A_{st} = 0.02410 \times 0.3776 \times 165000 = 1501 \text{ mm}^2$$

> **Provide 4 bars of 22 mm $\phi$** → $A_{st,\text{prov}} = 4 \times 380.13 = 1520.5$ mm² ✓

**Step 5: Side Face Reinforcement**

$$A_{st, \text{side}} = 0.001 \times 300 \times 550 = 165 \text{ mm}^2 \text{ per face}$$

> **Provide 2 bars of 12 mm $\phi$ on each side face** → $226$ mm² per face ✓

---

### Part (b) — Transverse Reinforcement

**Step 6: Estimate $p_t$ and $\tau_c$**

$$p_t = \frac{100 \times 1501}{300 \times 550} = 0.91\%$$

> From IS 456 Table 19, M20, $p_t \approx 0.91\%$: $\tau_c \approx 0.66$ N/mm²

**Step 7: Compute $A_{sv}/s_v$**

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{s_v} = \frac{V_e - \tau_c \, b \, d}{0.87 \, f_y \, d} = \frac{326.67 \times 10^3 - 0.66 \times 300 \times 550}{0.87 \times 415 \times 550}$$

> Numerator: $326670 - 108900 = 217770$ N
> Denominator: $198652.5$ N

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{s_v} = \frac{217770}{198652.5} = 1.0963$$

**Step 8: Choose 10 mm $\phi$ 2-legged stirrups:** $A_{sv} = 2 \times 78.54 = 157.08$ mm²

$$s_v = \frac{157.08}{1.0963} = 143.3 \text{ mm}$$

> **Provide 10 mm $\phi$ 2-legged stirrups @ 140 mm c/c**

**Step 9: Check spacing**

$$x_1 = 300 - 2(40) - 2(10) = 200 \text{ mm}$$
$$y_1 = 550 - 2(40) - 2(10) = 450 \text{ mm}$$
$$\frac{x_1 + y_1}{4} = \frac{650}{4} = 162.5 \text{ mm}$$

> Since $140 < 162.5 < 300$ → **Spacing is OK** ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **space truss analogy** for the design of RC beams under torsion. List the IS 456 provisions for **minimum longitudinal and transverse reinforcement** in a torsion beam. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported RC beam of effective span 6 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 30 kN/m (unfactored, including self-weight) over its entire span. The beam is connected transversely to a cantilever slab projecting 1.5 m on one side, which carries a live load of 10 kN/m². The beam section is 250 mm × 500 mm. Determine the **design bending moment, shear force, and torsional moment** at the support. Use M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

### Part (a) — Theory (Space Truss Analogy)

The **space truss analogy** (Lampert and Thürlimann, 1968) idealizes a torsionally loaded RC beam as a **three-dimensional truss**:

- **Top and bottom longitudinal bars** act as the **chords** (top and bottom).
- **Side longitudinal bars + closed stirrups** form the **two side faces** of the truss.
- **Diagonal concrete struts** (at ~45° to the axis) carry the compressive forces between the corners.

**IS 456 Minimum Reinforcement Provisions for Torsion Beams:**

| Type | Minimum Requirement |
|---|---|
| **Longitudinal steel** | $A_{st} \geq 0.85 \dfrac{f_y}{f_y} \dfrac{T_u}{b \, d} + \text{bending steel}$ — practically, side face steel = 0.1% of web area |
| **Transverse (stirrups)** | $\dfrac{A_{sv}}{s_v} \geq \dfrac{T_u}{1.6 \, b \, d}$ (in addition to shear requirement) |
| **Stirrup spacing** | $s_v \leq \dfrac{x_1 + y_1}{4}$ and $s_v \leq 300$ mm |
| **Stirrup anchorage** | Must be **closed** with 135° hooks (or 90° hooks with adequate anchorage length) |
| **Bar distribution** | Side-face bars must be **distributed** in the outer half of each side |

> **Key point:** Stirrups must be **closed stirrups**, not open ones, because torsion produces **stresses on all four faces** of the section.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
Span $L = 6$ m, $b = 250$ mm, $D = 500$ mm, $d = 450$ mm
UDL on beam: $w = 30$ kN/m (unfactored)
Cantilever slab projection: $a = 1.5$ m, Live Load: $L_L = 10$ kN/m²
Beam spacing (assumed): $s = 3$ m (typical)

**Step 1: Factored Loads on Beam**

Factored UDL: $w_u = 1.5 \times 30 = 45$ kN/m

Factored LL transferred from slab (assuming slab spans transversely between beams):
LL on slab per metre run of beam = $L_L \times s = 10 \times 3 = 30$ kN/m
Factored LL: $LL_u = 1.5 \times 30 = 45$ kN/m

Total factored UDL on beam: $w_{u,\text{total}} = 45 + 45 = 90$ kN/m

**Step 2: Torsional Moment**

The cantilever slab exerts a **factored moment per metre run** at the beam:
$m_{u,\text{slab}} = \frac{LL_u \times a^2}{2} = \frac{45 \times 1.5^2}{2} = 50.625 \text{ kN·m/m}$

Torsional moment at support (uniform along beam):
$$T_u = m_{u,\text{slab}} \times s = 50.625 \times 3 = 151.875 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3: Bending Moment and Shear at Support**

$$M_u = \frac{w_{u,\text{total}} \times L^2}{2} = \frac{90 \times 6^2}{2} = 1620 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$V_u = w_{u,\text{total}} \times \frac{L}{2} = 90 \times 3 = 270 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Check Section Adequacy**

$$M_{uv} = M_u + \frac{T_u(1 + D/b)}{1.7} = 1620 + \frac{151.875 \times 3}{1.7} = 1620 + 268.0 = 1888 \text{ kN·m}$$

$$M_{uv, \text{lim}} = 0.148 \times 20 \times 250 \times 500^2 \times 10^{-6} = 185 \text{ kN·m}$$

> **Section is grossly under-designed** (1888 >> 185) → Increase depth to ~1200 mm or use multiple beams.

> This is intentional in the problem — it shows how torsion combined with bending drastically increases design demand.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common Mistakes Costing 2–4 Marks Each:**
>
> 1. **Unit inconsistency in $V_e$ formula** — $T_u/b$ requires $T_u$ in N·mm and $b$ in mm, OR $T_u$ in kN·m and $b$ in metres. **Always show unit conversion explicitly.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting side face reinforcement** — IS 456 mandates 0.1% of web area as **side face steel** for beams under combined loading. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if omitted.
>
> 3. **Using wrong $\tau_c$ from Table 19** — $\tau_c$ depends on $p_t$ (percentage steel) and concrete grade. Always interpolate, don't blindly use $\tau_c = 0.6$.
>
> 4. **Stirrup spacing violation** — Even if your $A_{sv}/s_v$ works out to a large spacing, the $(x_1 + y_1)/4$ limit is the **binding constraint** in deep beams. Show this check explicitly.
>
> 5. **Confusing $D$ and $d$** — Use $D$ for $M_{uv}$ and $M_t$ formula, but $d$ for $A_{st}$ and $\tau_{ve}$. Drawing a clear cross-section sketch avoids this.
>
> 6. **Forgetting hanger bars at top** — Always provide nominal top steel (2 bars of 12 mm or 16 mm) to hold stirrups and resist construction loads.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Key Concepts (Rapid Revision)
- **Torsion never acts alone** → always combined with bending and shear.
- **Two types**: Equilibrium (must design fully) vs Compatibility (can be reduced or neglected).
- **Equivalent shear and moment** convert torsion into equivalent shear and bending contributions.

### 🔢 Must-Memorize Formulas
- $V_e = V_u + 1.6 T_u / b$
- $M_{uv} = M_u + T_u(1 + D/b)/1.7$
- $A_{st} = (f_{ck}/2f_y)[1 - \sqrt{1 - 4.6 M_{uv}/(f_{ck} b d^2)}] \, bd$
- $A_{sv}/s_v = (V_e - \tau_c b d)/(0.87 f_y d)$
- Side face steel = **0.1% of $b \cdot d$** per face
- $s_v \leq (x_1 + y_1)/4$ and $\leq 300$ mm

### 📋 Table 20 (Maximum Shear Stress $\tau_{c,\max}$)
| $f_{ck}$ (N/mm²) | $\tau_{c,\max}$ (N/mm²) |
|---|---|
| M20 | **2.8** |
| M25 | **3.1** |
| M30 | **3.5** |

### ✅ Design Checklist (Use in Exam)
1. ☐ Compute $V_e$ and $M_{uv}$.
2. ☐ Check $M_{uv} \leq 0.148 f_{ck} b D^2$ (for Fe415).
3. ☐ Check $\tau_{ve} \leq \tau_{c,\max}$.
4. ☐ Calculate $A_{st}$ using $M_{uv}$.
5. ☐ Add side face reinforcement (0.1% web area each face).
6. ☐ Calculate $A_{sv}/s_v$ for equivalent shear.
7. ☐ Check stirrup spacing limits.
8. ☐ Detail with closed stirrups and proper anchorage.

### 🎯 KTU High-Yield Points
- IS 456:2000 **Cl. 41.1** → When to consider torsion
- IS 456:2000 **Cl. 41.4** → Design of section
- IS 456:2000 **Annex B** → Detailing for torsion
- IS 456:2000 **Table 19** → $\tau_c$ values
- IS 456:2000 **Table 20** → $\tau_{c,\max}$ values

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Problems on Design and Analysis of Singly Reinforced Rectangular Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET404 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems on Design and Analysis of Singly Reinforced Rectangular Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 / IS 456:2000)

A **Singly Reinforced Rectangular Beam** is a flexural member whose cross-section is rectangular and which contains tensile reinforcement **only in the zone of tension** (bottom fibres for simply supported beams under gravity loads). The compression is resisted entirely by the concrete in the flexural compression zone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 / IS 456:2000 (Cl. 5.1.1, Cl. 26.5.1.1) — Canonical Definition**
> A beam shall be deemed to be *singly reinforced* when the area of compressive steel, $A_{sc}$, is not taken into account in the calculation of the moment of resistance. The design follows the **Limit State Method (LSM)** with the rectangular parabolic stress block for concrete (Cl. 38.1, Fig. 4 of IS 456) and a partial safety factor of **1.15 for steel** ($\gamma_m = 1.15$) giving a design stress of $0.87 f_y$.

The beam is classified into three failure modes based on the position of the **neutral axis depth** $x_u$ relative to the **limiting neutral axis depth** $x_{u,\max}$:

| Classification | Condition | Failure Type | Code Provision |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Balanced Section** | $x_u = x_{u,\max}$ | Simultaneous crushing & yielding | Theoretical limit |
| **Under-Reinforced** | $x_u < x_{u,\max}$ | Ductile (steel yields first) | **Recommended design** |
| **Over-Reinforced** | $x_u > x_{u,\max}$ | Sudden brittle (concrete crushes) | **Disallowed by IS 456** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** $x_{u,\max} = 0.48\, d$ for **Fe415** steel and $x_{u,\max} = 0.46\, d$ for **Fe500** steel (IS 456:2000, Table 12, Cl. 38.1.1).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Diving Board"

Imagine a **wooden diving board** fixed at one end and free at the other. When a person walks to the free end, the board **bends downward**:

- The **top surface** of the board is being **squeezed** (compression) → concrete resists this.
- The **bottom surface** of the board is being **stretched** (tension) → steel rebars resist this.

If you were to strengthen the board:
- You would **glue a thin steel strip on the bottom** (this is the *tensile steel*).
- You would **NOT bother reinforcing the top**, because the wood is already strong in compression.
- This is *exactly* a **singly reinforced beam** — one strip of steel on the tension side.

> [!NOTE]
> If the diving board is so heavily loaded that you wrap steel on the **top** as well, it becomes a **doubly reinforced beam** (covered in Module 2).

### 1.3 Key Design Constants (Highlight Box)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Code-Defined Constants — Memorize These**
> - **Partial safety factor for steel:** $\gamma_{ms} = \mathbf{1.15}$ → design stress $= f_y / 1.15 = 0.87\, f_y$
> - **Partial safety factor for concrete:** $\gamma_{mc} = \mathbf{1.5}$ → design stress $= 0.67\, f_{ck}$
> - **Average compressive stress factor in rectangular stress block:** $k_1 = \mathbf{0.36}$ (IS 456 Fig. 4)
> - **Centroid depth factor:** $k_2 = \mathbf{0.42}$ (so the resultant compression acts at $0.42\, x_u$ from top fibre)
> - **Modular ratio** (short-term, LSM): $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa; $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ MPa
