<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Underlying Philosophy — The LSM Equilibrium

The Limit State Method of design (IS 456:2000, Cl. 36) demands equilibrium of forces **at the limit state of collapse (flexure)**. Two conditions are simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Force Equilibrium:** Total compression in concrete $=$ Total tension in steel
2. **Moment Equilibrium:** Resisting moment $M_u = C \times z = T \times z$ where $z$ is the lever arm

### 2.2 Force and Moment Components

**Tensile force in steel:**
$$T = 0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}$$

**Compressive force in concrete** (rectangular parabolic block, simplified to uniform $0.36\, f_{ck}$ over depth $x_u$):
$$C = 0.36\, f_{ck}\, b\, x_u$$

**Lever arm** (distance between $C$ and $T$):
$$z = d - 0.42\, x_u$$

**Moment of resistance:**
$$M_u = 0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}\,(d - 0.42\, x_u) = 0.36\, f_{ck}\, b\, x_u\,(d - 0.42\, x_u)$$

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why 0.87 factor on $f_y$?** The design stress is the **characteristic yield strength** $f_y$ divided by the partial safety factor $\gamma_{ms} = 1.15$. This accounts for variability in steel quality and possible overloading. Hence $1/1.15 = 0.87$.
- **Why 0.36 factor on $f_{ck}$?** IS 456:2000 Cl. 36.4.1 (and Fig. 4) idealizes the actual parabolic-rectangular concrete stress block as a **uniform rectangular block** with intensity $0.36\, f_{ck}$ (a simplification yielding equivalent ultimate force for grade $\le$ M55).
- **Why $0.42\, x_u$ as centroid?** The simplified rectangular block has its resultant acting at $0.42\, x_u$ from the **extreme compression fibre**, matching the centroid of the actual stress block.
- **Why $x_{u,\max}$ exists?** Concrete is brittle — if too much steel is provided, the section becomes *over-reinforced* and concrete crushes *before* steel yields. This leads to sudden, catastrophic failure without warning (no cracks). IS 456 prohibits this to ensure **ductile failure** with visible deflection and cracking.

### 2.4 Limiting Moment of Resistance (Balanced Section)

Setting $x_u = x_{u,\max}$ in the $M_u$ expression:

$$M_{u,\lim} = 0.36\, f_{ck}\, b\, x_{u,\max}\,(d - 0.42\, x_{u,\max})$$

For **Fe415** ($x_{u,\max} = 0.48 d$):
$$M_{u,\lim} = 0.36 \cdot 0.48 \cdot (1 - 0.42 \cdot 0.48) f_{ck}\, b\, d^2 = \mathbf{0.138\, f_{ck}\, b\, d^2}$$

For **Fe500** ($x_{u,\max} = 0.46 d$):
$$M_{u,\lim} = 0.36 \cdot 0.46 \cdot (1 - 0.42 \cdot 0.46) f_{ck}\, b\, d^2 = \mathbf{0.133\, f_{ck}\, b\, d^2}$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Following Table Covers ~90% of All KTU Module 1 Problems. Memorize It.**

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Code / Source |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Design steel stress | $f_{yd} = 0.87\, f_y$ | Cl. 36.4.2 |
| 2 | Design concrete stress (rectangular block) | $f_{cd} = 0.36\, f_{ck}$ | Cl. 36.4.1 |
| 3 | Tensile force in steel | $T = 0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}$ | Equilibrium |
| 4 | Compressive force in concrete | $C = 0.36\, f_{ck}\, b\, x_u$ | Equilibrium |
| 5 | Lever arm | $z = d - 0.42\, x_u$ | Cl. 38.1 |
| 6 | Neutral axis depth (analysis) | $x_u = \dfrac{0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}}{0.36\, f_{ck}\, b}$ | $C = T$ |
| 7 | Limiting $x_u$ for Fe415 | $x_{u,\max} = 0.48\, d$ | Cl. 38.1.1 |
| 8 | Limiting $x_u$ for Fe500 | $x_{u,\max} = 0.46\, d$ | Cl. 38.1.1 |
| 9 | $M_{u,\lim}$ for Fe415 | $0.138\, f_{ck}\, b\, d^2$ | Derived |
| 10 | $M_{u,\lim}$ for Fe500 | $0.133\, f_{ck}\, b\, d^2$ | Derived |
| 11 | Resisting moment (under-reinforced) | $M_u = 0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}\,(d - 0.42\, x_u)$ | Cl. 38.1 |
| 12 | $A_{st}$ (design, given $M_u$) | $A_{st} = \dfrac{0.5\, f_{ck}\, b\, d}{f_y}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4.6\, M_u}{f_{ck}\, b\, d^2}}\right]$ | Solving the quadratic |
| 13 | Minimum tension steel | $A_{st,\min} = \dfrac{0.85}{f_y}\, b\, d$ | Cl. 26.5.1.1 |
| 14 | Maximum tension steel | $A_{st,\max} = 0.04\, b\, D$ | Cl. 26.5.1.1 |
| 15 | Effective span (simply supported) | $l_{eff} = $ lesser of $(l_{clear} + d)$ or $(l_{c/c})$ | Cl. 22.2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The factor **4.6** in the design $A_{st}$ formula comes from $4.6 = 4 \times 1.15$, where 1.15 is the partial safety factor for steel. Always cross-check your $x_u$ computed from the design $A_{st}$: it **must** satisfy $x_u \le x_{u,\max}$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Singly reinforced rectangular beams form the structural skeleton of virtually every concrete building, bridge deck, and culvert in Kerala. The design ensures that under Kerala's seismic Zone III considerations and tropical humid climate, beams fail in a *ductile, warning-providing* manner rather than brittlely. Mastery of these formulas enables safe design of residential slabs, lintels, roof beams, and pre-engineered building frames.

## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — The Moment of Resistance Expression

Starting from the rectangular parabolic stress block (IS 456:2000 Fig. 4), we idealize the parabolic part as a **uniform rectangular block** of intensity $0.36 f_{ck}$ acting over the **entire neutral axis depth** $x_u$. The depth of the equivalent uniform stress block equals $x_u$ (not a fraction of $x_u$ — this is a critical IS 456 simplification).

**Step 1:** Total compression in concrete (volume of stress prism):
$$C = (\text{average stress}) \times (\text{area}) = (0.36\, f_{ck}) \times (b) \times (x_u)$$

$$C = 0.36\, f_{ck}\, b\, x_u$$

**Step 2:** Total tension in steel (assuming steel has yielded at $0.87 f_y$):
$$T = (\text{design stress}) \times (\text{area}) = (0.87\, f_y) \times A_{st}$$

$$T = 0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}$$

**Step 3:** Apply **horizontal force equilibrium** $C = T$:
$$0.36\, f_{ck}\, b\, x_u = 0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the neutral axis depth:
$$x_u = \frac{0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}}{0.36\, f_{ck}\, b}$$

**Step 5:** The compression resultant $C$ acts at a depth $0.42\, x_u$ from the top fibre. Tension $T$ acts at depth $d$ from the top. Lever arm:
$$z = d - 0.42\, x_u$$

**Step 6:** Take moment about the tension steel line (Cl. 38.1):
$$M_u = C \cdot z = 0.36\, f_{ck}\, b\, x_u\,(d - 0.42\, x_u)$$

**Step 7:** Substituting $x_u$ from Step 4 and simplifying (this yields the $A_{st}$ design formula in Cheat Sheet row 12):
$$M_u = 0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}\, d \left[1 - \frac{A_{st}\, f_y}{b\, d\, f_{ck}}\right]$$

This is a quadratic in $A_{st}$. Solving for $A_{st}$:
$$A_{st} = \frac{0.5\, f_{ck}\, b\, d}{f_y}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6\, M_u}{f_{ck}\, b\, d^2}}\right]$$

> **Derivation Note:** The constant 4.6 emerges as $4 \times \gamma_{ms} = 4 \times 1.15 = 4.6$, where the factor 4 comes from the quadratic discriminant.

---

### 3.2 WORKED EXAMPLE 1 — ANALYSIS PROBLEM (Given Section, Find $M_u$)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style Question Pattern]**
>
> A simply supported rectangular beam has overall depth $D = 550$ mm, width $b = 250$ mm, effective cover $= 50$ mm, and is reinforced with **4 bars of 20 mm $\phi$** at the bottom. Materials: M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. Determine the **moment of resistance** of the section. Is the section safe for an applied factored moment of **180 kN·m**?

**Given:**
- $b = 250$ mm, $D = 550$ mm, cover = 50 mm
- $A_{st} = 4 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 4 \times 314.16 = 1256.6 \text{ mm}^2$
- $f_{ck} = 20$ MPa, $f_y = 415$ MPa
- $d = D - \text{cover} - \phi/2 \approx 550 - 50 - 10 = 490$ mm
- $M_{u,\text{applied}} = 180$ kN·m

**Step 1: Compute $x_u$ from equilibrium**

$$x_u = \frac{0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}}{0.36\, f_{ck}\, b}$$

$$x_u = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 1256.6}{0.36 \times 20 \times 250}$$

Let us compute the numerator:
$$0.87 \times 415 = 361.05 \text{ MPa}$$
$$361.05 \times 1256.6 = 453{,}631.83 \text{ N}$$

Compute the denominator:
$$0.36 \times 20 \times 250 = 1800 \text{ N/mm}$$

Therefore:
$$x_u = \frac{453{,}631.83}{1800} = 252.02 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Check section classification**

For Fe415, $x_{u,\max} = 0.48\, d = 0.48 \times 490 = 235.2$ mm

$$x_u = 252.02 \text{ mm} > x_{u,\max} = 235.2 \text{ mm}$$

> **Classification: OVER-REINFORCED section.**

**Step 3: Compute resisting moment**

Since $x_u > x_{u,\max}$, the actual neutral axis is *clamped* at $x_{u,\max}$ for design. Resisting moment:
$$M_u = M_{u,\lim} = 0.138\, f_{ck}\, b\, d^2$$

$$M_u = 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times (490)^2$$

$$M_u = 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times 240{,}100$$

$$M_u = 165{,}669{,}000 \text{ N·mm} \approx 165.67 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 4: Safety Check**

$$M_{u,\text{applied}} = 180 \text{ kN·m} > M_{u,\text{resisting}} = 165.67 \text{ kN·m}$$

> **Conclusion: The section is UNSAFE.** The applied factored moment exceeds the limiting moment of resistance. Also, the section being over-reinforced is **prohibited by IS 456**. Remedies: (i) increase depth, (ii) use higher grade of concrete, (iii) redesign as doubly reinforced.

**Step 5: Verify minimum steel requirement**
$$A_{st,\min} = \frac{0.85}{f_y}\, b\, d = \frac{0.85}{415} \times 250 \times 490 = 250.9 \text{ mm}^2$$
Since $A_{st} = 1256.6 \text{ mm}^2 > A_{st,\min}$, the minimum steel requirement is satisfied.

---

### 3.3 WORKED EXAMPLE 2 — DESIGN PROBLEM (Given $M_u$, Find $A_{st}$)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
>
> Design a singly reinforced rectangular beam for a simply supported span of **5 m** carrying a uniformly distributed live load of **20 kN/m** (excluding self-weight). Take $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa and $f_y = 500$ MPa.

**Given:**
- Span $l = 5$ m, LL = 20 kN/m
- $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa, $f_y = 500$ MPa
- Assume overall depth $D = 500$ mm, $b = 250$ mm, effective cover = 50 mm
- $d = 500 - 50 = 450$ mm

**Step 1: Compute design loads and factored moment**

Self-weight of beam:
$$\text{SW} = b \times D \times 25 = 0.25 \times 0.50 \times 25 = 3.125 \text{ kN/m}$$

Factored dead load (using $\gamma_f = 1.5$):
$$w_{d,f} = 1.5 \times 3.125 = 4.69 \text{ kN/m}$$

Factored live load (using $\gamma_f = 1.5$):
$$w_{L,f} = 1.5 \times 20 = 30 \text{ kN/m}$$

Total factored load:
$$w_u = 4.69 + 30 = 34.69 \text{ kN/m}$$

For a simply supported beam under UDL:
$$M_u = \frac{w_u\, l^2}{8} = \frac{34.69 \times 5^2}{8} = \frac{34.69 \times 25}{8} = \frac{867.25}{8} = 108.4 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 2: Check if the assumed section can resist $M_u$**

For Fe500:
$$M_{u,\lim} = 0.133\, f_{ck}\, b\, d^2 = 0.133 \times 25 \times 250 \times (450)^2$$

$$M_{u,\lim} = 0.133 \times 25 \times 250 \times 202{,}500$$

$$M_{u,\lim} = 168{,}253{,}125 \text{ N·mm} \approx 168.25 \text{ kN·m}$$

Since $M_u = 108.4$ kN·m $< M_{u,\lim} = 168.25$ kN·m, the section can be designed as **singly reinforced**.

**Step 3: Calculate $A_{st}$ required**

Using the design formula:
$$A_{st} = \frac{0.5\, f_{ck}\, b\, d}{f_y}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6\, M_u}{f_{ck}\, b\, d^2}}\right]$$

Compute the discriminant term:
$$\frac{4.6 \times M_u}{f_{ck}\, b\, d^2} = \frac{4.6 \times 108.4 \times 10^6}{25 \times 250 \times 450^2}$$

Numerator:
$$4.6 \times 108.4 \times 10^6 = 498.64 \times 10^6$$

Denominator:
$$25 \times 250 \times 202{,}500 = 1{,}265{,}625{,}000 = 1.266 \times 10^9$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{4.6\, M_u}{f_{ck}\, b\, d^2} = \frac{498.64 \times 10^6}{1.266 \times 10^9} = 0.3939$$

Since $0.3939 < 1.0$, the square root is real (section is feasible).

$$\sqrt{1 - 0.3939} = \sqrt{0.6061} = 0.7785$$

Substitute:
$$A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 25 \times 250 \times 450}{500} \times [1 - 0.7785]$$

Pre-factor:
$$\frac{0.5 \times 25 \times 250 \times 450}{500} = \frac{1{,}406{,}250}{500} = 2812.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

Bracket term:
$$1 - 0.7785 = 0.2215$$

Therefore:
$$A_{st} = 2812.5 \times 0.2215 = 622.97 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Verify the design (compute actual $x_u$ and check ductility)**

$$x_u = \frac{0.87\, f_y\, A_{st}}{0.36\, f_{ck}\, b} = \frac{0.87 \times 500 \times 622.97}{0.36 \times 25 \times 250}$$

Numerator: $0.87 \times 500 \times 622.97 = 270{,}991.95$ N

Denominator: $0.36 \times 25 \times 250 = 2250$ N/mm

$$x_u = \frac{270{,}991.95}{2250} = 120.44 \text{ mm}$$

Check ductility:
$$x_{u,\max} = 0.46 \times 450 = 207 \text{ mm}$$
$$x_u = 120.44 \text{ mm} < x_{u,\max} = 207 \text{ mm} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 5: Check minimum steel**
$$A_{st,\min} = \frac{0.85}{f_y}\, b\, d = \frac{0.85}{500} \times 250 \times 450 = 191.25 \text{ mm}^2$$

Since $A_{st} = 622.97$ mm² $> A_{st,\min} = 191.25$ mm² ✓

**Step 6: Bar selection**

Use 16 mm $\phi$ bars (area per bar = 201 mm²):
$$n = \frac{622.97}{201} = 3.10 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{provide } \mathbf{4 \text{ bars of } 16 \text{ mm } \phi}$$

Provided $A_{st} = 4 \times 201 = 804$ mm² (slightly over-designed — safe).

> **Final Design Summary:** Provide **4 \#16 mm bars** at bottom; assumed section $b = 250$ mm, $D = 500$ mm, $d = 450$ mm is **safe and code-compliant**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

> [!NOTE]
> **The Decision Matrix Below is the Most Critical Diagnostic Tool for KTU Problems.**

**Q: Given $A_{st}$, $b$, $d$, $f_{ck}$, $f_y$, find $M_u$?**
1. Compute $x_u$ from $C = T$.
2. If $x_u \le x_{u,\max}$ → use $M_u = 0.87 f_y A_{st} (d - 0.42 x_u)$.
3. If $x_u > x_{u,\max}$ → clamp at $M_{u,\lim} = Q \cdot f_{ck} b d^2$ (Q = 0.138 for Fe415, 0.133 for Fe500).

**Q: Given $M_u$, $b$, $d$, $f_{ck}$, $f_y$, find $A_{st}$?**
1. Compute $M_{u,\lim}$.
2. If $M_u \le M_{u,\lim}$ → singly reinforced. Use the design formula to find $A_{st}$.
3. If $M_u > M_{u,\lim}$ → redesign as doubly reinforced (Module 2) or increase section.

**Q: Given $M_u$, $b$, $f_{ck}$, $f_y$, find $d$ and $A_{st}$?**
1. Compute required $d$ from $M_{u,\lim}$: $d_{req} = \sqrt{M_u / (Q \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b)}$.
2. Round $d$ to a practical multiple of 25 mm. Adopt overall depth $D = d + \text{cover} + \phi/2$.
3. Use the design formula with the adopted $d$ to find $A_{st}$.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q1. (a) Distinguish between a singly reinforced and a doubly reinforced concrete beam. When is a beam classified as a doubly reinforced section?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **singly reinforced beam** contains tensile reinforcement only in the flexural tension zone, with concrete alone resisting the compression. A **doubly reinforced beam** contains *both* tensile steel (bottom) and *compressive steel* (top) deliberately added to carry part of the compressive force, thereby allowing the section to resist a moment greater than $M_{u,\lim}$ of a singly reinforced section of the same dimensions.

A beam is **classified as doubly reinforced** when the applied factored moment $M_u$ exceeds the **limiting moment of resistance** $M_{u,\lim}$ of the singly reinforced section of the given dimensions and material grades. Compressive steel $A_{sc}$ is then designed to supplement the concrete in resisting the additional moment $M_u - M_{u,\lim}$, while additional tensile steel $A_{st2}$ is provided to balance $A_{sc}$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Model]**

**Q1. (b) Define the term *'limiting moment of resistance'* of a singly reinforced rectangular section. State its value for (i) Fe415 and (ii) Fe500 grade steel with M20 concrete.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **limiting moment of resistance** ($M_{u,\lim}$) is the maximum moment that a *singly reinforced* section can resist while remaining *balanced*, i.e., with the neutral axis at the code-permitted maximum depth $x_{u,\max}$. At this point, the steel has just yielded and the concrete reaches its ultimate strain of 0.0035 simultaneously, ensuring the section fails in a ductile manner.

Values of $M_{u,\lim}$ for $f_{ck} = 20$ MPa:
- For **Fe415**: $M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times b \times d^2 = 2.76\, b\, d^2$ N·mm
- For **Fe500**: $M_{u,\lim} = 0.133 \times 20 \times b \times d^2 = 2.66\, b\, d^2$ N·mm

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

---

> **[Question A — KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style, 14 Marks]**

**Q2 (A).** A simply supported rectangular beam has an **effective span of 6 m**, supports a **superimposed dead load of 10 kN/m** (excluding self-weight) and a **live load of 18 kN/m**. Design the beam as a *singly reinforced section* using M25 concrete and Fe500 steel. Sketch the reinforcement detailing with bar schedule.

#### (a) Compute Factored Loads and Design Moment (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $l_{eff} = 6$ m, SDL = 10 kN/m, LL = 18 kN/m
- $f_{ck} = 25$ MPa, $f_y = 500$ MPa
- Assume $b = 250$ mm, $D = 550$ mm, cover = 50 mm, $d = 500$ mm

**Solution:**

Self-weight: $w_{sw} = 0.25 \times 0.55 \times 25 = 3.44$ kN/m

Factored load:
$$w_u = 1.5 \times (3.44 + 10 + 18) = 1.5 \times 31.44 = 47.16 \text{ kN/m}$$

Design moment:
$$M_u = \frac{w_u l^2}{8} = \frac{47.16 \times 6^2}{8} = \frac{47.16 \times 36}{8} = 212.2 \text{ kN·m}$$

Check section feasibility (Fe500):
$$M_{u,\lim} = 0.133 \times 25 \times 250 \times (500)^2 = 0.133 \times 25 \times 250 \times 250{,}000 = 207{,}812{,}500 \text{ N·mm} = 207.81 \text{ kN·m}$$

Since $M_u = 212.2$ kN·m $> M_{u,\lim} = 207.81$ kN·m, the section must be **redesigned**.

Try $D = 600$ mm, $d = 550$ mm:
$$M_{u,\lim} = 0.133 \times 25 \times 250 \times (550)^2 = 251{,}453{,}125 \text{ N·mm} = 251.45 \text{ kN·m}$$

Now $M_u = 212.2 < 251.45$ ✓ (Use $D = 600$ mm, $b = 250$ mm, $d = 550$ mm)

#### (b) Design Tension Steel and Detailing (7 marks)

$$A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 25 \times 250 \times 550}{500} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 212.2 \times 10^6}{25 \times 250 \times 550^2}}\right]$$

Compute discriminant:
$$\frac{4.6 \times 212.2 \times 10^6}{25 \times 250 \times 302{,}500} = \frac{976.12 \times 10^6}{1.891 \times 10^9} = 0.5163$$

$$\sqrt{1 - 0.5163} = \sqrt{0.4837} = 0.6955$$

$$A_{st} = \frac{0.5 \times 25 \times 250 \times 550}{500} \times (1 - 0.6955) = 3437.5 \times 0.3045 = 1046.7 \text{ mm}^2$$

Provide **4 bars of 20 mm $\phi$** ($A_{st,provided} = 4 \times 314.16 = 1256.6$ mm²) — this is over-provided by ~20% (safe).

Check minimum steel:
$$A_{st,\min} = \frac{0.85}{500} \times 250 \times 550 = 233.75 \text{ mm}^2 \;\; (1256.6 \gg 233.75)\; \checkmark$$

Check maximum steel:
$$A_{st,\max} = 0.04 \times 250 \times 600 = 6000 \text{ mm}^2 \;\; (1256.6 \ll 6000)\; \checkmark$$

**Detailing:** Nominal shear reinforcement of 8 mm $\phi$ 2-legged stirrups at 150 mm c/c throughout the span. Provide 50 mm clear cover all around. Anchor bottom bars with 90° hooks (for Fe500, hooks not relied upon per IS 456:2000 Cl. 26.2.2.2) — use straight bars with full development length.

---

> **[Question B — KTU University Exam – Jun 2024 Alternative Style, 14 Marks]**

**Q2 (B).** A reinforced concrete beam of rectangular cross-section, **300 mm wide** and **600 mm effective depth**, is reinforced with **6 bars of 20 mm diameter** at the bottom. Materials: M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. **Analyze the section** to determine: (a) the moment of resistance, and (b) whether the section can resist an applied factored moment of **300 kN·m**. If not, suggest remedial measures.

#### (a) Determine the Type of Section and Moment of Resistance (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $b = 300$ mm, $d = 600$ mm, $A_{st} = 6 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 6 \times 314.16 = 1884.96$ mm²
- $f_{ck} = 20$ MPa, $f_y = 415$ MPa

**Solution:**

Compute $x_u$:
$$x_u = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 1884.96}{0.36 \times 20 \times 300}$$

Numerator: $0.87 \times 415 = 361.05$ MPa; $361.05 \times 1884.96 = 680{,}571.0$ N
Denominator: $0.36 \times 20 \times 300 = 2160$ N/mm

$$x_u = \frac{680{,}571.0}{2160} = 315.08 \text{ mm}$$

Compare with $x_{u,\max}$ for Fe415:
$$x_{u,\max} = 0.48 \times 600 = 288 \text{ mm}$$

Since $x_u = 315.08$ mm $> x_{u,\max} = 288$ mm → **Over-reinforced section**.

Therefore $M_u = M_{u,\lim}$:
$$M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 300 \times (600)^2 = 0.138 \times 20 \times 300 \times 360{,}000$$

$$M_{u,\lim} = 298{,}080{,}000 \text{ N·mm} = 298.08 \text{ kN·m}$$

#### (b) Check Safety and Remedial Measures (7 marks)

$M_{u,\text{applied}} = 300$ kN·m $> M_{u,\lim} = 298.08$ kN·m → **UNSAFE**

Moreover, the section is **over-reinforced**, which is **prohibited by IS 456:2000** (Cl. 38.1.1) due to sudden brittle failure. Three remedial measures:

1. **Increase overall depth** $D$ (most economical if headroom permits). For 300 kN·m with $b = 300$ mm:
$$d_{req} = \sqrt{\frac{300 \times 10^6}{0.138 \times 20 \times 300}} = \sqrt{\frac{300 \times 10^6}{828}} = \sqrt{362{,}319} = 602 \text{ mm}$$
Provide $D = 650$ mm, $d = 600$ mm — required to use revised parameters.

2. **Increase concrete grade** to M25: 
$$M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \times 25 \times 300 \times 600^2 = 372.6 \text{ kN·m} > 300 \text{ kN·m} \;\checkmark$$

3. **Redesign as doubly reinforced section** by adding compression steel at top — this is the cleanest solution when section depth is fixed.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Examiners Deduct Marks — Top 5 Mistakes on This Topic**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.87 partial safety factor on $f_y$.** Students write $T = f_y A_{st}$ and $f_{yd} = f_y$. This is wrong under LSM. Always write $0.87 f_y$.
>
> 2. **Misclassifying the section.** When $x_u > x_{u,\max}$, the resisting moment is **not** $0.87 f_y A_{st} (d - 0.42 x_u)$ — it is *clamped* at $M_{u,\lim}$. Failing to make this distinction costs **3 marks** typically.
>
> 3. **Using 0.138 fck bd² for Fe500.** This is a *very* common error. The factor 0.138 is for Fe415 only. For Fe500, the factor is **0.133** because $x_{u,\max}$ drops from 0.48d to 0.46d.
>
> 4. **Omitting the minimum steel check.** IS 456:2000 Cl. 26.5.1.1 mandates $A_{st} \ge 0.85 b d / f_y$. Skipping this check loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Using the wrong lever arm.** The lever arm is $z = d - 0.42 x_u$, NOT $d - 0.5 x_u$. The 0.5 value belongs to *elastic (WSD) method*; IS 456 uses LSM with 0.42. Confusing these methods is the **most common 2-mark deduction** in KTU boards.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1 Numericals**

- [ ] **Singly reinforced = tension steel only**; compression is entirely by concrete.
- [ ] **Design stress in steel** $= 0.87 f_y$; **design stress in concrete** (rectangular block) $= 0.36 f_{ck}$.
- [ ] **$x_{u,\max}$** for Fe415 $= 0.48 d$; for Fe500 $= 0.46 d$.
- [ ] **$M_{u,\lim}$** for Fe415 $= 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$; for Fe500 $= 0.133 f_{ck} b d^2$.
- [ ] **Lever arm** $z = d - 0.42 x_u$ (NOT $d - 0.5 x_u$).
- [ ] **$x_u$ from $C = T$:** $x_u = (0.87 f_y A_{st}) / (0.36 f_{ck} b)$.
- [ ] **$A_{st}$ from $M_u$:** $A_{st} = (0.5 f_{ck} b d / f_y) [1 - \sqrt{1 - 4.6 M_u / (f_{ck} b d^2)}]$.
- [ ] **Always classify** the section: under, balanced, or over-reinforced.
- [ ] **$A_{st,\min} = 0.85 b d / f_y$** — IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.1.
- [ ] **$A_{st,\max} = 0.04 b D$** — IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.1.
- [ ] **Effective span** $l_{eff}$ = lesser of (clear span + $d$) or (centre-to-centre of supports).
- [ ] **Factored loads** use $\gamma_f = 1.5$ for both DL and LL; factored moment $M_u = w_u l^2 / 8$ for UDL on simply supported beam.
- [ ] **Effective cover** = clear cover + half the bar diameter; $d = D - \text{effective cover}$.
- [ ] **Ductility rule:** If $x_u > x_{u,\max}$, section is over-reinforced and **disallowed** — must redesign.
- [ ] **Bar selection round up:** if 4.1 bars needed, provide 5; never under-provide.
- [ ] **Stirrup detailing:** 8 mm $\phi$ 2-legged stirrups minimum, spacing $\le 0.75 d$ or 300 mm (whichever is less) — IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.6.
- [ ] **Units discipline:** $M$ in N·mm, $b, d$ in mm, $f$ in MPa = N/mm² — keep consistent to avoid factor-of-1000 errors.

---

*End of Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme, PBCET404, Module 1: Numerical Problems on Design and Analysis of Singly Reinforced Rectangular Beams*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Doubly Reinforced Beams: Theory and Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCET404 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Doubly Reinforced Beams: Theory and Problems # 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Doubly Reinforced Section (IS 456:2000, Cl. 5.1.2.1 context):** A reinforced concrete beam section is termed *doubly reinforced* when it is provided with **reinforcement in both the tension zone AND the compression zone**. The compression-zone steel (termed *compression reinforcement* or $A_{sc}$) is provided in addition to the tension reinforcement ($A_{st}$) primarily to augment the moment of resistance of the section beyond the limiting moment of resistance of a singly reinforced balanced section. In analytical terms, the design is based on **superposition**: the cross-section is conceptually split into two parts — - **Part 1:** A singly reinforced balanced section. - **Part 2:** A section carrying an additional moment through a *couple* formed between the additional tension steel and the compression steel. The applied factored moment $M_u$ is split as: $$M_u = M_{u,\text{lim}} + M_2$$ where $M_{u,\text{lim}}$ is the **limiting moment of resistance** of a balanced singly reinforced section, and $M_2$ is the **additional moment** resisted by the pair $(A_{sc}, A_{st,2})$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Standing Desk Problem:** > Imagine a wooden beam spanning two supports. You sit on it and it bends. If you add a second person (more load) but cannot increase the beam's depth (the architect says "no — the floor-to-ceiling height is fixed"), the beam in bending will fail. Your options are: > 1. **Option A:** Use a stronger (bigger) piece of wood. > 2. **Option B:** Add a steel cable pre-tensioned at the top of the beam to "pre-compress" it, so when the load is applied, the top steel *actively helps* resist the bending. > > The civil engineer cannot magically increase the section depth, so they do **Option B** — embed steel bars near the top face. As the beam bends, the top concrete *and* the top steel both resist compression. That is, in essence, a **doubly reinforced section**. ## 1.3 When Are Doubly Reinforced Beams Adopted? A doubly reinforced beam is designed when **either or both** of the following conditions exist: | **Situation** | **Engineering Justification** | |---|---| | **Architectural / dimensional constraint** | Section depth is restricted by headroom / floor-to-floor height, but the load moment exceeds $M_{u,\text{lim}}$ of a singly reinforced section. | | **Reversal of bending moment** | Continuous beams, frames, and seismic load cases have points where moment reverses sign; both top and bottom reinforcement are needed. | | **Reduction of long-term deflection** | Compression steel reduces the *effective* creep-induced curvature, lowering long-term deflection. | | **Reduction of live-load deflection** | Top steel, if in compression under sustained load, increases section stiffness. | | **Confinement at supports** | Sometimes placed at column–beam junctions for ductility and shear confinement. | ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key standard values per IS 456:2000 used in this Module:** > - **Limiting depth of neutral axis (Fe415):** $x_{u,\text{max}} = 0.531\,d$ > - **Limiting depth of neutral axis (Fe500):** $x_{u,\text{max}} = 0.46\,d$ > - **Partial safety factor for steel:** $\gamma_m = 1.15$ > - **Design yield stress:** $f_y = 0.87\,f_{y,\text{nominal}}$ > - **Maximum concrete strain at ultimate:** $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$ > - **Yield strain (Fe415):** $\varepsilon_y = 415 / (2 \times 10^5) = 0.002075$ > - **Yield strain (Fe500):** $\varepsilon_y = 500 / (2 \times 10^5) = 0.0025$ # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Fundamental Strain-Compatibility & Force Equilibrium > [!NOTE] > **Equilibrium philosophy of a doubly reinforced section:** Two independent equations govern the section — (a) **force equilibrium** ($\sum F = 0$) and (b) **moment equilibrium** ($\sum M = 0$). In the design approach, we *fix* the strain block at the limit (i.e., $x_u = x_{u,\text{max}}$) and then compute the additional steel needed to resist the *excess* moment. ### 2.1.1 Splitting the Section — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1:** The compression zone is divided into two equivalent rectangular stress blocks (conceptual superposition): $$ \begin{aligned} C_1 &= 0.36\,f_{ck}\,b\,x_u \quad \text{(concrete compression in part-1, balanced)} \\ C_2 &= A_{sc}\,(f_{sc} - 0.45\,f_{ck}) \quad \text{(net contribution of compression steel above concrete stress at that level)} \end{aligned} $$ > The factor $(f_{sc} - 0.45\,f_{ck})$ exists because the concrete *already* carries $0.45\,f_{ck}$ at the compression-steel level; only the *extra* stress in steel beyond this is mobilized as additional compression. **Step 2:** The tension steel is split into two parts: - $A_{st,1}$ — required to balance the balanced part (i.e., $C_1 = 0.87\,f_y\,A_{st,1}$) - $A_{st,2}$ — required to balance the additional compression-steel couple **Step 3:** The design moment is the sum of two resisting moments. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All key equations for doubly reinforced beam design per IS 456:2000:** | **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Applicable For** | |:--:|---|---|---| | 1 | Limiting NA depth (Fe415) | $x_{u,\text{max}} = 0.531\,d$ | Fe415 grade steel | | 2 | Limiting NA depth (Fe500) | $x_{u,\text{max}} = 0.46\,d$ | Fe500 grade steel | | 3 | Limiting resisting moment | $M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.36\,f_{ck}\,b\,x_{u,\text{max}}\,(d - 0.416\,x_{u,\text{max}})$ | Balanced singly reinforced | | 4 | Tension steel for Part-1 (Fe415) | $A_{st,1} = 0.48\,f_{ck}\,b\,d / f_y$ | Balanced section | | 5 | Tension steel for Part-1 (Fe500) | $A_{st,1} = 0.46\,f_{ck}\,b\,d / f_y$ | Balanced section | | 6 | Additional moment | $M_2 = M_u - M_{u,\text{lim}}$ | Excess moment | | 7 | Additional tension steel | $A_{st,2} = M_2 / \bigl[0.87\,f_y\,(d - d')\bigr]$ | Part-2 couple | | 8 | Compression steel | $A_{sc} = M_2 / \bigl[(f_{sc} - 0.45\,f_{ck})\,(d - d')\bigr]$ | Part-2 couple | | 9 | Total tension steel | $A_{st} = A_{st,1} + A_{st,2}$ | Final reinforcement | | 10 | Stress in comp. steel | $f_{sc} = 0.0035\,E_s\,(x_{u,\text{max}} - d') / x_{u,\text{max}} \le f_y$ | Strain compatibility | | 11 | $f_{sc}$ table values (Fe415) | $d'/d = 0.05 \Rightarrow 355.4\,\text{MPa}$; $d'/d = 0.10 \Rightarrow 342.8\,\text{MPa}$; $d'/d = 0.15 \Rightarrow 329.3\,\text{MPa}$ | IS 456 SP-16 table | | 12 | $f_{sc}$ table values (Fe500) | $d'/d = 0.05 \Rightarrow 434.8\,\text{MPa}$; $d'/d = 0.10 \Rightarrow 421.8\,\text{MPa}$ | IS 456 SP-16 table | ## 2.3 Why This System Matters in Real Engineering > [!NOTE] > **Engineering utility — beyond just 'extra capacity':** > 1. **Seismic ductility:** Earthquake codes (IS 13920) demand confinement reinforcement at joints — a *direct extension* of doubly reinforced philosophy. > 2. **Continuous beams in RCC frames:** Hogging (negative) moments at supports and sagging moments at mid-span mean the top reinforcement at the support acts as compression steel under the *next* loading case. > 3. **Long-span transfer girders:** In high-rise buildings, transfer girders span over columns and often require $M_u \gg M_{u,\text{lim}}$ without depth increase. > 4. **Bridge deck slabs and pier caps:** Restrictive depth + heavy live load = the textbook doubly-reinforced design situation. ## 2.4 Minimum and Maximum Reinforcement (IS 456:2000) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reinforcement limits (Cl. 26.5.1.1 and 26.5.1.2 of IS 456):** > - $A_{st,\text{min}} = \dfrac{0.85\,b\,d}{f_y}$ (for Fe415, simplifies to $A_{st,\text{min}} \approx 0.002\,b\,d$) > - $A_{st,\text{max}} = 0.04\,b\,D$ (where $D$ is the *overall* depth) > - Compression steel $A_{sc}$ must be enclosed by stirrups of diameter $\ge \phi_{\text{main}}/4$ at spacing $\le 12\phi_{\text{main}}$. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation of $M_{u,\text{lim}}$ (Limiting Moment of Resistance) The stress block in a balanced singly reinforced section at the *limit state* is rectangular (per IS 456) of intensity $0.446\,f_{ck}$ acting over the upper $0.42\,x_u$. For $x_u = 0.531\,d$: **Step 1:** Magnitude of compression force in concrete. $$C = 0.36\,f_{ck}\,b\,x_{u,\text{max}}$$ The factor $0.36$ arises as $0.446 \times 0.806$ where $0.806 = 0.42 \times (0.531/0.531)$... more precisely, the centroid of the rectangular block is at $0.416\,x_u$ from the extreme compression fibre. **Step 2:** Lever arm distance from the tension steel. $$ \begin{aligned} z &= d - 0.416\,x_{u,\text{max}} \\ &= d - 0.416 \times 0.531\,d \\ &= d\,(1 - 0.221) \\ &= 0.779\,d \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Resisting moment. $$ \begin{aligned} M_{u,\text{lim}} &= C \cdot z = 0.36\,f_{ck}\,b\,x_{u,\text{max}}\,(d - 0.416\,x_{u,\text{max}}) \\ &= 0.36\,f_{ck}\,b \times 0.531\,d \times (d - 0.416 \times 0.531\,d) \\ &= 0.191\,f_{ck}\,b\,d \times (1 - 0.221)\,d \\ &= 0.1488\,f_{ck}\,b\,d^2 \quad \text{(for Fe415)} \end{aligned} $$ Similarly for Fe500: $$ \begin{aligned} M_{u,\text{lim}} &= 0.36\,f_{ck}\,b \times 0.46\,d \times (d - 0.42 \times 0.46\,d) \\ &= 0.1656\,f_{ck}\,b\,d \times (1 - 0.1932)\,d \\ &= 0.1336\,f_{ck}\,b\,d^2 \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Compression-Steel Stress $f_{sc}$ At the limit state, the strain at the top fibre is $\varepsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$. The strain at depth $d'$ is: $$ \begin{aligned} \varepsilon_{s}' &= \varepsilon_{cu}\,\frac{x_{u,\text{max}} - d'}{x_{u,\text{max}}} \\ &= 0.0035\left(1 - \frac{d'}{x_{u,\text{max}}}\right) \end{aligned} $$ The corresponding stress (provided the steel has yielded) is: $$ f_{sc} = E_s \cdot \varepsilon_{s}' = 2 \times 10^5 \times 0.0035\left(1 - \frac{d'}{x_{u,\text{max}}}\right) $$ But $f_{sc}$ cannot exceed $f_y$. So: $$ f_{sc} = \min\bigl[700\,(1 - d'/x_{u,\text{max}}),\ f_y\bigr] \quad \text{MPa} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical check (Fe415, $d' = 0.05\,d$, $x_u = 0.531\,d$):** > $$ > f_{sc} = 700 \times (1 - 0.05/0.531) = 700 \times 0.9058 = 634.1 \text{ MPa} > $$ > This exceeds $f_y = 415\,\text{MPa}$, hence $f_{sc} = 415\,\text{MPa}$. (But IS 456 SP-16 lists **355.4 MPa** for $d'/d = 0.05$, because the table uses the design stress with $\gamma_m = 1.15$: $f_{sc,\text{design}} = 415/1.15 = 361$ MPa; the table accounts for the partial safety factor — **a classic KTU trap**.) ## 3.3 The Additional Couple ($A_{sc}$–$A_{st,2}$ Couple) The moment $M_2$ is resisted by an internal force couple: - **Force magnitude:** $F_2 = A_{st,2} \times 0.87\,f_y = A_{sc} \times (f_{sc} - 0.45\,f_{ck})$ - **Lever arm:** $z_2 = d - d'$ So: $$ \begin{aligned} M_2 &= F_2 \times z_2 = A_{st,2} \times 0.87\,f_y \times (d - d') \\ A_{st,2} &= \frac{M_2}{0.87\,f_y\,(d - d')} \\ A_{sc} &= \frac{M_2}{(f_{sc} - 0.45\,f_{ck})\,(d - d')} \end{aligned} $$ The total tension steel: $$ A_{st} = A_{st,1} + A_{st,2} = \frac{0.48\,f_{ck}\,b\,d}{f_y} + \frac{M_u - M_{u,\text{lim}}}{0.87\,f_y\,(d - d')} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Couple-equivalence check (KTU examiners love this):** > $$ > A_{st,2} \times 0.87\,f_y = A_{sc} \times (f_{sc} - 0.45\,f_{ck}) > $$ > You can verify any problem with this identity once $M_2$, $d$, $d'$, and $f_{sc}$ are all known. ## 3.4 Worked Analytical Derivation — Finding $x_u$ for a Given $A_{st}, A_{sc}$ (Analysis Problem) Given $A_{st}$, $A_{sc}$, $b$, $d$, $d'$, $f_{ck}$, $f_y$, find $x_u$ and $M_u$: **Equilibrium of horizontal forces:** $$ 0.36\,f_{ck}\,b\,x_u + A_{sc}\,(f_{sc} - 0.45\,f_{ck}) = 0.87\,f_y\,A_{st} $$ But $f_{sc}$ itself depends on $x_u$, so an iterative solution is needed: 1. **Assume** $x_u = x_{u,\text{max}}$ (i.e., $0.531\,d$ for Fe415). 2. Compute $f_{sc}$ from the strain formula — if $f_{sc} > f_y$, set $f_{sc} = f_y$. 3. Plug into the equilibrium to find a *new* $x_u$: $$ x_u = \frac{0.87\,f_y\,A_{st} - A_{sc}\,(f_{sc} - 0.45\,f_{ck})}{0.36\,f_{ck}\,b} $$ 4. If $|x_u^{\text{new}} - x_u^{\text{old}}| \le 1\,\text{mm}$, **converged**; else iterate. 5. Check $x_u \le x_{u,\text{max}}$. If yes, the section is **safe and under-reinforced**. 6. Compute $M_u$ from moment equilibrium. **Resisting moment** (taking moment about tension steel): $$ M_u = 0.36\,f_{ck}\,b\,x_u\,(d - 0.416\,x_u) + A_{sc}\,(f_{sc} - 0.45\,f_{ck})\,(d - d') $$ This two-term expression is **the gold standard** for KTU 14-mark analysis problems. # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Cross-Section of a Doubly Reinforced Beam (Mermaid Schematic) > [!NOTE] > **Note on diagram:** Mermaid does not natively render engineering beam sections with reinforcement bars. The block-level architecture below shows the *internal force-flow architecture* of the doubly reinforced section — the two superimposed couple systems. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["EXTREME COMPRESSION FIBRE
y = 0, strain = 0.0035"] nodeB["RECTANGULAR STRESS BLOCK
depth = 0.42 xu, intensity = 0.446 fck"] nodeC["CONCRETE COMPRESSION FORCE C1
= 0.36 fck b xu"] nodeD["COMPRESSION STEEL Asc
level d-prime, stress fsc"] nodeE["STEEL COMPRESSION FORCE C2
= Asc times fsc minus 0.45 fck"] nodeF["NEUTRAL AXIS
depth xu from top"] nodeG["TENSION STEEL PART 1 Ast1
level d, stress 0.87 fy
BALANCED FOR C1"] nodeH["TENSION STEEL PART 2 Ast2
level d, stress 0.87 fy
BALANCES Asc COUPLE"] nodeI["TOTAL TENSION FORCE T
= 0.87 fy Ast"] nodeJ["EXTREME TENSION FIBRE
y = d, strain = 0.0035 times d minus xu over xu"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeI nodeG --> nodeI nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Design Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["INPUT DATA
Mu, b, d, d-prime, fck, fy"] nodeB["COMPUTE xu max
0.531 d for Fe415"] nodeC["COMPUTE Mu lim
0.36 fck b xu max times d minus 0.416 xu max"] nodeD{"DECISION
Mu greater than Mu lim?"} nodeE["SINGLY REINFORCED
DESIGN PATH"] nodeF["DOUBLY REINFORCED
PART 1: Ast1"] nodeG["COMPUTE M2
Mu minus Mu lim"] nodeH["LOOK UP fsc
from IS 456 SP-16 table"] nodeI["COMPUTE Ast2 and Asc
from couple equations"] nodeJ["TOTAL Ast = Ast1 + Ast2
CHECK MIN and MAX"] nodeK["SELECT BAR DIAMETERS
and LAYOUT"] nodeL["DETAILING CHECK
cover, spacing, side bars"] nodeM["FINAL DESIGN"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD -->|"No"| nodeE nodeD -->|"Yes"| nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK nodeK --> nodeL nodeL --> nodeM ``` ## 4.3 Strain-Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["STRAIN AT TOP
eps_cu = 0.0035"] nodeB["STRAIN AT NA
= 0"] nodeC["STRAIN AT d-prime
eps_sc = 0.0035 times xu minus d-prime over xu"] nodeD["STRAIN AT d
eps_st = 0.0035 times d minus xu over xu
MUST EXCEED eps_y for tension steel to yield"] nodeE["STRESSES
fsc = Es times eps_sc but cap fy
fst = 0.87 fy for yielded steel"] nodeF["FORCES
C1 = 0.36 fck b xu
C2 = Asc times fsc minus 0.45 fck
T = 0.87 fy Ast"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF ``` # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Wrong $f_{sc}$ value:** Students often use $f_y$ even when $d'/d$ is high. Always read the *correct row* from IS 456 SP-16 Table 64 — partial safety factors are already applied. Common error: using $f_{sc} = 353$ MPa for $d'/d = 0.05$ when the problem specifies a different $d'$. > 2. **Forgetting to subtract $0.45\,f_{ck}$** in the $A_{sc}$ formula — this gives ~10–15% overestimation of $A_{sc}$, but produces an *unsafe* $A_{st,2}$ if you also forget to subtract it in the $A_{st,2}$ formula (which actually does NOT have this term — only $A_{sc}$ does). This asymmetry is the most-asked trick question in KTU 2024. > 3. **Units mix-up:** All formulas require $N$ and $mm$ OR $kN$ and $m$. Never mix mm with kN. $M_u$ in $N\cdot mm$ when $f_{ck}$ in $N/mm^2$. > 4. **Forgetting stirrup design around $A_{sc}$:** IS 456 mandates lateral ties around compression bars — marks lost in the detailing part. > 5. **Not checking minimum steel:** $A_{st,\text{total}} \ge 0.85\,b\,d / f_y$ is mandatory; doubly reinforced sections often have far more, but a design with light compression and heavy tension can occasionally slip below. --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 marks) ### **Question 1: Define a doubly reinforced section. State the conditions under which it is adopted. [3 marks]** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** A **doubly reinforced concrete section** is one in which reinforcement is provided in **both** the tension and compression zones, in addition to the main tension reinforcement. It is adopted in the following situations: 1. When the **applied factored moment $M_u$ exceeds the limiting moment of resistance $M_{u,\text{lim}}$** of a balanced singly reinforced section of given cross-section. 2. When the **section dimensions are restricted** by architectural requirements (e.g., headroom, floor-to-floor height). 3. When the **bending moment reverses** under different loading conditions, e.g., continuous beams, seismic loading — so steel is needed at both faces. 4. To **reduce long-term deflection** caused by creep and shrinkage. --- ### **Question 2: What is the role of compression steel in a doubly reinforced beam? [3 marks]** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **Model Answer:** The compression steel $A_{sc}$ serves **two main engineering purposes**: 1. **Strength contribution:** It resists part of the **additional moment** $M_2 = M_u - M_{u,\text{lim}}$ through an internal force couple with additional tension steel $A_{st,2}$. The lever arm is $(d - d')$. 2. **Serviceability contribution:** It **reduces long-term deflections** due to creep and shrinkage (IS 456:2000, Cl. 23.2.1b) by providing continuous restraint to the compression-side concrete. 3. **Ductility and confinement:** It improves the **ductility** of the section under cyclic or seismic loads and, when properly tied with stirrups, confines the concrete core. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice, 1 × 14 = 14 marks) --- ### **Question A: Design a doubly reinforced concrete beam for the following data:** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` > **Given:** > - Factored moment $M_u = 380$ kN·m > - Section: $b = 300$ mm, overall depth $D = 600$ mm, $d = 550$ mm, $d' = 50$ mm > - Materials: M25 grade concrete ($f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm²), Fe415 grade steel ($f_y = 415$ N/mm²) **(a) Determine the area of tension and compression steel. [7 marks]** **(b) Sketch the cross-section and check for minimum / maximum reinforcement. [7 marks]** --- ### **Model Solution — Question A (a):** **Step 1:** Compute $x_{u,\text{max}}$ and $M_{u,\text{lim}}$. $$ x_{u,\text{max}} = 0.531 \times 550 = 292.05\ \text{mm} $$ $$ M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.36 \times 25 \times 300 \times 292.05 \times (550 - 0.416 \times 292.05) $$ $$ = 9000 \times 292.05 \times (550 - 121.49) $$ $$ = 9000 \times 292.05 \times 428.51 $$ $$ = 1.1263 \times 10^9\ \text{N·mm} = 1126.3\ \text{kN·m} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Wait — recalculation error-check:** $M_{u,\text{lim}}$ is ~1126 kN·m, which is **far above** $M_u = 380$ kN·m. Therefore the section is **NOT doubly reinforced** under these values. To make the problem valid (and to align with a typical 14-mark KTU design problem), we should *lower* the limiting moment — or use a *smaller* $d$ and *larger* $M_u$. Let me re-do with revised geometry: > > **Revised input:** $b = 250$ mm, $D = 500$ mm, $d = 450$ mm, $d' = 50$ mm, $M_u = 280$ kN·m, M25 + Fe415. **Step 1 (Revised):** Compute $x_{u,\text{max}}$. $$ x_{u,\text{max}} = 0.531 \times 450 = 238.95\ \text{mm} $$ **Step 2:** Compute $M_{u,\text{lim}}$. $$ M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.36 \times 25 \times 250 \times 238.95 \times (450 - 0.416 \times 238.95) $$ $$ = 2250 \times 238.95 \times (450 - 99.40) $$ $$ = 2250 \times 238.95 \times 350.60 $$ $$ = 188.5 \times 10^6\ \text{N·mm} = 188.5\ \text{kN·m} $$ **Step 3:** Compare $M_u$ with $M_{u,\text{lim}}$. $$ M_u = 280\ \text{kN·m} > M_{u,\text{lim}} = 188.5\ \text{kN·m} $$ > Therefore, **doubly reinforced section is required.** ✅ **Step 4:** Additional moment $M_2$. $$ M_2 = 280 - 188.5 = 91.5\ \text{kN·m} = 91.5 \times 10^6\ \text{N·mm} $$ **Step 5:** Look up $f_{sc}$. $d'/d = 50/450 = 0.111 \approx 0.11$ By linear interpolation in IS 456 SP-16 Table (Fe415): - $d'/d = 0.10 \Rightarrow f_{sc} = 342.8$ MPa - $d'/d = 0.15 \Rightarrow f_{sc} = 329.3$ MPa $$ f_{sc} = 342.8 + \frac{0.11 - 0.10}{0.15 - 0.10} \times (329.3 - 342.8) = 342.8 + 0.2 \times (-13.5) = 342.8 - 2.7 = 340.1\ \text{MPa} $$ Use $f_{sc} = 340$ N/mm². **Step 6:** Compute $A_{st,1}$ (Part 1 — balanced). $$ A_{st,1} = \frac{0.48 \times f_{ck} \times b \times d}{f_y} = \frac{0.48 \times 25 \times 250 \times 450}{415} $$ $$ = \frac{1{,}350{,}000}{415} = 3253\ \text{mm}^2 $$ **Step 7:** Compute $A_{st,2}$ (additional tension steel). $$ A_{st,2} = \frac{M_2}{0.87\,f_y\,(d - d')} = \frac{91.5 \times 10^6}{0.87 \times 415 \times (450 - 50)} $$ $$ = \frac{91.5 \times 10^6}{0.87 \times 415 \times 400} = \frac{91.5 \times 10^6}{144{,}420} = 633.6\ \text{mm}^2 $$ **Step 8:** Compute $A_{sc}$ (compression steel). $$ A_{sc} = \frac{M_2}{(f_{sc} - 0.45\,f_{ck})\,(d - d')} = \frac{91.5 \times 10^6}{(340 - 0.45 \times 25) \times 400} $$ $$ = \frac{91.5 \times 10^6}{(340 - 11.25) \times 400} = \frac{91.5 \times 10^6}{328.75 \times 400} = \frac{91.5 \times 10^6}{131{,}500} = 695.8\ \text{mm}^2 $$ **Step 9:** Total tension steel. $$ A_{st} = A_{st,1} + A_{st,2} = 3253 + 633.6 = 3886.6\ \text{mm}^2 \approx 3887\ \text{mm}^2 $$ **Final Answer for (a):** > | Quantity | Value | > |---|---| > | $A_{st}$ | $\mathbf{3887\ mm^2}$ | > | $A_{sc}$ | $\mathbf{696\ mm^2}$ | > | $A_{st,1}$ | 3253 mm² | > | $A_{st,2}$ | 634 mm² | --- ### **Model Solution — Question A (b): Sketch and Checks:** **Sketch (descriptive — student must redraw):** - Section 250 mm wide × 500 mm deep. - Cover = 50 mm; stirrups of 8 mm dia assumed (typical). - Compression steel $A_{sc} = 696$ mm² at top: use **2 bars of 22 mm dia** = $2 \times 380 = 760$ mm² (slightly more — OK and conservative). - Tension steel $A_{st} = 3887$ mm² at bottom: use **5 bars of 32 mm dia** = $5 \times 804 = 4020$ mm² (acceptable, ~3.4% over — fine for safety). - Stirrups: **8 mm dia @ 150 mm c/c**, 2-legged (or 4-legged since beam is 250 mm wide). **Minimum steel check (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.1):** $$ A_{st,\text{min}} = \frac{0.85 \times b \times d}{f_y} = \frac{0.85 \times 250 \times 450}{415} = 230.4\ \text{mm}^2 $$ $A_{st} = 3887\ \text{mm}^2 \gg 230.4\ \text{mm}^2$ ✅ **Maximum steel check (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.2):** $$ A_{st,\text{max}} = 0.04 \times b \times D = 0.04 \times 250 \times 500 = 5000\ \text{mm}^2 $$ $A_{st} = 3887\ \text{mm}^2 < 5000\ \text{mm}^2$ ✅ **Side-face reinforcement (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.3):** For $D > 750$ mm, side-face steel is required. Here $D = 500$ mm < 750 mm, so **not required.** ✅ **Cover check:** Effective cover 50 mm → IS 456 mandates minimum 25 mm for mild exposure, 30 mm for moderate, 50 mm for severe. **50 mm is safe for moderate exposure.** ✅ **Spacing check (tension bars):** - Clear spacing between 32 mm bars = $(250 - 2 \times 50 - 5 \times 32) / 4 = (250 - 100 - 160) / 4 = -2.5$ mm — bars will not fit in one layer. - Use **two layers**: 3 bars in layer 1 + 2 bars in layer 2 with 25 mm gap. - Total width used = $2 \times 50 + 3 \times 32 + 2 \times 25 + 2 \times 32 = 100 + 96 + 50 + 64 = 310$ mm — *exceeds* 250 mm. - **Use 6 bars of 28 mm dia** = $6 \times 615.8 = 3695$ mm² (slightly under 3887 — close). - **Better option:** 4 bars of 32 mm (bottom layer) + 2 bars of 25 mm (top layer) = $4 \times 804 + 2 \times 491 = 4198$ mm² — fits if arranged properly. - **Acceptable practical layout:** 2 bars of 32 mm + 4 bars of 28 mm = $2 \times 804 + 4 \times 615.8 = 1608 + 2463 = 4071$ mm² in 2 layers. **Stirrup detailing around $A_{sc}$ (Cl. 26.5.1.4 of IS 456):** Every compression bar must be laterally tied. With 2 bars of 22 mm, the stirrup spacing should not exceed 16 times the smallest compression-bar diameter = $16 \times 22 = 352$ mm. Use 8 mm stirrups at **150 mm c/c** (conservative and meets code). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design summary table for Question A:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Section $b \times D$ | 250 mm × 500 mm | | $A_{st}$ provided | 4071 mm² (2-#32 + 4-#28 in 2 layers) | | $A_{sc}$ provided | 760 mm² (2-#22) | | Stirrups | 8 mm @ 150 mm c/c, 2-legged | | Cover | 50 mm (all faces) | | Status | **SAFE — design OK** | --- ### **Question B (Alternative Choice): An R.C. beam 300 mm wide and 600 mm overall depth is reinforced with 4 bars of 25 mm dia at the bottom and 2 bars of 20 mm dia at the top. Effective cover is 50 mm on both faces. Calculate the moment of resistance of the beam. Materials: M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. [14 marks]** `[KTU University Exam — May 2022]` > **Sub-parts:** > **(a) Find the depth of the neutral axis using the force-equilibrium method. [7 marks]** > **(b) Compute the ultimate moment of resistance and verify the design. [7 marks]** --- ### **Model Solution — Question B:** **Given Data:** - $b = 300$ mm, $D = 600$ mm, $d = 600 - 50 = 550$ mm, $d' = 50$ mm - Tension steel: 4-#25 = $4 \times 491 = 1964$ mm² ($A_{st}$) - Compression steel: 2-#20 = $2 \times 314 = 628$ mm² ($A_{sc}$) - M20: $f_{ck} = 20$ N/mm², Fe415: $f_y = 415$ N/mm² **Step 1:** Limiting NA depth for Fe415. $$ x_{u,\text{max}} = 0.531 \times 550 = 292.05\ \text{mm} $$ **Step 2 (a):** Assume $f_{sc} = 353$ N/mm² (for $d'/d = 50/550 = 0.091 \approx 0.10$, table value for Fe415; approximate). > Actually, using linear interpolation between $d'/d = 0.05$ ($f_{sc} = 355.4$) and $d'/d = 0.10$ ($f_{sc} = 342.8$): > $$ > f_{sc} = 355.4 + \frac{0.091 - 0.05}{0.10 - 0.05} \times (342.8 - 355.4) = 355.4 + 0.82 \times (-12.6) = 345.1\ \text{N/mm}^2 > $$ **Step 3 (a):** Equilibrium equation (horizontal forces): $$ 0.36\,f_{ck}\,b\,x_u + A_{sc}\,(f_{sc} - 0.45\,f_{ck}) = 0.87\,f_y\,A_{st} $$ Substituting: $$ 0.36 \times 20 \times 300 \times x_u + 628 \times (345.1 - 0.45 \times 20) = 0.87 \times 415 \times 1964 $$ $$ 2160\,x_u + 628 \times (345.1 - 9) = 361.05 \times 1964 $$ $$ 2160\,x_u + 628 \times 336.1 = 709{,}182 $$ $$ 2160\,x_u + 211{,}070.8 = 709{,}182 $$ $$ 2160\,x_u = 498{,}111.2 $$ $$ x_u = 230.6\ \text{mm} $$ **Step 4:** Verify $x_u \le x_{u,\text{max}}$. $$ x_u = 230.6\ \text{mm} < x_{u,\text{max}} = 292.05\ \text{mm} \quad ✅ $$ > Therefore the section is **under-reinforced and safe.** **Step 5 (b):** Compute $M_u$ (resisting moment about tension steel): $$ M_u = 0.36\,f_{ck}\,b\,x_u\,(d - 0.416\,x_u) + A_{sc}\,(f_{sc} - 0.45\,f_{ck})\,(d - d') $$ **Term 1 — Concrete contribution:** $$ M_1 = 0.36 \times 20 \times 300 \times 230.6 \times (550 - 0.416 \times 230.6) $$ $$ = 2160 \times 230.6 \times (550 - 95.93) $$ $$ = 2160 \times 230.6 \times 454.07 $$ $$ = 226.18 \times 10^6\ \text{N·mm} $$ **Term 2 — Compression-steel contribution:** $$ M_2 = 628 \times 336.1 \times (550 - 50) = 628 \times 336.1 \times 500 = 105.55 \times 10^6\ \text{N·mm} $$ **Total:** $$ M_u = 226.18 \times 10^6 + 105.55 \times 10^6 = 331.73 \times 10^6\ \text{N·mm} = 331.73\ \text{kN·m} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Note on the convention:** In this analysis, $M_1$ uses the *gross* concrete stress block coefficient 0.36, but the $A_{sc}$ contribution already accounts for the steel-stress *increment* above $0.45\,f_{ck}$. This is the **IS 456-accepted** formulation. (Some textbooks use 0.362 — this minor difference is within acceptable tolerance.) **Step 6:** Verification — section is under-reinforced; both tension and compression steels yield (or nearly so). | Check | Result | |---|---| | $x_u = 230.6$ mm $\le x_{u,\text{max}} = 292.05$ mm | ✅ | | $A_{st} = 1964 \ge A_{st,\text{min}} = 0.85 \times 300 \times 550 / 415 = 338$ mm² | ✅ | | $A_{st} = 1964 \le A_{st,\text{max}} = 0.04 \times 300 \times 600 = 7200$ mm² | ✅ | | Strain in tension steel: $\varepsilon_{st} = 0.0035 \times (550 - 230.6)/230.6 = 0.00485$ > $\varepsilon_y = 0.00208$ | ✅ Yields | | Strain in compression steel: $\varepsilon_{sc} = 0.0035 \times (230.6 - 50)/230.6 = 0.00274$ > $\varepsilon_y$ | ✅ Yields (justifies using $f_{sc} = 345$ MPa) | > **Final Answer for Question B:** > $$ > x_u = 230.6\ \text{mm}, \quad M_u = 331.73\ \text{kN·m} > $$ --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Dense revision summary — Doubly Reinforced Beams:** - **Definition:** Reinforcement in *both* tension and compression faces; $A_{st} = A_{st,1} + A_{st,2}$, plus $A_{sc}$. - **Adoption criteria:** $M_u > M_{u,\text{lim}}$ for given section; OR restricted depth; OR reversing moment. - **Two limit-state values to memorize:** $x_{u,\text{max}} = 0.531\,d$ (Fe415) and $x_{u,\text{max}} = 0.46\,d$ (Fe500). - **Limiting moment formula:** $M_{u,\text{lim}} = 0.36\,f_{ck}\,b\,x_{u,\text{max}}\,(d - 0.416\,x_{u,\text{max}})$. - **Decomposition:** $M_u = M_{u,\text{lim}} + M_2$, with $A_{st,1}$ resisting $M_{u,\text{lim}}$ and the $(A_{sc}, A_{st,2})$ couple resisting $M_2$. - **Key A_st,1 formulas:** Fe415 → $0.48\,f_{ck}\,b\,d / f_y$; Fe500 → $0.46\,f_{ck}\,b\,d / f_y$. - **$A_{st,2}$ formula:** $M_2 / [0.87\,f_y\,(d - d')]$ — **no** $0.45\,f_{ck}$ correction. - **$A_{sc}$ formula:** $M_2 / [(f_{sc} - 0.45\,f_{ck})\,(d - d')]$ — **yes** $0.45\,f_{ck}$ correction (subtract). - **$f_{sc}$ lookup:** Read from IS 456 SP-16 Table 64 based on $d'/d$ and steel grade; *do not* default to $f_y$. - **Couple check identity:** $A_{st,2} \times 0.87\,f_y = A_{sc} \times (f_{sc} - 0.45\,f_{ck})$ — verifies both equations. - **Detailing rules:** $A_{st,\text{min}} = 0.85\,b\,d / f_y$; $A_{st,\text{max}} = 0.04\,b\,D$; lateral ties around $A_{sc} \le 16\,\phi_{\text{smallest comp bar}}$. - **Strain compatibility test:** $\varepsilon_{sc} = 0.0035\,(x_u - d')/x_u \ge \varepsilon_y \Rightarrow f_{sc} = f_y$ (yield check). - **Common trap:** Forgetting the $0.45\,f_{ck}$ term in $A_{sc}$ but remembering it in $A_{st,2}$ (which is **wrong**). - **Reverse problem (analysis):** Two-step iteration: assume $x_u = x_{u,\text{max}}$, find $f_{sc}$, then recompute $x_u$ from equilibrium. - **Safety criterion:** $x_u \le x_{u,\text{max}}$ for under-reinforced, ductile failure. - **Service benefit:** Compression steel **reduces long-term deflection** by ~30–40% — a frequently asked *theory* question in Part A. - **Practical note:** Doubly reinforced sections are *uneconomical* in concrete; only use when geometrically constrained.

topics.title: Flanged Beams: Theory and IS Code Provisions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET404 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flanged Beams: Theory and IS Code Provisions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **flanged beam** is a structural member in reinforced concrete construction in which the beam and a portion of the slab it supports act **monolithically** as a single composite unit, producing a cross-section that is geometrically either **T-shaped** (T-beam) or **L-shaped** (L-beam) — as opposed to the idealized rectangular section assumed in basic flexural analysis.

In accordance with **IS 456:2000 (Clause 23.1.1, 23.1.2, 23.2 and Annex G)**, when the concrete slab and the supporting beam are cast integrally, the portion of the slab on either side of the beam web is considered to act as a **flange** in compression, while the rectangular portion of the web below the slab resists both compression (rare) and tension. The analysis of such sections is called **flanged beam analysis** and forms the second fundamental case of Limit State Design of flexural members, the first being rectangular sections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Flanged beams are essential because in **beam-slab floor systems** (which constitute 95% of all R.C.C. construction in Kerala), the slab concrete lying between the neutral axis and the top surface is always present, and *ignoring* it leads to uneconomical, overly conservative designs. The **effective width of flange ($b_f$)** is the key design parameter, not the full slab width.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Inverted Chair"

Imagine you are sitting on an **inverted chair** (legs pointing up). The seat of the chair is wide and flat — that is your **flange**. The two parallel legs pointing upward form a much narrower **web**. When a downward load is applied to the chair, you instinctively press down on the **flat seat** with the palms of your hands, distributing the load across a wide area. The two thin legs, however, carry this load down to the floor through their slender cross-section.

Now picture the chair made of reinforced concrete and the seat "fused" with a horizontal slab:
- The **wide top (flange)** is the slab acting in **compression** (good at compression because concrete is strong here).
- The **narrow bottom (web)** is the rib of the beam where **steel reinforcement** is placed to resist **tension** (since concrete is weak in tension).

This is precisely a **T-beam** in action: a **wide compression zone** (concrete) and a **narrow tension zone with steel**, giving the most efficient use of both materials. The flange (slab) and web (beam below slab) deform together because they are **monolithically cast** — there is no slip between them.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (IS 456:2000)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Material Constants (IS 456:2000):**
> - **Grade of concrete ($f_{ck}$):** M20, M25, M30, M35, M40, M45, M50
> - **Grade of steel ($f_y$):** **Fe 415** (most common in Kerala practice), Fe 500, Fe 550
> - **Partial safety factor for concrete ($\gamma_m$):** **1.5**
> - **Partial safety factor for steel ($\gamma_m$):** **1.15**
> - **Limiting depth of neutral axis ($x_{u,max}$):** depends on $f_y$ (e.g., **0.48d** for Fe 415, **0.46d** for Fe 500)
> - **Limiting moment of resistance factor ($M_{u,lim}/bd^2$):** For Fe 415 = **2.76 N/mm²**

### 1.4 Why Flanged Beams Cannot Be Designed as Rectangular Beams

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake by KTU Students:** Treating a T-beam as a rectangular beam with width equal to $b_f$ and depth $d$ leads to **grossly incorrect and unsafe** designs. The lever arm in a T-beam is computed differently, and the steel area depends on whether the **neutral axis lies in the flange or in the web**, leading to two distinct analysis cases.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Parameters of a T-Beam (Formal Notation)

The cross-section of a T-beam is described by the following symbols (per IS 456:2000):

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Range (Kerala) |
|:------:|:--------|:----------------------:|
| $b_f$ | Effective width of flange | 900 mm – 2400 mm |
| $b_w$ | Width of web (rib) | 230 mm – 300 mm |
| $D_f$ | Thickness of flange (= slab thickness $D_s$) | 100 mm – 200 mm |
| $d$ | Effective depth (top of slab to centroid of tension steel) | 400 mm – 900 mm |
| $D$ | Overall depth | $d$ + effective cover (40–60 mm) |
| $d_f$ | Effective cover of compression reinforcement in flange | 25–50 mm |
| $A_{st}$ | Area of tension reinforcement | To be designed |
| $A_{sc}$ | Area of compression reinforcement in web (if any) | 0 or small |
| $x_u$ | Depth of neutral axis from top | To be determined |
| $f_{ck}$ | Characteristic compressive strength of concrete | 20–40 N/mm² |
| $f_y$ | Characteristic yield strength of steel | 415–500 N/mm² |

### 2.2 Effective Flange Width $b_f$ — IS 456:2000 Provisions (Clause 23.1.2)

The effective width of flange is the **smaller** of the following three values:

#### Case 1: For T-Beams (Symmetric Flange on Both Sides)

The effective width of the flange shall be taken as the **least** of:

1. **One-third of the effective span of the T-beam:**
$$b_f \leq \dfrac{L_0}{3}$$

2. **Distance centre-to-centre between the webs of the adjoining T-beams:**
$$b_f \leq (b_1 + b_2 + b_w) \quad \text{(the full available slab width)}$$

3. **The breadth of the web plus twelve times the flange thickness ($D_f$):**
$$b_f \leq b_w + 12 D_f$$

#### Case 2: For L-Beams (Flange on One Side Only)

The effective width of the flange shall be taken as the **least** of:

1. **One-twelfth of the effective span of the L-beam:**
$$b_f \leq \dfrac{L_0}{12}$$

2. **Actual width of the slab available on one side of the web:**
$$b_f \leq b_1 + b_w \quad \text{(slab available on the slab side)}$$

3. **The breadth of the web plus six times the flange thickness:**
$$b_f \leq b_w + 6 D_f$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Mnemonic:** "For T-beams: **3, full, 12**. For L-beams: **1/12, half, 6**."

### 2.3 Two Analysis Cases — Decisive Logic

The **position of the neutral axis** governs the entire analysis. A T-beam is analyzed in one of two regimes:

#### **CASE A: Neutral Axis Lies Within the Flange ($x_u \leq D_f$)**

- The entire compression zone is rectangular with width $b_f$.
- The section **behaves as a rectangular beam** of width $b_f$ and effective depth $d$.
- **Condition:** $C_{f} \geq C_{u,total}$, where $C_f$ = ultimate compressive force in the overhangs + flange.
- Use **standard rectangular beam formulas** with $b = b_f$.

#### **CASE B: Neutral Axis Lies in the Web ($x_u > D_f$)**

- The compression zone has two distinct regions:
  - A **rectangular compression zone** in the web of width $b_w$ from top of slab to $x_u$.
  - A **"wing" compression zone** consisting of the two flange overhangs (or one overhang for L-beams) of total width $(b_f - b_w)$ and depth $D_f$.
- **Different lever arms** apply for each region.
- The section is **no longer equivalent to a rectangle** — its analysis is the "true" T-beam problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to Decide the Case at the Design Stage:**
> 1. Compute the **Limiting Moment of Resistance** of the flange: $M_{uf} = 0.36 \dfrac{x_{u,max}}{d}\left(1 - 0.42 \dfrac{x_{u,max}}{d}\right) f_{ck} b_f d^2$
> 2. Compare with the **applied factored moment** $M_u$.
>    - If $M_u \leq M_{uf}$ → **CASE A** (NA in flange).
>    - If $M_u > M_{uf}$ → **CASE B** (NA in web).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|
| 1 | $C_f = 0.447 f_{ck} b_f D_f$ | Ultimate compressive force carried by flange overhangs (rectangular stress block, $x_u > D_f$) |
| 2 | $C_w = 0.447 f_{ck} b_w x_u$ | Ultimate compressive force carried by web compression zone |
| 3 | $T = 0.87 f_y A_{st}$ | Tensile force in steel |
| 4 | $C_f + C_w = T$ | **Fundamental equilibrium** for CASE B |
| 5 | $z_f = d - \dfrac{D_f}{2}$ | Lever arm for flange overhangs |
| 6 | $z_w = d - \dfrac{x_u}{2}$ | Lever arm for web compression zone |
| 7 | $M_u = C_f z_f + C_w z_w$ | Moment of resistance for CASE B |
| 8 | $M_{uf} = 0.36 \dfrac{x_{u,max}}{d}\left(1 - 0.42 \dfrac{x_{u,max}}{d}\right) f_{ck} b_f d^2$ | Limiting moment when NA is at flange-web interface |
| 9 | $x_{u,max} = 0.48d$ | For Fe 415 (per IS 456 Table) |
| 10 | $x_{u,max} = 0.46d$ | For Fe 500 (per IS 456 Table) |
| 11 | $A_{sv}/b_w s_v = \dfrac{0.4}{0.87 f_y}\left(\dfrac{\tau_v - 0.4}{1 - 0.4}\right)$ | Shear reinforcement (IS 456 Cl. 40.4) |
| 12 | $\tau_{c,max}$ | Maximum shear stress (IS 456 Table 20) |
| 13 | $M_{u,lim,R} = 2.76 b d^2$ (N, mm) | For Fe 415 with rectangular section |
| 14 | $p_t = \dfrac{A_{st}}{bd} \times 100$ | Percentage of tension steel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The formulas for $C_f$, $C_w$, $z_f$, and $z_w$ are the *signature* of T-beam problems. **Memorize them in a 2×2 grid** — KTU examiners award the full 14 marks only when all four are written with the correct physical meaning.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Kerala Civil Engineering

Flanged beam analysis is **not academic** — it is the *exact* design procedure for:

- **Residential building floor slabs** (continuous T-beams with monolithic slab).
- **Bridge girders** (where the deck slab is the flange and the girder below is the web).
- **Ribbed/grid floor systems** in commercial complexes.
- **Roof beams** of industrial sheds supporting AC units and finishes.

In Kerala practice, the most common T-beam is a **secondary beam (B2)** of an interior panel, supported on **main beams (B1)** in a 2-way or 1-way slab system. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects you to compute $b_f$, decide the case, design $A_{st}$, and **check for shear and deflection**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Two Equilibrium Equations for CASE B (NA in Web)

**Starting Premise:** At the limit state of collapse (flexure), the section is in equilibrium. The internal forces must satisfy both **force equilibrium** and **moment equilibrium** simultaneously.

**Step 1 — Establish the geometry of the compression zone.**

When $x_u > D_f$, the compression zone is a **T-shaped region** above the neutral axis. We conceptually split this into two rectangles for the convenience of the rectangular stress block (IS 456, Cl. 38.1):

- **Zone 1 — Flange overhangs** (the two "wings" outside the web): area $= (b_f - b_w) D_f$, centroid at $D_f/2$ from top.
- **Zone 2 — Web compression zone**: area $= b_w x_u$, centroid at $x_u/2$ from top.

**Step 2 — Apply the IS 456 Rectangular Stress Block.**

Per IS 456, the ultimate compressive stress in concrete is taken as a **uniform** value of $0.67 f_{ck}/1.5 = 0.4467 f_{ck} \approx 0.447 f_{ck}$ over a depth of $0.42 x_u$ (so that the area of the rectangular block equals the area of the actual parabolic-rectangular block). The depth of the rectangular block is therefore:

$$x_{u,\text{rect}} = 0.42 x_u$$

The stress acts on the **entire compression zone** (both zones) uniformly.

**Step 3 — Force in the flange overhangs.**

Total compression force carried by the two flange overhangs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_f &= 0.447 f_{ck} \cdot (b_f - b_w) \cdot D_f
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Reasoning:* We use the full depth $D_f$ (not $0.42 D_f$) because the *block* height of $0.42 x_u$ exceeds $D_f$ when $x_u > D_f$, and the flange is fully within the rectangular block. The effective width of the overhangs is $(b_f - b_w)$, where $b_w$ is excluded (it is counted in the web zone below).

**Step 4 — Force in the web compression zone.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_w &= 0.447 f_{ck} \cdot b_w \cdot x_u
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Force equilibrium.**

The total compressive force equals the tensile force in the steel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_f + C_w &= T \\
0.447 f_{ck} (b_f - b_w) D_f + 0.447 f_{ck} b_w x_u &= 0.87 f_y A_{st}
\end{aligned}
$$

This single equation has **two unknowns** ($x_u$ and $A_{st}$), so we need a second equation — moment equilibrium.

**Step 6 — Take moments about the tension steel centroid (location of $A_{st}$).**

The lever arm is measured from the tension steel at depth $d$ from the top:

- For $C_f$ (acting at the centroid of the flange overhangs, $D_f/2$ from top): **lever arm** $= d - D_f/2$.
- For $C_w$ (acting at the centroid of the web compression zone, $x_u/2$ from top): **lever arm** $= d - x_u/2$.

Hence the **moment of resistance**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_u &= C_f \left(d - \dfrac{D_f}{2}\right) + C_w \left(d - \dfrac{x_u}{2}\right) \\
&= 0.447 f_{ck} (b_f - b_w) D_f \left(d - \dfrac{D_f}{2}\right) + 0.447 f_{ck} b_w x_u \left(d - \dfrac{x_u}{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Solving the design problem.**

Given $M_u$ (applied factored moment) and all geometric/material properties, the **moment equation** is a *quadratic in* $x_u$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.447 f_{ck} b_w \dfrac{x_u^2}{2} - 0.447 f_{ck} b_w d \cdot x_u - 0.447 f_{ck} (b_f - b_w) D_f \left(d - \dfrac{D_f}{2}\right) + M_u = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving this quadratic (taking the positive root) gives $x_u$. Then substitute into the **force equation** to obtain $A_{st}$.

**Step 8 — Check for $x_u \leq x_{u,max}$.**

If the computed $x_u$ exceeds $x_{u,max}$ (0.48d for Fe 415), the section is **over-reinforced** (brittle failure, IS 456 forbids it). Solutions:

- Increase $b_w$ (deeper web — uncommon).
- Increase $d$ (deeper beam — preferred).
- Add **compression steel** $A_{sc}$ (use SP-16 design charts).
- Increase $f_{ck}$.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Flow

| Applied Moment $M_u$ | Status | Procedure |
|:---------------------|:-------|:----------|
| $M_u \leq 0.446 f_{ck} b_f D_f (d - D_f/2)$ | NA in flange | Use $b = b_f$, $x_u \leq D_f$ |
| $0.446 f_{ck} b_f D_f (d - D_f/2) < M_u \leq 2.76 b_w d^2$ | NA in web, **singly reinforced** | Solve quadratic for $x_u$, then $A_{st}$ |
| $M_u > 2.76 b_w d^2$ | **Doubly reinforced** required | Use $A_{sc}$ design (SP-16 / IS 456 Annex G) |

### 3.3 Derivation of Effective Flange Width Criterion (Why $b_w + 12 D_f$?)

The IS 456 provision $b_f = b_w + 12 D_f$ is **empirical** (based on beam tests) but can be rationalized by the **shear lag phenomenon** in wide flanges. Due to shear deformation, the longitudinal compressive stress in the slab is **non-uniform** across the flange width — it is maximum directly above the web and decays approximately **exponentially** away from the web. The "effective width" is the equivalent uniform width that carries the same total compressive force.

The integration of an exponential stress distribution of the form $\sigma(y) = \sigma_0 e^{-ky}$ over a half-width of $6 D_f$ gives approximately the same force as a uniform block of total width $12 D_f$. Hence:

$$
b_f = b_w + 12 D_f \quad \text{(T-beams)}, \quad b_f = b_w + 6 D_f \quad \text{(L-beams)}
$$

This is why the **flange thickness $D_f$** controls the effective width — a thicker slab mobilizes more of the slab in compression, while a thin slab suffers severe shear lag and the effective flange shrinks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** If $D_f$ is very small (say < 100 mm) the criterion $b_w + 12 D_f$ becomes tight, and the beam effectively behaves as a **rectangular beam of width $b_w$** with a thin composite topping. In such cases, IS 456 permits ignoring the flange contribution altogether (Clause 23.1.1, Note).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Scaffold (For Self-Study)

**Problem:** A T-beam has the following data (a typical Kerala residential floor beam):

- Effective span $L_0 = 6.0$ m, centre-to-centre of ribs = 3.0 m.
- $b_w = 230$ mm, $D_f = 150$ mm, $d = 500$ mm.
- $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm², $f_y = 415$ N/mm².
- Factored moment $M_u = 200$ kN·m.

**Step A — Effective flange width:**

Using the three criteria for T-beams:
1. $L_0/3 = 6000/3 = 2000$ mm
2. $b_1 + b_2 + b_w = 3000$ mm (full spacing)
3. $b_w + 12 D_f = 230 + 12(150) = 2030$ mm

Therefore: $\boxed{b_f = 2000 \text{ mm (least)}}$

**Step B — Limiting moment of flange:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{uf} &= 0.447 f_{ck} b_f D_f \left(d - \dfrac{D_f}{2}\right) \\
&= 0.447 \times 25 \times 2000 \times 150 \times \left(500 - 75\right) \\
&= 0.447 \times 25 \times 2000 \times 150 \times 425 \\
&= 1424.06 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} \\
&= 1424 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $M_u = 200$ kN·m $\ll M_{uf} = 1424$ kN·m, **NA lies in the flange → CASE A**.

**Step C — Treat as rectangular section with $b = b_f = 2000$ mm:**

Since $M_u \ll M_{u,lim} = 2.76 \times 2000 \times 500^2 \times 10^{-6} = 1380$ kN·m, the section is **under-reinforced**. Use the standard formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{st} &= \dfrac{0.5 f_{ck}}{f_y}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2}}\right] b d
\end{aligned}
$$

(Students should complete this computation; the small $A_{st}$ confirms the T-beam is *very* efficient for this loading.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Key Insight from this example:** When $b_f$ is large (which is common in continuous interior T-beams), the **flange itself has tremendous capacity**, and the NA almost always lies within the flange. **CASE B is rare in practice** for slabs spanning ≤ 4 m. KTU examiners, however, **love to construct hypothetical problems** with small $b_f$ and high $M_u$ to force CASE B — so **always check**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Flowchart for T-Beam Design Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: T-Beam Design Problem"] --> B["Given: M_u, f_ck, f_y, b_w, d, D_f, L_0, spacing"]
    B --> C["Compute Effective Flange Width b_f"]
    C --> D{"b_f = minimum of L_0/3, full spacing, b_w + 12 D_f"}
    D --> E["Check Case: Compute M_uf"]
    E --> F{"M_u ≤ M_uf ?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["CASE A: NA in Flange"]
    G --> H["Treat as Rectangular Section b = b_f"]
    H --> I["Use standard rectangular formulas for A_st"]
    I --> J["Check for minimum steel A_st_min"]
    J --> K["End"]
    F -- No --> L["CASE B: NA in Web"]
    L --> M["Solve quadratic in x_u from moment equation"]
    M --> N{"x_u ≤ x_u_max?"}
    N -- No --> O["Doubly Reinforced: Add A_sc and redesign"]
    N -- Yes --> P["Compute A_st from force equilibrium"]
    P --> Q["Check for minimum and maximum steel"]
    Q --> R["Check for Shear: τ_v ≤ τ_c_max"]
    R --> S["Design Shear Reinforcement A_sv / s_v"]
    S --> T["Check for Deflection: L/d ratio"]
    T --> K["End"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T["T-BEAM CROSS-SECTION FUNCTIONAL MAP"]
        direction TB
        node1["FLANGE: b_f wide × D_f thick | In COMPRESSION | Concrete C25/C30"]
        node2["FLANGE OVERHANGS: (b_f - b_w) wide on each side | In COMPRESSION | C25/C30"]
        node3["WEB TOP ZONE: b_w wide × x_u deep | In COMPRESSION (partial) | C25/C30"]
        node4["WEB MIDDLE: Neutral Axis location at x_u from top | Zero stress | C25/C30"]
        node5["WEB BOTTOM: Below NA | In TENSION | Concrete weak, steel carries load"]
        node6["TENSION STEEL: A_st bars at effective depth d | In TENSION | Fe 415/500"]
        node7["STIRRUPS: A_sv vertical links | Resist SHEAR | Fe 415"]
    end
    node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4 --> node5 --> node6
    node1 -.-> node7
    node6 -.-> node7
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB

```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Effective Flange Width Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Determine b_f for Flanged Beam"] --> q1{"T-beam or L-beam?"}
    q1 -- T-Beam --> t1["Criterion 1: L_0 / 3"]
    q1 -- L-Beam --> l1["Criterion 1: L_0 / 12"]
    q1 -- T-Beam --> t2["Criterion 2: b_1 + b_2 + b_w"]
    q1 -- L-Beam --> l2["Criterion 2: b_1 + b_w"]
    q1 -- T-Beam --> t3["Criterion 3: b_w + 12 D_f"]
    q1 -- L-Beam --> l3["Criterion 3: b_w + 6 D_f"]
    t1 --> min["Take MINIMUM of 3 criteria = b_f"]
    l1 --> min
    t2 --> min
    l2 --> min
    t3 --> min
    l3 --> min
    min --> endx["End: b_f established"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q: Define an isolated T-beam. Under what conditions can a T-beam be designed as a rectangular beam?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **isolated T-beam** is one in which the flange and the web are so proportioned that the beam can be analyzed *independently* — the slab is not continuous on both sides of the web, and the flange dimensions are governed by the structural proportions alone (IS 456 Cl. 23.1.1). The flange width of an isolated T-beam is taken as $b_f = b_w + 4 D_f$ (or less if the actual slab width is smaller), instead of the $b_w + 12 D_f$ used for continuous T-beams. **(1 mark)**

A T-beam **may be designed as a rectangular beam** of width $b_f$ when the **depth of the neutral axis $x_u$ lies within the flange** ($x_u \leq D_f$). In this situation, the entire compression zone is rectangular, and the analysis reduces to that of a rectangular section of width $b_f$ and effective depth $d$. **(2 marks)**

#### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**Q: State the IS 456:2000 provisions for determining the effective flange width of a continuous T-beam.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The effective flange width $b_f$ of a continuous T-beam (per IS 456 Cl. 23.1.2) shall be taken as the **least** of the following three values: **(1 mark for statement, 1 mark each for criteria)**

1. **One-third of the effective span** of the T-beam: $b_f \leq L_0/3$.
2. **The distance centre-to-centre** of the webs of the adjoining T-beams (i.e., the full available slab width on either side of the web).
3. **The breadth of the web plus twelve times the flange thickness**: $b_f \leq b_w + 12 D_f$.

> **Note:** For an L-beam, the corresponding values are $L_0/12$, half the centre-to-centre distance, and $b_w + 6 D_f$. **(1 mark for note)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question B.1 (Choice A)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

A continuous T-beam in a residential floor system has the following data:
- Effective span $L_0 = 7.0$ m, spacing of T-beams centre-to-centre = **3.2 m**
- Web width $b_w = 250$ mm, flange thickness $D_f = 140$ mm
- Overall depth $D = 600$ mm, effective cover = 50 mm
- $f_{ck} = 25$ N/mm², $f_y = 415$ N/mm²
- Factored bending moment $M_u = 280$ kN·m, factored shear force $V_u = 180$ kN

**Design the T-beam as a doubly/singly reinforced section and detail the reinforcement.**

##### **Part (a) — 7 marks: Design the flexural reinforcement**

**Step 1 — Effective depth:**
$$d = D - \text{effective cover} = 600 - 50 = 550 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Effective flange width $b_f$ (T-beam):**
1. $L_0/3 = 7000/3 = 2333$ mm
2. Full spacing $= 3200$ mm
3. $b_w + 12 D_f = 250 + 12(140) = 250 + 1680 = 1930$ mm

$$\boxed{b_f = 1930 \text{ mm (least)}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $M_{uf}$ (moment when NA is at flange-web interface):**
$$M_{uf} = 0.447 \times 25 \times 1930 \times 140 \times \left(550 - 70\right) = 0.447 \times 25 \times 1930 \times 140 \times 480$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{uf} &= 0.447 \times 25 \times 1930 \times 140 \times 480 \\
&= 0.447 \times 25 = 11.175 \\
&\times 1930 = 21567.75 \\
&\times 140 = 3019485 \\
&\times 480 = 1449352800 \text{ N·mm} \\
&= 1449.35 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $M_u = 280$ kN·m $\ll M_{uf}$, **NA lies in the flange → CASE A (singly reinforced, rectangular behaviour).**

**Step 4 — Solve as rectangular section $b = 1930$ mm:**

Limiting moment check (per IS 456, Fe 415):
$$M_{u,lim} = 2.76 \times b \times d^2 = 2.76 \times 1930 \times 550^2 = 2.76 \times 1930 \times 302500$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{u,lim} &= 2.76 \times 1930 = 5326.8 \\
&\times 302500 = 1.6112 \times 10^9 \text{ N·mm} = 1611.2 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $M_u = 280$ kN·m $\ll 1611$ kN·m, the section is **under-reinforced** and the steel will not reach yield — we use the standard formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{st} &= \dfrac{0.5 f_{ck}}{f_y}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2}}\right] b d
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the term:
$$\dfrac{4.6 \times 280 \times 10^6}{25 \times 1930 \times 550^2} = \dfrac{1.288 \times 10^9}{1.4594 \times 10^{10}} = 0.0883$$

$$\sqrt{1 - 0.0883} = \sqrt{0.9117} = 0.9548$$

$$A_{st} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 25}{415}(1 - 0.9548)(1930 \times 550)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \dfrac{12.5}{415} \times 0.0452 \times 1061500 \\
&= 0.03012 \times 0.0452 \times 1061500 \\
&= 1445 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Provide 12 mm Ø bars (area of one bar = 113 mm²):**
$$n = 1445/113 = 12.79 \rightarrow \text{Provide } \mathbf{16 \text{ bars of } 12 \text{ mm Ø } (A_{st,\text{prov}} = 16 \times 113 = 1808 \text{ mm}^2)}$$

**Step 6 — Check minimum steel (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.1):**
$$A_{st,min} = \dfrac{0.85 b_w d}{f_y} = \dfrac{0.85 \times 250 \times 550}{415} = 281.6 \text{ mm}^2$$

$A_{st,\text{prov}} = 1808$ mm² $> 281.6$ mm² ✓ **(Safe)**
