##### **Part (b) — 7 marks: Shear Design and Detailing**

**Step 1 — Compute nominal shear stress:**
$$\tau_v = \dfrac{V_u}{b_w d} = \dfrac{180 \times 10^3}{250 \times 550} = 1.309 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Maximum shear stress for $p_t = \dfrac{1808}{250 \times 550} \times 100 = 1.315\%$:**
From IS 456 Table 20, $\tau_{c,max} \approx 3.1$ N/mm². So $\tau_v < \tau_{c,max}$ ✓ (Section is safe in shear).

**Step 3 — Shear strength of concrete $\tau_c$ (from IS 456 Table 19):**
For $p_t = 1.315\%$, $\tau_c \approx 0.79$ N/mm².

**Step 4 — Shear to be carried by reinforcement:**
$$V_{us} = V_u - \tau_c \cdot b_w \cdot d = 180 \times 10^3 - 0.79 \times 250 \times 550 = 180000 - 108625 = 71375 \text{ N} = 71.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Design 2-legged 8 mm Ø stirrups ($A_{sv} = 2 \times 50 = 100$ mm²):**
$$s_v = \dfrac{0.87 f_y A_{sv} d}{V_{us}} = \dfrac{0.87 \times 415 \times 100 \times 550}{71375} = 277.7 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Minimum shear reinforcement (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.6):**
$$s_{v,max} = \dfrac{0.87 f_y A_{sv}}{0.4 b_w} = \dfrac{0.87 \times 415 \times 100}{0.4 \times 250} = 361.05 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7 — Provide 8 mm Ø 2-legged stirrups @ 275 mm c/c.**

**Detailing:** The 16 bars of 12 mm Ø are arranged in **2 rows** (10 in the bottom row + 6 in the top row, with 25 mm clear gap). The clear cover is 50 mm. Stirrups are placed at 275 mm centre-to-centre throughout the span. Anchorage length beyond supports = $L_d$ for 12 mm bar in tension.

**Conclusion:** The T-beam is safe in flexure and shear with 16-12 mm Ø bars and 8 mm Ø 2L stirrups @ 275 mm c/c.

---

#### **Question B.1 (Choice B)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

A T-beam has $b_w = 300$ mm, $b_f = 1500$ mm, $D_f = 120$ mm, $d = 700$ mm, $f_{ck} = 30$ N/mm², $f_y = 415$ N/mm². It carries a **factored moment $M_u = 850$ kN·m**.

**Determine the position of the neutral axis and design the tension reinforcement.**

##### **Part (a) — 7 marks: Position of NA and Case Identification**

**Step 1 — Compute $M_{uf}$ (flange moment capacity):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{uf} &= 0.447 f_{ck} b_f D_f \left(d - \dfrac{D_f}{2}\right) \\
&= 0.447 \times 30 \times 1500 \times 120 \times \left(700 - 60\right) \\
&= 0.447 \times 30 = 13.41 \\
&\times 1500 = 20115 \\
&\times 120 = 2413800 \\
&\times 640 = 1544832000 \text{ N·mm} = 1544.83 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $M_u = 850$ kN·m $< M_{uf} = 1544.83$ kN·m, **NA lies in the flange → CASE A (rectangular analysis with $b = b_f$).**

**Step 2 — Limiting moment of resistance (Fe 415):**
$$M_{u,lim} = 2.76 b d^2 = 2.76 \times 1500 \times 700^2 = 2.76 \times 1500 \times 490000 = 2.029 \times 10^9 \text{ N·mm} = 2029 \text{ kN·m}$$

Since $M_u = 850$ kN·m $< 2029$ kN·m, **section is under-reinforced** (singly reinforced, no compression steel needed).

**Step 3 — Solve for $A_{st}$ using standard formula:**
$$\dfrac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2} = \dfrac{4.6 \times 850 \times 10^6}{30 \times 1500 \times 700^2} = \dfrac{3.91 \times 10^9}{2.205 \times 10^{10}} = 0.1773$$

$$\sqrt{1 - 0.1773} = \sqrt{0.8227} = 0.9070$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{st} &= \dfrac{0.5 f_{ck}}{f_y}(1 - 0.9070) b d \\
&= \dfrac{0.5 \times 30}{415} \times 0.0930 \times (1500 \times 700) \\
&= 0.03614 \times 0.0930 \times 1050000 \\
&= 3528 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

##### **Part (b) — 7 marks: Detailing and Bar Selection**

**Step 1 — Choose 20 mm Ø bars (area = 314 mm²):**
$$n = 3528/314 = 11.24 \rightarrow \text{Provide } \mathbf{12 \text{ bars of } 20 \text{ mm Ø } (A_{st,\text{prov}} = 12 \times 314 = 3768 \text{ mm}^2)}$$

**Step 2 — Minimum steel check:**
$$A_{st,min} = \dfrac{0.85 b_w d}{f_y} = \dfrac{0.85 \times 300 \times 700}{415} = 430.1 \text{ mm}^2 \ll 3768 \text{ mm}^2 \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Maximum steel check (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.2):**
$$A_{st,max} = 0.04 b_w D = 0.04 \times 300 \times (700 + 50) = 9000 \text{ mm}^2 \gg 3768 \text{ mm}^2 \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Arrangement:** 12 bars of 20 mm Ø can be placed in 2 rows: 7 bars in row 1 + 5 bars in row 2 (or 6 + 6) with 25 mm clear spacing.

**Step 5 — Curtailment rules (IS 456 Cl. 26.2.3.2):** Extend at least one-third of the bars (i.e., 4 bars) to a distance of $d$ or $12\phi$ (whichever is greater) past the point where they are no longer needed. For 20 mm bar: $12\phi = 240$ mm; $d = 700$ mm. **Use $d = 700$ mm.**

**Step 6 — Shear check (assumed $\tau_c$ from Table 19):**
For $p_t = \dfrac{3768}{300 \times 700} \times 100 = 1.79\%$, $\tau_c = 0.88$ N/mm². Maximum $\tau_{c,max} = 3.5$ N/mm².

Provide 10 mm Ø 2L stirrups @ $s_v = \dfrac{0.87 \times 415 \times 157 \times 700}{V_u - 0.88 \times 300 \times 700}$ (assume $V_u$ from companion data). (For a complete problem, the student would compute $V_u$ from the load combination.)

**Conclusion:** The T-beam is designed as a singly reinforced section with 12-20 mm Ø bars, $A_{st} = 3768$ mm².

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in T-Beam Problems (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Forgetting the slab in the compression zone** — treating a T-beam as a rectangular beam of width $b_w$ and depth $d$. This gives a grossly *over-designed* section and is **wrong** even if "safe". (-3 to -4 marks)
> 2. **Wrong choice of $b_f$ criteria** — many students use $L_0/3$ for L-beams or $L_0/12$ for T-beams. (-2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing up $D_f/2$ and $x_u/2$ lever arms in CASE B** — the most common algebraic error. (-2 marks)
> 4. **Using 0.67 f_ck instead of 0.447 f_ck** — forgetting the partial safety factor of 1.5. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Not checking $x_u \leq x_{u,max}$** — KTU examiners specifically look for this check. (-2 marks)
> 6. **Ignoring $A_{st,min}$** — minimum tension steel is mandatory per IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.1. (-1 mark)
> 7. **Not detailing the curtailment and anchorage** — answers lacking a sketch lose 2-3 marks.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Revision Points — Flanged Beams**

- [x] **Flanged beams** occur when the slab is *monolithically cast* with the beam; ignore the slab only if it is *very thin* (IS 456 Cl. 23.1.1 Note).
- [x] **Effective flange width $b_f$** is the **LEAST** of three criteria:
    - **T-beam:** $L_0/3$, full centre-to-centre spacing, $b_w + 12 D_f$
    - **L-beam:** $L_0/12$, half spacing, $b_w + 6 D_f$
- [x] **Two cases of analysis:**
    - **CASE A** ($x_u \leq D_f$): Use $b = b_f$, standard rectangular formulas.
    - **CASE B** ($x_u > D_f$): Split compression into $C_f$ (overhangs) and $C_w$ (web), with two lever arms.
- [x] **Decision metric:** Compare $M_u$ with $M_{uf} = 0.447 f_{ck} b_f D_f (d - D_f/2)$.
- [x] **Key formulas to memorize:**
    - $C_f = 0.447 f_{ck} (b_f - b_w) D_f$, lever arm $z_f = d - D_f/2$
    - $C_w = 0.447 f_{ck} b_w x_u$, lever arm $z_w = d - x_u/2$
    - $T = 0.87 f_y A_{st}$
- [x] **Limiting $x_u$ for Fe 415 = 0.48d; for Fe 500 = 0.46d.**
- [x] **Limiting $M_{u,lim}$ for Fe 415 = 2.76 b d² (N, mm).**
- [x] **Shear design** uses $b_w$ (not $b_f$) per IS 456 Cl. 40.1.
- [x] **Minimum tension steel:** $A_{st,min} = 0.85 b_w d / f_y$.
- [x] **Maximum tension steel:** $A_{st,max} = 0.04 b_w D$.
- [x] **Curtailment:** Extend at least 1/3 of bars $d$ or $12\phi$ past the theoretical cut-off point.
- [x] **Detailing** requires a clearly labelled cross-section sketch showing bar diameters, spacing, and cover.
- [x] **L-beam is rarer** — occurs at edge beams in a building frame.
- [x] **Isolated T-beam** uses $b_f = b_w + 4 D_f$ — important for **lintel beams** over openings.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: One-way Slabs: Basic Principles, Theory and Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCET404 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** One-way Slabs: Basic Principles, Theory and Design ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 / IS 456:2000 Terminology) A **slab** is a planar, two-dimensional structural element whose thickness is small compared to its other two dimensions (length and breadth). It is primarily designed to resist transverse loads (loads acting perpendicular to its plane) and transfer them to the supporting beams, walls, or columns. A **One-Way Slab** is defined (as per **IS 456:2000, Clause 24.1**) as a slab designed to bend in only **one principal direction** and transfer the loads to the two opposite supporting members. The structural action is predominantly unidirectional when the slab is supported on all four edges but the ratio of the longer span to the shorter span ($l_y/l_x$) is **greater than or equal to 2.0**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **IS 456:2000 Designation Rule (Clause 24.1):** > - **One-way slab:** $l_y / l_x \geq 2$ → Bends in the shorter direction. > - **Two-way slab:** $l_y / l_x < 2$ → Bends in both principal directions. > where $l_x$ = short span, $l_y$ = long span (both measured centre-to-centre of supports). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine holding a **rectangular wooden plank** (say 4 m long × 1 m wide) and resting it on two **bricks placed at the two short ends**. If you stand in the middle of the plank, it will **sag and bend along the short (1 m) direction**, while the long (4 m) direction remains relatively straight. The plank carries your weight to the two short-edge bricks only. A one-way slab behaves **exactly like this plank**: - The load path is unidirectional → shorter span. - The slab is essentially treated as a **wide beam of unit width (1 m strip)**. - The bending and reinforcement are designed for this 1 m wide strip running between the two parallel supports. > [!NOTE] > **Why "One-Way"?** Because the structural system only "feels" the support in one direction — the load travels to the supports along the **shorter span** as a beam-action, with negligible curvature along the long span. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used in KTU Design Problems | Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---| | Unit weight of **Plain Cement Concrete (PCC)** | **24 kN/m³** | | Unit weight of **Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC)** | **25 kN/m³** | | Unit weight of **Floor finish / Screed** | **20–24 kN/m³** (typically **22 kN/m³**) | | Partial Safety Factor for Dead Load ($\gamma_f$ for DL) | **1.5** | | Partial Safety Factor for Live Load ($\gamma_f$ for LL) | **1.5** | | Grade of Concrete (KTU preferred) | **M20** (or M25) | | Grade of Steel (KTU / IS 456) | **Fe415** (or Fe500) | | Concrete cover for slab (mild exposure) | **20 mm** | | Effective cover ($d'$ assumed) | **20 mm** (slabs) → so overall depth $D = d + 20$ mm | | Minimum reinforcement in slabs (Fe415) | **0.12 % of gross cross-sectional area** | | Maximum spacing of main bars | **3d** or **300 mm** (whichever is less) | | Maximum spacing of distribution bars | **5d** or **450 mm** (whichever is less) | | Effective span (IS 456, Cl. 22.2) | Lesser of: (i) clear span + effective depth, (ii) centre-to-centre of supports | ### 1.4 Why KTU Tests This Topic > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):** > One-way slab design is the **foundation problem** for Limit State Design (LSD). Every concept here — load factors, effective depth, $M_u$ calculation, $A_{st}$ derivation, deflection/cracking checks — is **reused directly** in continuous slabs, two-way slabs, beams, and footings. Marks are almost guaranteed in **Part A (3M)** and frequently in **Part B (14M)** of ESE. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework for One-Way Slab Design The KTU / IS 456 approach follows a **closed-loop design sequence**. Each step is a sub-output of the previous one. **Step 1 — Identify Slab Type (One-Way vs Two-Way)** - Compute $l_y / l_x$. If $\geq 2 \Rightarrow$ one-way. If $< 2 \Rightarrow$ two-way (NOT in this module). **Step 2 — Fix Preliminary Dimensions** - Adopt a trial overall depth $D$ based on **basic span-to-depth ratio** (IS 456, Cl. 23.2.1): for simply supported slabs with Fe415 steel, **$L/d \leq 20$**. - Therefore, $d \geq L/20$. Round up to a practical value (e.g., 120, 150, 175 mm). **Step 3 — Compute Effective Span ($l_{eff}$ or $l_d$)** - As per **IS 456, Clause 22.2(a)**: $l_{eff} = $ clear span $+ d$ *or* centre-to-centre distance between supports, **whichever is less**. - For slabs on masonry walls, generally $l_{eff} \approx$ clear span $+ d$. **Step 4 — Compute Loads (per m² and per m width)** - **Dead Load (DL)** = self-weight of slab + floor finish - $D = $ overall depth in m - Self-weight $= 25 \times D \times 1 \times 1$ kN/m² $= 25D$ kN/m² - Floor finish weight (given in problem, typically 1.0–1.5 kN/m²) - **Live Load (LL)** = as per **IS 875 (Part 2)**, problem-stated. - **Factored Load** $w_u = 1.5 \times (DL + LL)$ kN/m², computed per 1 m strip → $w_u$ in kN/m. **Step 5 — Compute Maximum Bending Moment ($M_u$)** - For a **simply supported** one-way slab: $$M_u = \frac{w_u \, l_{eff}^2}{8}$$ - For a **continuous** slab, use **IS 456, Table 12** coefficients (Clause 22.5.1): - Mid-span (positive moment) → coefficient $= + w_u l_x^2/8$ for end span, etc. - Support (negative moment) → coefficient $- w_u l_x^2/8$ or $- w_u l_x^2/10$, depending on end condition. **Step 6 — Check Effective Depth (Required)** - Using $M_u = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$ for Fe415: $$d_{req} = \sqrt{\frac{M_u}{0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b}}$$ where $b = 1000$ mm (per metre width). **Step 7 — Compute Area of Steel ($A_{st,req}$)** - Using the **limit-state flexure equation** (IS 456, Cl. 38.1, Annex G): $$M_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \, d \, \left(1 - \frac{A_{st} \, f_y}{b \, d \, f_{ck}}\right)$$ - Or directly via the **simplified coefficient**: $$p_t = \frac{0.5 \, f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \, M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2}} \right] \times 100 \quad (\%)$$ $$A_{st,req} = \frac{p_t \cdot b \cdot d}{100}$$ **Step 8 — Choose Bar Diameter and Spacing** - From $A_{st,req}$, pick a bar diameter (8, 10, 12 mm) → compute area of one bar → spacing $s = (a_{bar} / A_{st,req}) \times 1000$ mm. - **Check IS 456 minimum spacing rules** (Cl. 26.3.3): - Maximum spacing of main bars $= 3d$ or $300$ mm (lesser). - Maximum spacing of distribution bars $= 5d$ or $450$ mm (lesser). **Step 9 — Check for Shear (Nominal)** - $\tau_v = V_u / (b \cdot d)$ - $V_u = w_u l_{eff}/2$ for simply supported. - Compare $\tau_v$ with $\tau_{c,\max}$ from **IS 456, Table 20** (for $p_t$ and concrete grade) — slabs are almost always safe in shear, but a check is mandatory in KTU valuation. **Step 10 — Deflection and Cracking Check (Serviceability)** - IS 456, Cl. 23.2 — basic $L/d$ ratio. - IS 456, Cl. 43 — cracking (max spacing of bars based on stress $f_s$). **Step 11 — Detailing (Distribution Steel and Curtailment)** - **Distribution steel** = 0.12 % of cross-sectional area, placed along the **long span**, perpendicular to main steel. - **Curtailment**: 50 % bars can be curtailed at $0.1 l_{eff}$ from the support face (per IS 456 detailing rules). ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity | Formula | Reference / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Slab type check | $l_y/l_x \geq 2 \Rightarrow$ one-way | IS 456, Cl. 24.1 | | 2 | Effective span | $l_{eff} = \min(l_c + d,\; l_{c/c})$ | IS 456, Cl. 22.2(a) | | 3 | Basic $L/d$ (SS, Fe415) | $L/d \leq 20$ | IS 456, Cl. 23.2.1 | | 4 | Dead load of slab | $g = 25 \, D$ kN/m² ($D$ in m) | Standard | | 5 | Factored load (1 m strip) | $w_u = 1.5 \times (\text{DL} + \text{LL})$ kN/m | Cl. 36.4.1 (IS 456) | | 6 | Max BM (simply supported) | $M_u = w_u l_{eff}^2 / 8$ | Cl. 22.5.1 | | 7 | Max SF (simply supported) | $V_u = w_u l_{eff} / 2$ | Cl. 22.5.1 | | 8 | Moment of resistance coeff. | $M_u = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$ (Fe415, single) | IS 456, Annex G | | 9 | Required $d$ (preliminary) | $d_{req} = \sqrt{M_u / (0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b)}$ | mm | | 10 | Steel percentage | $p_t = \frac{50 f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2}} \right]$ | %, IS 456 | | 11 | Area of steel | $A_{st} = p_t \, b \, d / 100$ | mm² | | 12 | Spacing of bars | $s = (a_{bar} / A_{st}) \times 1000$ | mm | | 13 | Min. main steel (Fe415) | $A_{st,min} = 0.12 \, b \, D / 100$ | IS 456, Cl. 26.5.2.1 | | 14 | Max. spacing — main | $\min(3d, 300)$ mm | IS 456, Cl. 26.3.3(a) | | 15 | Max. spacing — dist. | $\min(5d, 450)$ mm | IS 456, Cl. 26.3.3(b) | | 16 | Nominal shear stress | $\tau_v = V_u / (b d)$ | N/mm² | | 17 | Permissible shear $\tau_c$ | IS 456, Table 19 (for $p_t$, M20) | N/mm² | | 18 | Distribution steel | $A_{sd} = 0.12 \, b \, D / 100$ (Fe415) | IS 456, Cl. 26.5.2.1 | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** The one-way slab design procedure is directly applied in **residential floor slabs, sun-shades, chajjas, staircase landings, and roof slabs of corridors** — any panel where the aspect ratio is $\geq 2$. ### 2.3 Concept Behind the Governing Equation The core flexure equation $M_u = 0.87 f_y A_{st} d (1 - A_{st} f_y / b d f_{ck})$ comes from **equilibrium of an under-reinforced rectangular section**: - The compression block is a **Whitney stress block** of depth $0.42 x_u$, with uniform stress $0.67 f_{ck}$. - The steel has yielded, with average stress reduced by the partial safety factor → $0.87 f_y$. - The lever arm is $(d - 0.42 x_u)$, which upon expansion produces the $1 - A_{st} f_y / b d f_{ck}$ term. This is the **heart of LSD** and is reused for beams, footings, and one-way slabs alike. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Flexure Equation (Limit State of Collapse in Flexure) **Starting assumption:** Rectangular section, singly reinforced, $b \times d$, concrete grade $f_{ck}$, steel grade $f_y$. **Step 1 — Equating tensile and compressive forces (force equilibrium):** $$C = T$$ $$0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}$$ **Step 2 — Solving for the neutral axis depth $x_u$:** $$x_u = \frac{0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b}$$ **Step 3 — Moment of resistance (taking moments of the force couple about the centroid of steel):** $$M_u = T \cdot z = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \cdot (d - 0.42 \, x_u)$$ **Step 4 — Substituting $x_u$ in terms of $A_{st}$:** Let $$k = \frac{0.87 \, f_y}{0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b}$$ Then $$x_u = k \cdot \frac{A_{st}}{b}$$ and the lever arm becomes $$z = d - 0.42 \, k \, \frac{A_{st}}{b}$$ **Step 5 — Expanding and rearranging to the canonical form:** $$ \begin{aligned} M_u &= 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \left[ d - \frac{0.42 \times 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b} \right] \\ M_u &= 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \, d \left[ 1 - \frac{0.42 \times 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d} \right] \\ M_u &= 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \, d \left[ 1 - \frac{A_{st} \, f_y}{b \, d \, f_{ck}} \right] \end{aligned} $$ > This is the **master equation** for limit-state flexural design. **Step 6 — Convert to the $p_t$ (percentage of steel) form:** Let $p_t = 100 A_{st} / (b d)$, so $A_{st} / (b d) = p_t / 100$. $$ \begin{aligned} M_u &= 0.87 \, f_y \, \left(\frac{p_t \, b \, d}{100}\right) d \left[ 1 - \frac{p_t \, f_y}{100 \, f_{ck}} \right] \\ \frac{M_u}{b \, d^2} &= \frac{0.87 \, f_y \, p_t}{100} \left[ 1 - \frac{p_t \, f_y}{100 \, f_{ck}} \right] \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Solving the quadratic for $p_t$:** Let $R = M_u / (b d^2)$. Expanding: $$\frac{0.87 \, f_y}{100} p_t - \frac{0.87 \, f_y^2}{10000 \, f_{ck}} p_t^2 = R$$ Multiply by $-10000 f_{ck} / (0.87 f_y^2)$: $$ \begin{aligned} p_t^2 - \frac{100 \, f_{ck}}{f_y} p_t + \frac{10000 \, f_{ck} \, R}{0.87 \, f_y^2} = 0 \end{aligned} $$ Using the quadratic formula $p_t = [-B \pm \sqrt{B^2 - 4AC}]/2A$: $$ p_t = \frac{50 \, f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \, M_u}{f_{ck} \, b \, d^2}} \right] \quad (\%) $$ This is the **direct percentage formula** used in KTU answer sheets. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical: Full Design of a One-Way Slab (KTU Typical Problem) **Problem Statement:** Design a simply supported one-way slab for a residential building with the following data: - Clear span $= 3.5$ m - Width of support (wall) $= 230$ mm - Live load $= 3.0$ kN/m² - Floor finish $= 1.0$ kN/m² - Concrete: **M20**, Steel: **Fe415** - Exposure: Mild - Slab supported on two RCC beams. **Step 1 — Preliminary depth (Basic L/d):** $$d_{req} = \frac{L}{20} = \frac{3500}{20} = 175 \text{ mm}$$ Provide overall depth $D = 200$ mm → $d = 200 - 20 = 180$ mm (assuming 20 mm cover and 10 mm bar). **Step 2 — Effective span (IS 456 Cl. 22.2(a)):** Clear span $+ d = 3500 + 180 = 3680$ mm Centre-to-centre $= 3500 + 230 = 3730$ mm Take $l_{eff} = 3680$ mm $= 3.68$ m (lesser value). **Step 3 — Load calculation (per m²):** Self-weight of slab: $25 \times 0.200 = 5.0$ kN/m² Floor finish: $1.0$ kN/m² Total DL: $6.0$ kN/m² LL: $3.0$ kN/m² Total load: $9.0$ kN/m² Factored load (per 1 m strip): $$w_u = 1.5 \times 9.0 = 13.5 \text{ kN/m}$$ **Step 4 — Design moment and shear:** $$ \begin{aligned} M_u &= \frac{w_u \, l_{eff}^2}{8} = \frac{13.5 \times 3.68^2}{8} = \frac{13.5 \times 13.5424}{8} = 22.85 \text{ kN·m} \\ V_u &= \frac{w_u \, l_{eff}}{2} = \frac{13.5 \times 3.68}{2} = 24.84 \text{ kN} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Check effective depth (for Fe415, $M_u = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$):** $$d_{req} = \sqrt{\frac{M_u \times 10^6}{0.138 \times 20 \times 1000}} = \sqrt{\frac{22.85 \times 10^6}{2760}} = \sqrt{8280} \approx 91 \text{ mm}$$ Provided $d = 180$ mm $\gg 91$ mm → **safe**. **Step 6 — Steel percentage:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{M_u}{b d^2} &= \frac{22.85 \times 10^6}{1000 \times 180^2} = \frac{22.85 \times 10^6}{32.4 \times 10^6} = 0.705 \text{ N/mm}^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} p_t &= \frac{50 \times 20}{415} \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 0.705}{20}} \right] \\ p_t &= 2.4096 \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.1622} \right] \\ p_t &= 2.4096 \left[ 1 - 0.9151 \right] \\ p_t &= 2.4096 \times 0.0849 = 0.2046 \% \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7 — Area of steel required:** $$A_{st,req} = \frac{0.2046 \times 1000 \times 180}{100} = 368.3 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 8 — Min. steel check (Fe415, 0.12 % of gross area):** $$A_{st,min} = \frac{0.12 \times 1000 \times 200}{100} = 240 \text{ mm}^2$$ $A_{st,req} = 368.3 > 240$ → use **368.3 mm²**. **Step 9 — Bar selection:** Try 10 mm dia bars: $a_{bar} = 78.5$ mm² Spacing: $$s = \frac{78.5}{368.3} \times 1000 = 213 \text{ mm}$$ Provide **10 mm dia @ 200 mm c/c** (gives $A_{st} = 78.5 \times 1000/200 = 392.5$ mm² $> 368.3$ ✓). **Step 10 — Spacing check (IS 456 Cl. 26.3.3):** - Max spacing of main bars $= \min(3 \times 180, 300) = \min(540, 300) = 300$ mm. - Provided 200 mm $< 300$ ✓ **Step 11 — Distribution steel:** $A_{sd} = 0.12 \times 1000 \times 200/100 = 240$ mm² Use 8 mm dia bars ($a_{bar} = 50.3$ mm²): $$s_d = \frac{50.3}{240} \times 1000 = 209 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow \text{provide 8 mm @ 200 mm c/c}$$ Max spacing dist. $= \min(5 \times 180, 450) = 450$ mm → 200 mm ✓. **Step 12 — Shear check:** $$\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b d} = \frac{24.84 \times 10^3}{1000 \times 180} = 0.138 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ $p_t$ provided $= 0.3925 \times 100 / (1000 \times 180) = 0.218\%$ From IS 456, Table 19 for M20 and $p_t = 0.218\%$, $\tau_c \approx 0.30$ N/mm². $\tau_v = 0.138 < 0.30$ → **safe**. **Step 13 — Deflection check (Basic L/d):** $$ \begin{aligned} \left(\frac{L}{d}\right)_{provided} &= \frac{3680}{180} = 20.44 \approx 20 \;\; \text{✓ (just within the basic ratio of 20 for SS slab, Fe415)} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Design Summary:** | Component | Provided | |---|---| | Overall depth $D$ | **200 mm** | | Effective depth $d$ | **180 mm** | | Main steel | **10 mm φ @ 200 mm c/c** along short span | | Distribution steel | **8 mm φ @ 200 mm c/c** along long span | | Concrete | M20, Cover: 20 mm | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Curtailment rule:** Provide **alternate bars bent up** at supports to take care of any negative moment and as **top steel at supports** in continuous slabs. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Failure Mode Checks | Check | Formula | IS 456 Reference | Required Condition | |---|---|---|---| | Flexure | $M_u \leq M_{u,lim} = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ (Fe415) | Cl. 38.1, G-1.1 | $M_u < M_{u,lim}$ | | Shear | $\tau_v \leq \tau_c$ | Cl. 40.1, Table 19 | $\tau_v < \tau_c$ | | Deflection (basic) | $L/d \leq (L/d)_{basic}$ | Cl. 23.2.1, Fig. 4 | $L/d$ provided $\leq 20$ | | Cracking | $s_{max}$ from Cl. 43, Fig. 8 | Cl. 43.1 | $s_{provided} < s_{max}$ | | Min. steel | $A_{st} \geq 0.12\% \, bD$ | Cl. 26.5.2.1 | $A_{st,prov} \geq A_{st,min}$ | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid) ### 4.1 One-Way Slab — Plan, Loading & Reinforcement Layout (Mermaid Block) ```mermaid graph TD A["Simply Supported One-Way Slab
Panel Plan ly by lx"] --> B{"ly by lx greater or equal 2"} B -- Yes --> C["One-Way Action Confirmed
Design 1 m wide strip
spanning along lx direction"] B -- No --> Z["Two-Way Slab
Not in this module"] C --> D["Effective Span leff
= min clear span plus d, c/c distance"] D --> E["Loads per m2
Self weight 25D kN/m2
plus Floor Finish
plus Live Load LL"] E --> F["Factored Load wu
= 1.5 times DL plus LL kN per m"] F --> G["Design Moment Mu
= wu leff squared by 8
Design Shear Vu = wu leff by 2"] G --> H["Compute d required
= sqrt Mu by 0.138 fck b"] H --> I["Compute p_t percentage
and Ast required in mm2"] I --> J["Choose bar dia and spacing
10 mm or 12 mm @ given c/c"] J --> K["Check shear, deflection,
cracking, min. steel"] K --> L["Provide distribution steel
along long span direction"] L --> M["Final detailing:
main steel short span
dist. steel long span
cover 20 mm"] ``` ### 4.2 Reinforcement Cross-Section (Conceptual Sketch via Flow Diagram) ```mermaid graph LR S1["Main steel 10 mm @ 200 mm c/c
at bottom, along short span
lever arm z = d minus 0.42 xu"] --> R1["Resists positive moment Mu
at mid-span of simply supported slab"] S2["Distribution steel 8 mm @ 200 mm c/c
at bottom, along long span
perpendicular to main bars"] --> R2["Resists temperature and
shrinkage stresses, distributes
concentrated loads"] S3["Top nominal steel at supports
alternate bars bent up or
separate 8 mm @ 200 mm c/c"] --> R3["Resists any negative moment
and acts as constructional
reinforcement at supports"] S4["Concrete cover 20 mm
clear cover bottom and sides"] --> R4["Durability as per IS 456,
mild exposure condition"] R1 --> OUT["Combined Detailing:
D = 200 mm, d = 180 mm
Main: 10 mm @ 200 c/c
Distribution: 8 mm @ 200 c/c
Top at supports: 8 mm @ 200 c/c"] R2 --> OUT R3 --> OUT R4 --> OUT ``` ### 4.3 Load Path & Bending Behaviour (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD LL1["Live Load kN per m2
from occupancy"] --> M1["Total Load W = DL + LL
per m2 on slab panel"] DL1["Dead Load = 25D + FF
Self-weight + floor finish"] --> M1 M1 --> M2["Factored load wu = 1.5 times W
kN per m on 1 m strip"] M2 --> M3["Bending: span lx direction
Cylindrical bending action
curvature only along short span"] M3 --> M4["Transfer to supports:
Two parallel beams or walls
along long edges"] M4 --> M5["Main steel at bottom
carries tension T = 0.87 fy Ast"] M4 --> M6["Concrete compression C
= 0.36 fck b xu above NA"] M5 --> M7["Equilibrium C = T
x_u = 0.87 fy Ast by 0.36 fck b"] M6 --> M7 M7 --> M8["Moment of resistance
Mu = T times z = T times d minus 0.42 xu"] M8 --> OUT["Safe slab designed
Checks complete"] ``` > [!NOTE] > The Mermaid diagrams above depict the **flow of design logic** and **load path**, since physical reinforcement drawings are best represented in CAD. These block diagrams can be directly copied into a Mermaid Live Editor to render a publication-quality figure. ### 4.4 ASCII Plan-Section Schematic (for Notebook Sketches) ``` SUPPORT (RCC Beam) ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐ ↑ │ ════════════════════════════════════ │ │ ly (long span) │ ─── 10 mm φ @ 200 c/c (main) ──── │ │ ≥ 2 × lx │ │ │ │ ::::: 8 mm φ @ 200 c/c (dist.) :::: │ │ │ │ ↓ └──────────────────────────────────────┘ ↑ ↑ └──────── lx ───────────┘ (short span, design direction) Cross-Section (1 m wide strip): ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐ │ 8 mm φ (dist. steel) │ ← Top │ │ │ ◯──◯──◯──◯──◯──◯──◯──◯──◯──◯ 10 mm │ d = 180 mm │ (main steel, bottom) │ ├─────────────────────────────────────┤ ◯── cover 20 mm ──◢ 200 mm overall depth ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Distinguish between a **one-way slab** and a **two-way slab** based on the IS 456 support ratio criterion. A floor panel has dimensions $4.5 \text{ m} \times 3.0 \text{ m}$. Is it a one-way or two-way slab? Justify. **Model Answer (3 marks):** As per **IS 456:2000, Clause 24.1**: - A **one-way slab** is one designed to bend in only **one direction** when $l_y / l_x \geq 2$. - A **two-way slab** bends in both principal directions when $l_y / l_x < 2$. Here, $l_y = 4.5$ m, $l_x = 3.0$ m. $$l_y / l_x = 4.5 / 3.0 = 1.5 < 2.0$$ Since the ratio is **less than 2**, the slab is a **two-way slab** and must be designed for bending in both directions. **(1 mark for criterion, 1 mark for calculation, 1 mark for conclusion)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the **minimum percentage of distribution steel** to be provided in a one-way slab as per IS 456, when Fe415 is used? What is its purpose? **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Minimum distribution steel** for Fe415 = **0.12 %** of the gross cross-sectional area of the slab. **(1 mark)** - For an overall depth $D$ and width $b = 1000$ mm, $A_{sd} = (0.12/100) \times 1000 \times D = 1.2D$ mm². **(1 mark)** - **Purpose:** To resist **temperature and shrinkage stresses**, to **distribute concentrated loads** across the slab width, and to act as **secondary reinforcement** binding the main bars. **(1 mark)** --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer (14 Marks) with Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified] > Design a **simply supported one-way slab** for the following data, using **M20 concrete** and **Fe415 steel** (Limit State Method): > - Clear span $= 4.0$ m > - Width of support $= 300$ mm > - Live load $= 4.0$ kN/m² > - Floor finish $= 1.5$ kN/m² > - Mild exposure conditions. > > **Sub-parts:** > (a) Compute effective depth, design moment, and area of main steel. **(7 marks)** > (b) Design and detail the distribution steel. Sketch the cross-section. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Sub-part (a) — 7 marks** **1. Preliminary depth:** $$d = L/20 = 4000/20 = 200 \text{ mm}$$ Provide $D = 230$ mm, $d = 210$ mm (with 20 mm cover + 10 mm bar). **(1 mark)** **2. Effective span:** Clear span $+ d = 4000 + 210 = 4210$ mm $c/c$ of support $= 4000 + 300 = 4300$ mm $l_{eff} = 4210$ mm $= 4.21$ m. **(1 mark)** **3. Loads:** - Self-weight $= 25 \times 0.230 = 5.75$ kN/m² - Floor finish $= 1.5$ kN/m² - DL $= 7.25$ kN/m²; LL $= 4.0$ kN/m² - $w = 11.25$ kN/m² - $w_u = 1.5 \times 11.25 = 16.875$ kN/m (per 1 m strip). **(1 mark)** **4. Design moment and shear:** $$M_u = 16.875 \times 4.21^2 / 8 = 16.875 \times 17.7241/8 = 37.38 \text{ kN·m}$$ $$V_u = 16.875 \times 4.21 / 2 = 35.52 \text{ kN}$$ **(1 mark)** **5. Required depth (Fe415):** $$d_{req} = \sqrt{37.38 \times 10^6 / (0.138 \times 20 \times 1000)} = \sqrt{13543} = 116 \text{ mm}$$ Provided 210 mm ≫ 116 mm → **safe.** **(1 mark)** **6. Steel percentage:** $$\frac{M_u}{b d^2} = \frac{37.38 \times 10^6}{1000 \times 210^2} = 0.847 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ $$p_t = \frac{50 \times 20}{415} \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 0.847}{20}} \right]$$ $$p_t = 2.4096 \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.1948} \right] = 2.4096 \times 0.1036 = 0.250\%$$ **(1 mark)** **7. Area of steel:** $$A_{st} = 0.250 \times 1000 \times 210 / 100 = 525 \text{ mm}^2$$ $A_{st,min} = 0.12 \times 1000 \times 230/100 = 276$ mm² → use **525 mm².** **(1 mark)** Provide **12 mm φ @ 200 mm c/c** → $A_{st} = 113.1 \times 1000/200 = 565$ mm² > 525 ✓. **Sub-part (b) — 7 marks** **1. Spacing check (IS 456 Cl. 26.3.3):** - Max. main bar spacing $= \min(3d, 300) = \min(630, 300) = 300$ mm. - Provided 200 mm < 300 mm ✓. **(1 mark)** **2. Shear check:** $$\tau_v = 35.52 \times 10^3 / (1000 \times 210) = 0.169 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ $p_t$ provided $= 565/(1000 \times 210) \times 100 = 0.269\%$ From Table 19, $\tau_c = 0.30$ N/mm². $\tau_v = 0.169 < 0.30$ → **safe.** **(1 mark)** **3. Deflection check:** $L/d$ provided $= 4210/210 = 20.05 \approx 20$ → **just within basic limit.** **(1 mark)** **4. Distribution steel:** $A_{sd} = 0.12 \times 1000 \times 230 / 100 = 276$ mm². Use **8 mm φ** ($a = 50.3$ mm²): $s = 50.3 \times 1000/276 = 182$ mm. Provide **8 mm φ @ 180 mm c/c.** Max spacing $= 5d = 1050$ mm (limited to 450 mm) → 180 mm ✓. **(2 marks)** **5. Sketch (Cross-Section):** ``` ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ 8 mm φ @ 180 c/c (dist. steel, top) │ │ │ D = 230 mm │ 12 mm φ @ 200 c/c (main steel, bot) │ d = 210 mm ├────────────────────────────────────────┤ ◯── 20 mm cover ──◢ bottom ``` **(1 mark)** **6. Curtailment & top steel:** At simple supports, provide **8 mm φ @ 200 c/c top steel** (alternate bars bent up) extending **0.1 l_eff** into the span. **(1 mark)** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks, Alternative)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified] > A continuous one-way slab spans over **three equal spans of 3.5 m each**, simply supported at the ends. Design the slab for the **end span and middle span** using **M20 concrete** and **Fe415 steel**, with a live load of **3.5 kN/m²** and a floor finish of **1.0 kN/m²**. Overall depth is **150 mm**. > > **Sub-parts:** > (a) Compute design moments using IS 456 Table 12 coefficients and design main steel for the end span. **(7 marks)** > (b) Check the slab for shear and deflection; design distribution steel. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (Outline):** **Sub-part (a):** - $D = 150$ mm, $d = 130$ mm, cover 20 mm. - $l_{eff} \approx 3.5 + 0.13 = 3.63$ m. - $w = 25 \times 0.15 + 1.0 + 3.5 = 8.25$ kN/m². - $w_u = 1.5 \times 8.25 = 12.375$ kN/m. - $M_u = 12.375 \times 3.63^2 / 8 = 20.39$ kN·m. - For end span mid-moment: $M_u^+ = +0.080 \times w_u l_x^2 = 0.080 \times 12.375 \times 3.63^2 = 13.05$ kN·m. - Negative moment at first interior support: $M_u^- = -0.040 \times w_u l_x^2 = -6.52$ kN·m (use IS 456 Table 12 coefficients). - Use Fe415: $p_t$ and $A_{st}$ derived per Section 2 formulas → provide 10 mm @ 200 c/c at midspan, 8 mm @ 200 c/c at top of support. **Sub-part (b):** - Shear check: $\tau_v$ at first interior support face ≈ 0.165 N/mm² < $\tau_c$ (0.30) → safe. - $L/d = 3630/130 = 27.9$. For continuous slab with Fe415, **modified basic L/d = 26 × 0.91 (tension steel reduction) = 23.7** → **L/d exceeds 23.7** → deflection check requires modification factor (IS 456 Fig. 4) and may need to increase $D$. - Distribution steel: $A_{sd} = 0.12 \times 1000 \times 150/100 = 180$ mm² → 8 mm @ 270 c/c → provide **8 mm @ 250 c/c.** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (One-Way Slab):** > 1. **Wrong span ratio check** — students often use $l_x/l_y$ (inverted). Always $l_y/l_x \geq 2$. > 2. **Forgetting 1.5 load factor** — IS 456 Cl. 36.4.1 mandates $1.5(DL + LL)$. > 3. **Not checking $A_{st,min}$** — For Fe415 it is 0.12 % of **gross** area $bD$, NOT $bd$. > 4. **Effective span** — many students pick the **c/c distance** blindly, but IS 456 says "**lesser of clear span + d, or c/c**." > 5. **Spacings** — for main bars $\min(3d, 300)$, for distribution $\min(5d, 450)$. Confusing these is a 1-mark loss. > 6. **Curtailment of bars** — not detailing top steel at supports loses 1 mark in detailing. > 7. **Deflection check** — using **gross $L/d$** without modification factors is a partial-mark trap. > 8. **Unit conversion** — kN·m to N·mm when plugging into $M_u = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$. Multiply by $10^6$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Slab classification** is governed by the ratio $l_y/l_x$, with the **cutoff at 2.0** (IS 456, Cl. 24.1). - **One-way slab** is designed as a **1 m wide rectangular beam** strip spanning the short direction. - **Partial safety factor** for loads is **1.5** for both DL and LL (no separate factor in LSD). - **Partial safety factor** for material: $1.5$ for concrete, $1.15$ for steel — folded into the **0.36, 0.67, 0.87** constants. - **Effective depth check** uses $M_u = 0.138 f_{ck} b d^2$ for Fe415 (singly reinforced, balanced). - **Steel percentage** $p_t$ is read directly from the formula — not from old SP-16 charts (KTU prefers formula approach). - **Minimum steel** for Fe415 slabs = **0.12 % of gross area** (Cl. 26.5.2.1). - **Maximum spacing of main bars** $= \min(3d, 300)$ mm. - **Maximum spacing of distribution bars** $= \min(5d, 450)$ mm. - **Curtailment** of 50 % main bars allowed at $0.1 l_{eff}$ from support face in simple spans. - **Deflection control** — basic $L/d$ for simply supported slab = 20 (Fe415). For continuous, it is 26. - **Shear** in slabs is rarely critical due to large width; still verify with $\tau_v$ vs $\tau_c$ from Table 19. - **Floor finish** must be included in dead load — a common KTU trap. - **Cracking check** (Cl. 43) governs the maximum bar spacing in tension zones; for Fe415 with $f_s \approx 200$ N/mm², $s_{max} \approx 300$ mm. - **Always provide nominal top steel** at supports (1/2 bars bent up or separate 8 mm @ 200 c/c). - **Unit weight constants to memorize:** PCC = 24, RCC = 25, screed = 22 kN/m³. - **IS 456:2000** is the **only** code reference to cite in KTU answers — no other textbook, no fictitious references. - The **master flexure equation** $M_u = 0.87 f_y A_{st} d (1 - A_{st} f_y / b d f_{ck})$ is **the most tested equation** in KTU 2024 scheme.

topics.title: Numerical Problems: Design of Two-way Slabs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET404 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems: Design of Two-way Slabs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Two-Way Slab** is a reinforced concrete slab panel in which the ratio of the longer span ($l_y$) to the shorter span ($l_x$) is less than or equal to **2.0**. Under gravity loading, the slab bends in a doubly-curved (saddle-shaped) deflected surface, and the load is transferred to the supporting beams/walls in **both orthogonal directions**. The bending moments, deflections, and reinforcement requirements must therefore be computed in both the short-span and long-span directions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (IS 456:2000, Cl. 24.1):**
> A slab panel shall be classified as a *two-way slab* when the ratio $\frac{l_y}{l_x} \le 2.0$. Beyond this, the slab behaves predominantly as a *one-way slab* even though some transverse bending occurs.

### 1.2 Physical Constants & Material Grades (Default KTU Board Values)

| Parameter | Standard Value Used in Numerical Problems |
|---|---|
| Grade of Concrete | **M20** ($f_{ck} = 20 \text{ N/mm}^2$) |
| Grade of Steel | **Fe415** ($f_y = 415 \text{ N/mm}^2$) |
| Unit weight of RCC | **25 kN/m³** |
| Live Load (typical floor) | **3 to 5 kN/m²** |
| Floor Finish | **1 to 1.5 kN/m²** |
| Clear cover (slab) | **20 mm** (for mild exposure) |
| Nominal max aggregate | **20 mm** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **Analogy — The "Floating Bookshelf" Model:** Imagine a thin, flat wooden plank resting on four short pillars (one at each corner). If you place a heavy book in the middle, the plank sags **downward in BOTH directions** — both towards the nearest short edge AND towards the nearest long edge. The fibres near the short-span centre experience greater curvature (and hence larger bending moment) than those along the long-span centre. This *doubly-curved sagging* is exactly how a two-way slab behaves. The steel reinforcement must therefore be provided in **two perpendicular meshes** — the heavier mesh along the shorter span (primary load-carrying path) and a lighter mesh along the longer span (secondary path).

### 1.4 When to Use Two-Way Slab Design (KTU Board Tip)

> [!NOTE]
> The **IS 456 Coefficient Method** (Cl. 24.4, Table 12) is the **mandatory** KTU examination approach for simply supported, restrained, or continuous two-way slab panels. Yield-Line theory and Direct Design Method are NOT part of the Module 2 syllabus. Students must master Table 12 coefficients and the $M_x = \alpha_x \cdot w \cdot l_x^2$ formulation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Design Logic (The "Why" Behind Each Step)

**Step 1 — Panel Identification & Classification**
- Compute the span ratio $r = l_y / l_x$.
- If $r \le 2.0 \rightarrow$ **Two-way slab** (use IS 456 Table 12).
- Identify the **boundary condition** of EACH edge: simply supported / continuous / discontinuous. This dictates the coefficients $\alpha_x$ and $\alpha_y$ from Table 12.

**Step 2 — Effective Span (IS 456 Cl. 22.2)**
- For simply supported / continuous slabs: $l_{eff} = \text{clear span} + d$ (where $d$ = effective depth) **OR** centre-to-centre of supports, **whichever is LESS**.

**Step 3 — Load Calculation**
- Dead load of slab = $0.025 \cdot D \text{ kN/m}^2$ (where $D$ = overall depth in metres)
- Dead load of floor finish = (as given) kN/m²
- Live load = (as given) kN/m²
- Total factored load: $w = 1.5 \cdot (DL + LL) \text{ kN/m}^2$

**Step 4 — Bending Moment Coefficients (Table 12, IS 456)**
- $M_x = \alpha_x \cdot w \cdot l_x^2$
- $M_y = \alpha_y \cdot w \cdot l_x^2$
- (Note: coefficient uses **shorter span $l_x$** in both equations)

**Step 5 — Check for Effective Depth (Deflection Control, IS 456 Cl. 23.2)**
- Basic $l/d$ ratio for simply supported = **20**, for continuous = **26**.
- Modify with reinforcement percentage $p_t$ and reference steel $f_s = 0.58 \cdot f_y$.

**Step 6 — Design Main Steel (Short Span)**
- $A_{st,x} = \frac{0.5 \cdot f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \cdot M_{u,x}}{f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2}} \right] \cdot b \cdot d$

**Step 7 — Design Distribution Steel (Long Span)**
- $A_{st,y} = \frac{0.5 \cdot f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \cdot M_{u,y}}{f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2}} \right] \cdot b \cdot d$

**Step 8 — Check for Shear**
- Nominal shear stress $\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b \cdot d}$
- Compare with $\tau_{c,\max}$ from IS 456 Table 20 for $p_t$ and grade of concrete.

**Step 9 — Detailing Rules (IS 456 Cl. 26)**
- Spacing: minimum of $3d$ or $300$ mm.
- Maximum diameter at midspan: not greater than $1/8$ of slab thickness.
- L-bends at discontinuous edges: $0.5 \cdot A_{st}$ must extend into the support.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Span Ratio | $r = l_y / l_x$ | — |
| 2 | Effective Span | $l_{eff} = \min(\text{clr span} + d, \text{c/c supports})$ | m |
| 3 | Dead Load | $DL = 0.025 \cdot D$ | kN/m² |
| 4 | Factored Load | $w = 1.5(DL + LL)$ | kN/m² |
| 5 | Short-Span BM | $M_{u,x} = \alpha_x \cdot w \cdot l_x^2$ | kN·m |
| 6 | Long-Span BM | $M_{u,y} = \alpha_y \cdot w \cdot l_x^2$ | kN·m |
| 7 | $M_{u,\lim}$ (M20, Fe415) | $0.138 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2 = 2.76 \cdot b \cdot d^2$ | N·mm |
| 8 | Steel Area | $A_{st} = \frac{0.5 f_{ck}}{f_y}\left[1-\sqrt{1-\frac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^2}}\right] b d$ | mm² |
| 9 | Minimum Steel | $A_{st,\min} = \frac{0.12}{100} \cdot b \cdot D$ | mm² |
| 10 | Max Steel | $A_{st,\max} = 0.04 \cdot b \cdot D$ | mm² |
| 11 | Nominal Shear | $\tau_v = V_u / (b \cdot d)$ | N/mm² |
| 12 | Basic $l/d$ | **20** (SS), **26** (continuous) | — |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:** Two-way slabs are the workhorse of modern floor systems in multi-storey buildings, parking decks, and commercial complexes. The IS 456 coefficient method provides rapid, conservative designs for panels with **uniformly distributed loads** and **regular geometry** — typically the case in residential and office floors. The coefficients themselves are derived from **yield-line analysis** of plastic collapse mechanisms, ensuring the method balances simplicity with theoretical accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Example

### 3.1 Complete KTU-Style Numerical Problem

> **PROBLEM STATEMENT:**
> Design a two-way restrained slab for the following data, using IS 456 coefficient method. Panel size: $3.5 \text{ m} \times 5.0 \text{ m}$ (clear). Slab is continuous over all four edges. Live Load = $4 \text{ kN/m}^2$. Floor Finish = $1.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$. Use M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. Assume mild exposure.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Classification**

$$
r = \frac{l_y}{l_x} = \frac{5.0}{3.5} = 1.43 \le 2.0
$$

$\therefore$ It is a **Two-Way Restrained Slab** (continuous on all four edges).

---

**Step 2 — Effective Span (IS 456 Cl. 22.2)**

Assume overall depth $D = 150$ mm and effective cover = 20 mm clear + 8 mm (half bar dia for 12 mm bar) = 28 mm. So $d = 150 - 28 = 122$ mm ≈ 120 mm (rounded for trial).

> **Reasoning:** We need to *assume* a depth initially to compute $l_{eff}$, then verify it against deflection limits in Step 5. This is a standard KTU trial-and-error approach.

$$
l_{x,\text{clr}} = 3.5 \text{ m}, \quad l_{y,\text{clr}} = 5.0 \text{ m}
$$

$$
l_{x,\text{eff}} = \min(3.5 + 0.12, \text{c/c supports}) \approx 3.62 \text{ m} \quad (\text{assume c/c } \approx 3.62 \text{ m})
$$

$$
l_{x,\text{eff}} = 3.62 \text{ m}, \quad l_{y,\text{eff}} = 5.12 \text{ m}
$$

Refined ratio: $r = 5.12 / 3.62 = 1.41$

---

**Step 3 — Load Calculation**

$$
\text{Dead load of slab} = 0.025 \times 0.150 = 0.00375 \text{ kN/mm}^2 \times 1000 = 3.75 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

Wait — let me redo the units properly. With $D = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$:

$$
DL_{slab} = 25 \times 0.15 = 3.75 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Floor finish} = 1.50 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Total Dead Load} = 3.75 + 1.50 = 5.25 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Live Load} = 4.00 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Total Working Load} = 5.25 + 4.00 = 9.25 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Factored Load}, \quad w_u = 1.5 \times 9.25 = 13.875 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

---

**Step 4 — Bending Moment Coefficients (IS 456 Table 12, Case 5: Continuous on all four edges)**

For $r = 1.41$ (interpolating between $r = 1.4$ and $r = 1.5$):

| $r$ | $\alpha_x$ | $\alpha_y$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1.4 | 0.049 | 0.037 |
| 1.5 | 0.050 | 0.035 |
| **1.41** | **0.0491** | **0.0368** |

> **Logic:** IS 456 Table 12 lists discrete values of $r$ from 1.0 to 2.0. KTU expects linear interpolation when $r$ lies between tabulated values. This is a **very common mark-fetching step**.

$$
M_{u,x} = \alpha_x \cdot w_u \cdot l_x^2 = 0.0491 \times 13.875 \times (3.62)^2
$$

$$
M_{u,x} = 0.0491 \times 13.875 \times 13.1044 = 8.92 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

$$
M_{u,y} = \alpha_y \cdot w_u \cdot l_x^2 = 0.0368 \times 13.875 \times 13.1044
$$

$$
M_{u,y} = 6.69 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

---

**Step 5 — Check for Effective Depth (Deflection Control, IS 456 Cl. 23.2.1)**

For continuous slab, basic $l/d = 26$. Modification factor for $p_t$ (assume 0.5%) and Fe415 ≈ 1.0 (no modification needed for low $p_t$).

$$
d_{\text{req}} = \frac{l_x}{26} = \frac{3620}{26} = 139.2 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $d = 140$ mm, overall $D = 140 + 28 = 168$ mm → take $D = 170$ mm. Provide clear cover 20 mm + bar dia/2 ≈ 30 mm.

> **Recalculate $l_{eff}$ with revised d = 140 mm:**

$$
l_{x,\text{eff}} = 3.5 + 0.14 = 3.64 \text{ m}; \quad l_{y,\text{eff}} = 5.0 + 0.14 = 5.14 \text{ m}
$$

$$
r = 5.14 / 3.64 = 1.412
$$

Re-iterpolate coefficients (negligible change): $\alpha_x = 0.0491$, $\alpha_y = 0.0368$.

**Revised Moments:**

$$
M_{u,x} = 0.0491 \times 13.875 \times (3.64)^2 = 0.0491 \times 13.875 \times 13.2496 = 9.02 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

$$
M_{u,y} = 0.0368 \times 13.875 \times 13.2496 = 6.77 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

---

**Step 6 — Check Limiting Moment of Resistance (M20, Fe415)**

For Fe415, $x_{u,\max}/d = 0.48$. For M20:

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 0.36 \cdot \frac{x_{u,\max}}{d} \left(1 - 0.42 \frac{x_{u,\max}}{d}\right) f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot d^2
$$

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 0.36 \times 0.48 \times (1 - 0.42 \times 0.48) \times 20 \times 1000 \times 140^2
$$

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 0.1728 \times 0.7984 \times 20 \times 1000 \times 19600
$$

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 1000 \times 19600 = 54.10 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm} = 54.10 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

Since $M_{u,x} = 9.02 \text{ kN·m} \ll 54.10 \text{ kN·m}$, the section is **under-reinforced**. ✅

---

**Step 7 — Design of Main Steel (Short Span, $b = 1000$ mm, $d = 140$ mm)**

For $M_{u,x} = 9.02 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$:

$$
A_{st,x} = \frac{0.5 \cdot 20}{415}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 9.02 \times 10^6}{20 \times 1000 \times 140^2}}\right] \times 1000 \times 140
$$

$$
= 0.0241 \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{41.49 \times 10^6}{392 \times 10^6}}\right] \times 140000
$$

$$
= 0.0241 \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.1058}\right] \times 140000
$$

$$
= 0.0241 \left[1 - \sqrt{0.8942}\right] \times 140000
$$

$$
= 0.0241 \left[1 - 0.9457\right] \times 140000
$$

$$
= 0.0241 \times 0.0543 \times 140000
$$

$$
= 0.001308 \times 140000 = 183.1 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

> **Check minimum steel (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.1):**
> $A_{st,\min} = \frac{0.12}{100} \times 1000 \times 170 = 204 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$

Since $183.1 < 204$, **govern $A_{st,x} = 204 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$**.

**Provide 10 mm $\phi$ bars @ 200 mm c/c:**

$$
A_{st,\text{provided}} = \frac{1000}{200} \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 10^2 = 5 \times 78.54 = 392.7 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m} \quad \gg 204 \quad ✅
$$

---

**Step 8 — Design of Distribution Steel (Long Span)**

For $M_{u,y} = 6.77 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$:

$$
A_{st,y} = 0.0241 \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 6.77 \times 10^6}{20 \times 1000 \times 140^2}}\right] \times 140000
$$

$$
= 0.0241 \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{31.14 \times 10^6}{392 \times 10^6}}\right] \times 140000
$$

$$
= 0.0241 \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.0794}\right] \times 140000
$$

$$
= 0.0241 \left[1 - 0.9595\right] \times 140000
$$

$$
= 0.0241 \times 0.0405 \times 140000 = 136.6 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

Govern by $A_{st,\min} = 204 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$.

**Provide 8 mm $\phi$ bars @ 200 mm c/c:**

$$
A_{st,\text{provided}} = \frac{1000}{200} \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 8^2 = 5 \times 50.27 = 251.3 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m} \quad > 204 \quad ✅
$$

---

**Step 9 — Check for Shear**

Maximum shear force at the face of support for a fully loaded panel (using IS 456 Cl. 24.4.2):

$$
V_u = w_u \cdot \frac{l_x}{2} = 13.875 \times \frac{3.64}{2} = 25.25 \text{ kN/m} = 25250 \text{ N/m}
$$

$$
\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b \cdot d} = \frac{25250}{1000 \times 140} = 0.18 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

For $p_t = \frac{100 \times 392.7}{1000 \times 140} = 0.28\%$, M20 concrete: $\tau_c = 0.36 \text{ N/mm}^2$ (IS 456 Table 19).

Since $\tau_v = 0.18 < \tau_c = 0.36$ — **shear is safe** (depth governed by flexure). ✅

---

**Step 10 — Detailing Summary**

| Layer | Bar Diameter | Spacing | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bottom (main) | 10 mm $\phi$ | 200 mm c/c | Along $l_x$ (short span) |
| Bottom (distribution) | 8 mm $\phi$ | 200 mm c/c | Along $l_y$ (long span) |
| Top (negative) at supports | 10 mm $\phi$ | 200 mm c/c | Cranked up at $l_x/4$ |
| Cover | 20 mm | — | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Design Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Panel Size and Loads] --> B{Compute ly/lx Ratio}
    B -->|Ratio less than or equal 2.0| C[Classify as Two-Way Slab]
    B -->|Ratio greater than 2.0| Z[Classify as One-Way Slab - Use Cl 24.1 IS456]
    C --> D[Identify Boundary Type: SS, Continuous, Discontinuous]
    D --> E[Assume Effective Depth d and Compute Effective Span]
    E --> F[Calculate Dead Load, Live Load, Factored Load wu]
    F --> G[Look up Alpha-x and Alpha-y from IS456 Table 12]
    G --> H[Compute Mux and Muy using wu and lx squared]
    H --> I[Check Deflection: d greater than lx by 26 for Continuous]
    I --> J{Depth Adequate?}
    J -->|No| E
    J -->|Yes| K[Compute Ast-x and Ast-y using Limiting Moment Formula]
    K --> L[Check Ast-min IS456 Cl 26.5.1.1]
    L --> M[Check Shear: tau-v less than tau-c Table 19]
    M --> N{Shear Safe?}
    N -->|No| O[Increase Depth or Use Shear Links]
    N -->|Yes| P[Provide Detailing: Main, Distribution, Cranked Bars]
    P --> Q[Final Design Complete]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Slab Reinforcement Layout (Plan View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Lx_Direction [Short Span Lx - Main Steel - 10mm phi at 200 c/c]
        S1[Bar 1] --> S2[Bar 2] --> S3[Bar 3] --> S4[Bar N]
    end
    subgraph Ly_Direction [Long Span Ly - Distribution Steel - 8mm phi at 200 c/c]
        D1[Bar 1] --> D2[Bar 2] --> D3[Bar 3] --> D4[Bar N]
    end
    subgraph Support_Top [Negative Moment Steel at Continuous Supports]
        N1[Cranked Bar at lx by 4] --> N2[Cranked Bar at lx by 4] --> N3[Cranked Bar at lx by 4]
    end
    Lx_Direction --- Ly_Direction
    Lx_Direction --- Support_Top
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Load Path Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Load[Total Factored Load wu kN per m2] --> Split{Bends in Both Directions}
    Split --> Path1[Short Span Path - Higher Curvature - More Steel]
    Split --> Path2[Long Span Path - Lower Curvature - Less Steel]
    Path1 --> Beam1[Support Beam 1 and Beam 2 - Short Edges]
    Path2 --> Beam3[Support Beam 3 and Beam 4 - Long Edges]
    Beam1 --> Column[Columns]
    Beam3 --> Column
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*State the IS 456:2000 criteria for classifying a slab panel as a two-way slab. A slab panel has clear dimensions $3 \text{ m} \times 4.5 \text{ m}$. State with reason whether it is a one-way or two-way slab.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
As per **IS 456:2000, Cl. 24.1**, a slab panel is classified as a two-way slab when the ratio of longer span to shorter span is $\le 2.0$.

For the given panel: $l_y / l_x = 4.5 / 3.0 = 1.5 \le 2.0$.

$\therefore$ The slab is a **two-way slab** and must be designed for bending in both directions. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*For a two-way slab continuous on all four edges, write the expressions for short-span and long-span design moments. What is the value of $\alpha_x$ and $\alpha_y$ for $l_y / l_x = 1.5$ (IS 456 Table 12)?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Design moments are computed using IS 456 Table 12 coefficients:

$$
M_{u,x} = \alpha_x \cdot w_u \cdot l_x^2 \quad ; \quad M_{u,y} = \alpha_y \cdot w_u \cdot l_x^2
$$

For a slab continuous on all four edges with $r = l_y / l_x = 1.5$:
$\alpha_x = 0.050$ and $\alpha_y = 0.035$ **(as per Table 12, Case 5)**. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**A.** Design a two-way slab for a room of clear dimensions $3.0 \text{ m} \times 4.0 \text{ m}$. The slab is **simply supported on all four edges** with adequate provision to resist torsion at corners. Take Live Load = $3 \text{ kN/m}^2$ and Floor Finish = $1.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$. Use M20 concrete and Fe415 steel. Adopt effective cover = 30 mm. Check for deflection, design main and distribution steel, and provide a neat sketch of reinforcement.

*Sub-part (a) — Design Loads, BM, and Depth Check (7 marks)*

*Sub-part (b) — Reinforcement Design, Shear Check, and Detailing (7 marks)*

---

#### Sub-part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1: Classification**

$$
r = \frac{l_y}{l_x} = \frac{4.0}{3.0} = 1.33 \le 2.0 \quad \text{(Two-way slab)}
$$

**Step 2: Effective Span (assume $D = 130$ mm, $d = 100$ mm)**

$$
l_{x,\text{eff}} = 3.0 + 0.10 = 3.10 \text{ m}; \quad l_{y,\text{eff}} = 4.0 + 0.10 = 4.10 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Loads**

$$
DL = 25 \times 0.13 + 1.0 = 3.25 + 1.0 = 4.25 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
LL = 3.0 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
w_u = 1.5 \times (4.25 + 3.0) = 1.5 \times 7.25 = 10.875 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Bending Moments (Table 12, Case 1: Simply supported on all four edges)**

For $r = 1.33$ (interpolate between $r = 1.3$ and $1.4$):

| $r$ | $\alpha_x$ | $\alpha_y$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1.3 | 0.087 | 0.066 |
| 1.4 | 0.084 | 0.062 |
| **1.33** | **0.0861** | **0.0648** |

$$
M_{u,x} = 0.0861 \times 10.875 \times (3.10)^2 = 0.0861 \times 10.875 \times 9.61 = 9.00 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

$$
M_{u,y} = 0.0648 \times 10.875 \times 9.61 = 6.77 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

**Step 5: Deflection Check (IS 456 Cl. 23.2.1, simply supported, basic $l/d = 20$)**

$$
d_{\text{req}} = \frac{3100}{20} = 155 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $D = 180$ mm, $d = 150$ mm. Recalculate: $l_{x,\text{eff}} = 3.15$ m, $l_{y,\text{eff}} = 4.15$ m.

$$
DL_{\text{new}} = 25 \times 0.18 + 1.0 = 5.50 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
w_{u,\text{new}} = 1.5 \times (5.5 + 3.0) = 12.75 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
M_{u,x,\text{new}} = 0.0861 \times 12.75 \times (3.15)^2 = 0.0861 \times 12.75 \times 9.9225 = 10.89 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

$$
M_{u,y,\text{new}} = 0.0648 \times 12.75 \times 9.9225 = 8.20 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

#### Sub-part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 6: Check Limiting Moment ($D = 180$, $d = 150$, M20, Fe415)**

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 1000 \times 150^2 = 62.1 \text{ kN·m/m} \gg M_{u,x} = 10.89 \text{ kN·m/m} \quad ✅
$$

**Step 7: Main Steel (Short Span)**

$$
A_{st,x} = 0.0241 \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 10.89 \times 10^6}{20 \times 1000 \times 150^2}}\right] \times 150000
$$

$$
= 0.0241 \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.1113}\right] \times 150000 = 0.0241 \times 0.0570 \times 150000 = 206 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

**Check $A_{st,\min}$:**

$$
A_{st,\min} = \frac{0.12}{100} \times 1000 \times 180 = 216 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

**Govern: $A_{st,x} = 216 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$. Provide 8 mm $\phi$ @ 230 mm c/c** (or 10 mm $\phi$ @ 300 mm c/c giving 262 mm²/m) — adopt **10 mm $\phi$ @ 300 mm c/c**. ✅

**Step 8: Distribution Steel**

$$
A_{st,y} = 0.0241 \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 8.20 \times 10^6}{20 \times 1000 \times 150^2}}\right] \times 150000 = 154 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

Govern by $A_{st,\min} = 216 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$. **Provide 8 mm $\phi$ @ 230 mm c/c** (gives 218 mm²/m). ✅

**Step 9: Shear Check**

$$
V_u = w_u \times l_x / 2 = 12.75 \times 3.15 / 2 = 20.08 \text{ kN/m}
$$

$$
\tau_v = \frac{20080}{1000 \times 150} = 0.134 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

For $p_t \approx 0.174\%$ and M20: $\tau_c = 0.30$ N/mm². Since $0.134 < 0.30$ — **shear is safe.** ✅

**Step 10: Detailing Sketch (described in words)**
- Bottom mesh: 10 mm $\phi$ @ 300 mm c/c (short span, main) with 8 mm $\phi$ @ 230 mm c/c (long span, distribution) tied together.
- Torsion reinforcement at corners: 8 mm $\phi$ @ 200 mm c/c for a length of $l_x/4$ in both directions (per IS 456 Cl. D-1.8).
- L-bends at all four edges.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to re-iterate $l_{eff}$ after depth revision** — once $d$ increases, $l_{eff}$ changes, $r$ changes, and coefficients may need re-interpolation. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if this iterative step is skipped.
> 2. **Not checking $A_{st,\min}$** — for thin slabs (small $M_u$), governing steel is always $A_{st,\min} = 0.12\% \cdot b \cdot D$. This is the single most common oversight.
> 3. **Using $l_y$ in the BM formula** — IS 456 specifies $l_x$ (the **shorter** span) in both $M_x$ and $M_y$ expressions. Students incorrectly substitute $l_y$, leading to wrong moments.
> 4. **Omitting torsion steel at corners** for simply-supported slabs — IS 456 (Annex D) requires special corner reinforcement to prevent diagonal cracking. Often asked as a 2-mark follow-up.
> 5. **Confusing 0.138 with 0.148** in $M_{u,\lim}$ formula — the 0.138 factor is specific to Fe415 ($x_{u,\max}/d = 0.48$). For Fe500 it becomes 0.133.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**B.** Design a two-way slab for a commercial floor panel of clear dimensions $3.5 \text{ m} \times 5.0 \text{ m}$. The slab is **continuous on two adjacent edges and discontinuous on the other two**. Live Load = $5 \text{ kN/m}^2$, Floor Finish = $1.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$. Use M25 concrete and Fe500 steel. Assume clear cover = 20 mm and effective cover = 30 mm.

*Sub-part (a) — Compute design moments (7 marks)*

*Sub-part (b) — Design reinforcement for both spans and check shear (7 marks)*

---

#### Sub-part (a) — Model Solution

**Step 1: Classification**

$$
r = \frac{l_y}{l_x} = \frac{5.0}{3.5} = 1.43 \le 2.0
$$

**Step 2: Table 12, Case 3 — Two adjacent edges continuous, two adjacent edges discontinuous**

For $r = 1.43$ (interpolate between 1.4 and 1.5):

| $r$ | $\alpha_x$ | $\alpha_y$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1.4 | 0.075 | 0.052 |
| 1.5 | 0.072 | 0.049 |
| **1.43** | **0.0741** | **0.0511** |

**Step 3: Trial Depth**

Assume $D = 175$ mm, $d = 145$ mm. Refine later if needed.

**Step 4: Effective Span**

$$
l_{x,\text{eff}} = 3.5 + 0.145 = 3.645 \text{ m}; \quad l_{y,\text{eff}} = 5.0 + 0.145 = 5.145 \text{ m}
$$

Refined $r = 5.145 / 3.645 = 1.41 \Rightarrow \alpha_x = 0.0742, \alpha_y = 0.0513$.

**Step 5: Loads**

$$
DL = 25 \times 0.175 + 1.5 = 4.375 + 1.5 = 5.875 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
LL = 5.0 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
w_u = 1.5 \times (5.875 + 5.0) = 1.5 \times 10.875 = 16.31 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 6: Design Moments**

$$
M_{u,x} = 0.0742 \times 16.31 \times (3.645)^2 = 0.0742 \times 16.31 \times 13.286 = 16.08 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

$$
M_{u,y} = 0.0513 \times 16.31 \times 13.286 = 11.11 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

#### Sub-part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 7: Deflection Check (continuous on two edges — use 26 with $l_x$)**

$$
d_{\text{req}} = \frac{3645}{26} = 140.2 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopted $d = 145$ mm $\ge 140$ mm — **SAFE.** ✅

**Step 8: Limiting Moment (M25, Fe500, $x_{u,\max}/d = 0.46$)**

$$
M_{u,\lim} = 0.36 \times 0.46 \times (1 - 0.42 \times 0.46) \times 25 \times 1000 \times 145^2
$$

$$
= 0.1656 \times 0.8068 \times 25 \times 1000 \times 21025 = 70.24 \text{ kN·m/m} \gg 16.08 \quad ✅
$$

**Step 9: Main Steel (Short Span)**

$$
A_{st,x} = \frac{0.5 \times 25}{500} \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 16.08 \times 10^6}{25 \times 1000 \times 145^2}}\right] \times 1000 \times 145
$$

$$
= 0.025 \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.1407}\right] \times 145000
$$

$$
= 0.025 \times 0.0733 \times 145000 = 265.7 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

$$
A_{st,\min} = \frac{0.12}{100} \times 1000 \times 175 = 210 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

Govern: $A_{st,x} = 265.7 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$. **Provide 10 mm $\phi$ @ 290 mm c/c** (271 mm²/m). ✅

**Step 10: Distribution Steel (Long Span)**

$$
A_{st,y} = 0.025 \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 11.11 \times 10^6}{25 \times 1000 \times 145^2}}\right] \times 145000
$$

$$
= 0.025 \left[1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.0972}\right] \times 145000 = 0.025 \times 0.0499 \times 145000 = 180.9 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

Govern by $A_{st,\min} = 210 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}$. **Provide 8 mm $\phi$ @ 220 mm c/c** (228 mm²/m). ✅

**Step 11: Shear Check**

$$
V_u = w_u \times l_x / 2 = 16.31 \times 3.645 / 2 = 29.72 \text{ kN/m} = 29720 \text{ N/m}
$$

$$
\tau_v = \frac{29720}{1000 \times 145} = 0.205 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

For $p_t = \frac{100 \times 271}{1000 \times 145} = 0.187\%$ and M25: $\tau_c = 0.42$ N/mm². Since $0.205 < 0.42$ — **shear is safe.** ✅

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (Consolidated)

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 KTU Board Pitfalls — Two-Way Slab Design:**
> 1. **Forgetting to iterate** $l_{eff}$ after revising depth $d$ — Causes wrong moments in 30% of answer scripts.
> 2. **Using $l_y$ instead of $l_x$** in $M_x = \alpha_x \cdot w \cdot l_x^2$ — IS 456 ALWAYS uses shorter span $l_x$.
> 3. **Ignoring IS 456 Table 12 Case numbering** — Students often quote wrong coefficients for the boundary condition.
> 4. **Not verifying $A_{st,\min}$** — Critical for low-loaded panels; minimum 0.12% of gross cross-section.
> 5. **Omitting torsion steel at corners** for simply supported slabs (per IS 456 Annex D).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Classification:** $l_y / l_x \le 2.0 \rightarrow$ Two-way slab.
- ✅ **Effective Span:** $l_{eff} = $ clear span $+ d$ **OR** c/c supports, **whichever is LESS** (IS 456 Cl. 22.2).
- ✅ **Bending Moments:** $M_{u,x} = \alpha_x \cdot w_u \cdot l_x^2$ and $M_{u,y} = \alpha_y \cdot w_u \cdot l_x^2$ — coefficients from **Table 12** (8 cases based on boundary conditions).
- ✅ **Load Factor:** **1.5** for DL + LL (IS 456 Cl. 18.2.1).
- ✅ **Deflection Limits:** Basic $l/d = 20$ (simply supported), **26** (continuous), **7** (cantilever).
- ✅ **Limiting Moment:** **0.138** $f_{ck} b d^2$ for Fe415; **0.133** $f_{ck} b d^2$ for Fe500.
- ✅ **Minimum Steel:** **$A_{st,\min} = 0.12\% \cdot b \cdot D$** (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.1).
- ✅ **Maximum Steel:** $A_{st,\max} = 4\% \cdot b \cdot D$.
- ✅ **Spacing Rules:** $\le 3d$ **or** $300$ mm (whichever is less).
- ✅ **Bar Diameter at Midspan:** $\le D/8$.
- ✅ **Corner Torsion Steel:** Required for slabs with $l_y / l_x \le 2.0$ and simply supported on both edges meeting at corner — mesh extending $l_x / 4$ in both directions.
- ✅ **Curtailment:** Negative moment steel at continuous supports should extend **$l_x/4$** from the face of the support.
- ✅ **L-bends:** $0.5 \cdot A_{st}$ at bottom must continue into the support (IS 456 Cl. 26.3.3-b-1).
- ✅ **Default Materials for KTU Numerical Problems:** **M20** + **Fe415** (unless explicitly stated).
- ✅ **Always verify** $\tau_v \le \tau_{c,\max}$ (IS 456 Table 20) — if exceeded, increase depth.
- ✅ **Sketch in exam** must show: (1) plan with bar layout in both directions, (2) cross-section through short span with cover, (3) curtailment/cranking details.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCET404 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Numerical Problems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Numerical Problems in Design of Concrete Structures (Module 2)** refer to the application of **Limit State Method (LSM)** of design as per **IS 456: 2000** to determine the cross-sectional dimensions and reinforcement details of flexural members—primarily **singly reinforced rectangular beams**, **doubly reinforced rectangular beams**, and **T-beams (flanged beams)**—subjected to bending moments and shear forces. The numerical problems test the student's ability to: - Classify a given beam as singly, doubly, or flanged reinforced. - Compute the **limiting moment of resistance** ($M_{u,lim}$) and compare it with the applied factored moment ($M_u$). - Design the tension and compression steel areas ($A_{st}$ and $A_{sc}$). - Verify the section for deflection and crack control. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 Focus:** Numerical problems in flexural design using **Limit State of Collapse (Flexure)** as per **IS 456: 2000** clauses 38 and 39, with materials **M20–M25 grade concrete** and **Fe415 / Fe500 grade steel**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bending Bookshelf" Imagine a wooden shelf loaded with heavy books. The shelf bends downward, and the **bottom fibres stretch** (tension) while the **top fibres compress**. If we glue a steel rod at the bottom, the steel takes the tension, while the wood (concrete) handles the compression. - **Singly Reinforced Beam** → Only steel at the bottom (tension zone); concrete at top handles compression alone. Works fine for **moderate spans/loads**. - **Doubly Reinforced Beam** → Steel at both top (compression) and bottom (tension). Needed when the **depth is restricted** (e.g., headroom constraints in a basement). - **T-Beam** → The slab and beam are **monolithically cast**; the slab acts as the top flange. Common in **building floor systems**. ### 1.3 Standard Design Constants (KTU Board Examination Values) | Parameter | Value | Source | |---|---|---| | Partial Safety Factor for Concrete ($\gamma_m$) | **1.5** | IS 456:2000, Cl. 36.4.2 | | Partial Safety Factor for Steel ($\gamma_m$) | **1.15** | IS 456:2000, Cl. 36.4.2 | | $x_{u,max}/d$ for Fe415 | **0.48** | IS 456:2000, Fig. 4 | | $x_{u,max}/d$ for Fe500 | **0.46** | IS 456:2000, Fig. 4 | | Design Compressive Stress in Concrete ($0.446 f_{ck}$) | **0.446 $f_{ck}$** | IS 456:2000, Cl. 38.1 | | Design Yield Stress in Steel ($0.87 f_y$) | **0.87 $f_y$** | IS 456:2000, Cl. 38.1 | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** The constants **0.36**, **0.42**, **0.48**, and **0.87** are derived from the rectangular parabolic stress block. Memorize their origin to handle derivation-based questions confidently. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of Limit State Method (Flexure) The Limit State Method of flexural design as per IS 456:2000 is built upon the following axioms: - **Plane sections remain plane** after bending → linear strain distribution across depth. - Maximum strain in concrete at the extreme compression fibre = **0.0035**. - Maximum usable strain at the level of centroid of tension steel = $f_y/(1.15E_s) + 0.002$ (cl. 38.1.b). - The **rectangular-parabolic stress block** is simplified to a **rectangular block** of depth $k_1 \cdot x_u$ with uniform stress $0.446 f_{ck}$ (where $k_1 = 0.42$ for $f_{ck} \le 35$ MPa). - The tensile strength of concrete is **ignored** in flexural design. - Perfect bond exists between concrete and steel (no slip). ### 2.2 Why $x_{u,max}$ is Restricted? The **IS Code enforces $x_{u,max}$** to ensure the section is **under-reinforced** (ductile failure). If $x_u > x_{u,max}$, the steel will NOT yield before concrete crushes—a sudden, brittle failure. The "Why": - A ductile failure gives **warning signs** (visible cracking, deflection) before collapse. - This is a fundamental life-safety requirement in seismic and general design. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Application | |---|---|---| | 1 | $M_{u,lim} = 0.36 \dfrac{x_{u,max}}{d} \left(1 - 0.42 \dfrac{x_{u,max}}{d}\right) f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$ | Limiting moment of resistance (singly reinforced) | | 2 | $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$ (Fe415) | Simplified form for Fe415 | | 3 | $M_{u,lim} = 0.133 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$ (Fe500) | Simplified form for Fe500 | | 4 | $A_{st,lim} = \dfrac{0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_{u,max}}{0.87 \, f_y}$ | Limiting steel area for singly reinforced beam | | 5 | $A_{st,lim} = \dfrac{0.479 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d}{1000}$ (Fe415) | Simplified form (Fe415) | | 6 | $M_{u1} = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st1} \, d \left(1 - \dfrac{A_{st1} f_y}{b \, d \, f_{ck}}\right)$ | Moment taken by balanced singly reinforced portion (doubly reinforced beams) | | 7 | $A_{st2} = \dfrac{M_{u2}}{0.87 \, f_y \, (d - d')}$ | Additional tension steel for $M_{u2} = M_u - M_{u1}$ | | 8 | $A_{sc} = \dfrac{A_{st2} \cdot f_y}{f_{sc}}$ | Compression steel; $f_{sc}$ from IS 456 Table for given $d'/d$ | | 9 | $A_{st,total} = A_{st1} + A_{st2}$ | Total tension steel (doubly reinforced) | | 10 | $M_u = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b_w \, x_u (d - 0.42 x_u) + 0.45 \, f_{ck} (b_f - b_w) \, y_f (d - y_f/2)$ | T-beam (Flanged beam) when $x_u > D_f$ | | 11 | $M_u = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b_f \, x_u (d - 0.42 x_u)$ | T-beam when $x_u \le D_f$ (rectangular behaviour) | | 12 | $y_f = 0.15 x_u + 0.65 D_f$ | Effective depth of flange in compression (T-beam) | | 13 | $A_{sf} = \dfrac{0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b_w \, x_u + 0.45 f_{ck}(b_f - b_w) y_f}{0.87 \, f_y}$ | Tension steel in T-beam | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on T-beam equations:** When $x_u > D_f$, the **flange overhang** $(b_f - b_w) D_f$ is treated as a uniform compression block of stress $0.45 f_{ck}$, and the **web compression** $b_w x_u$ uses the rectangular stress block of $0.36 f_{ck} \cdot x_u$. Some texts use $0.446 f_{ck}$ instead of $0.45 f_{ck}$ for the flange overhang—an **exact interpretation per IS 456 Cl. 38.1**. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Numerical design problems form the **backbone of structural design offices** in Kerala. Civil engineers design: - **Building floor beams** (T-beams monolithically cast with slabs) in apartments, hospitals, and commercial complexes. - **Crane girders in industrial sheds** (often doubly reinforced due to depth restrictions and high moments). - **Bridge deck slabs and girders** (T-beam or rectangular based on span). - **Sun-shades, lintels, and chajjas** (singly reinforced, small spans). > [!NOTE] > **Why LSM over Working Stress Method (WSM)?** LSM uses **factored loads** (1.5 DL + 1.5 LL), giving a uniform safety margin, and allows **redistribution of moments** in continuous beams—critical for KTU board exam answers. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Solutions ### 3.1 Derivation: Moment of Resistance from Rectangular Stress Block **Step 1:** From the strain diagram (rectangular block equivalent of the actual stress distribution), the compressive force in concrete is: $$C = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u$$ **Step 2:** The tensile force in steel is: $$T = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}$$ **Step 3:** Applying horizontal equilibrium ($C = T$): $$0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}$$ Solving for $x_u$: $$x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st}}{0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b}$$ **Step 4:** Taking moments about the **tension reinforcement**: $$M_u = C \cdot (d - 0.42 x_u) = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u (d - 0.42 x_u)$$ **Step 5:** Substituting $x_u = 0.48 d$ (for Fe415) to obtain $M_{u,lim}$: $$M_{u,lim} = 0.36 \times 0.48 (1 - 0.42 \times 0.48) \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2 = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$$ --- ### 3.2 Numerical Problem 1: Singly Reinforced Beam Design (KTU Standard) **Problem Statement:** Design a simply supported rectangular beam for a clear span of **6 m**, with a superimposed load of **25 kN/m** (excluding self-weight). The beam width is restricted to **250 mm**. Use **M20 concrete** and **Fe415 steel**. Adopt LSM. #### Solution (Step-by-Step): **Step 1: Calculate Design Loads** $$\text{Self-weight} = 0.25 \times 0.5 \times 25 = 3.125 \text{ kN/m} \quad (\text{assuming depth } d_{eff} = 500 \text{ mm})$$ $$\text{Total Working Load} = 3.125 + 25 = 28.125 \text{ kN/m}$$ **Step 2: Calculate Factored Load** $$w_u = 1.5 \times 28.125 = 42.19 \text{ kN/m}$$ **Step 3: Calculate Maximum Bending Moment** (simply supported, UDL) $$M_u = \dfrac{w_u L^2}{8} = \dfrac{42.19 \times 6^2}{8} = 189.86 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 4: Calculate Effective Depth Required** Using $M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^2$: $$189.86 \times 10^6 = 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times d^2$$ $$d^2 = \dfrac{189.86 \times 10^6}{0.138 \times 20 \times 250} = \dfrac{189.86 \times 10^6}{690} = 275159 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$d = 524.5 \text{ mm}$$ **Adopt** $d = 550$ mm. Therefore, **Overall depth** $D = d + \text{effective cover} = 550 + 50 = 600$ mm. **Step 5: Revise Self-weight and Re-check $M_u$** $$\text{Self-weight} = 0.25 \times 0.6 \times 25 = 3.75 \text{ kN/m}$$ $$w_u = 1.5 \times (3.75 + 25) = 43.125 \text{ kN/m}$$ $$M_u = \dfrac{43.125 \times 6^2}{8} = 194.06 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 6: Verify Singly Reinforced Suitability** $$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 250 \times 550^2 = 208.7 \text{ kN·m}$$ Since $M_u = 194.06 \text{ kN·m} < M_{u,lim} = 208.7 \text{ kN·m}$, the section can be designed as **singly reinforced**. ✓ **Step 7: Calculate Tension Steel $A_{st}$** Equating $M_u = 0.87 \, f_y \, A_{st} \, d (1 - \dfrac{A_{st} f_y}{b d f_{ck}})$: $$194.06 \times 10^6 = 0.87 \times 415 \times A_{st} \times 550 \left(1 - \dfrac{415 \cdot A_{st}}{250 \times 550 \times 20}\right)$$ $$194.06 \times 10^6 = 198577.5 \, A_{st} (1 - 0.0001509 A_{st})$$ $$194.06 \times 10^6 = 198577.5 \, A_{st} - 29.96 \, A_{st}^2$$ Rearranging: $$29.96 \, A_{st}^2 - 198577.5 \, A_{st} + 194.06 \times 10^6 = 0$$ Solving the quadratic (using coefficients in mm and N): $$A_{st} = 1108.6 \text{ mm}^2 \approx 1109 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 8: Provide Reinforcement** - Provide **4 bars of 20 mm diameter** ($A_{st,provided} = 4 \times \pi/4 \times 20^2 = 1256$ mm²). - **Spacing check**: $b/(n-1) = 250/3 = 83.3$ mm, with side cover + half bar = 20 + 10 = 30 mm. Minimum clear spacing = max(20 mm, $\phi_{bar}$) = 20 mm. **Adequate.** ✓ **Step 9: Check for Minimum Steel** (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.1) $$A_{st,min} = \dfrac{0.85}{f_y} \cdot b \cdot d = \dfrac{0.85}{415} \times 250 \times 550 = 281.6 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$A_{st,provided} = 1256 \text{ mm}^2 > A_{st,min} \quad ✓$$ **Step 10: Check for Maximum Steel** (IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.1) $$A_{st,max} = 0.04 \, b \cdot D = 0.04 \times 250 \times 600 = 6000 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$A_{st,provided} = 1256 \text{ mm}^2 < 6000 \text{ mm}^2 \quad ✓$$ **Final Design Summary:** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Overall Depth ($D$) | **600 mm** | | Effective Depth ($d$) | **550 mm** | | Width ($b$) | **250 mm** | | Tension Reinforcement | **4 #20 mm** ($A_{st} = 1256$ mm²) | --- ### 3.3 Numerical Problem 2: Doubly Reinforced Beam Design **Problem Statement:** A rectangular beam of width **300 mm** and effective depth **600 mm** is subjected to a factored moment of **420 kN·m**. The centre of compression steel is at $d' = 50$ mm. Design the section as a doubly reinforced beam using **M25 concrete** and **Fe415 steel**. #### Solution (Step-by-Step): **Step 1: Calculate $M_{u,lim}$** $$M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \times 25 \times 300 \times 600^2 = 372.6 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 2: Check Necessity of Doubly Reinforced Section** $$M_u = 420 \text{ kN·m} > M_{u,lim} = 372.6 \text{ kN·m}$$ Since $M_u > M_{u,lim}$, the section **cannot be designed as singly reinforced**. Therefore, design as **doubly reinforced**. ✓ **Step 3: Split the Moment** $$M_{u1} = M_{u,lim} = 372.6 \text{ kN·m}$$ $$M_{u2} = M_u - M_{u1} = 420 - 372.6 = 47.4 \text{ kN·m}$$ **Step 4: Calculate $A_{st1}$ (Tension Steel for $M_{u1}$)** $$A_{st1} = \dfrac{0.479 \times f_{ck} \times b \times d}{1000} = \dfrac{0.479 \times 25 \times 300 \times 600}{1000} = 2155.5 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 5: Calculate $A_{st2}$ (Additional Tension Steel)** $$A_{st2} = \dfrac{M_{u2}}{0.87 \, f_y (d - d')} = \dfrac{47.4 \times 10^6}{0.87 \times 415 \times (600 - 50)}$$ $$A_{st2} = \dfrac{47.4 \times 10^6}{0.87 \times 415 \times 550} = \dfrac{47.4 \times 10^6}{198577.5} = 238.7 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 6: Calculate $A_{sc}$ (Compression Steel)** For $d'/d = 50/600 = 0.0833$, from **IS 456 Table for $f_{sc}$** (Fe415): $$f_{sc} = 355 \text{ MPa}$$ $$A_{sc} = \dfrac{A_{st2} \cdot f_y}{f_{sc}} = \dfrac{238.7 \times 415}{355} = 279.0 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 7: Total Tension Steel** $$A_{st,total} = A_{st1} + A_{st2} = 2155.5 + 238.7 = 2394.2 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 8: Provide Reinforcement** - **Tension Steel:** Provide **8 #20 mm** ($A_{st,provided} = 8 \times 314 = 2512$ mm²) — 2 rows of 4 bars. - **Compression Steel:** Provide **2 #16 mm** ($A_{sc,provided} = 2 \times 201 = 402$ mm²) > 279 mm² ✓. --- ### 3.4 Numerical Problem 3: T-Beam Design **Problem Statement:** A T-beam has the following dimensions: web width $b_w = 300$ mm, flange width $b_f = 1200$ mm, flange thickness $D_f = 120$ mm, effective depth $d = 600$ mm, $d' = 50$ mm. Factored moment $M_u = 720$ kN·m.
