# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
> **Define the term "Compression Member" as per IS 456:2000. Differentiate between a short column and a slender column based on the slenderness ratio.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):
*(1 mark for definition)* **IS 456 (Cl. 25.1.1):** A compression member is a structural member subjected predominantly to compressive axial load, e.g., columns, pedestals, and walls.

*(1.5 marks for differentiation table)*

| Criterion | Short Column | Slender / Long Column |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Slenderness $\lambda$ | $\le 12$ | $> 12$ |
| Governing failure | Material crushing | Stability / buckling |
| IS 456 equation | $P_u \le 0.40 f_{ck} A_g + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$ | Apply $C_r$ reduction factor |

*(0.5 marks for the governing clause, Cl. 25.1.2)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
> **State the expression for minimum eccentricity of a column as per IS 456:2000. Why is it not designed as a purely axially loaded member?**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):
*(1.5 marks for expression)*

$$
e_{x,\min} = \dfrac{L_{ex}}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30} \ge 20 \text{ mm}
$$

where $L_{ex}$ is the effective length about x-axis (in mm) and $D$ is the lateral dimension in the direction of bending.

*(1.5 marks for reasoning)* In real construction, perfectly axial loading is impossible due to:
1. Construction tolerances in alignment.
2. Unintentional load eccentricities from adjoining beams.
3. Floor loads that are not exactly symmetric.

Hence, IS 456 (Cl. 25.4) prescribes a **notional minimum eccentricity** so that the column is designed to resist a **minimum moment** $M_o = P_u \times e_{\min}$ in addition to the axial load. This prevents unsafe designs that would otherwise be susceptible to *unanticipated* bending failures.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls in Column Design Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute $L_{ex}$ separately from $L$** — many students use $L$ directly. This loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Not using the correct end-condition factor $k$** for braced/unbraced frames. Loss: 1.5 marks.
> 3. **Using $A_c$ (net concrete area) instead of $A_g$ (gross area)** in Eq. 4 of IS 456. The 0.40 factor in IS 456 already accounts for concrete area. Loss: 2 marks.
> 4. **Not checking the minimum eccentricity clause 25.4** — 1 mark deducted.
> 5. **Failing to mention the slenderness limit check (Cl. 25.3)** — 1 mark deducted.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert $f_{ck}$ and $f_y$ units consistently** (N/mm² vs MPa) — calculation penalty.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

> A short reinforced concrete column of size **400 mm × 500 mm** carries a factored axial load of **2400 kN**. The column has an unsupported length of **3.2 m**, is part of a **braced frame** with both ends fixed against rotation, and is made of **M30 concrete** and **Fe500 steel**. The column is provided with **1.8% longitudinal steel**.
>
> **a)** Check the adequacy of the section using IS 456:2000 provisions. **(7 marks)**
> **b)** Design the lateral ties and verify all code requirements (minimum eccentricity, slenderness ratio, and longitudinal steel limits). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Effective length:**
Braced, both ends fixed: $k = 0.65$

$$
L_{ex} = 0.65 \times 3.2 = 2.08 \text{ m} = 2080 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Slenderness check:**

$$
\dfrac{L_{ex}}{D} = \dfrac{2080}{500} = 4.16 \le 12 \quad \checkmark \text{ Short column}
$$

$$
\dfrac{L_{ey}}{b} = \dfrac{2080}{400} = 5.20 \le 12 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Areas:**

$$
A_g = 400 \times 500 = 200000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
A_{sc} = 0.018 \times 200000 = 3600 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Axial capacity (IS 456 Eq. 4):**

$$
P_o = 0.40 \times 30 \times 200000 + 0.67 \times 500 \times 3600
$$

$$
P_o = 2{,}400{,}000 + 1{,}206{,}000 = 3{,}606{,}000 \text{ N} = 3606 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

$$
P_u = 2400 \text{ kN} < P_o = 3606 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}
$$

**Step 6 — Capacity utilization ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{P_u}{P_o} = \dfrac{2400}{3606} = 0.666 = 66.6\%
$$

A utilization between 60–80% is considered optimal in KTU board evaluations.

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Minimum eccentricity (Cl. 25.4):**

$$
e_{x,\min} = \dfrac{2080}{500} + \dfrac{500}{30} = 4.16 + 16.67 = 20.83 \text{ mm} > 20 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
e_{y,\min} = \dfrac{2080}{500} + \dfrac{400}{30} = 4.16 + 13.33 = 17.49 \text{ mm} < 20 \text{ mm}
$$

Adopt $e_{y,\min} = 20$ mm.

**Step 2 — Notional moments:**

$$
M_{ox} = 2400 \times 20.83 \times 10^{-3} = 49.99 \text{ kN·m}
$$

$$
M_{oy} = 2400 \times 20.00 \times 10^{-3} = 48.00 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 3 — Check longitudinal steel limits (Cl. 26.5.3.1):**
- Min $A_{sc} = 0.008 \times 200000 = 1600$ mm².
- Provided = 3600 mm² > 1600 mm² ✓
- Max $A_{sc} = 0.04 \times 200000 = 8000$ mm² (provided within limit) ✓

**Step 4 — Lateral ties design (Cl. 26.5.3.2):**

Tie diameter: $\ge \dfrac{1}{4} \times \text{largest main bar}$

Assume 20 mm $\phi$ main bars: tie $\ge 5$ mm → adopt **8 mm $\phi$**.

Tie spacing: $\le 300$ mm c/c, $\le 16 \times 20 = 320$ mm, $\le$ least lateral dimension = 400 mm.

Provide **8 mm $\phi$ ties @ 300 mm c/c**.

**Step 5 — Cover check (Cl. 26.4):** Minimum cover = 40 mm (for cast-in-place concrete exposed to weather). Adopt 40 mm cover.

**Step 6 — Minimum bars:** 4 bars (one in each corner). Provide 8 bars of 20 mm $\phi$ = 2513 mm². (4 corners + 4 intermediate) — sufficient.

**Step 7 — Slenderness limits (Cl. 25.3):** For braced: $L_{ex}/D = 4.16 < 60$ ✓ and $L_{ey}/b = 5.20 < 60$ ✓.

**Conclusion:** Section is safe and code-compliant.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

> A short square tied column of size **500 mm × 500 mm** is provided with **6 bars of 20 mm $\phi$**. It is part of a **braced frame** with both ends hinged (effectively held in position but not restrained against rotation). Unsupported length is **4.0 m**. Materials: **M25** and **Fe415**.
>
> **a)** Compute the safe factored axial load the column can carry as per IS 456:2000. **(7 marks)**
> **b)** If the column is to carry a factored load of **1900 kN**, determine the percentage of steel to be provided. Also check the section for slenderness and minimum eccentricity. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Effective length:**
Braced, both ends hinged (held in position, not restrained in rotation): $k = 1.00$

$$
L_{ex} = 1.00 \times 4.0 = 4.0 \text{ m} = 4000 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Check slenderness:**

$$
\dfrac{L_{ex}}{D} = \dfrac{4000}{500} = 8 \le 12 \quad \checkmark \text{ Short column}
$$

**Step 3 — Areas:**

$$
A_g = 500 \times 500 = 250000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
A_{sc} = 6 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 6 \times 314.16 = 1885 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Axial capacity (IS 456 Eq. 4):**

$$
P_o = 0.40 \times 25 \times 250000 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 1885
$$

$$
P_o = 2{,}500{,}000 + 524{,}924.5 = 3{,}024{,}924.5 \text{ N} \approx 3025 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Minimum eccentricity check:**

$$
e_{\min} = \dfrac{4000}{500} + \dfrac{500}{30} = 8 + 16.67 = 24.67 \text{ mm} > 20 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 6 — Steel percentage:**

$$
p = \dfrac{1885}{250000} \times 100\% = 0.754\%
$$

This is **less than the minimum** required 0.8% of $A_g$ (Cl. 26.5.3.1).

**Step 7 — Revise to minimum 0.8%:**

$$
A_{sc,\text{req}} = 0.008 \times 250000 = 2000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 8 — Recompute $P_o$ with revised steel:**

$$
P_o = 0.40 \times 25 \times 250000 + 0.67 \times 415 \times 2000
$$

$$
P_o = 2{,}500{,}000 + 556{,}100 = 3{,}056{,}100 \text{ N} \approx 3056 \text{ kN}
$$

**Answer:** The safe factored axial load is **≈ 3056 kN** (governed by minimum 0.8% steel).

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — For $P_u = 1900$ kN, find required $A_{sc}$:**

Using IS 456 Eq. 4:

$$
P_u = 0.40 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot A_g + 0.67 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sc}
$$

$$
1900 \times 10^3 = 0.40 \times 25 \times 250000 + 0.67 \times 415 \times A_{sc}
$$

$$
1900000 = 2{,}500{,}000 + 278.05 \cdot A_{sc}
$$

This yields $A_{sc} = -600000/278.05 < 0$, which is **impossible**.

**Interpretation:** The concrete section (M25) is **already over-designed** for the applied 1900 kN load. The minimum 0.8% steel (2000 mm²) is the **governing design**.

**Step 2 — Adopt minimum 0.8% steel:** $A_{sc} = 2000$ mm².

Choose bars: 8 bars of 18 mm $\phi$ (4 corners + 4 intermediate).

$$
A_{sc} = 8 \times 254.47 = 2035.76 \text{ mm}^2 \approx 2000 \text{ mm}^2 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Slenderness check (already verified):** $L/D = 8 < 12$ (short) and $< 60$ (limit) ✓.

**Step 4 — Minimum eccentricity check:**

$$
e_{\min} = 24.67 \text{ mm (as before)} > 20 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Notional moment:**

$$
M_o = 1900 \times 0.02467 = 46.87 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 6 — Ties:** $\ge \dfrac{1}{4} \times 18 = 4.5$ mm → adopt 8 mm $\phi$ @ 300 mm c/c (or closer if seismic).

**Step 7 — Comment on the design:** The column is **massively under-stressed** (utilization = 62%). Either:
- Reduce the column size to 400 mm × 400 mm (re-check capacity), or
- Accept the over-design (conservative design, common in KTU practical buildings).

**Answer:** Provide 8 bars of 18 mm $\phi$ (≈ 0.8% steel), safe and code-compliant.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **IS 456:2000 Cl. 25.1.1** defines compression members (column / pedestal / wall).
- **Short vs Slender:** Threshold is $L_e/D = 12$ (and $L_e/b = 12$).
- **Braced vs Unbraced** dictates the effective length factor $k$.
- **Effective length** values: $0.65L, 0.80L, 1.00L, 1.20L, 1.50L, 2.00L$ depending on end conditions.
- **Minimum eccentricity** (Cl. 25.4): $e_{\min} = L_e/500 + D/30 \ge 20$ mm.
- **Axial capacity (Cl. 39.3, Eq. 4):** $P_o = 0.40 f_{ck} A_g + 0.67 f_y A_{sc}$.
- **Reduction factor (Cl. 39.3, Eq. 5):** $C_r = 1.25 - \lambda/48$ for $12 < \lambda \le 24$; $C_r = 0.50$ for $\lambda > 24$.
- **Minimum longitudinal steel:** **0.8%** of $A_g$ (Cl. 26.5.3.1).
- **Maximum longitudinal steel:** **4.0%** of $A_g$, but practically limited to **2–3%** to avoid congestion.
- **Minimum column dimension:** **300 mm** (general), **350 mm** (IS 13920 seismic).
- **Cover:** 40 mm (earth), 30 mm (general), 25 mm (light exposure).
- **Slenderness limits (Cl. 25.3):** Braced ≤ 60, Unbraced ≤ 30.
- **Lateral ties:** $\phi \ge L/4$ of largest main bar; spacing $\le 300$ mm, $\le 16\phi_{main}$, $\le$ least lateral dim.
- **Partial safety factors:** $\gamma_c = 1.5$, $\gamma_s = 1.15$.
- **Min. number of bars:** 4 (rectangular), 6 (circular).
- **Pedestals:** $L_e < 3D$, may be designed as plain concrete if $P_u \le 0.1 f_{ck} A_g$.
- **Always convert units:** $f_{ck}$ in N/mm², $A$ in mm², $P$ in N (or kN = N/1000).
- **For pure axial design, do NOT use SP-16 charts** — they are for combined axial + uniaxial/biaxial bending.
- **One-equation summary for board exam:** $P_u = C_r \cdot (0.4 f_{ck} A_g + 0.67 f_y A_{sc})$ — short column has $C_r = 1.0$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip (Final Tip):** In 14-mark Part B problems on compression members, the *order* of writing matters. Always follow this order in your answer book:
> 1. Given data and assumed cross-section.
> 2. Effective length calculation with $k$-factor justification.
> 3. Slenderness ratio check and classification.
> 4. Steel area selection and IS 456 Eq. 4 application.
> 5. Minimum eccentricity and notional moment.
> 6. Slenderness limit check (Cl. 25.3).
> 7. Ties and detailing.
> 8. Conclusion with safety statement.
> 
> Marks are awarded **stepwise** — partial credit is given even if the final answer is wrong if the steps are visible.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definitions, Classifications, Guidelines and Assumptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET404 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Definitions, Classifications, Guidelines and Assumptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Staircase

A **staircase** is a structural assembly of steps (treads and risers) arranged in a series of flights separated by landings, designed to provide vertical circulation between different floor levels of a building while safely transferring the imposed loads to the supporting structural elements (beams, walls, or slabs) through a structural component called the **waist slab** (or stair slab).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A staircase is a Load Distributing Element (LDE) where the load path travels from the step → waist slab → landing beam → column → foundation. It is analysed and designed using **Limit State Method (LSM)** as per **IS 456:2000**.

---

## 1.2 Anatomical Components of a Staircase

A staircase, although geometrically complex, is built from a small set of well-defined geometric and structural components. Mastering these is a mandatory prerequisite for any design problem.

| Component | Geometric Definition | Structural Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Tread (T)** | Horizontal top surface of a step where the foot is placed | Non-structural finish, contributes to dead load |
| **Riser (R)** | Vertical face between two consecutive treads | Geometric, contributes to dead load |
| **Flight** | A continuous series of steps between two landings | Transfers load as a slab |
| **Landing** | Horizontal platform at floor level or mid-height | Acts as a rigid support/beam |
| **Waist Slab** | Inclined slab supporting the steps | Primary load-carrying flexural member |
| **Going** | Horizontal projection of one tread (≈ T) | Used to compute total horizontal span |
| **Nosing** | Small projection of tread beyond the riser face | Architectural, not structural |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Inclined Spine of the Building"

> [!NOTE]
> 🪜 **Think of a staircase as the spine of a multi-storey building.** Just as the human spine connects the head (upper floor) to the pelvis (lower floor) while flexing and transferring weight, the staircase connects floors and transfers vertical/lateral loads. Each step is like a *vertebra*, the landing is a *pelvic platform*, and the waist slab is the *flexible backbone* that carries bending and shear.

This analogy helps students remember that a stair slab behaves **like a simply-supported inclined slab** spanning between two landings — which is the central assumption behind its analysis.

---

## 1.4 Why Study Definitions & Classifications First?

> [!TIP]
> Every design problem in Module 3 begins with the question *"Which type of staircase?"* Without a clear classification framework, you cannot pick the right **effective span formula**, the right **support condition**, or the right **load path**. The first 3 marks in any KTU staircase question are almost always from this section.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Staircases (IS 456 + SP 34)

Staircases are classified on three primary bases. KTU typically examines the **geometric/plan classification**.

### 2.1.1 Based on Plan / Geometric Shape

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Straight Stair** | Single flight, no change in direction | Short spans, low-rise buildings |
| **Dog-Legged Stair** | Two flights opposite each other separated by a mid-landing (180° turn) | Most common in residential & commercial |
| **Open-Well Stair** | Two flights with a clear gap (well) between them (90° or U-turn) | Public buildings, hospitals |
| **Quarter-Turn Stair** | 90° turn at landing | Corner staircases |
| **Half-Turn (180°) Stair** | 180° turn (dog-legged or with open well) | Standard residential |
| **Spiral / Helical Stair** | Steps radiate around a central pole | Architectural, fire-escape stairs |
| **Cantilever Stair** | Steps cantilevered from a wall | Modern architecture |

### 2.1.2 Based on Structural Action

| Type | Load Path | Bending Direction |
|---|---|---|
| **Waist Slab Type** (most common) | Steps → Waist Slab → Landing Beam | Across the slab width (transverse bending) |
| **Tread-Riser Slab Type** | Each step acts as cantilever | In each step individually |

### 2.1.3 Based on Span / Geometry

- **Single-flight stair** – One continuous flight only.
- **Multi-flight stair** – Two or more flights with intermediate landing(s).
- **Geometric stair** – Spans in 3D (curved, helical).

---

## 2.2 Design Guidelines (IS 456:2000 & SP 34)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These values are **board-exam favourites** and appear as 2-mark definitions almost every semester.

| Parameter | Recommended Value | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Tread (T)** | **250 mm – 300 mm** | SP 34 |
| **Riser (R)** | **150 mm – 190 mm** | SP 34 |
| **2R + T Relation** | **580 mm – 640 mm** (Empirical rule) | SP 34 |
| **Width of stair (residential)** | ≥ **1000 mm** | IS 456, NBC |
| **Width of stair (public)** | ≥ **1500 mm – 2000 mm** | NBC |
| **Head Room** | ≥ **2200 mm** (clear) | NBC |
| **Landing width** | ≥ **Width of stair** | IS 456 |
| **Live Load (residential)** | **3.0 kN/m²** | IS 875 Part 2 |
| **Live Load (public/office)** | **4.0 – 5.0 kN/m²** | IS 875 Part 2 |
| **Floor finish load** | **1.0 – 1.5 kN/m²** | IS 875 Part 1 |
| **Concrete grade** | M20 – M25 (min) | IS 456 |
| **Steel grade** | Fe415 / Fe500 | IS 456 |

---

## 2.3 Design Assumptions (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> These assumptions are the foundation on which every KTU staircase design problem rests. Losing a single assumption = losing **1–2 marks** in the ESE.

1. **Support Condition:** The staircase is **simply supported** between the two landings (or between landing and floor beam). Partial fixity is conservatively neglected.
2. **Span Direction:** For dog-legged and open-well stairs, the **flight slab spans in the direction of the flight (along the slope)**. The main reinforcement is provided in the **bottom of the waist slab, parallel to the flight direction**.
3. **Effective Span Formula:** As per **IS 456, Clause 33.1(b)**, the effective span is taken as the **lesser** of:
   - Center-to-center distance of supports, OR
   - Clear horizontal span + effective bearing width (or effective depth of landing slab, usually the half-thickness on each side).
4. **Inclined Span Correction:** Loads are computed on the **horizontal projected area** because floor finish and live load are applied on horizontal projections. The **self-weight of waist slab is multiplied by (1/cos θ)** where θ is the slope angle of the flight.
5. **Tread-Riser Self-Weight:** The triangular wedge of steps (tread + riser) contributes an equivalent dead load. The standard IS assumption is: **Weight of steps = (R/2) × T × γ_concrete per unit projected area**, but practically the additional step weight (over the rectangular waist slab) = **(R/2) × unit weight of concrete**.
6. **Slab Continuity:** In a dog-legged stair, each flight is analysed as a **simply supported inclined slab** spanning between two landings, ignoring continuity at the landing.
7. **Effective Depth (d) Check:** Minimum overall thickness of waist slab = span/20 to span/30, where span is the **effective horizontal span**.
8. **Concrete Stress Block:** Rectangular parabolic stress block as per **IS 456, Clause 38.1** (Limit State Method).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> ⭐ Memorise this table — it covers ~70 % of any KTU staircase numerical.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Angle of inclination | $\tan \theta = R / T$ | degrees |
| 2 | Slope correction | $\sec \theta = 1 / \cos \theta$ | – |
| 3 | Effective span (lesser) | $L_{eff} = \min \left( L_{c/c}, L_{clear} + b_{eff} \right)$ | m |
| 4 | Self-wt of waist slab | $w_{slab} = D \times 1 \times 25 \times \sec\theta$ | kN/m |
| 5 | Self-wt of steps (triangular portion) | $w_{steps} = \frac{R}{2} \times 0.3 \times 24$ per m width | kN/m² |
| 6 | Floor finish | $w_{FF} = (\text{load per } m^2) \times 1 \text{ m width}$ | kN/m |
| 7 | Live load | $w_{LL} = (\text{load per } m^2) \times 1 \text{ m width}$ | kN/m |
| 8 | Total factored load | $w_u = 1.5 \times (w_{DL} + w_{LL})$ | kN/m |
| 9 | Max Bending Moment | $M_u = w_u L_{eff}^{2} / 8$ | kN·m |
| 10 | Max Shear Force | $V_u = w_u L_{eff} / 2$ | kN |
| 11 | Minimum waist thickness | $D \approx L_{eff} / 20$ to $L_{eff} / 30$ | m |
| 12 | Effective cover | Cover + (Ø/2) for main steel | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> Always compute loads on a **strip of unit width (1 m)** measured on the **horizontal projection** — this is the KTU board-valuation trap. Loads on inclined length cause wrong answers.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Architectural studios & apartments:** Dog-legged stairs for compact floor plans.
- **Public buildings (malls, hospitals):** Open-well stairs for aesthetics and clear circulation.
- **Fire escape:** Spiral stairs — saves space, but governed by **IS 875 / NBC fire codes**.
- **Bridge abutments & metro stations:** Geometric/helical stairs for irregular layouts.

> Every civil engineer designing a multi-storey building will face staircase design — often the *first* design problem taught in design studios due to its simplicity and pedagogical clarity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Effective Span Formula (IS 456)

> The IS 456 code gives two competing definitions for effective span in a simply supported member. For staircases, we must use the appropriate one.

**Statement:** The effective span of a stair slab shall be the **lesser** of the following two distances:

- **(a)** Clear horizontal span + effective depth of waist slab (when landing beam depth ≤ waist slab depth).
- **(b)** Center-to-center horizontal distance between the two landing supports.

**Derivation logic (with annotated steps):**

1. The structural span of a simply supported beam is defined as the distance between the centroids of its supports where the bending moment is theoretically zero.
2. For a stair slab supported on landing walls/beams, the **true support location** is the centreline of the landing beam, not the face of the wall.
3. However, when the landing is a flat slab (no beam), the support is taken at a distance equal to the **effective depth $d$** beyond the inner face of the landing.
4. Hence the safe definition becomes the **minimum** of these two interpretations.

$$
L_{eff} = \min \left( L_{c/c}, \quad L_{clear} + d_{slab} \right)
$$

> **Conversion logic:** $L_{clear}$ is the *horizontal clear distance* between landing faces; $d_{slab}$ is the effective depth of the waist slab at the support.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Total Dead Load on Waist Slab

### 3.2.1 Self-weight of Waist Slab (per m horizontal width)

The slab is inclined at angle θ. The **thickness measured normal to slope = D**. The **slab length along the slope** for a 1 m horizontal projection is **sec θ**.

Therefore, the volume per m width per m horizontal projection is:

$$
\text{Volume} = D \times 1 \text{ m (width)} \times \sec\theta
$$

Multiplying by unit weight of reinforced concrete $\gamma_c = 25 \text{ kN/m}^3$:

$$
w_{slab} = 25 \cdot D \cdot \sec\theta \quad \text{(kN/m)}
$$

> **Step-by-step logic:**
> - The slab is inclined, so a 1 m horizontal run corresponds to $\sec\theta$ m of *sloped length*.
> - Multiply by thickness $D$ and unit width (1 m) to get the volume.
> - Multiply by $\gamma_c$ to get the weight (load per metre horizontal run).

### 3.2.2 Weight of Steps (Triangular Wedge Portion)

The steps form a triangular cross-section sitting on top of the waist slab. The **triangular height = R** (riser), and the **base = T** (tread). The **cross-sectional area of one step = (1/2) × T × R**.

For a strip of **1 m width**, volume per metre horizontal projection (since each step occupies a horizontal length of T):

$$
\text{Vol/m width} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot T \cdot R \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T}\right) = \frac{R}{2}
$$

Multiplying by $\gamma_c = 24 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (plain concrete for steps):

$$
w_{steps} = 24 \cdot \frac{R}{2} = 12 R \quad \text{(kN/m}^2\text{)}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The horizontal projection of each step is T m. Number of steps per metre = 1/T. Area per metre horizontal = (1/2)·T·R·(1/T) = R/2. This is the classic IS-code simplification.

### 3.2.3 Total Unfactored Load (per m width)

$$
w = w_{slab} + w_{steps} + w_{FF} + w_{LL}
$$

### 3.2.4 Total Factored (Design) Load

$$
w_u = 1.5 \times (w_{slab} + w_{steps} + w_{FF} + w_{LL})
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Bending Moment and Shear

> For a simply supported slab (or beam) with uniformly distributed load $w_u$ over effective span $L_{eff}$:

**Bending moment** (using standard SS-beam formula, with simply supported end conditions and UDL):

$$
M_u = \frac{w_u L_{eff}^{2}}{8}
$$

**Shear force** (max at support):

$$
V_u = \frac{w_u L_{eff}}{2}
$$

> **Step-by-step logic:**
> - Reactions at each support = $w_u L_{eff} / 2$ (equilibrium).
> - Maximum BM occurs at mid-span where shear = 0; by integration $M_{max} = w_u L_{eff}^2 / 8$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Design Parameters

| Parameter | KTU Suggested Value | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Concrete grade | M20 | Economic for residential, IS minimum |
| Steel grade | Fe415 | High yield, easier detailing |
| Clear cover | 15 mm | For moderate exposure (IS 456) |
| Slab thickness | L/20 to L/30 | Deflection control, IS minimum |
| Effective depth | d = D – cover – Ø/2 | Standard LS design |
| Percentage steel | 0.12 % to 4 % (IS 456 limits) | Crack control & ductility |
| Development length | $L_d = \frac{\phi \sigma_s}{4 \tau_{bd}}$ | IS 456 Cl. 26.2 |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Demonstration of Loads (Illustrative)

> **Example setup:** Waist slab thickness D = 150 mm, riser R = 150 mm, tread T = 250 mm, floor finish 1.5 kN/m², live load 3.0 kN/m².

Step 1 — Compute slope angle:

$$
\tan\theta = R/T = 150/250 = 0.6
$$

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.6) = 30.96^\circ
$$

Step 2 — Compute sec θ:

$$
\sec\theta = 1/\cos(30.96^\circ) = 1.166
$$

Step 3 — Slab self-weight per m width:

$$
w_{slab} = 25 \times 0.150 \times 1.166 = 4.375 \text{ kN/m}
$$

Step 4 — Step wedge weight per m²:

$$
w_{steps} = 12 \times R = 12 \times 0.150 = 1.80 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

Step 5 — Add FF + LL per m², then convert to per m width (multiply by 1 m):

$$
w_{FF+LL} = 1.5 + 3.0 = 4.5 \text{ kN/m}
$$

Step 6 — Total unfactored load:

$$
w = 4.375 + 1.80 + 4.5 = 10.675 \text{ kN/m}
$$

Step 7 — Factored design load:

$$
w_u = 1.5 \times 10.675 = 16.01 \text{ kN/m}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** All loads are expressed per **metre horizontal width** of the stair slab. The factor **1.5** is the **partial safety factor for load combinations** as per IS 456, Table 18 (for DL + LL).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Dog-Legged Staircase

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Floor Beam at Upper Level] --> B[Landing Slab L1]
    B --> C[Flight 1 - Inclined Waist Slab]
    C --> D[Mid Landing Slab L2]
    D --> E[Flight 2 - Inclined Waist Slab]
    E --> F[Ground Floor Landing Slab L3]
    F --> G[Floor Beam at Lower Level]
    B -. supports .- C
    D -. supports .- C
    D -. supports .- E
    F -. supports .- E
    H[Steps Tread + Riser] --> C
    I[Steps Tread + Riser] --> E
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The waist slab is *sandwiched* between two landing slabs, which act as simple supports. Loads flow: step → waist slab → landing → beam → column → foundation.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Classification Tree of Staircases

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Staircase Types] --> B[Geometric Classification]
    A --> C[Structural Classification]
    A --> D[Span Classification]
    B --> B1[Straight]
    B --> B2[Dog Legged 180 deg]
    B --> B3[Open Well U Turn]
    B --> B4[Quarter Turn 90 deg]
    B --> B5[Spiral Helical]
    B --> B6[Cantilever]
    C --> C1[Waist Slab Type]
    C --> C2[Tread Riser Cantilever]
    D --> D1[Single Flight]
    D --> D2[Multi Flight]
    D --> D3[Geometric 3D]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Load Path Architecture of a Stair

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Live Load 3 to 5 kN per sqm] --> S[Waist Slab Flexural Member]
    P2[Floor Finish 1.0 to 1.5 kN per sqm] --> S
    P3[Self Weight of Steps 12R] --> S
    P4[Self Weight of Slab 25 D sec theta] --> S
    S --> M[Bending Moment Mu and Shear Vu]
    M --> R1[Main Steel at Bottom of Waist]
    M --> R2[Distribution Steel Transverse]
    R1 --> F[Force Transfer to Landing Beam]
    F --> COL[Column]
    COL --> FO[Foundation]
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — Design Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Identify Staircase Type] --> S2[Step 2 Fix Geometric Inputs R T Width]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Compute Effective Span per IS 456]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Estimate Waist Slab Thickness L by 20]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Calculate Loads DL LL FF on Horizontal Projection]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Apply Load Factor 1.5 to get wu]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Compute Mu and Vu]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Design Main Steel using LSM]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Check for Shear Deflection Cracking]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10 Detailing as per SP 34]
```

> [!TIP]
> Examiners appreciate diagrams in the answer sheet — even a simple flowchart of the design sequence earns a **1–2 mark cushion** if the calculation has minor slips.

---

## 4.5 Cross-Section Sketch of Waist Slab (ASCII Visualisation)

```
                  │
                  │  Riser (R)
              ────┤─────────────  Tread (T)
             /    │             /
            /     │            /
           /  ▲   │           /  ▲
          /  │ │  │          /   │
         /   │ │  │         /    │
        / Steps  │         / Steps
       / (tri.)  │        / (tri.)
      /_____│____│_______/  │
      │         │         │
      │ Waist Slab (D)    │  ← Cross-section view
      │   thickness       │     showing waist + steps
      └─────────┴─────────┘
       ←─── Span (slope) ──→
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a staircase. List any four components of a staircase with a one-line description.**

> **Model Answer:**
> A staircase is a structural assembly of steps arranged in flights separated by landings, providing vertical circulation between floors.
> 1. **Tread** – Horizontal top surface of a step on which the foot is placed.
> 2. **Riser** – Vertical face between two consecutive treads.
> 3. **Flight** – Continuous series of steps between two landings.
> 4. **Landing** – Horizontal platform at floor level or mid-height.
> 5. **Waist slab** – Inclined slab that supports the steps and transfers the load to landings. *(Any 4)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the IS 456 provisions for the effective span of a stair slab.**

> **Model Answer:**
> As per **IS 456:2000, Clause 33.1(b)**, the effective span of a stair slab shall be taken as the **lesser** of:
> - (a) The clear horizontal span between the supports (landing faces) plus the effective depth of the slab.
> - (b) The center-to-center horizontal distance between the supports.
>
> This is because the slab is treated as a simply supported flexural member between two landing slabs, and the support width (landing depth) must be considered to avoid under-estimating the moment.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**
> **Design a dog-legged stair for a residential building with the following data:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Floor-to-floor height | **3.2 m** |
| Rise (R) | **160 mm** |
| Tread (T) | **250 mm** |
| Width of stair | **1.10 m** |
| Live load | **3.0 kN/m²** |
| Floor finish | **1.0 kN/m²** |
| Materials | M20 concrete, Fe415 steel |
| Landing width | **1.20 m** |

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Design

#### **Part (a)** — Compute Effective Span, Loads, BM, and Shear. (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Number of risers and treads per flight:**

$$
\text{No. of risers per flight} = \frac{3200/2}{160} = 10
$$

$$
\text{No. of treads per flight} = 10 - 1 = 9 \text{ (between two landings)}
$$

**Step 2 — Horizontal span of one flight (going × no. of treads):**

$$
L_{clear,flight} = 9 \times 0.250 = 2.25 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective span (lesser of):**

- (a) c/c of supports = $2.25 + 1.20/2 + 1.20/2 = 3.45$ m
- (b) Clear span + effective depth ≈ $2.25 + 0.13 = 2.38$ m

> **Effective span** $L_{eff} = 2.38$ m (lesser)

**Step 4 — Slope angle:**

$$
\tan\theta = 160/250 = 0.64 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = 32.6^\circ
$$

$$
\sec\theta = 1.087
$$

**Step 5 — Assume waist slab thickness D = 150 mm (L/15 ≈ 160 mm, take 150 mm).**

**Step 6 — Loads (per m width on horizontal projection):**

- Slab self-weight: $25 \times 0.150 \times 1.087 = 4.08$ kN/m
- Step wedge: $12 \times 0.160 = 1.92$ kN/m²
- Floor finish: $1.0$ kN/m²
- Live load: $3.0$ kN/m²

$$
w = (4.08/1.087) + 1.92 + 1.0 + 3.0 = 3.75 + 1.92 + 1.0 + 3.0 = 9.67 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

*(Slab weight converted to per-m² by dividing by secθ; or kept per m width — both yield the same total when width is 1 m.)*

**Step 7 — Factored load:**

$$
w_u = 1.5 \times 9.67 = 14.5 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 8 — Maximum BM and SF:**

$$
M_u = \frac{w_u L_{eff}^{2}}{8} = \frac{14.5 \times 2.38^{2}}{8} = 10.27 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

$$
V_u = \frac{w_u L_{eff}}{2} = \frac{14.5 \times 2.38}{2} = 17.26 \text{ kN/m}
$$

> **Answer (a):** $L_{eff} = 2.38$ m, $w_u = 14.5$ kN/m², $M_u = 10.27$ kN·m/m, $V_u = 17.26$ kN/m. ✓

#### **Part (b)** — Design the Waist Slab for Flexure and Shear. (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Effective depth (assume 10 mm bar + 15 mm cover):**

$$
d = 150 - 15 - 5 = 130 \text{ mm} = 0.130 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Design constants (M20, Fe415):**

$$
f_{ck} = 20 \text{ N/mm}^2, \quad f_y = 415 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Required area of steel (using LSM):**

$$
M_{u,lim} = 0.138 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d^{2} = 0.138 \times 20 \times 1000 \times 130^{2} = 46.6 \text{ kN·m/m}
$$

Since $M_u = 10.27$ kN·m < $M_{u,lim}$, **singly reinforced section is safe**.

$$
A_{st,req} = \frac{0.5 f_{ck}}{f_y} \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 M_u}{f_{ck} b d^{2}}} \right] b d
$$

$$
A_{st,req} = \frac{0.5 \times 20}{415} \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4.6 \times 10.27 \times 10^{6}}{20 \times 1000 \times 130^{2}}} \right] \times 1000 \times 130
$$

$$
= 0.0241 \left[ 1 - \sqrt{1 - 0.1396} \right] \times 130000
$$

$$
= 0.0241 \times 0.0730 \times 130000 = 228.7 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Minimum steel (IS 456, Cl. 26.5.1.1):**

$$
A_{st,min} = \frac{0.12}{100} \times b \times D = \frac{0.12}{100} \times 1000 \times 150 = 180 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

> **Provide $A_{st,req} = 228.7$ mm²/m → use 10 mm Ø bars @ 300 mm c/c ($A_{st} = 262$ mm²/m)** ✓

**Step 5 — Distribution steel (IS 456, Cl. 26.5.2.1):**

$$
A_{st,dist} = \frac{0.12}{100} \times 1000 \times 150 = 180 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}
$$

> **Provide 8 mm Ø @ 250 mm c/c** ($A_{st} = 201$ mm²/m) ✓

**Step 6 — Shear check:**

$$
\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b \, d} = \frac{17.26 \times 10^{3}}{1000 \times 130} = 0.133 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

For M20 + Fe415 with $p_t = 0.20\%$, $\tau_{c,max} = 0.28 + 0.22 = 0.50$ N/mm² (approx). $k \cdot \tau_c \approx 0.30$ N/mm² > 0.133 → **Shear is safe without shear reinforcement.** Provide nominal shear reinf. of 6 mm legged stirrups.

> **Answer (b):** Main steel 10 mm Ø @ 300 mm c/c (bottom, parallel to flight); distribution 8 mm @ 250 mm c/c (transverse). ✓

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Concept + Design Sketch)

#### **Part (a)** — Classify staircases and explain dog-legged stair in detail. (7 Marks)

> **Model Answer Outline:**
> 1. **Classification** (as in Section 2.1) with neat flow diagram.
> 2. **Dog-legged stair detailed:**
>    - Two flights parallel, 180° turn at mid-landing.
>    - Mid-landing slab supported on landing beam.
>    - Each flight is a simply supported inclined slab.
>    - Main steel at bottom of waist slab, parallel to flight direction.
>    - Distribution steel transverse.
> 3. **Sketch** of plan, cross-section showing waist slab, steps, landings, beams.

#### **Part (b)** — A stair has floor-to-floor height 3.6 m, R = 150 mm, T = 270 mm, width 1.20 m. Compute the effective span of a dog-legged stair, assuming the landing width = 1.20 m. Take M20 & Fe415. (7 Marks)

> **Solution sketch:**
> - Risers per flight = 3600/(2×150) = 12
> - Treads per flight = 11
> - Clear span per flight = 11 × 0.270 = 2.97 m
> - c/c distance = 2.97 + 1.20 = 4.17 m
> - Effective span = lesser of (4.17 m, 2.97 + 0.13) = **3.10 m**
> - Loads, BM, SF, design as per Question A workflow.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> 🚨 **Where students lose marks in staircase problems:**
> 1. **Load calculation trap** – Loads on inclined surface vs horizontal projection. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting $\sec\theta$** on slab self-weight. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Wrong effective span** – Using clear span only without adding d. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Not using M20/Fe415** values in design (interchanging 25/24 for plain/RCC concrete). (–1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping nominal shear reinforcement** even when shear is low. (–1 mark)
> 6. **No distribution steel mention** – IS 456 mandates it. (–1 mark)
> 7. **Main steel direction wrong** – Should be at bottom, parallel to flight. (–2 marks if reversed)

> [!TIP]
> Always draw a **plan + cross-section** diagram first, mark the **direction of main steel**, and **highlight the span direction**. This is a guaranteed 1–2 mark bonus.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

- 📌 **Staircase** = inclined flexural member providing vertical circulation.
- 📌 **Component kit:** Tread, Riser, Flight, Landing, Waist Slab, Nosing, Going.
- 📌 **Most common type** in KTU problems = **Dog-legged stair**.
- 📌 **Effective span** = lesser of (c/c supports) OR (clear span + effective depth), per **IS 456 Cl. 33.1**.
- 📌 **Tread:** 250–300 mm; **Riser:** 150–190 mm; **2R + T:** 580–640 mm.
- 📌 **Slope correction factor** $\sec\theta$ applied **only to waist slab self-weight**, not to FF/LL.
- 📌 **Loads always computed on horizontal projection** (per m width).
- 📌 **Step wedge weight** $= 12R$ kN/m² (using plain concrete $\gamma = 24$).
- 📌 **Partial safety factor** = 1.5 (DL + LL) per IS 456 Table 18.
- 📌 **Bending moment** $M_u = w_u L^2/8$; **Shear** $V_u = w_u L/2$.
- 📌 **Main steel** at **bottom of waist slab**, parallel to the flight direction.
- 📌 **Distribution steel** transverse, minimum 0.12 % of cross-sectional area.
- 📌 **Waist slab thickness** ≈ $L_{eff}/20$ to $L_{eff}/30$.
- 📌 **Code trio:** **IS 456:2000** + **IS 875 (Parts 1 & 2)** + **SP 34**.

> 🎯 **Last-line mnemonic (for viva & exam):**
> *"Tread-Riser-Flight-Landing-Waist — that's the staircase, and main steel goes where the load goes — to the bottom of the waist, along the flight."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modes of Failure and Analysis of Short Axially Loaded Compression Members under Axial Load with Uniaxial and Biaxial Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCET404 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Modes of Failure and Analysis of Short Axially Loaded Compression Members under Axial Load with Uniaxial and Biaxial Bending ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Compression Members — The Formal KTU Definition A **compression member** is a structural element that primarily resists axial compressive forces combined with bending moments and shears. In reinforced concrete construction, these are typically called **columns** (vertical) or **struts** (inclined/horizontal). The term encompasses pedestals, wall-like members, and piers. A **short column** is a compression member whose effective length-to-least-lateral-dimension ratio is small enough that **lateral buckling (secondary effects) can be neglected** in design. According to **IS 456:2000, Clause 25.1.2**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **IS 456:2000 Limit for Short Columns** > - For **rectangular sections**: $\dfrac{L_{ef}}{D} \leq 12$ > - For **circular sections**: $\dfrac{L_{ef}}{D} \leq 4$ > > where $L_{ef}$ = effective length, $D$ = least lateral dimension, and $D$ = diameter for circular sections. > > Beyond these limits, the member is a **slender (long) column** and second-order effects must be considered. ### 1.2 Modes of Failure — The Three Fundamental States > [!NOTE] > **Three Modes of Failure of a Compression Member (Per IS 456, SP-16 Framework)** A short column under combined axial load ($P_u$) and uniaxial/biaxial bending can fail in **one of three distinct modes** depending on the magnitude of eccentricity ($e = M_u / P_u$): 1. **Pure Compression Failure** (Axial / Concentric Dominant) - Occurs when the load is very nearly axial, $e \approx 0$ (or below minimum eccentricity). - **Entire section is in uniform compression.** Failure occurs by **crushing of concrete** when the combined stress reaches the limiting compressive strength. - Tension reinforcement is **not yielded**; in fact it is in compression. - Governing equation: $P_{uz} = 0.4\, f_{ck}\, A_g + 0.67\, f_y\, A_{sc}$ 2. **Compression Failure with Tension Cracking** (Eccentricity small but non-zero) - $e$ is small; the section has a small tension zone that cracks, but the neutral axis is **inside** the section. - **Tension steel yields** and **compression concrete crushes** — both occur near ultimate. - Section is "**compression-controlled**" but with a partial tension zone. 3. **Tension Failure** (Bending Dominant) - $e$ is large; the **neutral axis lies outside the section** (essentially a beam-like behaviour). - Tension steel yields **first**, but **concrete does not crush** at the limit state. - Failure is initiated by **excessive cracking and deflection** in the tension zone. ### 1.3 The Balanced Condition — Boundary Between Modes 2 and 3 The **balanced condition** is the transition point where **both tension steel yielding and concrete crushing occur simultaneously**. It is a critical reference in the interaction diagram. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Balanced Eccentricity for a Symmetrically Reinforced Rectangular Column** (approximate): > $$e_{bal} \approx \frac{0.17\,D + 0.67\,p\,f_y\,(d - d')}{2}$$ > > where $p = \dfrac{A_{sc}}{b\,d}$ is the steel ratio, $D$ = overall depth. > > - If $e < e_{bal}$ → **Compression failure** (Modes 1 or 2) > - If $e > e_{bal}$ → **Tension failure** (Mode 3) ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Leaning Soda Can > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Intuition: The Soda Can Analogy** > > Imagine an empty **soda can standing on a table**: > - **Straight, axial load (Mode 1)**: Push straight down on the can. The entire wall crushes uniformly. This is **pure compression failure**. > - **Slight tilt (Mode 2)**: Push down at a small angle. One side of the can buckles a little, the other side cracks slightly. The wall is partially crushed, partially bent. > - **Large tilt (Mode 3)**: Push the can sideways. The far side crumples; the near side may even develop a tear. The failure is dominated by **bending**, not crushing. > > The role of the can's wall thickness is similar to that of **concrete cover + steel**; reinforcement is what resists the "tear" on the tension side. ### 1.5 Uniaxial vs. Biaxial Bending - **Uniaxial bending**: The column bends about **one principal axis** only (e.g., due to wind in one direction, or a beam framing in on one side). The eccentricity $e_x$ (or $e_y$) exists; the other is essentially zero. - **Biaxial bending**: The column bends about **both principal axes simultaneously** (e.g., a corner column receiving beams from two perpendicular directions). Both $e_x$ and $e_y$ are non-zero. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Corner columns and edge columns of buildings are typical biaxial bending cases** and are extremely common in real KTU project designs. --- ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Uniaxial Bending Interaction Diagram (P–M Curve) For a short column under axial load $P_u$ and uniaxial moment $M_u$ about the X-axis, the design strength is given by a **P–M interaction curve** (a 2D curve), which is essentially a 2D slice of the full 3D failure surface. The curve has **5 key points** that fully describe it (per SP-16): | Point | Description | Tension Steel Status | Compression Steel Status | Concrete | |-------|-------------|----------------------|--------------------------|----------| | **A** | Pure bending, $P_u = 0$ | Yields | May yield or not | Crushing | | **B** | Tension failure limit (low $P_u$, high $e$) | Yields | Yields | Not crushing | | **C** | **Balanced** condition | Yields | Yields | Crushing | | **D** | Compression failure limit (high $P_u$, low $e$) | Yields | Yields | Crushing | | **E** | Pure axial, $M_u = 0$ | Compression | Compression | Crushing | > [!NOTE] > In **SP-16 design charts**, dimensionless interaction diagrams are given as a family of curves for various steel ratios ($p/f_{ck}$), reinforcement layouts, and grades of concrete and steel. The KTU board exam almost always allows direct use of these charts. ### 2.2 Concentric Axial Load Capacity (Point E on the Curve) Per **IS 456:2000, Clause 25.5.1 (and G.2)**: $$P_{uz} = 0.45\, f_{ck}\, A_c + 0.75\, f_y\, A_{sc}$$ The **design strength** of an axially loaded short column is then $P_u = 0.4 \times P_{uz}$ (i.e., $P_{uz}$ is further reduced by a capacity reduction factor of 0.4 due to accidental eccentricities): $$P_{u,\,conc} = 0.4 \left(0.45\, f_{ck}\, A_c + 0.75\, f_y\, A_{sc}\right) = 0.18\, f_{ck}\, A_c + 0.30\, f_y\, A_{sc}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The factor **0.4** in $P_u = 0.4\,P_{uz}$ is an additional safety multiplier on top of the usual $\gamma_m$ partial safety factors, applied because actual columns are never perfectly concentric. ### 2.3 Minimum Eccentricity (IS 456, Cl. 25.4) For design, even a "concentric" column must be designed for **at least the minimum eccentricity**: $$e_{x,min} = \max\left(\dfrac{L}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30},\ 20\,\text{mm}\right) \quad ; \quad e_{y,min} = \max\left(\dfrac{L}{500} + \dfrac{B}{30},\ 20\,\text{mm}\right)$$ ### 2.4 Biaxial Bending — The 3D Failure Surface For biaxial bending, the failure surface is a **3D interaction surface in (P, M_x, M_y) space**. The intersection of this surface with constant-$P$ planes gives biaxial bending "contour" curves, and the intersection with $M_y = 0$ gives the uniaxial P–M curve of the previous section. Three classical methods are used in practice: #### Method 1 — Bresler's **Reciprocal Load Method** (1951) For a section with given reinforcement, the **ultimate load under biaxial bending** is: $$\boxed{\;\dfrac{1}{P_u} = \dfrac{1}{P_{ux}} + \dfrac{1}{P_{uy}} - \dfrac{1}{P_{uz}}\;}$$ where: - $P_u$ = ultimate load under biaxial bending (with $M_x = P_u e_x$ and $M_y = P_u e_y$) - $P_{ux}$ = ultimate load under uniaxial bending about X-axis alone, with eccentricity $e_x$ - $P_{uy}$ = ultimate load under uniaxial bending about Y-axis alone, with eccentricity $e_y$ - $P_{uz}$ = ultimate load under pure axial compression > [!NOTE] > This is a **design method** (find $P_u$ for a given section) — used to check a trial section against a known load $P$. #### Method 2 — Bresler–Parme Method (1958) A **dimensionless** version of Bresler's method, with eccentricities normalized by section dimensions $e_x/D$ and $e_y/B$. The read-out $P_{u,bi}$ is obtained from a family of biaxial interaction charts, and the same reciprocal form holds: $$\dfrac{1}{P_{u,bi}} = \dfrac{1}{P_{ux}} + \dfrac{1}{P_{uy}} - \dfrac{1}{P_{uz}}$$ #### Method 3 — IS 456:2000, Clause 39.5 (Simplified Indian Code Approach) > [!IMPORTANT] > **IS 456:2000 Cl. 39.5 — Design Under Axial Load and Biaxial Bending** > > For a section with **load $P_u$ and biaxial moments $M_x$ and $M_y$**, define: > - $P_{uz}$ = pure axial ultimate load > - $M_{ux}$ = uniaxial moment capacity **with axial load $P_u$** (about X-axis) > - $M_{uy}$ = uniaxial moment capacity **with axial load $P_u$** (about Y-axis) > - $M_{ax}$, $M_{ay}$ = pure bending moment capacity about X / Y axes > > The design check is: > > **Case (i) — When $P_u / P_{uz}$ is large (compression-controlled):** > $$\dfrac{P_u}{P_{uz}} + \dfrac{M_x}{M_{ux}} + \dfrac{M_y}{M_{uy}} \leq 1.0$$ > > **Case (ii) — When $P_u / P_{uz}$ is small (tension-controlled, $P_u \leq 0.1\,P_{uz}$):** > $$\dfrac{M_x}{M_{ax}} + \dfrac{M_y}{M_{ay}} \leq 1.0$$ > > **Generalised form (linear interaction envelope):** > $$\left(\dfrac{M_x}{M_{ax}}\right)^{\alpha_1} + \left(\dfrac{M_y}{M_{ay}}\right)^{\alpha_2} \leq 1.0$$ > where $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ are exponents obtained from SP-16 charts (typically $\alpha \approx 1$ to $2$). ### 2.5 Eccentricity Thresholds for Biaxial Bending (IS 456, Cl. 25.4) > [!IMPORTANT] > **When to Treat as Uniaxial vs. Biaxial** > - If $e_x \leq 0.05\,D$ **and** $e_y \leq 0.05\,B$ → **Pure axial** (no biaxial check) > - If $e_x > 0.05\,D$ **and** $e_y \leq 0.05\,B$ → **Uniaxial about X-axis only** > - If $e_x \leq 0.05\,D$ **and** $e_y > 0.05\,B$ → **Uniaxial about Y-axis only** > - If $e_x > 0.05\,D$ **and** $e_y > 0.05\,B$ → **Biaxial bending design required** ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Short column limit (rectangular) | $L_{ef} / D \leq 12$ | IS 456 Cl. 25.1.2 | | 2 | Short column limit (circular) | $L_{ef} / D \leq 4$ | IS 456 Cl. 25.1.2 | | 3 | Min. eccentricity | $e_{min} = \max\left(\dfrac{L}{500} + \dfrac{D}{30},\ 20\right)$ | mm; Cl. 25.4 | | 4 | Pure axial capacity (gross) | $P_{uz} = 0.45\,f_{ck}\,A_c + 0.75\,f_y\,A_{sc}$ | N | | 5 | Design axial capacity (concentric) | $P_u = 0.4\,P_{uz}$ | N; with $\gamma_m$ already in 0.45/0.75 | | 6 | Balanced eccentricity (approx.) | $e_{bal} \approx 0.17\,D$ for typical sections | mm | | 7 | Bresler reciprocal load | $1/P_u = 1/P_{ux} + 1/P_{uy} - 1/P_{uz}$ | N | | 8 | IS 456 — uniaxial check | $P_u/P_{uz} + M_x/M_{ux} \leq 1$ | dimensionless | | 9 | IS 456 — biaxial (low P) | $M_x/M_{ax} + M_y/M_{ay} \leq 1$ | dimensionless | | 10 | Biaxial eccentricity threshold | $e > 0.05\,D$ or $0.05\,B$ | triggers biaxial check | > **Note on table**: In the table above, all absolute values such as $\left\vert\,e\,\right\vert$ are rendered with `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Role of Biaxial Design | |-------------|------------------------| | **Corner columns in multi-storey buildings** | Receive beams from two perpendicular directions → biaxial bending critical | | **Bridge piers under skewed loading** | Skew angle produces biaxial moment | | **Seismic columns** | Inertia forces in both X and Y directions → biaxial design governs | | **Columns supporting crane beams** | Vertical load + lateral surge + longitudinal force → biaxial | | **Sign and hoarding posts** | Wind from any direction → biaxial moment | --- ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Pure Axial Load Capacity $P_{uz}$ (Per IS 456) We consider a short axially loaded column with: - Gross cross-sectional area $A_g$ - Area of concrete $A_c = A_g - A_{sc}$ - Area of compression steel $A_{sc}$ - Compressive strength of concrete $f_{ck}$, yield strength of steel $f_y$ **Step 1 — Strain compatibility under uniform strain $\varepsilon_c$** The whole section is under uniform compressive strain $\varepsilon_c$. The corresponding stresses are: - Concrete: $f_{c} = 0.67\,f_{ck}$ (already including the material partial safety factor $\gamma_m = 1.5$, since $0.67 \approx 1/1.5$) - Steel: $f_{sc} = 0.67\,f_y$ (similarly, the design yield of steel is $0.67\,f_y$) **Step 2 — Force equilibrium** $$P_{uz} = f_c \cdot A_c + f_{sc} \cdot A_{sc}$$ Substituting the design stresses: $$P_{uz} = (0.67\,f_{ck})\,(A_g - A_{sc}) + (0.67\,f_y)\,A_{sc}$$ **Step 3 — Re-arranging** $$P_{uz} = 0.67\,f_{ck}\,A_g - 0.67\,f_{ck}\,A_{sc} + 0.67\,f_y\,A_{sc}$$ $$\boxed{\;P_{uz} = 0.67\,f_{ck}\,A_g + 0.67\,(f_y - f_{ck})\,A_{sc}\;}$$ **Step 4 — IS 456 simplified form (Cl. G.2)** IS 456:2000 simplifies this by setting the steel's contribution directly: $$P_{uz} = 0.45\,f_{ck}\,A_c + 0.75\,f_y\,A_{sc}$$ The constants 0.45 and 0.75 incorporate both $\gamma_m$ and an additional reduction for accidental eccentricity. The design axial capacity is then $P_u = 0.4\,P_{uz}$. > [!NOTE] > The **0.4 factor** is a strict KTU-board-favourite figure. Examiners expect the full chain: $P_u = 0.4\,P_{uz} = 0.4\,(0.45\,f_{ck}\,A_c + 0.75\,f_y\,A_{sc})$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Bresler's Reciprocal Load Equation **Step 1 — Define the failure surface** Let $F(P, M_x, M_y) = 0$ describe the failure surface in load space. This is a **convex** surface (a property established experimentally by Bresler, 1951). **Step 2 — Pick three reference points on the surface** - **Point A** (uniaxial bending about X): $(P_{ux},\ P_{ux}\,e_x,\ 0)$ - **Point B** (uniaxial bending about Y): $(P_{uy},\ 0,\ P_{uy}\,e_y)$ - **Point C** (pure axial): $(P_{uz},\ 0,\ 0)$ **Step 3 — Construct a hyperboloid approximation** For a convex surface passing through A, B, C, Bresler proposed the **hyperboloid of one sheet**: $$\dfrac{1}{P} = a_0 + a_1\,M_x + a_2\,M_y + a_3\,M_x\,M_y$$ To make the surface pass through points A, B, C and the uniaxial reference slopes, we set $M_x M_y$ cross-term coefficient to zero and obtain: $$\dfrac{1}{P} = a_0 + a_1\,M_x + a_2\,M_y$$ **Step 4 — Apply the three boundary conditions** - At point A ($M_x = P_{ux} e_x$, $M_y = 0$): $\dfrac{1}{P_{ux}} = a_0 + a_1\,P_{ux}\,e_x$ - At point B ($M_x = 0$, $M_y = P_{uy} e_y$): $\dfrac{1}{P_{uy}} = a_0 + a_2\,P_{uy}\,e_y$ - At point C ($M_x = M_y = 0$): $\dfrac{1}{P_{uz}} = a_0$ **Step 5 — Solve for $a_0, a_1, a_2$** $$a_0 = \dfrac{1}{P_{uz}} \quad ; \quad a_1 = \dfrac{1}{P_{ux}\,e_x}\left(\dfrac{1}{P_{ux}} - \dfrac{1}{P_{uz}}\right) \quad ; \quad a_2 = \dfrac{1}{P_{uy}\,e_y}\left(\dfrac{1}{P_{uy}} - \dfrac{1}{P_{uz}}\right)$$ **Step 6 — Substitute back to obtain the working equation** For a general load point with $M_x = P_u\,e_x$ and $M_y = P_u\,e_y$: $$\dfrac{1}{P_u} = \dfrac{1}{P_{uz}} + \dfrac{1}{P_{ux}\,e_x}\left(\dfrac{1}{P_{ux}} - \dfrac{1}{P_{uz}}\right) \cdot P_u\,e_x + \dfrac{1}{P_{uy}\,e_y}\left(\dfrac{1}{P_{uy}} - \dfrac{1}{P_{uz}}\right) \cdot P_u\,e_y$$ Simplifying: $$\dfrac{1}{P_u} = \dfrac{1}{P_{uz}} + \left(\dfrac{1}{P_{ux}} - \dfrac{1}{P_{uz}}\right) + \left(\dfrac{1}{P_{uy}} - \dfrac{1}{P_{uz}}\right)$$ $$\boxed{\;\dfrac{1}{P_u} = \dfrac{1}{P_{ux}} + \dfrac{1}{P_{uy}} - \dfrac{1}{P_{uz}}\;}$$ This is **Bresler's Reciprocal Load Equation** — exact within the convex-surface assumption. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of the IS 456 Simplified Biaxial Check **Step 1 — Define a generalised load-moment envelope** Consider a non-dimensional form of the biaxial interaction surface: $$\left(\dfrac{M_x}{M_{ux}}\right)^{\alpha} + \left(\dfrac{M_y}{M_{uy}}\right)^{\beta} \leq 1.0$$ For a **linear envelope** (conservative, $\alpha = \beta = 1$): $$\dfrac{M_x}{M_{ux}} + \dfrac{M_y}{M_{uy}} \leq 1.0 \quad \text{(for small } P_u\text{)}$$ **Step 2 — Add the axial load term** When $P_u$ is non-negligible, the load itself "uses up" a portion of the cross-section's capacity. The simplest extension is the **Newmark linear approximation**: $$\dfrac{P_u}{P_{uz}} + \dfrac{M_x}{M_{ux}} + \dfrac{M_y}{M_{uy}} \leq 1.0$$ This is the **IS 456 Cl. 39.5.1 simplified check** for compression-controlled sections. **Step 3 — Justify the limit for $P_u / P_{uz}$** At low $P_u$, the capacity is governed by **pure bending**; at high $P_u$, by the uniaxial-bending-with-axial-load state. The transition is smooth, and IS 456 conservatively allows either form depending on which is more critical. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Uniaxial Bending with Axial Load) > **KTU-Style Problem:** A short column, $400 \times 500\,\text{mm}$, $f_{ck} = 20\,\text{N/mm}^2$, $f_y = 500\,\text{N/mm}^2$, has 6 bars of 20 mm dia. Compute its **design strength** under: > 1. Pure axial load > 2. Axial load with uniaxial bending about the major axis (eccentricity $e_x = 100\,\text{mm}$) **Solution:** **Step 1 — Section properties** $$A_g = 400 \times 500 = 200{,}000\,\text{mm}^2$$ $$A_{sc} = 6 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 6 \times 314.16 = 1884.96\,\text{mm}^2$$ **Step 2 — Pure axial capacity (per IS 456 Cl. G.2)** $$P_{uz} = 0.45 \times 20 \times (200{,}000 - 1885) + 0.75 \times 500 \times 1885$$ $$P_{uz} = 0.45 \times 20 \times 198{,}115 + 0.75 \times 500 \times 1885$$ $$P_{uz} = 1{,}783{,}035 + 706{,}875 = 2{,}489{,}910\,\text{N} \approx 2490\,\text{kN}$$ **Design concentric capacity:** $$P_u = 0.4 \times 2{,}490 = 996\,\text{kN}$$ **Step 3 — Uniaxial capacity with $e_x = 100$ mm** From SP-16 charts (or analytical computation of the P–M curve) at $e_x = 100$ mm: $$P_{ux} \approx 1200\,\text{kN} \quad \text{(read from chart)}$$ > In a board exam, students would quote the SP-16 read-off and then apply **Bresler's formula** for the biaxial case. For uniaxial, the check is direct. --- ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Flowchart — Decision Logic for Axial + Bending Column Design ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Start: Given Pu, Mx, My] --> A1[Compute eccentricities
