How is the balanced condition used to distinguish between them?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **The three modes of failure are:** > 1. **Pure compression failure** — occurs under nearly concentric loading ($e \approx 0$). The whole section is in uniform compression, and failure is by crushing of concrete. Steel does not yield. > 2. **Compression failure with tension cracking** — occurs at small eccentricities. The neutral axis lies inside the section. Tension steel yields and concrete crushes nearly simultaneously. > 3. **Tension failure** — occurs at large eccentricities. The neutral axis lies outside the section. Tension steel yields first, but concrete does not crush. > > **Balanced condition:** It is the boundary between Mode 2 and Mode 3, where tension steel yielding and concrete crushing occur simultaneously. It is characterised by a balanced eccentricity $e_{bal}$, with $P_{bal}$ and $M_{bal}$. (1 mark for the list, 1 mark for explaining the balanced condition, 1 mark for the discrimination between modes.) #### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Define a short column as per IS 456:2000. Under what conditions does IS 456 permit a column to be designed as axially loaded (ignoring biaxial bending)?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition of a short column (IS 456 Cl. 25.1.2):** A column is classified as short if its effective length to least lateral dimension ratio is small enough that slenderness effects can be neglected. Specifically: $L_{ef}/D \leq 12$ for rectangular sections, and $L_{ef}/D \leq 4$ for circular sections. (1.5 marks) > > **Conditions for ignoring biaxial bending (IS 456 Cl. 25.4):** A column may be designed as axially loaded when **both** the following hold: > - $e_x \leq 0.05\,D$ (eccentricity about X-axis is at most 5% of section depth), **and** > - $e_y \leq 0.05\,B$ (eccentricity about Y-axis is at most 5% of section width). > > (1.5 marks for stating both threshold conditions and reference to Cl. 25.4.) --- ### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice #### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **A short reinforced concrete column of size 400 mm × 500 mm is subjected to a service axial load of 1500 kN and service moments $M_{x,sx} = 75$ kN·m and $M_{y,sx} = 50$ kN·m about the major and minor axes respectively. Use M20 concrete and Fe500 steel. Design the longitudinal reinforcement using the IS 456:2000 simplified biaxial bending check. Assume effective cover = 50 mm and 1.5% longitudinal steel.** ##### (a) [7 Marks] — Compute the factored loads, eccentricities, and check whether biaxial bending design is required. Determine $P_{uz}$, $M_{ax}$, $M_{ay}$ from the given data. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Factored loads (load factor 1.5)** $$P_u = 1.5 \times 1500 = 2250\,\text{kN}$$ $$M_{x,u} = 1.5 \times 75 = 112.5\,\text{kN·m}$$ $$M_{y,u} = 1.5 \times 50 = 75.0\,\text{kN·m}$$ **Step 2 — Eccentricities** $$e_x = \dfrac{M_{x,u}}{P_u} = \dfrac{112.5 \times 10^6}{2250 \times 10^3} = 50\,\text{mm}$$ $$e_y = \dfrac{M_{y,u}}{P_u} = \dfrac{75 \times 10^6}{2250 \times 10^3} = 33.33\,\text{mm}$$ **Step 3 — Threshold check (IS 456 Cl. 25.4)** $$0.05 \times D = 0.05 \times 500 = 25\,\text{mm}; \quad e_x = 50\,\text{mm} > 25\,\text{mm}\ \checkmark$$ $$0.05 \times B = 0.05 \times 400 = 20\,\text{mm}; \quad e_y = 33.33\,\text{mm} > 20\,\text{mm}\ \checkmark$$ > **Biaxial bending design is required.** (1 mark) **Step 4 — Section & steel data** $$A_g = 400 \times 500 = 2 \times 10^5\,\text{mm}^2$$ $$A_{sc} = 0.015 \times 2 \times 10^5 = 3000\,\text{mm}^2$$ $$A_c = A_g - A_{sc} = 2 \times 10^5 - 3000 = 1.97 \times 10^5\,\text{mm}^2$$ **Step 5 — Pure axial capacity $P_{uz}$ (Cl. G.2)** $$P_{uz} = 0.45 \times 20 \times 1.97 \times 10^5 + 0.75 \times 500 \times 3000$$ $$P_{uz} = 1.773 \times 10^6 + 1.125 \times 10^6 = 2.898 \times 10^6\,\text{N} = 2898\,\text{kN}$$ **Step 6 — Pure bending capacities $M_{ax}$ and $M_{ay}$ (SP-16 / standard formula for Fe500, M20)** For 1.5% steel, M20, Fe500, the SP-16 dimensionless moment coefficient $K_{max} = 0.207$ (approx.). $$M_{ax} = K_{max} \cdot f_{ck} \cdot B \cdot D^2 = 0.207 \times 20 \times 400 \times 500^2 = 0.207 \times 20 \times 400 \times 250000 = 4.14 \times 10^8\,\text{N·mm} = 414\,\text{kN·m}$$ $$M_{ay} = K_{max} \cdot f_{ck} \cdot D \cdot B^2 = 0.207 \times 20 \times 500 \times 400^2 = 3.31 \times 10^8\,\text{N·mm} = 331\,\text{kN·m}$$ > (7 marks: 1 mark load factors, 1 mark eccentricities, 1 mark threshold check, 1 mark $P_{uz}$, 1 mark $M_{ax}$, 1 mark $M_{ay}$, 1 mark section/steel data.) ##### (b) [7 Marks] — Using the IS 456 simplified biaxial check, verify whether the section is safe. If not, suggest a revision. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute load ratio** $$\dfrac{P_u}{P_{uz}} = \dfrac{2250}{2898} = 0.776$$ Since $P_u / P_{uz} = 0.776 > 0.2$, we use the **Case (i) — compression-controlled** form: $$\dfrac{P_u}{P_{uz}} + \dfrac{M_x}{M_{ux}} + \dfrac{M_y}{M_{uy}} \leq 1.0$$ **Step 2 — Read $M_{ux}$ and $M_{uy}$ from SP-16 for the given $P_u$** From SP-16 chart for $p = 1.5\%$, M20, Fe500, at $P_u / f_{ck} A_g = 2250 \times 10^3 / (20 \times 2 \times 10^5) = 0.5625$: - $M_{ux} \approx 0.55 \times M_{ax} = 0.55 \times 414 = 228\,\text{kN·m}$ (read from chart) - $M_{uy} \approx 0.55 \times M_{ay} = 0.55 \times 331 = 182\,\text{kN·m}$ (read from chart) **Step 3 — Apply the check** $$\dfrac{2250}{2898} + \dfrac{112.5}{228} + \dfrac{75.0}{182} = 0.776 + 0.493 + 0.412 = 1.681 > 1.0$$ > **Result:** The section is **NOT SAFE** under the given factored loads. (2 marks) **Step 4 — Suggest revisions** - **Increase section size**: Try $450 \times 550$ mm (recompute the check). - **Increase steel ratio**: Try $p = 2.5\%$ (6 bars of 25 mm + 4 bars of 20 mm, with similar layout). - **Use high-grade concrete**: M25 or M30 increases $P_{uz}$ and $M_{ux}$, $M_{uy}$ substantially. - **Provide closer stirrups and confinement** to improve ductility. The most economical revision is usually a **combination of slight size increase + slightly higher steel** (e.g., $450 \times 500$ mm with 2.2% steel). (2 marks) **Step 5 — Re-verify with Bresler's reciprocal load method (as an alternative check)** $$P_{ux} \text{ at } e_x = 50\,\text{mm} = 2400\,\text{kN (from SP-16)}$$ $$P_{uy} \text{ at } e_y = 33.33\,\text{mm} = 2600\,\text{kN (from SP-16)}$$ $$\dfrac{1}{P_{u,bi}} = \dfrac{1}{2400} + \dfrac{1}{2600} - \dfrac{1}{2898} = 0.000417 + 0.000385 - 0.000345 = 0.000457$$ $$P_{u,bi} = 2188\,\text{kN} < P_u = 2250\,\text{kN}$$ > **Confirms the section is not safe** — both methods agree. (3 marks) --- #### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` **A short tied column of size 300 mm × 450 mm is reinforced with 8 bars of 20 mm diameter. Use M25 concrete and Fe415 steel. Determine the design strength of the column under: (a) Pure axial load, and (b) Biaxial bending with $e_x = 80$ mm and $e_y = 60$ mm. Use Bresler's reciprocal load method with SP-16 read-offs.** ##### (a) [7 Marks] — Compute the pure axial load capacity and explain the modes of failure of this column. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Section data** $$A_g = 300 \times 450 = 135{,}000\,\text{mm}^2$$ $$A_{sc} = 8 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 8 \times 314.16 = 2513.3\,\text{mm}^2$$ $$A_c = 135{,}000 - 2513 = 132{,}487\,\text{mm}^2$$ $$p = \dfrac{A_{sc}}{A_g} = \dfrac{2513}{135{,}000} = 1.862\%$$ **Step 2 — Pure axial load capacity (Cl. G.2)** $$P_{uz} = 0.45 \times 25 \times 132{,}487 + 0.75 \times 415 \times 2513$$ $$P_{uz} = 1{,}490{,}478 + 782{,}300 = 2{,}272{,}778\,\text{N} \approx 2273\,\text{kN}$$ **Step 3 — Design concentric capacity** $$P_u = 0.4 \times P_{uz} = 0.4 \times 2273 = 909.1\,\text{kN}$$ **Step 4 — Minimum eccentricity check (Cl. 25.4)** Assume $L_{ef} = 3.0$ m = 3000 mm (short column). $$e_{x,min} = \max\left(\dfrac{3000}{500} + \dfrac{450}{30},\ 20\right) = \max(6 + 15,\ 20) = 21\,\text{mm}$$ $$e_{y,min} = \max\left(\dfrac{3000}{500} + \dfrac{300}{30},\ 20\right) = \max(6 + 10,\ 20) = 20\,\text{mm}$$ These are negligible; the section qualifies as a "concentrically loaded" column in design. **Step 5 — Modes of failure discussion (3 marks)** - If the load is exactly concentric → **Mode 1: Pure compression failure** (concrete crushing). This is the most likely mode for a column with no moment. - If the column is loaded with small eccentricities ($e_x, e_y < 0.05D, 0.05B$) → still mode 1. - At larger eccentricities (e.g., 80 mm, 60 mm) → mode 2 or mode 3 (in part b). - The **balanced eccentricity** for this section: $e_{bal} \approx 0.17D = 0.17 \times 450 \approx 76$ mm. So $e_x = 80$ mm is just past balanced (tension side). > (7 marks: 1 mark section data, 1 mark $P_{uz}$, 1 mark $P_u$, 1 mark min ecc check, 3 marks modes of failure.) ##### (b) [7 Marks] — Using Bresler's reciprocal load method, find the biaxial load capacity of the column with $e_x = 80$ mm, $e_y = 60$ mm. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Verify biaxial requirement** $$0.05\,D = 22.5\,\text{mm}; \quad e_x = 80 > 22.5 \ \checkmark$$ $$0.05\,B = 15\,\text{mm}; \quad e_y = 60 > 15 \ \checkmark$$ > **Biaxial bending is required.** (1 mark) **Step 2 — Read $P_{ux}$ from SP-16 (M25, Fe415, p = 1.86%, uniaxial bending about X-axis, $e_x = 80$ mm)** $$P_{ux} \approx 1100\,\text{kN}$$ **Step 3 — Read $P_{uy}$ from SP-16 (uniaxial bending about Y-axis, $e_y = 60$ mm)** $$P_{uy} \approx 1300\,\text{kN}$$ **Step 4 — Recall $P_{uz}$ from part (a)** $$P_{uz} = 2273\,\text{kN}$$ **Step 5 — Apply Bresler's reciprocal load equation** $$\dfrac{1}{P_{u,bi}} = \dfrac{1}{P_{ux}} + \dfrac{1}{P_{uy}} - \dfrac{1}{P_{uz}}$$ $$\dfrac{1}{P_{u,bi}} = \dfrac{1}{1100} + \dfrac{1}{1300} - \dfrac{1}{2273}$$ $$\dfrac{1}{P_{u,bi}} = 0.000909 + 0.000769 - 0.000440 = 0.001238$$ $$P_{u,bi} = \dfrac{1}{0.001238} = 807.8\,\text{kN}$$ **Step 6 — Summary and result** The biaxial design strength of the column is **$\approx 808$ kN**. > Note that this is significantly less than the uniaxial capacities ($P_{ux} = 1100$ kN, $P_{uy} = 1300$ kN) and also less than the pure axial capacity ($P_{uz} = 2273$ kN). The biaxial effect reduces the load capacity by **~26%** compared to the uniaxial case — a substantial reduction that KTU students must appreciate. (2 marks) > (7 marks: 1 mark threshold check, 1.5 marks $P_{ux}$, 1.5 marks $P_{uy}$, 2 marks Bresler equation, 1 mark final result + interpretation.) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting the 0.4 factor**: Many students write $P_{uz}$ as the design strength. Always remember $P_u = 0.4 \cdot P_{uz}$ for **concentric** design. > 2. **Confusing $M_{ax}$ vs. $M_{ux}$**: $M_{ax}$ is the **pure bending** moment capacity. $M_{ux}$ is the moment capacity **at a particular $P_u$** (read from the P–M chart for that specific axial load). Mixing these up loses **3 marks** immediately. > 3. **Forgetting the minimum eccentricity**: IS 456 Cl. 25.4 mandates $e_{min}$ — never assume truly axial loading in design. > 4. **Wrong Bresler form**: The equation is $1/P_u = 1/P_{ux} + 1/P_{uy} - 1/P_{uz}$, **not** $1/P_u = 1/P_{ux} + 1/P_{uy} - 1/P_{u,bi}$. The reciprocal is of the **uniaxial capacities**, not the biaxial one. > 5. **Mixing load factors**: $P_{uz}$ uses **unfactored** ($f_{ck}$, $f_y$ with 0.45, 0.75 already including $\gamma_m$). The **applied** load must be **factored** ($1.5 \times$ service). > 6. **SP-16 not allowed in some exam settings**: Some KTU exams disallow SP-16 charts; you must derive $P_{ux}$ analytically using strain compatibility. Always read your question paper carefully. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 3 — Short Column Under Axial Load & Bending: Quick-Fire Revision** > > ✅ **Short column limits** (IS 456 Cl. 25.1.2): $L_{ef}/D \leq 12$ (rect.), $\leq 4$ (circular). > > ✅ **Three modes of failure**: > 1. Pure compression (crushing of concrete) > 2. Compression + tension cracking (tension steel yields + concrete crushes) > 3. Tension failure (tension steel yields; concrete does not crush) > > ✅ **Pure axial design capacity**: $P_u = 0.4\,P_{uz} = 0.4\,(0.45\,f_{ck}\,A_c + 0.75\,f_y\,A_{sc})$. > > ✅ **Minimum eccentricity** (Cl. 25.4): $e_{min} = \max(L/500 + D/30,\ 20\,\text{mm})$ in each direction. > > ✅ **Biaxial bending threshold** (Cl. 25.4): Biaxial design is required **only** when $e_x > 0.05\,D$ **and** $e_y > 0.05\,B$. > > ✅ **Bresler's reciprocal load equation**: > $$\dfrac{1}{P_u} = \dfrac{1}{P_{ux}} + \dfrac{1}{P_{uy}} - \dfrac{1}{P_{uz}}$$ > $P_{ux}$ = uniaxial capacity with $e_x$ only; $P_{uy}$ = uniaxial capacity with $e_y$ only; $P_{uz}$ = pure axial capacity. > > ✅ **Bresler–Parme method**: Same formula but using dimensionless $e_x/D$ and $e_y/B$ read from charts. > > ✅ **IS 456:2000 Cl. 39.5 simplified check**: > - For $P_u$ large: $\dfrac{P_u}{P_{uz}} + \dfrac{M_x}{M_{ux}} + \dfrac{M_y}{M_{uy}} \leq 1.0$ > - For $P_u$ small: $\dfrac{M_x}{M_{ax}} + \dfrac{M_y}{M_{ay}} \leq 1.0$ > > ✅ **SP-16** is the standard design-aid publication for interaction charts (universally accepted in KTU exams unless explicitly disallowed). > > ✅ **$M_{ax}$ vs. $M_{ux}$ distinction**: $M_{ax}$ = pure bending capacity ($P=0$); $M_{ux}$ = moment capacity at the **specific applied** $P_u$ (read off the uniaxial chart at the correct $P_u$). > > ✅ **Practical real-world cases**: corner columns, bridge piers under skew/seismic loading, signage posts, crane columns — all are biaxial. > > ✅ **Reduction in capacity due to biaxiality**: Typically 20–30% lower than the uniaxial case — a critical design insight for corner columns of multi-storey buildings.

topics.title: Numerical Problems on Design of Short Columns under Axial Load with Uniaxial and Biaxial Bending
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCET404 - DESIGN OF CONCRETE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems on Design of Short Columns under Axial Load with Uniaxial and Biaxial Bending

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **short column** is a compression member in which the slenderness ratios $\lambda_x = l_{ex}/D$ and $\lambda_y = l_{ey}/b$ are governed by the **IS 456:2000** limits (Cl. 25.1.2). For a braced column, the limit is **12** (minor axis) and for an unbraced column, the limit is **10**. Short columns fail by **material crushing** (compressive failure) rather than by elastic buckling, so the design is governed by **combined axial load and bending** (P–M interaction).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 456:2000 Cl. 25.1.2 (Slenderness Limits for Short Columns)**
> - Braced column: $\lambda \leq 12$ (minor axis) and $\lambda \leq 60$ (major axis limit is normally not critical for short columns)
> - Unbraced column: $\lambda \leq 10$
> - Unsupported length $l = 0.85\,l_0$ for simply supported ends, etc., governed by Cl. 25.2 (effective length)

The design situations covered in this module are:

| Loading Type | IS 456 Clause | Typical Member |
|---|---|---|
| Pure Axial + Uniaxial Bending | Cl. 39.3 | Edge column (one-way frame) |
| Pure Axial + Biaxial Bending | Cl. 39.4 | Corner column (two-way frame) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **flagpole** planted at the corner of a building. When wind blows only from the West, the pole bends only East–West — this is **uniaxial bending**. When wind blows diagonally (say, from the South-West), the pole must resist bending about both its own vertical axes simultaneously — this is **biaxial bending**. The flagpole (column) also has its own weight pushing down — the **axial load**.

In an actual building:
- A **middle column** of a multi-storey frame carries only axial load.
- An **edge column** of a frame (with slabs on one side and beams in one direction) carries axial load + bending in one plane (uniaxial).
- A **corner column** of a frame (slabs on two sides, beams in two directions) carries axial load + bending in two planes (biaxial).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real Engineering Insight:** In KTU-endorsed design practice (IS 456:2000), a column is NEVER designed as "axially loaded" alone. The code mandates a **minimum eccentricity** ($e_{x,min} = 0.05D$ in that direction, or 20 mm, whichever is greater) to account for construction imperfections, material variability, and the absence of perfect symmetry. Hence every KTU problem on "axially loaded columns" technically reduces to an axial load + uniaxial/biaxial bending problem.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (IS 456:2000 Defaults)

- **Minimum eccentricity** in any direction: $e_{min} = \max(0.05D, 20\text{ mm})$
- **Design strength of concrete**: $\sigma_{cbc} = 0.45\,f_{ck}$ (short-term, in combined P–M)
- **Design strength of steel** in compression: $f_{yd} = 0.87\,f_y$ (mild) or $0.75\,f_y$ for bond in SP-16
- **Partial safety factors** for ultimate limit state: $\gamma_c = 1.5$, $\gamma_s = 1.15$
- **Maximum axial load** (pure compression, Cl. 39.3): $P_{u,max} = 0.45\,f_{ck}\cdot A_c + 0.75\,f_y\cdot A_{sc}$
- **Maximum allowed steel**: **6 %** of gross cross-section ($A_g$), minimum **0.8 %**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Columns (IS 456:2000 Cl. 25.1)

A compression member is classified as **short** if the slenderness ratios are less than the **effective length / lateral dimension** thresholds. Beyond these limits, the column is **slender** and additional moments by Cl. 39.7 must be considered (out of scope for this module).

## 2.2 Effective Length (Cl. 25.2 — to be used in stiffness calculations only)

For a short column design in Module 3, the effective length $l_{ex}, l_{ey}$ is required only to **check the slenderness limit**, not to amplify moments. Typical values used in KTU problems:

| End Conditions (Braced) | Effective Length $l_{ex} = K L$ |
|---|---|
| Both ends hinged | **1.0 L** |
| One end fixed, other hinged | **0.8 L** |
| Both ends fixed | **0.65 L** |
| One end fixed, other free (cantilever) | **2.0 L** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** For *braced* frames (in-fill walls / cross-bracing present in both directions), $l_{ex} = l_{ey} = 1.0 L$ for hinged-hinged. Many students forget to subtract the depth of the beam from the clear story height to get the unsupported length $L$. The unsupported length used in slenderness check is **clear height between beam–column joints**.

## 2.3 Design Equations for Combined Axial Load and Bending (IS 456:2000 Cl. 39.3)

The interaction equation for design is solved using **SP-16 Design Charts** (P–M interaction diagrams pre-computed for $f_{ck} = 15, 20, 25, 30, 35$ MPa and $f_y = 415, 500$ MPa). The two **non-dimensional parameters** read from the chart are:

$$P_u^{*}=\frac{P_u}{f_{ck}\,b\,D} \quad\text{and}\quad M_u^{*}=\frac{M_u}{f_{ck}\,b\,D^{2}}$$

where $b$ is the width and $D$ is the depth **in the plane of bending**.

The required percentage of steel is read from the appropriate SP-16 chart for a given $d'/D$ (effective cover ratio) and $f_y$. The number of bars and bar diameter are then chosen to provide $A_{sc} = p \cdot b \cdot D / 100$.

## 2.4 Biaxial Bending — Bresler's Reciprocal Load Method (Cl. 39.4)

For a column subjected to axial load $P_u$ and moments $M_{ux}$ and $M_{uy}$ about the two principal axes, **Bresler's reciprocal load equation** is:

$$\frac{1}{P_{uz}}=\frac{1}{P_{ux}}+\frac{1}{P_{uy}}-\frac{1}{P_{u0}}$$

where:
- $P_{uz}$ = ultimate axial capacity under biaxial bending (the unknown)
- $P_{ux}$ = capacity under axial load + uniaxial bending about x-axis only (i.e., applied $M_{uy} = 0$)
- $P_{uy}$ = capacity under axial load + uniaxial bending about y-axis only (i.e., applied $M_{ux} = 0$)
- $P_{u0}$ = pure axial capacity = $0.45\,f_{ck}\cdot A_c + 0.75\,f_y\cdot A_{sc}$

Once $P_{uz}$ is found, it must satisfy the **Corner Column Check (Cl. 39.4.1)**:

$$\left(\frac{M_{ux}}{M_{ux1}}\right)^{\alpha_n}+\left(\frac{M_{uy}}{M_{uy1}}\right)^{\alpha_n} \le 1.0$$

where $M_{ux1}, M_{uy1}$ are the uniaxial moment capacities for the same axial load $P_u$, and $\alpha_n$ is an exponent given in IS 456 Table 19 (typically $\alpha_n \approx 0.7$ for low axial loads and $1.0$ for high axial loads).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Clause |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{u,max} = 0.45\,f_{ck}\,A_c + 0.75\,f_y\,A_{sc}$ | Upper bound on axial capacity (max steel) | Cl. 39.3.1 |
| 2 | $P_{u,min} = 0.45\,f_{ck}\,A_g \cdot (1-0.008)$ | Lower bound check using gross area | Cl. 39.3.1 |
| 3 | $p/f_{ck}$ and $m/(f_{ck}\,h)$ | Non-dimensional parameters for SP-16 | SP-16 |
| 4 | $e_{min} = \max(0.05D, 20\text{ mm})$ | Minimum design eccentricity in any direction | Cl. 25.4 |
| 5 | $A_{sc} = p \cdot b \cdot D / 100$ | Steel area from percentage $p$ | — |
| 6 | $1/P_{uz} = 1/P_{ux} + 1/P_{uy} - 1/P_{u0}$ | Bresler's reciprocal load (biaxial) | Cl. 39.4 / SP-16 |
| 7 | $(M_{ux}/M_{ux1})^{\alpha_n} + (M_{uy}/M_{uy1})^{\alpha_n} \le 1.0$ | Corner column safety check | Cl. 39.4.1 |
| 8 | $A_{c,min} = P_u / (0.45\,f_{ck})$ | First-trial sizing of column | Quick design |
| 9 | $\sigma_{cc,allow} = 0.45\,f_{ck}$ | Permissible concrete stress (combined) | Cl. 39.1 |
| 10 | $\sigma_{sc,allow} = 0.75\,f_y$ | Permissible steel stress (compression) | SP-16 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The P–M interaction method using SP-16 is the **standard of practice** in Indian structural design offices. Software like **ETABS, SAP2000, SAFE** uses these same non-dimensional parameters internally to generate interaction surfaces. Mastering this module makes you directly employable in RCC consultancy firms across Kerala.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Numerical Problems and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 PROBLEM 1 — Uniaxial Bending (Full Numerical Solution)

**Problem Statement:**
Design a short rectangular column of size **400 mm × 600 mm** to carry a factored axial load $P_u = 1500\text{ kN}$ with a factored bending moment $M_{ux} = 200\text{ kN·m}$ about the major axis. Use **M25 concrete** and **Fe415 steel**. The column is **braced**, with both ends hinged, unsupported length $L = 4\text{ m}$. Effective cover $d' = 50\text{ mm}$.

### Step 1 — Verify Slenderness (Confirm "Short" Classification)

For both ends hinged, $l_{ex} = l_{ey} = 1.0 \cdot L = 4.0\text{ m}$.

$$\lambda_x = \frac{l_{ex}}{D} = \frac{4000}{600} = 6.67 \le 12 \;\;(\text{OK})$$

$$\lambda_y = \frac{l_{ey}}{b} = \frac{4000}{400} = 10.0 \le 12 \;\;(\text{OK})$$

The column is **short** → design by combined axial and bending using SP-16.

### Step 2 — Compute the Applied Moment Magnitude

We are given $M_{ux} = 200\text{ kN·m}$ directly (factored ULS moment from frame analysis).

### Step 3 — Compute Non-Dimensional Parameters for SP-16

Width $b = 400\text{ mm}$, depth $D = 600\text{ mm}$ (depth in plane of bending), $f_{ck} = 25\text{ N/mm}^2$, $f_y = 415\text{ N/mm}^2$, $d' = 50\text{ mm}$, hence $d'/D = 50/600 = 0.0833$.

$$P_u^{*} = \frac{P_u}{f_{ck}\cdot b\cdot D} = \frac{1500\times 10^3}{25 \times 400 \times 600}$$

$$P_u^{*} = \frac{1500000}{6000000} = 0.250$$

$$M_u^{*} = \frac{M_{ux}}{f_{ck}\cdot b\cdot D^{2}} = \frac{200\times 10^6}{25 \times 400 \times 600^{2}}$$

$$M_u^{*} = \frac{200\times 10^6}{3.6\times 10^9} = 0.0556$$

### Step 4 — Read SP-16 Chart

For the SP-16 chart corresponding to $d'/h = 0.10$ (closest standard) and $f_y = 415$ MPa, the intersection of $P_u^{*}=0.250$ and $M_u^{*}=0.0556$ lies on a curve. From the chart:

$$p/f_{ck} \approx 0.20$$

Therefore:

$$p = 0.20 \times 25 = 5.0\%$$

### Step 5 — Compute Required Steel Area

$$A_{sc} = \frac{p \cdot b \cdot D}{100} = \frac{5.0 \times 400 \times 600}{100} = 12000\text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 6 — Choose Bars

Number of 25 mm dia. bars giving $A_{sc}$: each bar area = $\pi/4 \times 25^2 = 490.87\text{ mm}^2$.

$$N = \frac{12000}{490.87} = 24.4 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Provide 24 bars of 25}\phi \text{ (not practical — redesign)}$$

The high steel % is impractical. **Re-design by increasing the section.**

### Step 7 — Re-design with Larger Section 450 × 600 mm

$\lambda_y = 4000/450 = 8.89 \le 12$ (OK).

$$P_u^{*}=\frac{1500\times 10^3}{25\times 450\times 600}=0.222$$

$$M_u^{*}=\frac{200\times 10^6}{25\times 450\times 600^{2}}=0.0494$$

From SP-16 chart for $d'/h=0.10$ and $f_y=415$ MPa: $p/f_{ck} \approx 0.08$, hence $p \approx 2.0\%$.

$$A_{sc} = \frac{2.0 \times 450 \times 600}{100} = 5400\text{ mm}^2$$

Provide **12 bars of 25 mm diameter**: total area = $12 \times 490.87 = 5890\text{ mm}^2$ ≈ $2.18\%$ of $A_g = 450 \times 600 = 270000\text{ mm}^2$. This is within the 0.8%–6% limit.

### Step 8 — Provide Lateral Ties

Diameter of ties $\ge \max(25/4, 6) = 6\text{ mm}$. Pitch $\le \min(300, 16\times 25) = 300\text{ mm}$ and $\le$ least lateral dimension = 450 mm. **Use 8 mm dia. ties @ 300 mm c/c**.

### Final Design Summary (Problem 1)

> **Column size: 450 × 600 mm; Reinforcement: 12 nos. 25 mm dia. longitudinal bars; Lateral ties: 8 mm dia. @ 300 mm c/c. Concrete M25, Steel Fe415.**

---

## 3.2 PROBLEM 2 — Biaxial Bending using Bresler's Formula

**Problem Statement:**
A **corner column** of size **400 mm × 500 mm** is subjected to a factored axial load $P_u = 1800\text{ kN}$ and factored moments $M_{ux} = 200\text{ kN·m}$ (about major axis) and $M_{uy} = 150\text{ kN·m}$ (about minor axis). The column has effective cover $d' = 50\text{ mm}$. Use **M25 concrete** and **Fe415 steel**, braced, both ends fixed, $L = 3\text{ m}$.

### Step 1 — Slenderness Check

For both ends fixed, $l_{ex} = l_{ey} = 0.65 L = 0.65 \times 3000 = 1950\text{ mm}$.

$$\lambda_x = \frac{1950}{500} = 3.9 \le 12 \;\;(\text{OK})$$

$$\lambda_y = \frac{1950}{400} = 4.875 \le 12 \;\;(\text{OK})$$

Column is short → use Bresler's reciprocal load method.

### Step 2 — Assume Initial Steel % and Compute Pure Axial Capacity $P_{u0}$

**Trial 1: Assume $p = 2.0\%$**, $A_{sc} = 0.02 \times 400 \times 500 = 4000\text{ mm}^2$.

Gross area $A_g = 400 \times 500 = 200000\text{ mm}^2$. Net concrete area $A_c = A_g - A_{sc} = 196000\text{ mm}^2$.

$$P_{u0} = 0.45\cdot f_{ck}\cdot A_c + 0.75\cdot f_y\cdot A_{sc}$$

$$P_{u0} = 0.45 \times 25 \times 196000 + 0.75 \times 415 \times 4000$$

$$P_{u0} = 2205000 + 1245000 = 3450000\text{ N} = 3450\text{ kN}$$

### Step 3 — Compute $P_{ux}$ (Axial Capacity with $M_{ux}$ applied, $M_{uy}=0$)

Using SP-16 chart with $b=400$, $D=500$, $M_{ux}=200$ kN·m:

$$P_{ux}^{*} = \frac{P_{ux}}{f_{ck}\cdot b\cdot D} = \frac{P_{ux}}{25\times 400\times 500} = \frac{P_{ux}}{5\times 10^6}$$

$$M_{ux}^{*} = \frac{200\times 10^6}{25\times 400\times 500^{2}} = 0.080$$

From SP-16 chart at $M_{ux}^{*}=0.080$ and $p=2.0\%$:

$$P_{ux}^{*} \approx 0.30 \;\Rightarrow\; P_{ux} = 0.30 \times 5\times 10^6 = 1500\text{ kN}$$

### Step 4 — Compute $P_{uy}$ (Axial Capacity with $M_{uy}$ applied, $M_{ux}=0$)

Now bending is about the y-axis, so depth in plane of bending becomes $b=400$ mm, width becomes $D=500$ mm.

$$M_{uy}^{*} = \frac{M_{uy}}{f_{ck}\cdot D\cdot b^{2}} = \frac{150\times 10^6}{25\times 500\times 400^{2}} = 0.075$$

From SP-16 chart (for $b=500, D=400$) at $M_{uy}^{*}=0.075$ and $p=2.0\%$:

$$P_{uy}^{*} \approx 0.32 \;\Rightarrow\; P_{uy} = 0.32 \times 25\times 500\times 400 = 1600\text{ kN}$$

### Step 5 — Apply Bresler's Reciprocal Load Equation

$$\frac{1}{P_{uz}} = \frac{1}{P_{ux}} + \frac{1}{P_{uy}} - \frac{1}{P_{u0}}$$

$$\frac{1}{P_{uz}} = \frac{1}{1500} + \frac{1}{1600} - \frac{1}{3450}$$

$$\frac{1}{P_{uz}} = 0.000667 + 0.000625 - 0.000290 = 0.001002$$

$$P_{uz} = \frac{1}{0.001002} = 998\text{ kN}$$

### Step 6 — Compare $P_{uz}$ with Applied $P_u$

Since $P_{uz} = 998\text{ kN} < P_u = 1800\text{ kN}$, the assumed section with $p=2.0\%$ is **inadequate**. Increase steel % and repeat.

### Step 7 — Re-iteration with $p = 4.0\%$

$A_{sc} = 0.04 \times 200000 = 8000\text{ mm}^2$, $A_c = 192000\text{ mm}^2$.

$$P_{u0} = 0.45\times 25\times 192000 + 0.75\times 415\times 8000 = 2160000 + 2490000 = 4650\text{ kN}$$

Reading SP-16 chart at higher $p=4.0\%$:

- $P_{ux} \approx 1900\text{ kN}$ (for $M_{ux}^{*}=0.080$)
- $P_{uy} \approx 2000\text{ kN}$ (for $M_{uy}^{*}=0.075$)

$$\frac{1}{P_{uz}} = \frac{1}{1900} + \frac{1}{2000} - \frac{1}{4650}$$

$$\frac{1}{P_{uz}} = 0.000526 + 0.000500 - 0.000215 = 0.000811$$

$$P_{uz} = 1233\text{ kN} < 1800\text{ kN} \;\;(\text{still inadequate})$$

### Step 8 — Final Iteration with $p = 6.0\%$ (Max Limit)

$A_{sc} = 0.06 \times 200000 = 12000\text{ mm}^2$, $A_c = 188000\text{ mm}^2$.

$$P_{u0} = 0.45\times 25\times 188000 + 0.75\times 415\times 12000 = 2115000 + 3735000 = 5850\text{ kN}$$

SP-16 chart reading at $p=6.0\%$:

- $P_{ux} \approx 2300\text{ kN}$
- $P_{uy} \approx 2400\text{ kN}$

$$\frac{1}{P_{uz}} = \frac{1}{2300} + \frac{1}{2400} - \frac{1}{5850}$$

$$\frac{1}{P_{uz}} = 0.000435 + 0.000417 - 0.000171 = 0.000681$$

$$P_{uz} = 1469\text{ kN} < 1800\text{ kN} \;\;(\text{still inadequate})$$

### Step 9 — Increase Column Size to 500 × 600 mm

**New section: 500 mm × 600 mm, $L = 3$ m, both ends fixed, $d' = 50$ mm, $p = 3.0\%$.**

$A_g = 500 \times 600 = 300000\text{ mm}^2$, $A_{sc} = 0.03 \times 300000 = 9000\text{ mm}^2$, $A_c = 291000\text{ mm}^2$.

$$P_{u0} = 0.45\times 25\times 291000 + 0.75\times 415\times 9000$$

$$P_{u0} = 3273750 + 2801250 = 6075000\text{ N} = 6075\text{ kN}$$

Bending about x-axis: depth = 600, width = 500. $M_{ux}^{*}=200\times 10^6/(25\times 500\times 600^2) = 0.0444$. From chart at $p=3.0\%$: $P_{ux} \approx 0.42 \times 25 \times 500 \times 600 = 3150\text{ kN}$.

Bending about y-axis: depth = 500, width = 600. $M_{uy}^{*}=150\times 10^6/(25\times 600\times 500^2) = 0.0400$. From chart at $p=3.0\%$: $P_{uy} \approx 0.44 \times 25 \times 600 \times 500 = 3300\text{ kN}$.

$$\frac{1}{P_{uz}} = \frac{1}{3150} + \frac{1}{3300} - \frac{1}{6075}$$

$$\frac{1}{P_{uz}} = 0.000317 + 0.000303 - 0.000165 = 0.000456$$

$$P_{uz} = 2194\text{ kN} > 1800\text{ kN} \;\;(\text{ADEQUATE})$$

### Step 10 — Corner Column Safety Check (Cl. 39.4.1)

For $P_u/f_{ck} b D = 1800000/(25 \times 500 \times 600) = 0.24$ and $p = 3.0\%$, read uniaxial moment capacities:

- $M_{ux1}$: From SP-16 with $P_u^{*}=0.24$, $p=3.0\%$, $M_{ux1}^{*} \approx 0.155$ → $M_{ux1} = 0.155 \times 25 \times 500 \times 600^2 = 697.5\text{ kN·m}$
- $M_{uy1}$: Similarly, $M_{uy1} \approx 700\text{ kN·m}$ (by symmetry of the cross-section & approximation, conservatively use 0.10 from chart)

Taking $\alpha_n = 0.7$ (interpolated from IS 456 Table 19 for $P_u/P_{u0} = 1800/6075 = 0.30$):

$$\left(\frac{200}{697.5}\right)^{0.7} + \left(\frac{150}{700}\right)^{0.7}$$

$$= (0.287)^{0.7} + (0.214)^{0.7}$$

$$= 0.401 + 0.331 = 0.732 \le 1.0 \;\;(\text{SAFE})$$

### Final Design Summary (Problem 2)

> **Corner column: 500 × 600 mm; Reinforcement: 12 nos. 32 mm dia. longitudinal bars (12 × 804 = 9648 mm² ≈ 3.2%); Lateral ties: 10 mm dia. @ 300 mm c/c. The Corner Column Check is satisfied with a factor of safety of 1/0.732 = 1.37.**

---

## 3.3 Summary of the Analytical Matrix (Design Iterations)

| Iteration | Section (mm) | $p$ (%) | $P_{u0}$ (kN) | $P_{ux}$ (kN) | $P_{uy}$ (kN) | $P_{uz}$ (kN) | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 400 × 500 | 2.0 | 3450 | 1500 | 1600 | 998 | Inadequate |
| 2 | 400 × 500 | 4.0 | 4650 | 1900 | 2000 | 1233 | Inadequate |
| 3 | 400 × 500 | 6.0 | 5850 | 2300 | 2400 | 1469 | Inadequate |
| 4 | 500 × 600 | 3.0 | 6075 | 3150 | 3300 | 2194 | **Adequate** |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Column Design Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START: Given Pu, Mux, Muy, fck, fy, L, end conditions] --> A1{Compute slenderness ratios lex/D and ley/b}
    A1 -- Both less than 12 --> A2[CLASSIFY AS SHORT COLUMN]
    A1 -- Exceeds 12 --> A1E[REDESIGN - increase section or reduce L]
    A2 --> A3[Compute minimum eccentricity e_min in both directions]
    A3 --> A4{Is Muy non-zero?}
    A4 -- NO --> B1[UNIAXIAL BENDING PATH]
    A4 -- YES --> C1[BIAXIAL BENDING PATH]
    B1 --> B2[Compute p/fck and m/fck-h non-dimensional parameters]
    B2 --> B3[Read SP-16 chart for required steel percentage p]
    B3 --> B4[Compute Asc = p.b.D per hundred]
    B4 --> B5[Choose bar diameter and number of bars]
    B5 --> B6[Check p between 0.8% and 6% of Ag]
    B6 --> B7[Provide lateral ties per Cl 25.5]
    B7 --> B8[FINAL DESIGN: UNIAXIAL COLUMN]
    C1 --> C2[Guess initial steel percentage p]
    C2 --> C3[Compute Pu0 = 0.45 fck Ac + 0.75 fy Asc]
    C3 --> C4[Read Pux from SP-16 with Mux only]
    C4 --> C5[Read Puy from SP-16 with Muy only]
    C5 --> C6[Apply Bresler reciprocal: 1/Puz = 1/Pux + 1/Puy - 1/Pu0]
    C6 --> C7{Puz greater than or equal to Pu?}
    C7 -- NO --> C8[Increase p or section size, iterate]
    C8 --> C2
    C7 -- YES --> C9[Apply Corner Column Check: Mux per Mux1 power alphan plus Muy per Muy1 power alphan less than or equal 1.0]
    C9 -- SAFE --> C10[Choose bar diameter and number of bars]
    C9 -- UNSAFE --> C8
    C10 --> C11[Provide lateral ties]
    C11 --> C12[FINAL DESIGN: BIAXIAL CORNER COLUMN]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of Forces on a Short Column

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SECTION[Column Cross Section at mid-height]
        N1[Axial Load Pu acting at centroid C]
        Mx[Bending Moment Mux about X axis]
        My[Bending Moment Muy about Y axis]
    end
    N1 --> R1[Resultant load Pu with eccentricities ex and ey]
    Mx --> R1
    My --> R1
    R1 --> R2[Equivalent uniaxial moment about resultant axis]
    R2 --> R3[SP-16 design via Bresler method]
```

## 4.3 Typical SP-16 Chart Reading Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Input: Pu, Mu, fck, fy, b, D, d prime] --> S2[Compute non-dimensional Pu asterisk and Mu asterisk]
    S2 --> S3[Select correct SP-16 chart for d prime per D and fy]
    S3 --> S4[Locate Pu asterisk on vertical axis and Mu asterisk on horizontal axis]
    S4 --> S5[Find curve of constant p per fck that passes through the point]
    S5 --> S6[Read p per fck value at the curve]
    S6 --> S7[Compute p = value times fck]
    S7 --> S8[Compute required Asc = p times b times D divided by 100]
    S8 --> S9[Output: Asc and number and diameter of bars]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above schematics map the **logical flow** of IS 456:2000 design decisions. Although the physical stress–strain and P–M interaction surfaces are 3-D in nature, the KTU-endorsed SP-16 methodology reduces them to a **2-D chart reading** using pre-computed non-dimensional parameters — making this an elegantly efficient office tool.
